1. A 7-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because of a 1-month history of urinary incontinence. During this time, he also has passed occasional hard, painful stools. He has not had pain with urination or changes in appetite. He has had occasional episodes of daytime enuresis at school since he was toilet trained at the age of 4 years; until last month, these episodes were rare. He has wet the bed four times weekly during the past month. His mother cannot identify any new stressors at home. He has no history of serious illness and receives no medications. Vaccinations are up-to-date. He receives grades of A’s in school. He is at the 25th percentile for height and weight and 35th percentile for BMI. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Administration of an osmotic laxative</br>b. Desmopressin therapy</br>c. Recommendation for use of a bed-wetting alarm</br>d. Renal ultrasonography</br>e. Reassurance that this is normal behavior</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>• Administration of an osmotic laxative (eg, polyethylene glycol)  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should jump out on first read:<br /></br>1. “1-month history of urinary incontinence” – a NEW problem in a child who had previously been mostly dry.<br /></br>2. “Passed occasional hard, painful stools” – classic wording for functional constipation / stool withholding.<br /></br>3. No dysuria, normal urinalysis – argues against UTI.<br /></br>4. Both daytime and nocturnal wetting – makes isolated primary nocturnal enuresis unlikely.<br /></br>5. Otherwise healthy, no stressors, growth normal, neuro exam normal – points away from psychosocial issues, neurologic defects, or structural renal disease.  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: In children, rectal distention from retained stool mechanically compresses the bladder and irritates the detrusor, producing urgency, frequency, and incontinence. This “bowel–bladder dysfunction” is one of the most common reversible causes of secondary enuresis.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for similar questions:<br /></br>• Anytime you see NEW urinary symptoms + constipation, think “treat the poop first.”<br /></br>• Normal UA + normal exam + constipation = give laxatives.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise reasoning:<br /></br>1. Secondary enuresis (loss of previously achieved continence) → look for an acquired trigger.<br /></br>2. Hard, painful stools → functional constipation → stool mass in the rectum can:<br /></br>   – Decrease bladder capacity (mechanical compression).<br /></br>   – Cause detrusor overactivity (irritation).<br /></br>   – Obstruct the urethra during voiding leading to incomplete emptying/overflow.<br /></br>3. Therefore, first-line action is to relieve constipation with an osmotic agent. If successful, the urinary symptoms usually remit.  </p></br><p>Take-home concept: Always evaluate bowel habits in any pediatric patient with urinary incontinence; laxative therapy often cures both problems.  </p></br><p>What would be documented in the chart?<br /></br>• Assessment: Secondary daytime &amp; nocturnal enuresis likely due to functional constipation. UA negative.<br /></br>• Plan: Polyethylene glycol 1–1.5 g/kg/day, high-fiber diet, timed toileting, follow-up in 2–4 weeks.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Functional constipation: Stool withholding → fecal retention → rectal distention → decreased rectal sensation &amp; overflow soiling. Distended rectum presses on posterior bladder wall → detrusor instability + bladder outlet obstruction → urinary accidents.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: low-fiber diet, inadequate fluid intake, behavioral stool withholding, painful stools, school toilet avoidance.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Management algorithm for bowel-bladder dysfunction:<br /></br>1. Disimpaction if needed (oral polyethylene glycol “clean-out” or enemas).<br /></br>2. Maintenance osmotic laxative (polyethylene glycol; alternatives: lactulose, magnesium hydroxide).<br /></br>3. Behavioral: scheduled toilet sits after meals, reward system, ↑water/fiber.<br /></br>4. Address urinary symptoms only if they persist after bowel regimen (anticholinergics, desmopressin, alarms, or urology referral).  </p></br><p>No imaging or lab studies are necessary unless red-flag signs appear (recurrent UTIs, hematuria, abnormal renal function, abnormal neuro exam).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“POOP pushes PEE”: Retained POOP compresses bladder → child PEE-s pants.<br /></br>“Constipation = incontinence until proven otherwise.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Desmopressin therapy – Appropriate for primary monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis in children ≥5 yr with NO daytime symptoms and normal bowel habits. This child has daytime accidents and documented constipation, so desmopressin treats the wrong mechanism.  </p></br><p>c. Bed-wetting alarm – First-line behavioral therapy for nocturnal enuresis, but would not address daytime incontinence or the underlying constipation.  </p></br><p>d. Renal ultrasonography – Indicated for structural abnormalities, hydronephrosis, recurrent febrile UTIs, or abnormal renal function. Normal UA, no infection or flank pain; non-invasive test but unnecessary here and delays effective treatment.  </p></br><p>e. Reassurance that this is normal behavior – New onset secondary enuresis at age 7 plus constipation is NOT normal; needs active management. Passive reassurance risks ongoing bladder dysfunction and possible UTIs.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In a school-age child with new urinary incontinence and hard, painful stools, treat constipation first with an osmotic laxative; this frequently resolves the urinary symptoms.</p>
2. A 14-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his mother because she has had difficulty awakening him in the morning since the start of the school year 3 months ago. She reports that last year he would always get up on his own. She now has to rouse him several times every morning. She says that he has the same group of friends as he did last year. When interviewed alone, the patient says that during the summer he discovered a talk show at 10 PM that he enjoys watching. He states that he is not tired at 10 PM, which was his bedtime last year. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Urine toxicology screening</br>b. Use of an over-the-counter sleeping medication as needed</br>c. Modafinil use in the morning</br>d. Zolpidem use at bedtime</br>e. No further treatment is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. No further treatment is indicated  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age 14 years → normal pubertal development brings a physiologic delay in the endogenous melatonin surge (“delayed sleep-phase physiology”).<br /></br>• New bedtime: 10 PM talk show; “not tired at 10 PM” → classic description of delayed sleep onset, not pathologic insomnia.<br /></br>• Only problem: trouble waking for school; no daytime sleep attacks, naps, cataplexy, mood change, or change in peer group (argues against substance use, depression, narcolepsy).<br /></br>• Physical exam normal.</p></br><p>Metacognitive framework for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Ask “Is this a pathologic sleep disorder or normal circadian shift?”<br /></br>2. Look for red-flag symptoms (daytime somnolence interfering with function, cataplexy, parasomnias, drug/alcohol clues).<br /></br>3. If none, reassure and recommend basic sleep-hygiene counseling; pharmacologic or laboratory interventions are unnecessary.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify the sleep complaint (difficulty awakening, not falling asleep).<br /></br>2. Link timing to adolescence and new bedtime habit.<br /></br>3. Exclude red flags → normal physiologic variant.<br /></br>Key takeaway: Most teenagers experience a shifted circadian rhythm; management is reassurance and sleep-hygiene education, not medications or tests.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real life): counsel on consistent sleep schedule, lights-off routine, avoiding electronics late, target 8–10 h sleep.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Puberty → melatonin secretion shifts ≈2 h later.<br /></br>• Adolescents therefore prefer later bedtimes/wake times (“night owls”).<br /></br>• Early school start times create a mismatch → morning sleep inertia.<br /></br>• Not associated with abnormal polysomnography or neurologic disease.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>First-line: education on sleep hygiene and consistent sleep/wake timing.<br /></br>No labs, imaging, stimulants, or hypnotics needed unless daytime functioning is significantly impaired or another sleep disorder is suspected.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Teens = Tired at Ten? Think again!”<br /></br>• Teenagers naturally fall asleep ≈Two hours later (T = Two-hour shift).<br /></br>Remember: Only Treat when Trouble persists despite good hygiene.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Urine toxicology screening<br /></br>• Would be appropriate if there were personality change, new friends, mood lability, or physical signs of drug use. None are present.</p></br><p>b. Over-the-counter sleep medication as needed (e.g., diphenhydramine, melatonin)<br /></br>• Unnecessary; could cause next-day grogginess and does not correct the underlying physiologic delay.</p></br><p>c. Modafinil in the morning<br /></br>• A wake-promoting agent for narcolepsy, shift-work disorder, or obstructive sleep apnea residual somnolence—not indicated when sleep deprivation is self-imposed.</p></br><p>d. Zolpidem at bedtime<br /></br>• Hypnotic reserved for short-term insomnia in adults; carries risk of dependence and abnormal behaviors. Not approved in children and unnecessary here.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A 14-year-old who stays up later and struggles to wake for school most likely exhibits normal adolescent delayed sleep phase. Provide reassurance and sleep-hygiene advice; no medications or tests are required.</p>
3. An asymptomatic 37-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination after an HIV antibody test was positive. Her last HIV antibody test 5 years ago was negative. She has no previous history of sexually transmitted disease. During the past 10 years, she has been sexually active with eight male partners; she uses an oral contraceptive. She does not know of any partners who are HIV positive. She exercises regularly. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. A complete blood count and serum electrolyte, urea nitrogen, and creatinine concentrations are within the reference range. Her CD4+ T-lymphocyte count is 410/mm3 (Normal≥500), and plasma HIV viral load is 30,000 copies/mL. Urine pregnancy test is negative. Serologic testing for hepatitis and a PPD skin test are negative. Liver function tests show no abnormalities. Antiretroviral therapy is begun. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to prevent infections in this patient?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Fluconazole therapy</br>b. Isoniazid therapy</br>c. Pyrimethamine and sulfamethoxazole therapy</br>d. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole therapy</br>e. 23-Valent pneumococcal vaccine</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. 23-Valent pneumococcal vaccine  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Newly diagnosed HIV infection, asymptomatic.<br /></br>• CD4⁺ count = 410 cells/mm³ (mild immunosuppression; above 200).<br /></br>• All baseline blood work, hepatitis serology, and PPD are negative/normal.<br /></br>• Antiretroviral therapy (ART) has already been started.  </p></br><p>How these clues narrow the question:<br /></br>1. Because CD4⁺ is &gt;200, she does NOT yet meet thresholds for most opportunistic‐infection drug prophylaxis.<br /></br>2. The stem specifically asks for “the most appropriate next step to prevent infections,” which shifts the focus from treatment of opportunistic infections to routine preventive care (vaccination).<br /></br>3. In HIV care, the next evidence-based preventive step for every newly diagnosed patient with CD4⁺ &gt;200 is immunization with the pneumococcal vaccines (PCV13 followed by PPSV23 ≥8 weeks later, or PPSV23 if PCV13 already received). NBME often simplifies this to “23-valent pneumococcal vaccine.”  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When the CD4 count is &gt;200, think “vaccinate, screen, counsel” rather than “chemoprophylaxis.” Reserve drug prophylaxis decisions for specific CD4 thresholds or positive screening tests.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-by-Step)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Confirm baseline immune status → CD4 410: no need yet for PCP (Pneumocystis) or MAC prophylaxis.  </li></br><li>Review infection-prevention checklist for all HIV patients:<br /></br>   • Vaccinations: influenza (annual), Tdap, HBV, HPV (≤45 yr), meningococcal, HAV (if risk), and pneumococcal (PCV13 then PPSV23).<br /></br>   • Latent TB: INH only if PPD/IGRA positive or high-risk exposure.<br /></br>   • Opportunistic infection drug prophylaxis: use CD4 cut-offs (≤200, ≤100, ≤50).  </li></br><li>Only option listed that applies universally at CD4 410 is pneumococcal vaccination.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: At diagnosis of HIV, administer age-appropriate inactivated vaccines—particularly pneumococcal—regardless of CD4 count (as long as &gt;200).  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In the chart you would record consent, vaccine lot #, site, and schedule follow-up dose, as well as patient education about expected arm soreness/fever.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• HIV infection impairs humoral immunity early, increasing risk for encapsulated bacteria, especially Streptococcus pneumoniae.<br /></br>• Polysaccharide (23-valent) vaccine induces B-cell–mediated opsonizing antibodies against 23 common serotypes.<br /></br>• Giving it before severe CD4 decline maximizes immune response.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm Summary  </p></br><p>Opportunistic prophylaxis thresholds (memorize):<br /></br>• CD4 &lt;200: TMP-SMX for Pneumocystis jirovecii ± toxoplasmosis if IgG+.<br /></br>• CD4 &lt;100 &amp; Toxoplasma IgG+: TMP-SMX double strength.<br /></br>• CD4 &lt;50: Azithromycin weekly for Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC).<br /></br>• Positive PPD/IGRA or known exposure: Isoniazid + B6 for 9 months, regardless of CD4.<br /></br>None of these apply at CD4 410.  </p></br><p>Vaccines in HIV (all inactivated):<br /></br>“IMMUNE SHIP” mnemonic<br /></br>I – Influenza annually (inactivated)<br /></br>M – Meningococcal (MenACWY q5 yr)<br /></br>M – MMR if CD4 &gt;200 &amp; seronegative (live: defer if &lt;200)<br /></br>U – HBV (if not immune)<br /></br>N – HPV through age 45<br /></br>E – HAV if MSM/chronic liver disease<br /></br>S – Shingles (Shingrix, recombinant)<br /></br>H – HiB if not immune (asplenia, etc.)<br /></br>I – IPV if needed<br /></br>P – Pneumococcal (PCV13 → PPSV23 8 wk later)  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Fluconazole therapy<br /></br>• Used for candidal esophagitis treatment or prophylaxis in certain transplant patients. Routine primary prophylaxis against candidiasis is NOT recommended in HIV, even at low CD4, because oral/topical therapy is effective if infection occurs and fluconazole resistance/toxicity can develop.  </p></br><p>b. Isoniazid therapy<br /></br>• Appropriate only if latent TB screen is positive (PPD ≥5 mm or positive IGRA) or recent exposure. Her PPD is negative. Giving INH now would be unnecessary and carries hepatotoxic risk.  </p></br><p>c. Pyrimethamine and sulfamethoxazole therapy<br /></br>• (Often worded as pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin) indicated for treatment or secondary prophylaxis of toxoplasmosis. Primary prophylaxis is instead TMP-SMX when CD4 &lt;100 and Toxo IgG+. She is IgG-unknown and CD4 410.  </p></br><p>d. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole therapy<br /></br>• First-line prophylaxis against Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and toxoplasmosis, but only when CD4 &lt;200 or oropharyngeal candidiasis present. Starting it now is premature and exposes her to sulfa hypersensitivity risk.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><ol></br><li>“200-100-50” ladder for prophylaxis drugs.<br /></br>   • 200 → PCP/Toxo (TMP-SMX)<br /></br>   • 100 → continuation, possibly double-strength<br /></br>   • 50 → MAC (Azithro)  </li></br><li>“Vaccines first, pills later” for CD4 &gt;200.  </li></br><li>Pneumo sequence: “13 before 23—teen before adult.” Give PCV13, then PPSV23 ≥8 wk after.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Visualization: Picture a new HIV patient climbing a staircase. At the top (CD4 &gt;200) she puts on her armor (vaccines). As she descends to 200 she picks up a TMP-SMX shield; at 50 she grabs an azithro helmet. The earlier she puts on armor, the fewer arrows (encapsulated bacteria) can strike her.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>For an asymptomatic HIV-positive adult with CD4⁺ 410, the single most important next preventive step is immunization with the 23-valent pneumococcal vaccine (following PCV13 if not yet given). Drug prophylaxis against opportunistic infections is deferred until lower CD4 thresholds or positive screening tests are reached.</p>
4. A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-week history of progressive left-sided weakness. Six months ago, she underwent an uncomplicated pancreatic transplant because of type 1 diabetes mellitus. Her medications are mycophenolate mofetil and prednisone. Vital signs are within normal limits. Muscle strength is 4/5 in the left lower aspect of the face and left upper extremity. Deep tendon reflexes are increased on the left. Sensation is intact. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. An MRI of the brain shows a homogenously enhancing right frontotemporal mass with surrounding edema. A biopsy specimen of the lesion shows proliferation of monoclonal B lymphocytes. Which of the following is the most likely causal organism?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Cytomegalovirus</br>b. Epstein-Barr virus</br>c. Human herpesvirus 6</br>d. JC virus</br>e. Varicella-zoster virus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Epstein–Barr virus (EBV)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues:<br /></br>  – Solid-organ transplant 6 months ago → chronically immunosuppressed (mycophenolate + prednisone).<br /></br>  – Subacute, progressive focal neurologic deficit (left facial/arm weakness, ↑DTRs) → right hemispheric lesion.<br /></br>  – MRI: single, homogeneously enhancing right frontotemporal mass with vasogenic edema.<br /></br>  – Biopsy: “proliferation of monoclonal B lymphocytes.”  </p></br><p>• Pattern recognition: In an immunocompromised host, a solitary, homogeneously enhancing brain mass composed of monoclonal B cells = primary CNS lymphoma / post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD). Classic etiologic trigger = latent EBV reactivation.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for future questions:<br /></br>1. Ask: “Is the patient immunocompromised?”<br /></br>2. If yes and focal neuro findings → obtain imaging.<br /></br>3. Solitary, uniformly enhancing lesion of B-cell origin → think EBV-driven CNS lymphoma/PTLD.<br /></br>   (Multiple ring-enhancing lesions = usually Toxoplasma; non-enhancing white-matter lesions = JC virus/PML.)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• EBV infects B cells and establishes latency.<br /></br>• Normal cytotoxic T cells keep EBV-infected B cells in check.<br /></br>• Immunosuppression (transplant drugs, HIV/AIDS) removes this control ⇒ uncontrolled monoclonal B-cell proliferation manifesting as:<br /></br>  – PTLD (systemic nodes, GI, lung)<br /></br>  – Isolated primary CNS lymphoma.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: EBV reactivation in the setting of T-cell immunosuppression is the primary driver of PTLD/primary CNS lymphoma.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-life): note timing after transplant, immunosuppressive regimen, new focal neuro deficit, MRI findings, and plan for reducing immunosuppression ± rituximab.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• EBV = γ-herpesvirus; infects B cells via CD21.<br /></br>• Gene products (LMP-1, EBNA-2) drive B-cell proliferation; usually controlled by CD8+ T cells.<br /></br>• When T-cell surveillance is blunted (cyclosporine, tacrolimus, mycophenolate, steroids, HIV), proliferation proceeds unchecked → monoclonal lymphoma.<br /></br>• CNS lesions often periventricular, basal ganglia, corpus callosum; avid, homogeneous contrast enhancement; restricted diffusion on DWI.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment<br /></br>• Diagnostic work-up: MRI ± PET, CSF cytology, stereotactic biopsy (confirms monoclonal B-cell population &amp; EBV-encoded RNA in situ hybridization).<br /></br>• Management:<br /></br>  1. LOWER or stop immunosuppression if possible.<br /></br>  2. Rituximab (anti-CD20), ± methotrexate-based chemo or whole-brain radiation.<br /></br>  3. Prognosis improves if recognized early and immunosuppression is tapered.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“EBV = Every B-cell Virus” → unchecked B-cell growth when T-cells are knocked out.<br /></br>PTLD mnemonic:<br /></br>P = Post-transplant<br /></br>T = T-cell suppression<br /></br>L = Lymphoma (B-cell, EBV-driven)<br /></br>D = Don’t forget to drop the drugs (reduce immunosuppression).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Cytomegalovirus – causes ventriculo-encephalitis, retinitis, colitis in transplant pts; pathology shows intranuclear “owl-eye” inclusions, not monoclonal B-cell proliferation. Brain lesions are often periventricular inflammation rather than a solid tumor.  </p></br><p>c. Human herpesvirus-6 – reactivation → limbic encephalitis (seizures, memory loss) or marrow suppression after BMT; MRI shows bilateral medial temporal T2 hyperintensity, not a solid, enhancing mass of B cells.  </p></br><p>d. JC virus – produces progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML): non-enhancing demyelinating lesions in white matter comprised of oligodendrocyte infection; no mass effect, no monoclonal B-cell proliferation.  </p></br><p>e. Varicella-zoster virus – reactivation in CNS leads to vasculopathy (stroke), meningitis, or radiculitis; imaging shows multifocal infarcts or enhancement along cranial nerves, not a homogeneously enhancing tumor.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only EBV is consistently linked to monoclonal B-cell CNS tumors in immunosuppressed patients.</p>
5. A previously healthy 52-year-old woman is brought to the physician because of a 1-month history of progressive fatigue and low back pain. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows diffuse tenderness to fist percussion over the lumbar spine. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit            30 percent</br>Leukocyte count       9500/mm³</br>  Segmented neutrophils 65 percent</br>  Bands                5 percent</br>  Lymphocytes          10 percent</br>  Monocytes            20 percent</br>Platelet count        200,000/mm³</br></br>Serum</br>Ca2+                  14 mg/dL</br>Urea nitrogen         60 mg/dL</br>Urine protein         4+</br></br>A protein electrophoresis shows monoclonal gammopathy. X-rays of the back show severe osteoporosis of the lumbar and thoracic spine without fracture. Examination of the bone marrow shows sheets of plasma cells. Cytogenetic analysis of the bone marrow shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is most likely to be increased in this patient?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Erythroid stem cells</br>b. Megakaryocytic stem cells</br>c. Monoclonal B-lymphocytes</br>d. Monoclonal T-lymphocytes</br>e. Myeloid stem cells</br>f. Pluripotent stem cells</br>g. Polyclonal B-lymphocytes</br>h. Polyclonal T-lymphocytes</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Monoclonal B-lymphocytes  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis (How to dissect this stem quickly)<br /></br>1. Demographics: 52-year-old woman (middle-aged/older adult = plasma cell disorders rise).<br /></br>2. Key “CRAB” findings of multiple myeloma:<br /></br>   • C = HyperCalcemia (serum Ca²⁺ 14 mg/dL)<br /></br>   • R = Renal insufficiency (BUN 60 mg/dL, 4+ proteinuria from light chains)<br /></br>   • A = Anemia (Hct 30 %)<br /></br>   • B = Bone pain/lytic disease (low-back pain, diffuse spine tenderness, osteoporosis on x-ray).<br /></br>3. Confirmatory data:<br /></br>   • Serum/urine monoclonal spike (electrophoresis).<br /></br>   • Bone-marrow biopsy: “sheets of plasma cells.”<br /></br>   • Cytogenetics: “no abnormalities” does not change the diagnosis—some myelomas are cytogenetically silent.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework: When a question shows CRAB + monoclonal spike + plasma cell infiltrate, think “multiple myeloma → malignant, monoclonal, terminally-differentiated B-cell (plasma cell) proliferation.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-by-step logic linking stem to answer:<br /></br>1. Identify disease = multiple myeloma.<br /></br>2. Ask: “What cellular population is neoplastically expanded in myeloma?” Answer = plasma cells.<br /></br>3. Plasma cells originate from B-lymphocytes and, in neoplasia, are all derived from a single clone → monoclonal B-cell lineage.<br /></br>Therefore, the cell population increased is monoclonal B-lymphocytes.  </p></br><p>Key takeaway: Multiple myeloma = clonal proliferation of plasma cells (terminal B-cells) producing a single immunoglobulin species or light chain.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl (real life): You would record evidence of CRAB criteria, monoclonal spike level, % plasma cells in marrow, renal function, and skeletal survey.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Etiology: Ig heavy-chain translocations (t(11;14), etc.) or hyperdiploidy; IL-6 drives proliferation.<br /></br>• Mechanisms of symptoms:<br /></br>  – Osteolysis → ↑RANKL, osteoclast activation → hyperCa²⁺, bone pain.<br /></br>  – Light-chain (Bence-Jones) deposition &amp; cast nephropathy → renal failure, proteinuria.<br /></br>  – Marrow crowding &amp; cytokines → normocytic anemia.<br /></br>• Immunology: Single clone secretes identical IgG or IgA (less often IgD, IgE) or free κ/λ light chains; produces an “M-spike” on SPEP.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests snapshot<br /></br>Diagnostics: SPEP/UPEP with immunofixation, free light-chain assay, skeletal survey (lytic “punched-out” lesions), bone marrow ≥10 % plasma cells.<br /></br>Management first-line:<br /></br>• Triplet induction (bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone) → autologous stem-cell transplant in eligible patients.<br /></br>• Bisphosphonate (e.g., zoledronic acid) for bone disease.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“CRAB MM eats bones.”<br /></br>C = Calcium ↑<br /></br>R = Renal failure<br /></br>A = Anemia<br /></br>B = Bone pain/lytic lesions<br /></br>MM = Monoclonal Myeloma of plasma (B) cells.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Erythroid stem cells – would be decreased or suppressed, causing anemia.<br /></br>b. Megakaryocytic stem cells – platelet count is normal; no proliferation here.<br /></br>d. Monoclonal T-lymphocytes – plasma cells are B-cell lineage, not T-cell.<br /></br>e. Myeloid stem cells – myeloid series (neutrophils, etc.) normal; myeloproliferative disorders present differently (↑WBC, splenomegaly).<br /></br>f. Pluripotent stem cells – involvement here would produce pan-cytosis/pancytopenia, not isolated plasma-cell proliferation.<br /></br>g. Polyclonal B-lymphocytes – reactive (e.g., infection) processes show a broad-based spike, not a sharp M-spike; bone marrow would not show sheets of identical cells.<br /></br>h. Polyclonal T-lymphocytes – same reactive concept; unrelated to plasma-cell myeloma.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: confusing “myeloid” (granulocytes/erythrocytes) with “myeloma” (plasma cells). Remember: “Myeloma = B cell malignancy.”</p>
6. A 22-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of yellowing skin and increasing shortness of breath on exertion. She has not had abdominal pain or weight loss. She does not smoke cigarettes or use illicit drugs. She drinks one to two beers on weekends. Her pulse is 80/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Examination shows scleral icterus and jaundice. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Abdominal examination shows a liver span of 10 cm, and the spleen tip is palpated 3 cm below the left costal margin. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Her hematocrit is 20 percent, and reticulocyte count is 10 percent. Additional serum studies are most likely to show which of the following?  </br>Indirect Bilirubin (mg/dL) Lactate Dehydrogenase (U/L) Alkaline Phosphatase (U/L)	Correct answer: G</br></br>a. 0.5 50 50   </br>b. 0.5 50 500   </br>c. 0.5 500 50   </br>d. 0.5 500 500   </br>e. 3.5 50 50   </br>f. 3.5 50 500   </br>g. 3.5 500 50   </br>h. 3.5 500 500</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>g. 3.5 mg/dL indirect bilirubin  500 U/L LDH  50 U/L alkaline phosphatase  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 22-year-old otherwise healthy woman → congenital hemolytic processes more likely than liver disease or malignant obstruction.<br /></br>   • 2-week history of “yellowing skin” + scleral icterus → jaundice.<br /></br>   • Severe normovolemic anemia (Hct 20%) with a high reticulocyte count (10%) → marrow response to peripheral RBC loss/destruction.<br /></br>   • Palpable spleen tip → splenic hyperactivity from clearing abnormal/damaged RBCs (extravascular hemolysis).<br /></br>   • Normal liver size, no RUQ pain, no cholestatic symptoms (pruritus, pale stools, dark urine).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Positive: jaundice, splenomegaly, reticulocytosis.<br /></br>   • Negative: no abdominal pain, pruritus, dark urine, or elevated liver span → argues against hepatocellular injury or biliary obstruction.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Diagnostic pattern<br /></br>   • Classic “hemolytic jaundice” triad: anemia + ↑ unconjugated bilirubin + normal LFTs except LDH.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Strategic approach:<br /></br>   1. Categorize jaundice (pre-hepatic, hepatic, post-hepatic).<br /></br>   2. Look for hemolysis markers (retics ↑, LDH ↑, haptoglobin ↓, splenomegaly).<br /></br>   3. Match pattern to answer grid.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Hemolysis releases hemoglobin. Heme → unconjugated bilirubin (indirect), rapidly loads the liver’s conjugation capacity → isolated indirect hyperbilirubinemia. Cellular breakdown also releases LDH (predominantly LDH-1 found in RBCs). Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) rises only with cholestasis or bone turnover, neither present here; thus ALP remains normal (≈50 U/L).  </p></br><p>Take-home concept:<br /></br>Any patient with 1) anemia, 2) reticulocytosis, 3) splenomegaly, and 4) isolated indirect hyperbilirubinemia = hemolysis until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): record severity of anemia, evidence of hemolysis (LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin fractions), and plan for etiology work-up (peripheral smear, Coombs, osmotic fragility, etc.).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Extravascular hemolysis (e.g., hereditary spherocytosis, warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia):<br /></br>• RBCs destroyed in spleen → splenomegaly.<br /></br>• Heme catabolism → ↑ indirect bilirubin → jaundice, pigmented gallstones long-term.<br /></br>• LDH spills into plasma from lysed RBCs.<br /></br>• Haptoglobin binds free Hb; therefore serum haptoglobin levels fall (not tested in grid).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics (high-yield quick review)  </p></br><p>Immediate: folic acid supplementation, treat underlying trigger.<br /></br>Specific etiologies:<br /></br>• Hereditary spherocytosis → splenectomy if severe; watch for parvovirus aplastic crises.<br /></br>• Autoimmune hemolysis → steroids, IVIG, rituximab, splenectomy if refractory.<br /></br>Work-up: CBC with smear (spherocytes, schistocytes), direct Coombs, osmotic fragility, cold agglutinin titers, G6PD level if suspect episodic hemolysis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Pre-hepatic HEMO-Lysis”:<br /></br>H – High LDH<br /></br>E – Elevated unconjugated Bilirubin<br /></br>M – More retics<br /></br>O – OK Alkaline phosphatase (normal)  </p></br><p>Mnemonic to separate cholestasis vs hemolysis:<br /></br>ALP is “ALP-high” in obstALPruction (obstruction) but “ALP-fine” in hemo-ly$in’.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. 0.5/50/50 – Normal bilirubin &amp; enzymes: would not explain visible jaundice.<br /></br>b. 0.5/50/500 – Normal bilirubin but high ALP: pattern of cholestasis without jaundice—clinically inconsistent.<br /></br>c. 0.5/500/50 – High LDH only: you would not see jaundice with normal bilirubin.<br /></br>d. 0.5/500/500 – Same bilirubin issue plus cholestasis pattern.<br /></br>e. 3.5/50/50 – Correct bilirubin but LDH too low for active hemolysis.<br /></br>f. 3.5/50/500 – Hyperbilirubinemia with cholestatic ALP elevation, inconsistent with normal exam &amp; hemolysis clues.<br /></br>h. 3.5/500/500 – Would indicate mixed hemolysis + cholestasis (e.g., sickle cell with biliary obstruction); no signs of biliary disease present.  </p></br><p>Only option g pairs isolated indirect hyperbilirubinemia with hemolysis-level LDH and normal ALP, fitting the vignette perfectly.</p>
7. A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his family because of bizarre behavior for 3 hours. He has a 10-year history of schizophrenia treated with olanzapine. He and his family are driving across the country, and he was seen in another emergency department last night for an episode of agitation; his agitation resolved after intramuscular administration of haloperidol. On arrival, he is moaning and agitated and lies on the examination table with his arms held rigidly at his side. The mouth is open, and there is maximal upward deviation of the eyes. When asked to try to relax and look at the examiner, the patient says he cannot. His pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 154/86 mm Hg. Examination shows muscular rigidity. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. CT scan of the head</br>b. Administration of diphenhydramine</br>c. Administration of haloperidol</br>d. Administration of trazodone</br>e. EEG</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Administration of diphenhydramine (25–50 mg IV/IM)</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that immediately narrow the differential to an acute drug-induced dystonic reaction:<br /></br>• Recent exposure to a high-potency dopamine D2 antagonist – intramuscular haloperidol given the night before.<br /></br>• Very short latency (hours) from drug exposure to symptoms.<br /></br>• Sustained, involuntary muscle contraction patterns:<br /></br>  – “Arms held rigidly at his side” (axial/appendicular dystonia).<br /></br>  – “Mouth is open” and “maximal upward deviation of the eyes” → classic oculogyric crisis.<br /></br>• Patient remains fully awake and is able to answer (“I cannot”), arguing against a seizure or structural brain lesion.<br /></br>• Mild tachycardia/HTN are typical due to pain/anxiety.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Ask “What medication was given recently?”<br /></br>2. Know the four extrapyramidal syndromes (EPS) time course: dystonia (&lt; 24 h), akathisia (days), parkinsonism (weeks), tardive dyskinesia (months-years).<br /></br>3. Acute dystonia → give an anticholinergic (benztropine) or antihistamine with anticholinergic properties (diphenhydramine).</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-by-step linkage:<br /></br>• Dopamine blockade in the nigrostriatal pathway by haloperidol → relative cholinergic excess → sustained muscle contraction.<br /></br>• First-line reversal = restore central dopaminergic/cholinergic balance with an anticholinergic (benztropine) or antihistamine (diphenhydramine).<br /></br>Take-home: Any acute focal or generalized muscle rigidity (esp. neck, jaw, eyes) within hours of a typical antipsychotic = treat STAT with IV/IM diphenhydramine or benztropine.</p></br><p>Documentation if real patient: “Likely haloperidol-induced acute dystonia. Administered 50 mg diphenhydramine IV with rapid symptom resolution.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Potent D2 blockade (haloperidol, fluphenazine, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine).<br /></br>• Mechanism: ↓ dopamine → relative ↑ acetylcholine in striatum → excessive excitation of motor output → sustained contractions.<br /></br>• Risk factors: Young males, high-potency agents, parenteral dosing, prior dystonic reactions.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><ol></br><li>Immediate therapy<br /></br>   • Diphenhydramine 25–50 mg IV/IM  (or) Benztropine 1–2 mg IV/IM.<br /></br>   • Symptoms usually abate within minutes.  </li></br><li>Prevention<br /></br>   • Lower dose or switch to lower-potency (e.g., olanzapine, quetiapine).<br /></br>   • Consider scheduled oral benztropine for 2–3 days after reaction.</li></br></ol></br><p>No imaging or labs are needed unless presentation is atypical.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“ABC of EPS”<br /></br>A – Acute dystonia → Benztropine/Benadryl<br /></br>B – akathisia → ß-blocker (propranolol)<br /></br>C – Parkinsonism → Cogwheel rigidity → benztropine/amantadine<br /></br>D – Tardive Dyskinesia → Done with dopamine blocker (stop/switch), consider Deutetrabenazine</p></br><p>Another quick hook: “B for Benadryl fixes Bizarre posturing.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. CT scan of the head<br /></br>   • Would be considered for focal neurologic deficits, altered LOC, or signs of intracranial hemorrhage/stroke. Here the classic EPS timeline + exam makes drug-induced dystonia far more likely; imaging delays definitive therapy.</p></br><p>c. Administration of haloperidol<br /></br>   • Gives more D2 blockade → worsens dystonia. Occasionally used for agitation, but contraindicated during an EPS event.</p></br><p>d. Administration of trazodone<br /></br>   • Sedative antidepressant; does not treat EPS and may cause additional hypotension/sedation.</p></br><p>e. EEG<br /></br>   • Evaluates seizures or encephalopathy. Patient is awake, interactive, and manifesting sustained muscle contraction—not rhythmic seizure activity.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>Acute dystonia occurs within hours after exposure to high-potency dopamine antagonists. The fastest, safest treatment is an anticholinergic agent—benztropine or diphenhydramine—administered parenterally for rapid relief.</p>
8. A 25-year-old man who is a nursing student comes to the office because of anxiety about his ability to pass his courses. He says that yesterday, during his first practical session, he had what he thought was a panic attack at the sight of blood. He says, "My heart began racing. I felt light-headed, and I almost got sick to my stomach." He says he has always felt squeamish at the sight of blood but thought he would eventually get used to it. He does not think he can be a nurse unless he receives help for this problem. He worries about how he will pay back his student loans and how he will explain his failure to his family. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he appears anxious and has a full range of affect. His thought process and content are clear and goal directed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Acute stress disorder</br>b. Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition</br>c. Generalized anxiety disorder</br>d. Panic disorder</br>e. Social anxiety disorder (social phobia)</br>f. Specific phobia</br>g. Normal reaction to a stressful event</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>f. Specific phobia (“blood-injection-injury” type)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that steer you away from the broad anxiety differential and toward a single phobia:</br>• Trigger is discrete and circumscribed – “at the sight of blood.”<br /></br>• Reproducible physiologic response – heart racing, light-headedness, nausea.<br /></br>• Lifelong pattern – “He has always felt squeamish at the sight of blood.”<br /></br>• Anticipatory anxiety/avoidance – now doubts entire nursing career unless treated.<br /></br>• Normal physical exam and baseline vitals – rules out medical cause.<br /></br>Cognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>1. Ask “Are the attacks unexpected or situation-bound?”<br /></br>2. If bound to one stimulus → consider specific phobia.<br /></br>3. Identify which phobia subtype (animal, natural environment, situational, blood-injection-injury).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Panic-like episode occurred, but ONLY when he saw blood.<br /></br>2. No history of spontaneous (unexpected) panic attacks.<br /></br>3. Major worry revolves around having to encounter the trigger in nursing school, not about myriad life domains → rules out GAD.<br /></br>4. Fear is not about scrutiny or embarrassment by others → not social anxiety.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Panic symptoms can occur in many disorders; timing relative to a specific stimulus is diagnostic for specific phobia.</p></br><p>Real-world documentation points: document the exact trigger, physiologic reaction, degree of functional impairment, and absence of broader anxiety phenomena.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Specific phobia = excessive, persistent (&gt;6 mo) fear of a clearly defined object or situation.<br /></br>• Blood-injection-injury subtype is unique: often produces biphasic sympathetic surge followed by vagal drop → fainting/light-headedness.<br /></br>• Learned via classical conditioning (past negative experience) or modeling; maintained by avoidance (negative reinforcement).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>No labs/imaging required.<br /></br>First-line therapy:<br /></br>1. Graduated exposure therapy (systematic desensitization).<br /></br>2. For blood-injection-injury, “applied tension” technique to counteract vasovagal syncope.<br /></br>3. Short-term benzodiazepine only if exposure therapy unavailable and acute exposure unavoidable (e.g., surgery), but not first-line.<br /></br>SSRIs have limited role unless comorbid mood/anxiety disorder exists.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>ACRONYM “FOBIA” for Specific Phobia Features<br /></br>F – Focused fear (one object/situation)<br /></br>O – Out of proportion to actual danger<br /></br>B – Beyond 6 months<br /></br>I – Immediate anxiety response<br /></br>A – Avoidance/anticipatory anxiety leading to impairment</p></br><p>Visual cue: Picture a syringe dripping blood spelling “BIIB” (Blood-Injection-Injury Phobia).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Acute stress disorder – Requires exposure to actual or threatened death/serious injury followed by intrusive memories, dissociation, etc., lasting 3 days-1 mo. Merely seeing blood in lab is not traumatic exposure.<br /></br>b. Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition – Normal vitals, exam, and no underlying illness point away from this.<br /></br>c. Generalized anxiety disorder – Characterized by ≥6 mo of excessive worry about multiple events (school, health, finances) plus ≥3 somatic symptoms (restlessness, muscle tension, insomnia). Here, anxiety is stimulus-specific.<br /></br>d. Panic disorder – Must have recurrent, unexpected panic attacks and ≥1 mo of persistent worry about additional attacks. His episode is situational (expected) and there is no history of spontaneous attacks.<br /></br>e. Social anxiety disorder – Fear of scrutiny, humiliation, or negative evaluation during social or performance situations (e.g., public speaking). His fear is of blood itself, not of being judged.<br /></br>g. Normal reaction to a stressful event – Symptoms are excessive and lead to functional impairment (career derailment), exceeding a normal reaction.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Panic symptoms that occur exclusively in response to a single, well-defined stimulus = specific phobia. Recognizing the relationship between trigger and physiologic response is the key diagnostic step on NBME/Step 2 questions.</p>
9. A 27-year-old man with an 8-year history of bipolar disorder comes to the physician because of depressed mood for 6 weeks. He reports that he no longer has an interest in his job as a salesclerk or in recreational activities. He has had a decreased appetite and low level of energy. He had been taking lithium carbonate for 6 years but stopped 3 months ago because he felt he no longer needed it. He currently takes no medications. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he says he has thought about suicide occasionally but has no plans to kill himself. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy at this time?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Lithium carbonate</br>b. Nortriptyline</br>c. Risperidone</br>d. Sertraline</br>e. Valproic acid</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Lithium carbonate  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Chronic disease label: “8-year history of bipolar disorder” → patient has BIPOLAR, not unipolar MDD.<br /></br>• Medication history: “Had been taking lithium for 6 years but stopped 3 months ago.” Lithium was effective maintenance.<br /></br>• Current episode: 6-week DEPRESSED phase (anhedonia, ↓ energy, ↓ appetite), mild passive SI, no plan.<br /></br>• No signs of mania, psychosis, or medical contraindication to lithium.<br /></br>Key pattern: Bipolar patient off mood stabilizer who now presents with depression → first step is to REINSTITUTE a proven mood stabilizer.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Identify mood disorder type (bipolar vs unipolar).<br /></br>2. Ask: “Is a mood stabilizer on board?” If no → start/ restart one before touching antidepressants.<br /></br>3. Choose the agent previously effective and tolerated unless contraindicated.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Goal in bipolar depression = treat current depression AND prevent switch to mania.<br /></br>• Lithium is first-line for bipolar depression and maintenance, lowers suicide risk, and patient already tolerated it for years.<br /></br>• Starting an antidepressant alone (SSRI, TCA) can precipitate mania/hypomania—NBME likes to test this pitfall.<br /></br>• Valproate is an alternative mood stabilizer but reserved when lithium is ineffective/not tolerated.<br /></br>Take-home: In bipolar patients, always secure a mood stabilizer (often lithium) before or instead of an antidepressant.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real life): document mood symptoms, SI assessment, reason for stopping lithium, education on adherence, plan to restart lithium, baseline labs (BMP, TSH) and lithium level scheduling.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Bipolar disorder = episodic mood swings due to dysregulated monoamine and second-messenger pathways.<br /></br>• Lithium’s mechanism: inhibits inositol monophosphatase → stabilizes neuronal signaling; proven to cut suicide risk by ~60%.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Monitoring<br /></br>1. Restart lithium 300 mg PO BID–TID (adjust per levels).<br /></br>2. Baseline tests: BMP (kidney), TSH, pregnancy (if female), ECG if &gt;40.<br /></br>3. Target serum level 0.6–1.2 mEq/L; check 5-7 days after dose change then q3 mo.<br /></br>4. Education: hydration, drug interactions (NSAIDs, ACE-i, thiazides), toxicity signs (tremor, ataxia).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Bipolar DEP” → “Plug in Li-ion” (Lithium ion). Visualize a depleted battery (depression) being replugged into a lithium-ion charger.<br /></br>Rule: “No SSRI SOLO in BP”—write SSRI in a red circle with a slash.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>b. Nortriptyline (TCA)<br /></br>   • Potent antidepressant but monotherapy in bipolar ≈ 20–40 % risk of manic switch; lethal in overdose.<br /></br>c. Risperidone<br /></br>   • Effective for acute mania/mixed states and as adjunct, but not first-line for bipolar depression and doesn’t address suicide risk as well as lithium.<br /></br>d. Sertraline (SSRI)<br /></br>   • Same manic switch risk as other antidepressants; only considered WITH a mood stabilizer after failure of first-line options.<br /></br>e. Valproic acid<br /></br>   • Acceptable mood stabilizer, especially for mania, but patient previously did well on lithium, which has superior antisuicidal benefit. Unless lithium was contraindicated or poorly tolerated, valproate is second choice.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In a bipolar patient who discontinued lithium and now presents with depression, reinstitute lithium (or the previous effective mood stabilizer) before considering any antidepressant therapy.</p>
10. A 17-year-old boy is bought to the emergency department by his parents because of slurred speech, sleepiness, and an inability to walk properly since he returned home from his friend’s house 30 minutes ago. The patient’s parents say that he has been socializing with the “wrong” crowd during the past year; they worry he has been experimenting with drugs and alcohol because he shows little interest in family activities. On arrival, he responds only to sternal rub. His pulse is 100/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. The pupils are equal and reactive to light. Speech is slurred. Pulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. The remainder of the neurologic examination shows no focal findings. His serum glucose concentration is 35 mg/dL, and arterial pH is 7.3. Toxicity from which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient’s symptoms?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Cocaine</br>b. Diazepam</br>c. Ethanol</br>d. Heroin</br>e. Marijuana</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Ethanol  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Adolescent coming from a friend’s house (“partying” context)<br /></br>  – Profound CNS depression: only arouses to sternal rub, slurred speech, ataxia<br /></br>  – Vital signs: mild tachycardia (100/min), hypotension (90/60), only mildly ↓ respirations (12/min)<br /></br>  – Pupils equal &amp; reactive (not pinpoint, not markedly dilated)<br /></br>  – Serum glucose 35 mg/dL (marked hypoglycemia)<br /></br>  – Arterial pH 7.3 (anion-gap metabolic acidosis expected)  </p></br><p>• Pertinent positives/negatives that narrow the differential<br /></br>  – Severe hypoglycemia + acidosis → classic for acute alcohol intoxication in a fasting adolescent.<br /></br>  – Normal–size pupils &amp; only mild respiratory depression argue against opioid overdose.<br /></br>  – Absence of HTN, agitation, mydriasis argues against cocaine/other stimulants.<br /></br>  – Far greater CNS depression than would be expected from marijuana alone.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. ABCs, check glucose!! Unexplained obtundation in kids/teens = “finger-stick first.”<br /></br>2. Think “alcohol, insulin, oral hypoglycemics” when you see profound hypoglycemia + altered mental status.<br /></br>3. Match toxidromes:<br /></br>   – Sympathomimetic (cocaine) = hyperdynamic, mydriatic, agitated.<br /></br>   – Opioid (heroin) = miosis, bradypnea.<br /></br>   – Sedative–hypnotic (benzo, ethanol) = slurred speech, ataxia, but ethanol uniquely triggers hypoglycemia &amp; metabolic acidosis.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach<br /></br>1. Recognize life-threatening findings: low glucose, depressed mental status.<br /></br>2. Link biochemical effect of ethanol: It drives the NADH/NAD⁺ ratio ↑ → blocks gluconeogenesis, producing hypoglycemia &amp; lactic acidosis.<br /></br>3. No signs pointing toward other toxidromes → ethanol most congruent.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Ethanol intoxication, especially after prolonged fasting or in adolescents with limited glycogen stores, commonly produces profound hypoglycemia and a mild anion-gap metabolic (lactic) acidosis in addition to classic CNS depression.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>• Point-of-care glucose result &amp; immediate dextrose administration<br /></br>• GCS, vitals, airway status<br /></br>• Suspected ethanol use; plan for thiamine + dextrose, toxicology screen, observation  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Ethanol metabolism (ADH → acetaldehyde; ALDH → acetate) converts NAD⁺ to NADH. Excess NADH:<br /></br>• Pyruvate → lactate → lactic acidosis<br /></br>• Oxaloacetate → malate → ↓ gluconeogenesis → hypoglycemia<br /></br>• Dihydroxyacetone phosphate → glycerol-3-P → ↑ triglycerides/steatosis  </p></br><p>Risk factors for severe hypoglycemia: adolescents/children, malnourished, prolonged time since last meal.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>Immediate:<br /></br>1. Airway protection, O₂, monitor.<br /></br>2. Thiamine 100 mg IV first (prevents Wernicke) → THEN IV dextrose (D50 or D10 infusion).<br /></br>3. IVF for hypotension.<br /></br>4. Serum &amp; urine tox screen, BMP, ABG, ethanol level.  </p></br><p>No role for naloxone unless opioid co-ingestion suspected; no flumazenil (risks seizures) because benzodiazepine toxicity is not likely.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“EtOH = ‘E-TWO-H’ ”<br /></br>E – Excess NADH<br /></br>T – Thiamine before sugar<br /></br>WO – Wobbly (ataxia) &amp; Obtunded<br /></br>H – Hypoglycemia / Hypotension  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture a teenager holding an empty beer can, slipping on a glucometer reading “35,” surrounded by a lactic acid puddle.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Cocaine<br /></br>• Produces agitation, hypertension, tachycardia, mydriasis, hyperthermia—not profound CNS depression or hypoglycemia.  </p></br><p>b. Diazepam (benzodiazepine)<br /></br>• Can cause sedation &amp; ataxia but typically preserves vitals (normal BP) and does NOT precipitate hypoglycemia or metabolic acidosis. Pupils usually normal, but glucose would be normal.  </p></br><p>d. Heroin (opioid)<br /></br>• Classic triad: miosis, respiratory depression, and CNS depression. Marked bradypnea (&lt; 10/min) usually present. Glucose &amp; pH generally normal unless prolonged hypoxia.  </p></br><p>e. Marijuana<br /></br>• Mild sympathetic activation (tachycardia), conjunctival injection, hunger (“munchies”). Does not cause severe obtundation, acidosis, or hypoglycemia.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Severe hypoglycemia + lactic acidosis in an obtunded adolescent after “partying” is virtually diagnostic of ethanol intoxication. Always give thiamine before glucose in suspected alcohol intoxication to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.</p>
11. A 6-month-old female infant is brought to the office because of a 4-day history of irritability and difficulty sucking and a 2-week history of constipation. She is exclusively breast-fed but has stopped latching during the past 4 days. Her mother typically gives her a pacifier dipped in honey to quiet her when necessary, but the patient will no longer take the pacifier. She has not had fever, cough, or vomiting. She appears lethargic and has a weak cry. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 40/min, and blood pressure is 70/50 mm Hg. Examination shows bilateral ptosis, facial weakness, and poor head control. The pupils are sluggishly reactive to light. Gag reflex is diminished. Muscle strength is 2/5 in the upper extremities and 3/5 in the lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are absent in all extremities. Sensation is intact throughout. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Blood culture</br>b. Lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis</br>c. MRI of the spine</br>d. Muscle biopsy</br>e. Repetitive nerve stimulation study</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>E. Repetitive nerve stimulation study (electrodiagnostic test of neuromuscular transmission)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis – How we know it is infant botulism</br>1. Age: 6-month-old (classically &lt;12 months).<br /></br>2. Classic prodrome: “2-week history of constipation” → autonomic dysfunction from botulinum toxin precedes weakness.<br /></br>3. Honey exposure: pacifier dipped in honey → spores colonize infant gut.<br /></br>4. Descending, symmetric, flaccid paralysis: cranial nerves first (weak cry, poor suck, bilateral ptosis, facial weakness, diminished gag) → neck → upper limbs (2/5) → lower limbs (3/5).<br /></br>5. Autonomic findings: sluggish pupils, constipation.<br /></br>6. Normal sensation, absent deep tendon reflexes.</p></br><p>Pattern recognition: “Honey + constipation + bulbar palsies + floppy baby” = infant botulism.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:</br>• Read stem once for age/exposures, then scan for order of neuro deficits (descending vs ascending) and for autonomic clues (pupils, constipation).<br /></br>• Always ask: “CNS, spinal cord, muscle, or neuromuscular junction?” Normal sensation + cranial nerve palsies + flaccidity → NMJ.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning – Why repetitive nerve stimulation?</br>• Goal: confirm a presynaptic neuromuscular junction disorder.<br /></br>• In botulism, repetitive stimulation (20–50 Hz) shows an incremental (facilitation) response because rapid firing partially overcomes the ACh release blockade.<br /></br>• This study is quick, non-invasive, and differentiates botulism from motor neuron disease or myopathy, guiding antitoxin therapy.</p></br><p>Take-home: When an infant has suspected botulism, the most useful next diagnostic step is an electrodiagnostic study demonstrating impaired, facilitative neuromuscular transmission.</p></br><p>Documentation you would add in real practice:</br>“Infant with suspected C. botulinum toxin–mediated neuromuscular junction blockade. Plan: urgent repetitive nerve stimulation/EMG, send stool for C. botulinum toxin, consult pediatric neurology, order BabyBIG (human botulism immune globulin).”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review</br>• C. botulinum spores germinate in the immature infant gut → toxin formed in situ.<br /></br>• Botulinum toxin cleaves SNARE proteins (e.g., SNAP-25) → prevents ACh vesicle fusion → ↓ ACh release at presynaptic terminal.<br /></br>• Results: autonomic dysfunction (constipation, poor pupillary reactivity), flaccid paralysis descending from bulbar muscles.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Further Work-up</br>1. Supportive care (airway watch, possible intubation).<br /></br>2. Human-derived botulism immune globulin (BabyBIG) as soon as diagnosis is suspected—do NOT delay for confirmatory tests.<br /></br>3. Stool/serum toxin assay is confirmatory but takes days; order concurrently.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Honey makes babies Flop DOWN”<br /></br>• Honey exposure<br /></br>• Floppy (hypotonia)<br /></br>• Descending paralysis<br /></br>• Autonomic (constipation, pupils)<br /></br>• Weak Neuromuscular junction</p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a baby bear licking honey, then slowly slumping from head to toe as the honey drips downward.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Incorrect</br>A. Blood culture<br /></br>   • Bacteremia/sepsis would present with fever, irritability, hemodynamic instability; cranial nerve palsies and absent reflexes are not typical.</p></br><p>B. Lumbar puncture<br /></br>   • CSF is normal in botulism; LP is for meningitis, encephalitis, Guillain-Barré (would show albuminocytologic dissociation but GBS produces ascending—not descending—weakness).</p></br><p>C. MRI of the spine<br /></br>   • Used to evaluate cord compression, transverse myelitis; sensory deficits and upper motor neuron signs would be expected; not helpful for NMJ pathology.</p></br><p>D. Muscle biopsy<br /></br>   • Helpful for congenital myopathies or muscular dystrophies (would reveal structural muscle abnormalities, not a neurotransmitter release problem). Normal sensation and autonomic dysfunction point away from primary muscle disease.</p></br><p>E. Repetitive nerve stimulation study – correct, demonstrates presynaptic NMJ blockade characteristic of botulism.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway</br>In a floppy infant with honey exposure, constipation, and descending paralysis, think infant botulism. The best immediate diagnostic confirmation is an electrodiagnostic repetitive nerve stimulation study, while simultaneously starting BabyBIG.</p>
12. A 77-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his son 4 hours after he became dizzy and was unable to stand. He has a history of intermittent atrial fibrillation and hypertension well controlled with digoxin and hydrochlorothiazide. His speech is dysarthric. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min and irregularly irregular, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. Examination shows right-beating nystagmus and left ptosis. The left pupil is smaller than the right, especially in the dark. Sensation to pain and temperature is decreased over the left side of the face and right side of the body. The left upper and lower extremities are ataxic. An ECG shows atrial fibrillation. A CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities. Carotid ultrasonography shows 40 percent stenosis of the left internal carotid artery and 60 percent stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management to prevent recurrent cerebral infarction in this patient?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Aspirin therapy</br>b. Clopidogrel therapy</br>c. Warfarin therapy</br>d. Left carotid endarterectomy</br>e. Right carotid endarterectomy</br></br></br><p>Correct answer (per key): Warfarin therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Sudden-onset vertigo, ipsilateral Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis), ipsilateral loss of pain/temperature on the face with contralateral loss on the body, and ipsilateral limb ataxia = classic pattern for a lateral medullary (Wallenberg) infarct.<br /></br>• Vascular territory: posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) or vertebral artery – NOT a carotid distribution.<br /></br>• 77-year-old with documented atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular pulse, on digoxin) → high risk of cardio-embolic stroke.<br /></br>• Non-contrast head CT is often normal in the first few hours after posterior-circulation ischemia, so a “normal” CT does not rule out stroke.<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: In any acute focal neuro deficit + AF, think “cardio-embolism” first unless proven otherwise. The management question then shifts to anticoagulation vs antiplatelet vs carotid intervention.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Identify etiology → cardio-embolic stroke from AF.<br /></br>Step 2: Secondary stroke prevention guidelines:<br /></br>   • Cardio-embolic (AF, mechanical valve, mural thrombus) → long-term oral anticoagulation.<br /></br>   • Large-artery atherosclerosis → antiplatelet ± carotid endarterectomy (CEA) if severe stenosis and anterior circulation symptoms.<br /></br>Step 3: Choose drug → For Step 2 CK/NBME, warfarin (goal INR 2–3) remains the prototype answer for AF-related stroke prevention. (DOACs are acceptable in practice but are rarely listed on exam options.)<br /></br>Take-home: Cardio-embolic source = anticoagulate.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Atrial fibrillation → stasis in left atrial appendage → thrombus → embolization to vertebral artery → infarction of lateral medulla. Loss of descending sympathetic fibers (Horner), spinal trigeminal nucleus (ipsi face pain/temp), spinothalamic tract (contra body pain/temp), inferior cerebellar peduncle (ataxia), vestibular nuclei (nystagmus/vertigo).  </p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>1. Initiate warfarin with heparin bridge (or start DOAC in real life).<br /></br>2. Target INR 2.0–3.0; document baseline CBC, PT/INR, liver function.<br /></br>3. Monitor for hemorrhagic conversion before starting (usually 24–48 h repeat imaging if large infarct).<br /></br>4. Carotid US useful here but stenoses are 40% and 60%—below CEA threshold and not responsible for posterior circulation stroke.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“AF = Anti-coagulate Fully.”<br /></br>Think “CAP”:<br /></br>   C – Cardio-embolic →<br /></br>   A – Anticoagulation (warfarin/DOAC)<br /></br>   P – Posterior circulation or any territory  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Aspirin therapy – First-line antiplatelet for non-cardio-embolic (small-vessel lacunar, atherosclerotic) stroke. Inferior to anticoagulation for AF; still leaves high embolic risk.<br /></br>b. Clopidogrel therapy – Same rationale as aspirin; antiplatelet, not adequate for AF.<br /></br>d. Left carotid endarterectomy – Only for symptomatic high-grade (≥70%) ipsilateral carotid stenosis causing anterior-circulation TIA/stroke. Patient’s stroke is posterior circulation; stenosis only 40%.<br /></br>e. Right carotid endarterectomy – 60% is moderate, below surgery threshold; also wrong territory.  </p></br><p>Key takeaway: In atrial fibrillation–associated stroke, long-term oral anticoagulation (warfarin, INR 2–3) is the proven strategy to prevent recurrent cerebral infarction.</p>
13. A 47-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 12-hour history of a spinning sensation that worsens when he closes his eyes. He has no history of similar symptoms or serious illness. He takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows horizontal-rotatory nystagmus on right lateral gaze. Hearing acuity is normal bilaterally. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Basilar migraine</br>b. Brain stem hemorrhage</br>c. Cerebellar infarction</br>d. Meniere disease</br>e. Vestibular neurionitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Vestibular neuritis (vestibular neuronitis)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS  </p></br><p>Key words &amp; clues<br /></br>• “12-hour history of a spinning sensation” → acute, continuous vertigo.<br /></br>• “Worsens when he closes his eyes” → the patient relies on visual fixation to suppress symptoms → peripheral vestibular cause.<br /></br>• “Horizontal-rotatory nystagmus on right lateral gaze” → unidirectional, mixed torsional-horizontal nystagmus = peripheral vestibular pattern.<br /></br>• “Hearing acuity is normal bilaterally” → rules out labyrinthitis &amp; Ménière disease (both cause sensorineural hearing loss).<br /></br>• “No other neurologic deficits” → argues against brain-stem or cerebellar pathology.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Decide central vs peripheral vertigo (nystagmus type, hearing, neuro signs, fixation suppression).<br /></br>2. If peripheral and hearing preserved → think vestibular neuritis or benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV). BPPV causes brief (&lt;1 min) positional episodes, not continuous 12-h vertigo.<br /></br>3. Acute, severe, prolonged vertigo + fixation suppression + no hearing loss = vestibular neuritis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING &amp; TAKEAWAY  </p></br><p>Vestibular neuritis = acute inflammation of the vestibular branch of CN VIII, usually post-viral.<br /></br>• Presentation: sudden onset severe vertigo, nausea/vomiting, gait unsteadiness lasting days; no hearing symptoms.<br /></br>• Exam: unidirectional horizontal-torsional nystagmus that ↑ when visual fixation is removed (eyes closed), positive head-impulse test toward affected side, no other focal deficits.<br /></br>Core concept: Peripheral vertigo with preserved hearing.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): onset/duration of vertigo, otologic symptoms, neuro exam, nystagmus direction, head-impulse, gait, hydration status.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW  </p></br><p>• Viral URI → inflammation of vestibular portion of CN VIII → impaired afferent firing from one labyrinth → mismatch between the two sides → constant vertigo and nystagmus.<br /></br>• Visual fixation partially compensates; removing it (closing eyes, darkness) unmasks symptoms.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>TREATMENT &amp; DIAGNOSTIC PEARLS  </p></br><p>Acute management:<br /></br>1. Symptom control (24–48 h) – ondansetron or promethazine for nausea; short course of meclizine or dimenhydrinate; consider benzodiazepine (diazepam) if severe.<br /></br>2. Corticosteroids within 72 h (e.g., oral prednisone 60 mg ×5 d taper) may hasten vestibular recovery.<br /></br>3. Early vestibular rehabilitation exercises improve long-term balance.<br /></br>Imaging: MRI brain if any central red flags (vertical or direction-changing nystagmus, focal neuro deficits, severe headache, risk factors for stroke).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>MEMORIZATION TIPS  </p></br><p>“Neuritis = NO (hearing) loss.”<br /></br>Think “V” in Vestibular neuritis for Viral &amp; Visual fixation helps.<br /></br>Central causes = “BAD”: Bidirectional nystagmus, Ataxia severe, Diplopia/dysmetria.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>INCORRECT ANSWER ANALYSIS  </p></br><p>a. Basilar migraine<br /></br>• Vertigo occurs with occipital throbbing headache, visual aura, or other migraine features (photophobia, phonophobia). Absent here.</p></br><p>b. Brain-stem hemorrhage<br /></br>• Would present with acute vertigo PLUS focal deficits (dysarthria, weakness, gaze palsy), often dramatic hypertension and severe headache. Normal vitals and neuro exam argue against.</p></br><p>c. Cerebellar infarction<br /></br>• Central vertigo with severe truncal ataxia, dysmetria, possible headache; nystagmus often vertical or direction-changing and NOT suppressed by visual fixation. Needs emergent MRI.</p></br><p>d. Ménière disease<br /></br>• Triad: episodic vertigo (minutes–hours), fluctuating unilateral sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus ± ear fullness. Hearing is normal in this vignette.</p></br><p>e. Vestibular neuritis — matches all findings (correct).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-yield takeaway:<br /></br>Continuous vertigo lasting hours–days, unidirectional horizontal-torsional nystagmus that worsens when eyes are closed, with preserved hearing and no focal neuro deficits = vestibular neuritis, a peripheral post-viral CN VIII inflammation.</p>
14. A 9-month-old girl is brought to the office because of a 5-day history of a rash in the diaper area. She has had two episodes of otitis media treated with oral antibiotic therapy but has been otherwise healthy. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F). A photograph of the rash is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Intravenous amphotericin B</br>b. Oral cephalexin</br>c. Oral ketoconazole</br>d. Topical mupirocin</br>e. Topical nystatin</br>f. No pharmacotherapy is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Topical nystatin  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Setting/age: 9-month-old in diaper → high-risk zone for moisture-associated rashes.<br /></br>• Time course: 5 days, persistent despite routine diaper care → think infectious, not simple irritant dermatitis.<br /></br>• Key visual buzzwords (photo, often described on NBME): “Beefy-red” plaques involving skin folds with multiple small red “satellite” papules/pustules beyond the main rash border.<br /></br>• Recent antibiotics: alters gut flora → Candida overgrowth → autoinoculation of perineal skin.  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition framework for diaper rashes:<br /></br>1. Irritant contact (most common): erythema only on convex surfaces, spares skin creases, improves with barrier cream.<br /></br>2. Candida diaper dermatitis: beefy red, involves skin folds and satellites, often after antibiotics.<br /></br>3. Bacterial (Staph/Strep) infection: honey-colored crusts or bullae (impetigo) or sharply demarcated perianal redness (perianal Strep).  </p></br><p>The vignette fits pattern #2 → candidal diaper dermatitis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Stepwise approach:<br /></br>1. Identify rash morphology + location → diaper area with satellite lesions.<br /></br>2. Recognize risk factor (recent antibiotics).<br /></br>3. Match to most effective first-line therapy: a topical antifungal active against Candida (nystatin or an azole). Nystatin is classic, inexpensive, and NBME-favorite.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Beefy red diaper rash with satellite lesions = Candida → treat with topical nystatin or clotrimazole.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): appearance, distribution, presence of satellite lesions, prior antibiotics, parental education on diaper hygiene, Rx nystatin cream/ointment 4 × daily until 3 days after rash clears.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Candida albicans colonizes GI tract → warm, moist occluded diaper environment + skin maceration + disrupted bacterial flora (antibiotics) allow overgrowth.<br /></br>• Skin folds are preferentially involved because of retained moisture. Hyphae invade stratum corneum → inflammatory erythema and pustules that rupture → satellite papules.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>First-line:<br /></br>• Topical nystatin cream/ointment, 4–6 times daily.<br /></br>• Alternative: topical imidazoles (clotrimazole, miconazole).<br /></br>• Continue barrier cream (zinc oxide/petrolatum) concurrently.<br /></br>Diagnostics: none required; clinical diagnosis. Culture only if refractory &gt;2 wk.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Beefy red bottom with ‘satellites’ from space → needs a ‘NY’ (nystatin) landing pad.”<br /></br>OR<br /></br>Candida “satellite” rash: remember NASA → NystA(tin) for Satellite Area.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. IV amphotericin B – Reserved for severe systemic or invasive candidiasis; far too aggressive and toxic for localized diaper rash.<br /></br>b. Oral cephalexin – Targets gram-positive skin bacteria; would be used for cellulitis or impetigo without satellite lesions. Does not treat fungi.<br /></br>c. Oral ketoconazole – Systemic azole carries hepatotoxicity risk, not recommended in infants for superficial rash; topical therapy is sufficient.<br /></br>d. Topical mupirocin – Active against Staph/Strep; used for impetigo or MRSA colonization. Impetigo shows honey-colored crusts, not beefy red plaques with satellites.<br /></br>f. No pharmacotherapy – Appropriate for simple irritant dermatitis (spares folds, no satellites); here fungal infection is evident, so treatment is required.  </p></br><p>Key difference: Only choices with antifungal activity (topical nystatin/azoles) match Candida diaper dermatitis; all others either target bacteria, are systemically toxic, or leave infection untreated.</p>
15. An 18-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 4-year history of acne over her face.
