She has not sought treatment before today, and she is concerned about having clear skin before she leaves for college in 3 months. She uses water-based skin products and does not wear cosmetics. She has no history of serious illness, takes no medications, and has no known allergies. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals. She is not sexually active. She is a competitive swimmer and trains 2 to 3 hours daily. Examination shows mild comedonal and papular acne over the face but no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Use of bactericidal soap</br>b. Topical retinoid therapy</br>c. Oral contraceptive therapy</br>d. Oral isotretinoin therapy</br>e. Intralesional corticosteroid therapy</br>f. Intramuscular medroxyprogesterone therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Topical retinoid therapy  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>• Key words/phrases:<br /></br>  – “18-year-old woman” (common age for acne vulgaris)<br /></br>  – “4-year history … concerned … 3 months until college” (wants reasonably fast, first-line intervention)<br /></br>  – “Mild comedonal and papular acne over the face” (no nodules/cysts, no scarring)<br /></br>  – “Regular menses, not sexually active” (no obvious hyper-androgenism, contraception not a priority)<br /></br>  – “No prior treatment” (this is true first-line decision)  </p></br><p>• Pertinent positives: open/closed comedones ± small papules only.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no pustules, nodules, cysts, scarring, hirsutism, menstrual irregularity.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Classify severity (comedonal → mild).<br /></br>2. Ask “Has anything been tried yet?” (No).<br /></br>3. Apply standard algorithm:<br /></br>   – Mild comedonal/papular: topical retinoid ± benzoyl peroxide.<br /></br>   – Mild inflammatory: add topical antibiotic.<br /></br>   – Moderate/severe: oral antibiotic ± combined OCP.<br /></br>   – Nodulocystic/scarring: oral isotretinoin.<br /></br>4. Pick the lowest-risk, evidence-based starting point.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why B?)  </p></br><p>Topical retinoids (e.g., tretinoin, adapalene, tazarotene) are first-line for mild comedonal acne because they:<br /></br>• Normalize follicular keratinization → prevent new comedones.<br /></br>• Possess some anti-inflammatory activity for papules.<br /></br>• Have an acceptable side-effect profile (limited to local irritation, photosensitivity).<br /></br>• Are safe in women not on contraception (topical exposure &lt; systemic).  </p></br><p>Take-home message: ALWAYS start mild, non-inflammatory acne with a topical retinoid; escalate only if inadequate after 2–3 months or if disease is more severe at presentation.  </p></br><p>Documentation note: Lesion type/count, anatomic distribution, current regimen (“none”), patient counseling re nightly application, sun protection, and follow-up plan in 8–12 weeks.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Acne vulgaris = follicular hyper-keratinization + sebum over-production + Cutibacterium (Propionibacterium) acnes colonization + inflammation. Retinoids target the first step (keratin plug).  </p></br><p>Risk factors: puberty (androgens), occlusive products, stress, genetics. Water-based products and no cosmetics lessen but don’t eliminate risk.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Mild comedonal/papular:<br /></br>• 1st-line: Topical retinoid nightly ± benzoyl peroxide wash.<br /></br>• If inflammatory component → add topical clindamycin or dapsone.  </p></br><p>Moderate inflammatory or truncal involvement: add oral doxycycline/minocycline.  </p></br><p>Severe nodulocystic/scarring or refractory: oral isotretinoin with iPLEDGE pregnancy precautions.  </p></br><p>Hormonal therapy (combined OCP, spironolactone) reserved for women with moderate inflammatory acne, androgen excess, or who need contraception.  </p></br><p>No labs needed here.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“ABC-I” algorithm (from least to most aggressive):<br /></br>A – Adapalene (or any topical Retinoid)<br /></br>B – Benzoyl +/- topical clinda<br /></br>C – Cycline (oral doxy/mino)<br /></br>I – Isotretinoin  </p></br><p>Picture the acne ladder: start on the bottom rung (retinoid), climb only if necessary.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Bactericidal soap<br /></br>• Over-cleansing and harsh soaps dry/irritate skin without correcting follicular hyper-keratinization; not recommended as therapeutic monotherapy.  </p></br><p>c. Oral contraceptive therapy<br /></br>• Helpful for moderate-to-severe inflammatory acne or androgen-related flares; unnecessary in a patient with mild comedonal disease and no desire for contraception. Takes 3–6 months to work.  </p></br><p>d. Oral isotretinoin therapy<br /></br>• Reserved for severe, nodulocystic, or scarring acne refractory to other treatments. Requires stringent teratogenic precautions. Over-treatment here.  </p></br><p>e. Intralesional corticosteroid therapy<br /></br>• Used for a few large inflamed nodules/cysts to hasten resolution and prevent scarring. She has no such lesions.  </p></br><p>f. Intramuscular medroxyprogesterone therapy<br /></br>• A progestin-only contraceptive; can actually worsen acne due to androgenic activity of progestin, and has no role in mild comedonal acne treatment.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Key Takeaway:<br /></br>For first-presentation mild comedonal/papular acne, always begin with a topical retinoid ± benzoyl peroxide; escalate step-wise only if inadequate or if severity warrants.</p>
16. A 50-year-old man has had a swollen painful right elbow for 2 days. There is no history of trauma. He has rheumatoid arthritis involving the shoulders, fingers, elbows, knees, and ankles that has been well controlled with prednisone therapy for 4 months. He also takes ibuprofen and penicillin. He had a painful vesicular rash in a dermatome distribution below his left breast 6 months ago. Two years ago, he was told that his rheumatoid process was affecting his lungs. He has been monogamous for 25 years. His temperature is 38.2°C (100.7°F), pulse is 80/min and regular, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 140/80 mm Hg. His fingers, wrists, knees, and ankles show evidence of synovial proliferation; pain is minimal, and there is no warmth. His right elbow is swollen, warm, erythematous, and extremely tender. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin                        10.5 g/dL</br>Leukocyte count                   16,800/mm3</br>Segmented neutrophils             80 percent</br>Bands                             10 percent</br>Lymphocytes                       10 percent</br>Erythrocyte sedimentation rate    50 mm/h</br>Analysis of synovial fluid from the right elbow is turbid and shows a leukocyte count of 200,000/mm3 (98 percent segmented neutrophils and 2 percent monocytes) and a glucose concentration of 10 mg/dL. Gram stain is negative. An x-ray of the chest shows a small right pleural effusion. Which of the following is the most likely causal organism?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Borrelia burgdorferi</br>b. Coccidioides immitis</br>c. Herpes zoster</br>d. Mycobacterium tuberculosis</br>e. Staphylococcus aureus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Staphylococcus aureus  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; “How to Read It”  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key buzz-phrases that should light up your “septic arthritis” radar: <br /></br>   • “Swollen, warm, erythematous, and extremely tender” mono-articular joint (right elbow).<br /></br>   • Fever 38.2 °C and marked leukocytosis with a left shift.<br /></br>   • Synovial fluid: turbid, WBC 200 000/mm³ (98 % PMNs), glucose 10 mg/dL.<br /></br>      – WBC &gt;50 000 with &gt;75 % neutrophils + low glucose = classic for septic arthritis.<br /></br>   • Gram stain negative ≠ rules it out (false-negative in ~50 % of cases).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Risk factors buried in the stem:<br /></br>   • Rheumatoid arthritis (damaged synovium, frequent joint instrumentation).<br /></br>   • Chronic prednisone (immunosuppression).<br /></br>   • Mild anemia of chronic disease, high ESR—typical background findings in RA, not explanatory for the acute elbow findings.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives that help rule OUT alternatives:<br /></br>   • No travel or tick exposure (rules against Borrelia).<br /></br>   • Onset is abrupt over 48 h (unlike TB or fungal).<br /></br>   • Vesicular rash six months ago is remote (rules against active zoster).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When an NBME vignette gives an RA patient with one joint that is suddenly MUCH hotter and more painful than the others, immediately think “super-infection of an RA joint” → septic arthritis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why Staph aureus?)  </p></br><p>• Epidemiology: Staphylococcus aureus is the single most common cause of septic arthritis in adults overall and is especially prevalent in:<br /></br>  – Patients with RA or other chronic joint disease.<br /></br>  – Those on glucocorticoids or other immunosuppressives.<br /></br>  – Prosthetic joints, skin breaches, IV drug use.  </p></br><p>• Path to the elbow: Skin colonization → transient bacteremia → seeding of the already-inflamed synovium.  </p></br><p>Take-home algorithm for real life &amp; exams:<br /></br>1. Hot, swollen joint? → Do NOT wait; aspirate.<br /></br>2. If WBC &gt;50 k PMNs or low glucose → treat as septic arthritis<br /></br>   – Empiric IV antibiotics targeting staph (vancomycin ± ceftriaxone) after cultures.<br /></br>3. Orthopedic consultation for serial drainage or surgical washout.  </p></br><p>Document: H&amp;P findings, arthrocentesis results, blood cultures, empiric antibiotic start time, and infectious-disease/ortho consults.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Snapshot  </p></br><p>• Staph aureus produces adhesins that bind to synovial tissue and extracellular matrix proteins, facilitating joint seeding.<br /></br>• Bacterial toxins trigger massive neutrophilic influx → protease release → rapid cartilage destruction (hence the urgency).<br /></br>• Glucocorticoids blunt host immune response, allowing unchecked proliferation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Review  </p></br><ol></br><li>Immediate labs: joint fluid Gram stain, culture, crystal analysis; blood cultures ×2.  </li></br><li>Empiric IV therapy:<br /></br>   • Nafcillin or cefazolin if MSSA likely.<br /></br>   • Vancomycin if MRSA risk or until sensitivities return.<br /></br>   • Add gram-negative coverage (e.g., ceftriaxone) if the patient is elderly, immunocompromised, or uncertain source.  </li></br><li>Joint drainage: serial needle aspiration or arthroscopic washout.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>• “RA + Rapidly Angry (red-hot) Articulation = think StAph.”<br /></br>• Septic arthritis synovial mnemonic:<br /></br>  – S = Staph/strep common<br /></br>  – P = PMNs &gt;75 %<br /></br>  – T = Turbid/tender<br /></br>  – I = Immediate antibiotics<br /></br>  – C = Culture before drugs  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Borrelia burgdorferi (Lyme): Usually subacute, migratory large-joint arthritis (classically the knee). Fluid WBC 10-25 k, often lymphocyte-predominant; patient needs tick exposure in endemic area.  </p></br><p>b. Coccidioides immitis: Desert Southwest exposure; fungal arthritis is indolent, granulomatous, not an acutely purulent process; fluid WBC and glucose values far less dramatic.  </p></br><p>c. Herpes zoster: Prior shingles is irrelevant; VZV causes radicular pain or post-herpetic neuralgia, not purulent joint infection.  </p></br><p>d. Mycobacterium tuberculosis: Chronic, insidious mono-arthritis with moderate WBC (&lt;20 k), lymphocyte predominance, and normal‐to-lowish glucose; acid-fast stain not gram stain.  </p></br><p>e. Staphylococcus aureus: Matches the rapid onset, high neutrophil count, low glucose, classic risk profile—therefore correct.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core “Shelf/Step 2” Takeaway  </p></br><p>Any patient—especially one with RA or on steroids—who develops an acutely hot, swollen, exquisitely tender single joint must be presumed to have septic arthritis; Staphylococcus aureus is the #1 culprit until proven otherwise.</p>
17. A 3-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of a 6-week history of intermittent, increasingly severe knee pain. Her mother says that initially the pain involved only the right knee, but during the past 2 weeks, her daughter also has had pain in the left knee, and the knees have felt warm and slightly swollen. The patient has not had fever or rash. She is alert and walks with a limp. She is at the 50th percentile for height and 25th percentile for weight. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 106/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 86/48 mm Hg. Pupillary light reflexes are normal, and ocular movements are intact. The conjunctivae appear normal. No masses are noted in the neck. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. The abdomen is soft and nontender. Examination of the knees shows mild swelling and decreased range of motion because of pain. Laboratory studies show:</br>Erythrocyte sedimentation rate    32 mm/h</br>Antinuclear antibody              positive (1:640)</br>Rheumatoid factor                 negative</br></br>Which of the following is the most appropriate screening test for this patient?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Slit-lamp examination</br>b. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br>c. Bone scan</br>d. Echocardiography</br>e. MRI of the hips</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Slit-lamp examination  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 3-year-old girl → peak age for oligoarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA).<br /></br>   • ≥6 wk of asymmetric knee pain, warmth, swelling; no fever or rash → chronic arthritis pattern, not acute infection.<br /></br>   • Limited to ≤4 joints over first 6 mo (both knees) → oligoarticular subtype.<br /></br>   • ANA positive (high titer 1:640), RF negative → classic laboratory pattern for oligo-JIA.<br /></br>   • ESR mildly ↑ (32 mm/h) → supports inflammatory process.<br /></br>   • No systemic findings (normal vitals except mild tachycardia, no rash, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly) → rules out systemic-onset JIA, leukemia, rheumatic fever, etc.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No fever → septic joint unlikely.<br /></br>   • No conjunctival injection or eye pain → uveitis can be silent, so lack of ocular symptoms does NOT exclude it.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: Once the stem gives you a preschool girl with oligoarticular arthritis + ANA positivity, immediately think “screen for asymptomatic anterior uveitis → slit-lamp exam.”</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Classify the arthritis (oligo, poly, systemic).<br /></br>2. Recall major complications of each subtype.<br /></br>   • Oligo-JIA: asymptomatic chronic anterior uveitis/iridocyclitis.<br /></br>3. Identify required screening modality.<br /></br>   • Only way to detect early, asymptomatic uveitis is periodic slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist (every 3 mo in high-risk patients: ANA positive, age &lt;7 yr, oligoarticular).  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Children with oligoarticular JIA, especially ANA-positive girls &lt;7 yr, must undergo regular slit-lamp eye exams to prevent vision-threatening complications.</p></br><p>Documentation in real practice: Plan section would read “Refer to ophthalmology for baseline and q3 month slit-lamp exams to screen for iridocyclitis.”</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Juvenile idiopathic arthritis = autoimmune inflammation of synovium.<br /></br>• Oligoarticular subtype: ≤4 joints in first 6 mo, knees &amp; ankles most common, female predominance.<br /></br>• Risk factors for uveitis: young age, female sex, high-titer ANA.<br /></br>• Uveitis mechanism: autoimmune attack on anterior uveal tract → synechiae, cataracts, glaucoma, blindness if untreated.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Snapshot  </p></br><p>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Baseline labs: ESR/CRP, ANA, RF to classify disease.<br /></br>• Imaging only if joint damage suspected (not for routine screening).<br /></br>• Ophthalmology slit-lamp exam schedule:<br /></br>  – High-risk (ANA+ &amp; age &lt;7): every 3 mo.<br /></br>  – Moderate risk: every 6–12 mo.  </p></br><p>Treatment:<br /></br>1. NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen) first-line for mild disease.<br /></br>2. Intra-articular glucocorticoids if persistent.<br /></br>3. Methotrexate or TNF-α inhibitors for refractory cases.<br /></br>4. Uveitis treatment: topical steroids ± systemic immunomodulators.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“ANA-positive Little girl with Achy knees → Always Need An ophthalmologist.”<br /></br>or<br /></br>O-JIA mnemonic:<br /></br>O = Oligo joints<br /></br>O = Ophthalmology (slit-lamp)<br /></br>O = Often ANA positive  </p></br><p>Visualize: Picture a preschool girl holding two swollen knees and wearing oversized glasses—reminding you to examine her eyes.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Serum TSH – Thyroid disease can co-occur with autoimmune disorders, but it is not the critical, vision-threatening complication that requires immediate routine screening in oligo-JIA. No symptoms of hypo/hyperthyroidism.  </p></br><p>c. Bone scan – Useful for occult fractures, infection, or malignancy; not indicated for screening asymptomatic joints in JIA.  </p></br><p>d. Echocardiography – Screening test for congenital heart disease or rheumatic carditis, not for JIA. Systemic-onset JIA can have pericarditis, but this subtype would present with high fevers and rash.  </p></br><p>e. MRI of the hips – Helpful if hip involvement suspected; hips are asymptomatic here and MRI is not a screening tool for uveitis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In ANA-positive oligoarticular JIA, silent anterior uveitis is the major preventable morbidity—therefore, schedule routine slit-lamp eye examinations.</p>
18. A 52-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 3-day history of severe pain in his left hip and thigh. He has a 3-year history of a daily cough productive of up to three tablespoons of yellow-tinged sputum. He also has a 10-year history of well-controlled hypertension. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 30 years. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows marked restriction of movement of the left hip as a result of pain with movement. AP x-rays of the left lower extremity followed by CT scan show an impending fracture of the proximal femur in the area of a 3 × 3-cm radiolucent defect with focal breaching of the cortex laterally. CT scans of the chest and abdomen show a 5 × 3-cm spiculated density in the central right upper lobe of the lung; there is no lymphadenopathy or abdominal masses. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Adenocarcinoma</br>b. Enchondroma</br>c. Giant cell tumor of bone</br>d. Hodgkin disease</br>e. Osteosarcoma</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A.  Adenocarcinoma (primary lung cancer with metastatic spread to bone)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that immediately frame the differential:</br>• 52-year-old long-time smoker → high pre-test probability of lung malignancy.<br /></br>• 3-day history of severe hip/thigh pain + imaging that shows a 3 × 3-cm radiolucent (lytic) lesion in the proximal femur with cortical breach → classic appearance of a metastatic lytic bone lesion producing an impending pathologic fracture.<br /></br>• CT chest: 5 × 3-cm spiculated mass in the right upper lobe (RUL) with no mediastinal or abdominal disease → solitary, highly malignant-appearing pulmonary primary.<br /></br>• No systemic “B” symptoms or lymphadenopathy suggesting lymphoma; no local bone periosteal reaction or sunburst pattern suggesting primary osteosarcoma.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:</br>1. Ask “Is the painful bone lesion primary or metastatic?”<br /></br>   – Age &gt;40 yr + lytic appearance + smoker + pulmonary mass → “metastatic until proven otherwise.”<br /></br>2. Identify likely primary based on demographics and radiology.<br /></br>   – Lung &gt; breast &gt; thyroid &gt; kidney &gt; prostate are the 5 classic sources of bone mets.<br /></br>3. Decide which lung histology best fits.<br /></br>   – Adenocarcinoma is now the most common lung cancer overall and is the histology most likely to present as a spiculated mass with early hematogenous spread to bone.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:</br>1. Painful femoral lytic lesion → think metastatic carcinoma.<br /></br>2. Look for primary site → CT chest reveals spiculated RUL mass.<br /></br>3. Cigarette exposure favors non-small cell subtypes (adenocarcinoma, squamous cell) or small-cell carcinoma.<br /></br>4. Central vs peripheral: “Spiculated” alone is not location-specific, but the vignette calls it “central RUL.”<br /></br>   • Squamous cell &amp; small-cell are classically central, but they typically cavitate or present with hilar mass and often have paraneoplastic findings (hyper-Ca²⁺ for squamous, SIADH/ACTH for small-cell). None are mentioned.<br /></br>   • Adenocarcinoma is most likely overall, can be central or peripheral, and frequently metastasizes early to bone.<br /></br>5. Therefore, adenocarcinoma is the best single diagnosis that ties lung primary + lytic bone metastasis.</p></br><p>Take-home message:<br /></br>In adults &gt;40 yr with a lytic bone lesion plus a spiculated lung mass, metastatic lung adenocarcinoma is the most common scenario tested.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life):</br>• Describe impending pathologic fracture; consult ortho for prophylactic fixation.<br /></br>• Stage lung cancer (PET-CT, brain MRI).<br /></br>• Obtain tissue diagnosis (CT-guided lung biopsy).<br /></br>• Discuss smoking cessation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Adenocarcinoma of the lung</br>• Arises from bronchial/alveolar epithelium; mutations: EGFR, ALK, KRAS, etc.<br /></br>• Most common lung cancer in smokers AND overall.<br /></br>• Propensity for early hematogenous spread → brain, liver, adrenal, bone.<br /></br>• Histology: glandular pattern, mucin-positive.<br /></br>• Radiology: peripheral or central spiculated nodule/mass.</p></br><p>Bone metastases</br>• Lytic: lung, renal cell, thyroid, melanoma, multiple myeloma.<br /></br>• Blastic: prostate; mixed: breast.<br /></br>• Present with pain, fractures, hyper-Ca²⁺ (from bone resorption).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management &amp; Diagnostics (high-yield overview)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Confirm diagnosis: core needle biopsy of lung lesion or femoral lesion.  </li></br><li>Stage disease: PET-CT, brain MRI.  </li></br><li>Treat bone metastasis:<br /></br>   • Orthopedic stabilization if fracture risk (Mirels score).<br /></br>   • Radiation for pain control.<br /></br>   • Bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) or denosumab to reduce skeletal-related events.  </li></br><li>Systemic therapy for lung adenocarcinoma guided by driver mutations (EGFR inhibitors, ALK inhibitors, PD-1/PD-L1 immunotherapy, platinum-based chemo).</li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“BLT with a Kosher Pickle”<br /></br>• Breast, Lung, Thyroid, Kidney (renal cell), Prostate → the five classic bone metastasis primaries.<br /></br>“Lytic likes Lung &amp; Kidney, Blastic likes Prostate, Mixed likes Breast.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Enchondroma<br /></br>• Benign cartilage tumor, usually asymptomatic, arises in hands/feet of younger pts; no lung mass.</p></br><p>C. Giant cell tumor (osteoclastoma)<br /></br>• Typically 20-40 yr, epiphyseal lesion around the knee, “soap-bubble” x-ray; no lung primary.</p></br><p>D. Hodgkin disease<br /></br>• Presents with painless lymphadenopathy, B symptoms; bone involvement rare, would have mediastinal adenopathy rather than solitary lung nodule.</p></br><p>E. Osteosarcoma<br /></br>• Primary malignant bone tumor in teenagers or elderly with Paget disease; shows sclerotic, sunburst, Codman triangle appearance; lung mets are secondary, not primary lung lesion.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core concept: In adults with new destructive bone pain, always search for a visceral primary. A solitary spiculated lung mass in a smoker almost always signifies metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, most commonly adenocarcinoma.</p>
19. A 32-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of right groin pain. He is unable to work at his construction job because of his pain. He has no history of trauma or serious illness. He usually drinks six alcoholic beverages daily and occasionally has episodes of binge drinking. On examination, there is a 10-degree flexion contracture of the right hip. Any attempt at passive movement of the right hip produces pain. Flexion of the right hip is limited to 80 degrees, and internal rotation of the right hip is limited to the neutral position. X-rays of the right hip show lytic and sclerotic lesions of the femoral head. The joint space is intact. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Osteoarthritis</br>b. Osteomalacia</br>c. Osteonecrosis</br>d. Osteoporosis</br>e. Septic arthritis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Osteonecrosis (avascular necrosis) of the femoral head  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key words &amp; clues<br /></br>  – 32-year-old (young adult: degenerative arthritis is less likely).<br /></br>  – Chronic heavy alcohol use → classic non-traumatic risk factor for avascular necrosis.<br /></br>  – Painful, limited hip motion, especially internal rotation (earliest ROM lost in AVN).<br /></br>  – X-ray: “lytic and sclerotic lesions of the femoral head” with an INTACT joint space. Mixed lucent/sclerotic patchy areas = collapse of subchondral bone; preservation of the joint space = cartilage still healthy → very characteristic of early osteonecrosis.<br /></br>  – Flexion contracture &amp; pain on any passive movement = intra-articular process, not peri-articular muscle injury.</p></br><p>Approach framework<br /></br>1. Ask: Is the problem articular, peri-articular, or systemic? → Articular.<br /></br>2. Is it inflammatory/infectious, degenerative, or ischemic? → No fever, subacute course, normal joint space → think ischemia.<br /></br>3. Screen risk factors: “ASEPTIC” mnemonic (Alcohol, Steroids, Emboli, Pancreatitis, Trauma, Idiopathic, Caisson/Coagulopathy). Alcohol present → strong match.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Avascular necrosis = ischemia of the subchondral bone of the femoral head → bone death → collapse if untreated.<br /></br>• Pain is activity-related early, then at rest.<br /></br>• Earliest physical-exam loss is internal rotation, followed by abduction.<br /></br>• Plain films may be normal early; once mixed lytic/sclerotic changes appear, disease is established but the articular cartilage (joint space) is still preserved.<br /></br>• MRI is the most sensitive confirmatory test.<br /></br>Management:<br /></br>1. Non-weight bearing, bisphosphonates.<br /></br>2. Core decompression ± bone grafting for pre-collapse lesions.<br /></br>3. Total hip arthroplasty after collapse.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)<br /></br>– Risk-factor review (alcohol, steroids, trauma).<br /></br>– Neurovascular exam.<br /></br>– Baseline pain and ROM measurements.<br /></br>– Plan for MRI and orthopedic referral.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Blood supply of femoral head = medial femoral circumflex artery (retinacular vessels). Alcohol (or steroids) causes fat-cell hypertrophy &amp; micro-embolization → increased intra-osseous pressure → venous outflow obstruction → ischemia.<br /></br>• Bone death → collapse → secondary osteoarthritis if the articular cartilage eventually degenerates.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Mnemonic / Memory Aid<br /></br>“ASEPTIC” causes of AVN:<br /></br>A – Alcohol<br /></br>S – Steroids / Sickle cell<br /></br>E – Emboli<br /></br>P – Pancreatitis/Pregnancy<br /></br>T – Trauma<br /></br>I – Idiopathic/Infection (gaucher)<br /></br>C – Caisson disease (the bends)/Coagulopathy</p></br><hr /></br><p>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Osteoarthritis<br /></br>• Age &gt;50 typical, chronic years-long pain, osteophytes &amp; joint-space narrowing on x-ray. Here the space is preserved; patient is 32.</p></br><p>b. Osteomalacia<br /></br>• Diffuse bone pain, Looser zones, low vitamin D/Ca/Phos, not an isolated femoral-head process; x-ray shows generalized decreased mineralization, not patchy lytic/sclerotic lesions.</p></br><p>d. Osteoporosis<br /></br>• Asymptomatic until fracture; plain films show global decreased density and vertebral compression fractures, not focal hip pain with mixed lesions.</p></br><p>e. Septic arthritis<br /></br>• Acute hours-to-days, fever, systemic symptoms, elevated WBC/ESR/CRP; x-ray early normal, later shows joint-space narrowing/destruction, not preserved space with mixed lesions.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Young adult with hip pain + alcoholism + preserved joint space with mixed lucent/sclerotic femoral-head changes = osteonecrosis (avascular necrosis). Recognizing risk factors and imaging pattern is essential for rapid NBME/Step 2 CK identification.</p>
20. A 47-year-old man has tachycardia 3 days after undergoing emergency sigmoid colectomy for a perforated diverticulitis. One year ago, he had coronary artery stents inserted after sustaining an acute myocardial infarction. He has been receiving scheduled doses of propranolol. His pulse is 210/min; other vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. A 12-lead ECG is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this patient?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Adenosine therapy</br>b. Amiodarone therapy</br>c. Digoxin therapy</br>d. Metoprolol therapy</br>e. Electrical cardioversion</br>f. Placement of a pacemaker</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Adenosine therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – New‐onset tachycardia 3 days post-op (common time-frame for catecholamine-driven SVT).<br /></br>  – Heart rate ≈ 210/min and REGULAR.<br /></br>  – “Other vital signs are within normal limits” → patient is HEMODYNAMICALLY STABLE.<br /></br>  – No exam abnormalities, no mention of wide QRS or irregular rhythm.<br /></br>  – 12-lead ECG (not shown) in NBME usually depicts a narrow-complex, regular tachycardia without discernible P waves = paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT, most often AV-nodal re-entrant tachycardia).  </p></br><p>Framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Look at rhythm strip:<br /></br>   • Narrow + regular → think AVNRT/AVRT.<br /></br>   • Narrow + irregular → atrial fibrillation/flutter.<br /></br>   • Wide + regular → VT or SVT with aberrancy.<br /></br>2. Ask “Stable or Unstable?” (AMS, chest pain, hypotension, HF)<br /></br>3. If stable, follow ACLS ladder: vagal → adenosine (for AV-node dependent rhythms) → rate-/rhythm-control drugs.<br /></br>4. If unstable, synchronized cardioversion.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• This rhythm is AV-node-dependent; transient AV-node block terminates the re-entry circuit.<br /></br>• Adenosine (6 mg rapid IV push, may repeat 12 mg) is first-line for STABLE, narrow-complex, regular SVT.<br /></br>• Propranolol was being given for CAD prophylaxis, but the catecholamine surge of surgery can overcome its effect; we still use adenosine for acute termination.<br /></br>Take-home: “Stable narrow-complex regular tachycardia at ~200 = Give adenosine.”  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient)<br /></br>• Rhythm description, vitals, level of consciousness.<br /></br>• Time, dose, and response to adenosine.<br /></br>• Plans for telemetry and electrolyte evaluation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• AVNRT: Two pathways in AV node (slow &amp; fast). Premature atrial beat → impulse travels down slow pathway while fast pathway is refractory → re-enters retrograde = rapid circular conduction 150-250 bpm.<br /></br>• Adenosine → activates A1 receptors → ↑ K⁺ efflux, ↓ Ca²⁺ influx → transient (&lt;10 s) AV-node block → breaks the circuit.<br /></br>   – Side effects: flushing, chest burning, bronchospasm (caution in severe asthma/COPD).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment Algorithm (Narrow-complex, Regular, Stable)<br /></br>1. Vagal maneuvers (Valsalva, carotid massage).<br /></br>2. Adenosine 6 mg → 12 mg → 12 mg if needed.<br /></br>3. If refractory: IV β-blocker (eg, metoprolol), diltiazem, or amiodarone.<br /></br>4. If unstable at any point: synchronized cardioversion.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>• “AVA”: Adenosine-Vagal-Adenosine for stable SVT.<br /></br>• Rate ~200 and regular = “NODAL problem” → N (Narrow) → N → Node → adenosine.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Amiodarone therapy – Slower onset; reserved for refractory SVT or wide-complex tachycardias. Not first-line for stable narrow SVT.<br /></br>c. Digoxin therapy – Provides AV-node blockade but onset is hours; useful for rate control in atrial fibrillation/flutter, not acute PSVT termination.<br /></br>d. Metoprolol therapy – β-blocker could slow AV conduction, but IV onset is slower than adenosine and patient is already on propranolol. Adenosine remains guideline first-line.<br /></br>e. Electrical cardioversion – Appropriate ONLY if patient is unstable (hypotension, ischemia, altered mental status) or drug therapy fails. This patient is stable.<br /></br>f. Pacemaker – Used for bradyarrhythmias or tachyarrhythmia prevention (overdrive pacing), not acute management of PSVT.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: For a hemodynamically stable, regular, narrow-complex tachycardia at ~150-250 bpm, adenosine is the rapid, diagnostic, and therapeutic drug of choice.</p>
21. A 57-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-month history of intermittent chest pain. He describes the pain as a squeezing sensation in the center of his chest that lasts for 2 to 3 minutes. The pain worsens when he walks outside or becomes upset. He also has noted the pain after eating large meals. He has not had nausea or vomiting. He has hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, and osteoarthritis. Medications are hydrochlorothiazide, simvastatin, and acetaminophen. Family history is noncontributory. The patient does not smoke cigarettes. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 102 kg (225 lb); BMI is 32 kg/m2. His pulse is 80/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 142/92 mm Hg. Lungs are clear to auscultation. On cardiac examination, a regular rhythm and prominent S4 are heard with no murmurs. ECG shows nonspecific ST-T wave abnormalities in leads V3–V4. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Angina pectoris</br>b. Esophageal spasm</br>c. Gastroesophageal reflux disease</br>d. Myocardial infarction</br>e. Pulmonary embolism</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Angina pectoris  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Demographics/Risk: 57-year-old man with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, obesity (classic coronary artery disease risk factors).<br /></br>• Quality &amp; Timing: “Squeezing” substernal pain lasting 2–3 min (brief, &lt;10 min).<br /></br>• Precipitating factors: Walking outside, emotional stress, large meals (all increase myocardial O2 demand).<br /></br>• Relief: Although not explicitly stated, cessation of activity implies relief—typical of exertional (stable) angina.<br /></br>• Exam/ECG: Prominent S4 (LVH from long-standing HTN) and nonspecific ST-T changes—common baseline findings in chronic CAD; no acute ischemic changes.  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: Substernal + Exertional + Transient (&lt;10 min) = classic triad for stable angina.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework for chest-pain questions (think “OPQRST + 4 Ps”):<br /></br>1. Onset/Provocation → exertion, stress, big meals → angina.<br /></br>2. Quality → pressure/squeezing vs pleuritic/sharp/burning.<br /></br>3. Radiation → neck, jaw, left arm (often in angina).<br /></br>4. Duration → &lt;10 min (stable angina), &gt;30 min (MI), seconds (musculoskeletal).<br /></br>5. Associated symptoms &amp; risk factors.<br /></br>4 Ps to recall precipitants of stable angina: Physical exertion, Psychological stress, Post-prandial (large meals), cold air (Polar).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-by-step thought process:<br /></br>1. Ask: “Does the pain sound ischemic?” Substernal squeezing + classic triggers → yes.<br /></br>2. Duration is short and reproducible with the same threshold of activity → chronic stable angina, not acute coronary syndrome.<br /></br>3. No rest pain, no crescendo pattern, no biomarker data suggesting infarction → rules out unstable angina/NSTEMI/MSTEMI.<br /></br>Take-home: Stable angina is episodic chest discomfort precipitated by increased oxygen demand and relieved by rest or nitroglycerin, due to fixed (&gt;70%) atherosclerotic coronary narrowing.  </p></br><p>Documentation (if real patient): Character, location, duration, provoking/relieving factors, CAD risk profile, baseline ECG, plan for exercise stress test and antianginal therapy.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Core problem: Fixed atherosclerotic plaque → supply-demand mismatch during stress.<br /></br>• LVH from HTN (S4) further increases demand and impairs diastolic filling, lowering subendocardial perfusion reserve.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management (high-yield recap)  </p></br><p>Evaluation:<br /></br>1. Baseline ECG (already done).<br /></br>2. Functional testing (exercise ECG or nuclear/pharmacologic stress) if able to exercise and baseline ECG interpretable.<br /></br>3. If high risk or abnormal stress, proceed to coronary angiography.  </p></br><p>Treatment algorithm:<br /></br>• Immediate symptom control: Sublingual nitroglycerin PRN.<br /></br>• Chronic therapy:<br /></br>  – β-blocker (eg, metoprolol) first-line to ↓ HR &amp; contractility.<br /></br>  – Long-acting nitrates, calcium-channel blockers, or ranolazine if symptoms persist.<br /></br>• Prognostic therapy:<br /></br>  – Aspirin (or clopidogrel) and high-intensity statin.<br /></br>  – ACE inhibitor/ARB for HTN, DM, or LV dysfunction.<br /></br>  – Lifestyle: smoking cessation, diet, weight loss, exercise.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “SET” for Stable angina:<br /></br>S = Substernal squeezing<br /></br>E = Exertional/emotional/meal trigger<br /></br>T = Transient (&lt;10 min, relieved by rest or nitro)  </p></br><p>Picture a traffic “Stop-light” (Stable angina = Yellow): only occurs when you “step on the gas” (exert), stops when you “let off.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Esophageal spasm<br /></br>• Can mimic angina and be relieved by nitro, but usually unrelated to exertion; often severe dysphagia or odynophagia; triggered by temperature extremes. No GI motility history given.  </p></br><p>C. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)<br /></br>• Burning epigastric pain worsens when lying down or after spicy/fatty food; not classically exertional; often has sour taste/regurgitation.  </p></br><p>D. Myocardial infarction<br /></br>• Pain &gt;30 min, often at rest, accompanied by diaphoresis, nausea, autonomic symptoms; ECG would likely show ST-segment elevation or new Q-waves if STEMI, or specific ST-T changes if NSTEMI, not just “nonspecific abnormalities.”  </p></br><p>E. Pulmonary embolism<br /></br>• Sudden pleuritic pain, dyspnea, tachycardia, hypoxia; risk factors (recent surgery, immobilization) typically present; chest pain not predictably exertional and usually sharp, not squeezing.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: A middle-aged patient with CAD risk factors who experiences brief, reproducible exertional chest pressure that resolves with rest has classic stable (effort) angina pectoris.</p>
22. A 68-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of shortness of breath with exertion and a sensation of pressure in her mid chest. Initially, her symptoms occurred after she walked up a second flight of stairs to her office; they resolved with 10 minutes of rest. During the past 4 months, her symptoms have begun as she finishes walking up the first flight of stairs. If she rests for 5 minutes, she is able to walk up the second flight of stairs. She has not had pain at rest, during the night, or after eating. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. There is no family history of serious illness. The patient has never smoked cigarettes. She appears in no distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. On cardiac examination, a grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur is heard best at the base of the heart, and there is an S4; there is radiation to the carotid arteries. Fasting serum lipid studies show:</br>Total cholesterol   217 mg/dL</br>HDL-cholesterol     59 mg/dL</br>LDL-cholesterol     140 mg/dL</br>Triglycerides       90 mg/dL</br></br>Results of other laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. An ECG shows non-specific ST-T wave changes. Echocardiography shows an ejection fraction of 45 percent and severe aortic valve stenosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Afterload and preload reduction</br>b. Afterload reduction only</br>c. Balloon valvotomy</br>d. Preload reduction only</br>e. Valve replacement</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>E. Valve replacement (surgical [SAVR] or trans-catheter [TAVR])</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Chief complaints: 6-month history of exertional dyspnea and “pressure” (angina equivalent) that is clearly progressive → symptomatic.<br /></br>• Murmur: harsh, crescendo–decrescendo systolic ejection murmur, best at the base with carotid radiation + S4 → classic for aortic stenosis (AS).<br /></br>• Echocardiogram: “severe aortic valve stenosis” + EF 45 % (abnormal).<br /></br>• “SAD” symptom triad of AS (Syncope, Angina, Dyspnea) – she already has A &amp; D.<br /></br>Key test-day rule: Symptomatic severe AS (or EF ≤50 % even if asymptomatic) = valve replacement, not medical therapy.</p></br><p>How to approach similar stems</br>1. Identify murmur location, radiation, and timing.<br /></br>2. Confirm severity on echo.<br /></br>3. Ask: Is the patient symptomatic or is EF &lt;50 %? If yes → replace valve.<br /></br>4. Resist reaching for “medical optimization” answers; they do NOT improve survival in severe degenerative AS.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process:</br>1. Diagnose AS from physical exam.<br /></br>2. Echo already tells you severity (“severe”).<br /></br>3. Patient has limiting symptoms → class I indication for definitive therapy.<br /></br>4. Only effective definitive therapy = mechanical correction (SAVR or TAVR, depending on surgical risk).<br /></br>Core takeaway: In adults, once severe AS becomes symptomatic, median survival ≈ 2–3 yrs without valve replacement; medical therapy is palliative at best and may be harmful.</p></br><p>Documentation items (real life): symptom description &amp; NYHA class, echo parameters (valve area, gradient, EF), surgical-risk discussion, referral to cardiothoracic surgery/TAVR team.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Etiology in elderly = calcific degeneration → concentric LV hypertrophy → ↑LVEDP → heart can no longer augment stroke volume on exertion → angina &amp; dyspnea.<br /></br>• Fixed obstruction means cardiac output is preload-dependent and afterload-insensitive; vasodilators or diuretics can precipitate hypotension.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests</br>Definitive:<br /></br>• SAVR (bioprosthetic or mechanical) OR TAVR if high/intermediate surgical risk.<br /></br>Temporary/Bridge:<br /></br>• Balloon aortic valvuloplasty – children/young adults with congenital bicuspid valves, or a palliative bridge while awaiting TAVR; high restenosis rate in calcific AS.<br /></br>Medical therapy:<br /></br>• No mortality benefit. Use cautiously (low-dose diuretics for volume overload, cautious β-blockers for rate control).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Severe AS? SAD Pt? Send to Surgery!”<br /></br>SAD = Syncope, Angina, Dyspnea → automatic referral.<br /></br>Mnemonic “SAVeR” = Severe Aortic Valve → Replace.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Afterload and preload reduction (e.g., ACE-I + diuretics)<br /></br>   • Vasodilators can drop coronary perfusion pressure; diuretics lower preload needed to maintain CO → syncope. Provides no survival benefit.</p></br><p>b. Afterload reduction only<br /></br>   • Same concern; obstruction is fixed, so vasodilation doesn’t meaningfully reduce gradient and can cause hypotension.</p></br><p>c. Balloon valvotomy<br /></br>   • Useful in rheumatic or congenital AS in younger patients. In older adults with calcific, heavily sclerotic valves, restenosis occurs within months; not definitive.</p></br><p>d. Preload reduction only<br /></br>   • Diuretics may alleviate pulmonary congestion but can precipitate severe hypotension/low output in a preload-dependent lesion; again, no mortality benefit.</p></br><p>e. Valve replacement – correct<br /></br>   • Only therapy proven to improve symptoms and survival in symptomatic severe AS (and/or EF &lt; 50 %). Median survival after symptom onset without surgery is poor (angina 5 yrs, syncope 3 yrs, dyspnea 2 yrs).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>Symptomatic severe aortic stenosis (or LV EF &lt; 50 %) is a class I indication for aortic valve replacement; medical therapy or balloon valvotomy is inadequate in an elderly patient with calcific disease.</p>
23. A 55-year-old woman comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. She feels well. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Her mother had hypertension and died of a cerebral infarction at the age of 78 years. The patient does not smoke cigarettes. She drinks one glass of red wine daily. She is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 70 kg (155 lb); BMI is 24 kg/m2. Her blood pressure is 162/99 mm Hg. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Serum studies show:</br>Creatinine        0.8 mg/dL</br>Cholesterol, total 240 mg/dL</br>HDL-cholesterol    45 mg/dL</br>LDL-cholesterol   160 mg/dL</br>Triglycerides     140 mg/dL</br></br>Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Cardiac stress scintigraphy</br>b. Losartan therapy</br>c. Recommendation for cessation of alcohol consumption</br>d. Renal artery ultrasonography</br>e. Repeat blood pressure measurement in 1 week</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Repeat blood pressure measurement in 1 week</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key data: 55-year-old, completely asymptomatic, single office BP 162/99 mm Hg, normal BMI, no end-organ findings, normal creatinine, routine health-maintenance visit.<br /></br>• Stage 2 range (&gt;160 systolic or &gt;100 diastolic) but still only ONE documented reading.<br /></br>• No red flags (e.g., papilledema, headache, neurologic deficit, flash pulmonary edema, creatinine rise) suggesting hypertensive emergency or urgency that would mandate immediate therapy.<br /></br>• First step when an elevated BP is found in an otherwise stable patient is to confirm the diagnosis with a repeat measurement on a different day (ACC/AHA and JNC 8: at least two readings on two separate encounters unless BP ≥180/120 or acute end-organ damage).</p></br><p>Cognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Ask: Is this a hypertensive emergency? (No.)<br /></br>2. If not, has HTN been documented on ≥2 separate occasions? (No.)<br /></br>3. Therefore: schedule a repeat measurement within days-weeks based on the level (≥160/100 → within 1 week).</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Ruling in essential hypertension requires reproducibility; white-coat effect and measurement error are common.<br /></br>• Early confirmation prevents over-diagnosis and unnecessary lifelong therapy.<br /></br>• Documentation needed: time of day, arm used, cuff size, position, and instructions given for repeat visit.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Always confirm elevated BP with a second reading on a different day unless the patient is in hypertensive crisis.</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (Essential Hypertension)<br /></br>• Multifactorial: genetic predisposition, increased peripheral vascular resistance, possible sympathetic overactivity, renal sodium retention.<br /></br>• Long-term risks: CAD, stroke, renal failure, heart failure, aortic aneurysm.</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Management Algorithm (simplified)<br /></br>1. Confirm with repeat office BP (or home/ambulatory monitoring).<br /></br>2. Stage BP and assess ASCVD risk/compelling indications.<br /></br>3. Begin non-pharmacologic therapy for all (DASH diet, exercise, ↓Na⁺, weight loss, limit EtOH).<br /></br>4. Add pharmacologic agents (thiazide, ACE-I/ARB, CCB, etc.) if ≥130/80 (ACC/AHA) or ≥140/90 (JNC 8) after confirmation—or immediately for very high readings or TOD.</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“2-2-2 Rule” for uncomplicated HTN: <br /></br>• 2 readings<br /></br>• on 2 separate visits<br /></br>• over ≥2 weeks<br /></br>…before labeling a patient hypertensive.</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Cardiac stress scintigraphy – Indicated for chest pain evaluation or high ASCVD risk with symptoms; not for initial work-up of asymptomatic HTN.<br /></br>b. Losartan therapy – Reasonable first-line drug once HTN is confirmed; premature without a second BP reading.<br /></br>c. Cessation of alcohol – Moderate (≤1 drink/day for women) is acceptable; no evidence of excess intake contributing to HTN here.<br /></br>d. Renal artery ultrasonography – Screen for renovascular HTN when onset is sudden &lt;30 y or refractory, or if abdominal bruit, flash pulmonary edema, or unexplained rise in creatinine with ACE-I/ARB. None present.</p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In an asymptomatic patient with a single stage-2 BP reading, the next appropriate step is to repeat the measurement within 1 week to confirm the diagnosis before initiating therapy.</p>
24. A 72-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of swelling and discoloration of his right leg. He describes his symptoms as mildly uncomfortable but not painful. He received the diagnosis of deep venous thrombosis 8 months ago after undergoing total knee replacement; he received warfarin therapy for 6 months, and his INR remained at 2 to 3 during this period. He has hyperlipidemia treated with atorvastatin. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination of the right lower extremity shows reddish-brown hyperpigmentation below the knee; there is edema and tenderness to palpation below the knee. There are well-healed arthroscopic scars over the knee. Distal pulses are intact. Venous duplex ultrasonography shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Pelvic vein thrombosis</br>b. Postphlebitic syndrome</br>c. Superficial phlebitis</br>d. Thrombophlebitis</br>e. Varicose veins</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Post-phlebitic syndrome (also called post-thrombotic syndrome)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that steer you to chronic venous insufficiency after a prior DVT:<br /></br>• “72-year-old man … 3-month history” → subacute/chronic timing, not acute.<br /></br>• “Swelling and discoloration … mildly uncomfortable but not painful” → venous stasis findings rather than the severe pain of an acute DVT.<br /></br>• “Deep venous thrombosis 8 months ago … therapeutic INR the entire 6-month course” → he had appropriate treatment, so a new clot is unlikely.<br /></br>• Physical exam: “reddish-brown hyperpigmentation below the knee, edema, tenderness” → classic stasis dermatitis/hemosiderin deposition from chronically elevated venous pressure.<br /></br>• “Venous duplex ultrasonography shows no abnormalities” → rules out a current DVT.  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: Prior DVT + normal duplex now + chronic edema/skin changes = post-phlebitic (post-thrombotic) syndrome.</p></br><p>Test-day cognitive approach<br /></br>1. Ask: Acute vs chronic?<br /></br>2. Check imaging: No clot → think of sequelae.<br /></br>3. Look for skin changes: brown discoloration, lipodermatosclerosis, ulcer risk.<br /></br>4. Link to prior DVT → valve damage → venous hypertension → post-phlebitic syndrome.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology<br /></br>• A DVT can destroy venous valves.<br /></br>• Loss of valvular competence → venous reflux and chronic venous hypertension.<br /></br>• Results: dependent pitting edema, brown hemosiderin deposition, stasis dermatitis, venous leg ulcers, aching that worsens with prolonged standing and improves with elevation.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>– Chronic venous insufficiency secondary to previous DVT.<br /></br>– Extent of edema, skin changes, pulses.<br /></br>– Counsel on compression stockings, leg elevation, ulcer prevention.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot<br /></br>First-line:<br /></br>1. Graduated compression stockings (≥30 mm Hg).<br /></br>2. Leg elevation, daily walking/exercise, weight control.<br /></br>3. Skin care to prevent ulceration; treat dermatitis with topical steroids if inflamed.  </p></br><p>Refractory cases:<br /></br>• Interventional radiology or vascular surgery evaluation (e.g., venous stenting, valve reconstruction) if disabling.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid<br /></br>“P-O-S-T”:<br /></br>P: Previous DVT<br /></br>O: Outflow valve damage<br /></br>S: Stasis changes (brown Skin)<br /></br>T: Tibia &amp; calf swelling  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the other choices are wrong  </p></br><p>A. Pelvic vein thrombosis<br /></br>• Would still produce leg swelling, but duplex of the leg veins would be negative AND symptoms would be more acute; also usually painful. No chronic hyperpigmentation.  </p></br><p>C. Superficial phlebitis<br /></br>• Presents with a tender, palpable cord along a superficial vein, erythema localized to the tract; does not cause diffuse edema or brown discoloration.  </p></br><p>D. Thrombophlebitis (acute DVT)<br /></br>• Characterized by acute unilateral calf pain/swelling, warmth. Duplex would show thrombus. Patient is asymptomatic for 8 months and imaging is negative.  </p></br><p>E. Varicose veins<br /></br>• Tortuous, visible superficial veins with heaviness/aching. Hyperpigmentation and significant edema are late findings and usually occur after many years, not 3 months after surgery.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Take-home Message<br /></br>After an adequately treated DVT, destruction of venous valves can lead to chronic venous hypertension manifested by edema, brown stasis dermatitis, and mild discomfort—classic post-phlebitic (post-thrombotic) syndrome. Recognize it by timing (months-to-years after DVT), physical findings, and normal duplex ultrasonography.</p>
25. A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician for a routine examination. She feels well. Two years ago, she underwent hysterectomy because of leiomyomata uteri. Her only medications are a multivitamin and calcium and vitamin D supplementation. She received the BCG vaccine as a child. Her mother was diagnosed with breast cancer at the age of 61 years. The patient emigrated from China 3 years ago. She does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol. She eats a "prudent" diet. She does not exercise. She works as a biologist studying obesity with murine models. She is sexually active and monogamous with her husband of 6 years. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. A PPD skin test shows 12 mm of induration at 48 hours. A chest x-ray shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Fasting serum lipid studies</br>b. Hepatitis A vaccination</br>c. Hepatitis B vaccination</br>d. Isoniazid therapy</br>e. Mammography</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Isoniazid therapy (with pyridoxine supplementation)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. “Emigrated from China 3 years ago” → Recent immigrant (&lt; 5 years) from a country with high tuberculosis prevalence.<br /></br>2. “PPD skin test shows 12 mm of induration” → Meets the ≥ 10-mm threshold that defines a positive test for recent immigrants.<br /></br>3. “Chest x-ray shows no abnormalities” → Rules out active pulmonary disease, points toward latent TB infection (LTBI).<br /></br>4. “Received BCG vaccine as a child” → BCG can cause mild false-positive PPD responses, but ≥ 10 mm in a high-risk patient is still considered positive; BCG does NOT change management in US guidelines.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>• Step 1—Know the three size cutoffs (5, 10, 15 mm).<br /></br>• Step 2—Place the patient in one of the risk categories.<br /></br>• Step 3—If CXR is normal → LTBI → treat.<br /></br>• Memorize with “5-10-15” rule and the mnemonic HI-INH (High-risk 5 mm, Immigration 10 mm, No-risk 15 mm → INH for LTBI).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• This patient is high-risk for TB exposure (recent immigrant) → positive at ≥ 10 mm.<br /></br>• Normal CXR rules out active disease → latent TB.<br /></br>• Standard first-line regimen for LTBI is 6–9 months of INH + pyridoxine.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Any patient with a positive PPD/IGRA based on risk-stratified cutoffs and no evidence of active disease receives LTBI therapy.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real-world):<br /></br>• PPD result with induration size and risk category.<br /></br>• CXR report confirming no infiltrate/cavitation.<br /></br>• Start INH 300 mg daily + pyridoxine 25–50 mg daily; counsel on hepatotoxicity and need for baseline/periodic LFTs.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Mycobacterium tuberculosis enters alveoli, survives in macrophages → granuloma formation.<br /></br>• Latent phase: organism dormant, asymptomatic, non-infectious. Risk of reactivation ≈ 5–10 % lifetime (higher in immunosuppressed).<br /></br>• INH inhibits mycolic-acid synthesis, sterilizing latent bacilli.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Test Algorithm<br /></br>1. Positive screening test (PPD ≥ cutoff or IGRA).<br /></br>2. CXR to exclude active disease.<br /></br>3. If CXR negative → INH x 9 mo (alt: rifampin x 4 mo).<br /></br>4. Add pyridoxine to prevent peripheral neuropathy.<br /></br>5. Baseline LFTs; stop drug if transaminases &gt; 5× ULN or symptomatic hepatitis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>• “5-10-15 inches of tuberculin”:<br /></br>  – 5 mm → HIV, recent contact, transplant, immunosuppressed.<br /></br>  – 10 mm → Immigrant (&lt; 5 y), IV drugs, healthcare, kids &lt; 4 y.<br /></br>  – 15 mm → Everybody else.<br /></br>• “INH for Inactive (latent) iNfection.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Fasting serum lipid studies<br /></br>   – USPSTF: Women without CVD risk factors are screened at ≥ 45 y (men ≥ 35 y). She is 32 with no risk factors—screening not yet indicated.</p></br><p>b. Hepatitis A vaccination<br /></br>   – Recommended for chronic liver disease, MSM, illicit drug use, or travel to endemic areas. She has none of these indications.</p></br><p>c. Hepatitis B vaccination<br /></br>   – Indicated for healthcare workers with blood exposure, MSM, ESRD, etc. A murine-model biologist has minimal occupational risk; vaccine would be reasonable but not the best next step compared with treating proven LTBI.</p></br><p>e. Mammography<br /></br>   – Average-risk women: begin at 50 y (USPSTF) or 40 y (ACS). Start 10 y before first-degree relative’s diagnosis if that was &lt; 50 y. Mother diagnosed at 61 → initiate at 40. She is only 32.</p></br><p>Key difference: Only isoniazid addresses an actual, proven pathology today (latent tuberculosis), whereas other options are either premature or not indicated.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Take-Home Message<br /></br>A ≥ 10-mm PPD in a recent immigrant is positive regardless of prior BCG. Normal CXR → latent TB → treat with INH + pyridoxine to prevent future reactivation.</p>
26. A 92-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of an enlarging lump in her neck. She has congestive heart failure and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her medications are digoxin, hydrochlorothiazide, and metformin. She had smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 55 years but quit 17 years ago. She appears well developed and well nourished. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min and irregular, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 150/80 mm Hg. Examination of the ears, nose, mouth, and posterior pharynx shows no abnormalities. A 3-cm, firm mass is palpated at the left mandibular angle. The remainder of the examination, including chest x-ray, shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. PPD skin test</br>b. Kveim test</br>c. CT scan of the chest</br>d. MRI of the brain and neck</br>e. Fine-needle aspiration of the mass</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: e. Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of the mass</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – “92-year-old” + “2-month history” + “enlarging lump” → Any new, persistent neck mass in a patient &gt;40 yrs must be assumed malignant until proven otherwise.<br /></br>  – Location: “left mandibular angle” = typical site for metastatic or primary salivary/lymph-node tumors.<br /></br>  – No infectious symptoms (afebrile, normal oropharynx) and no pulmonary findings on CXR → makes benign or inflammatory etiologies less likely.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: firm, 3-cm, progressive.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives that narrow the differential: no URI, no dental infection, no systemic sarcoidosis clues, normal chest x-ray.<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: For an adult neck mass the algorithm is 1) thorough H&amp;P 2) obtain tissue. The least invasive, highest-yield way to get tissue is FNA. Imaging is for staging or surgical planning after diagnosis.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Stepwise approach:<br /></br>1. Suspicious mass → rule out malignancy.<br /></br>2. First diagnostic move = tissue cytology/histology (FNA).<br /></br>3. If malignant cells → then select imaging to stage (CT, MRI, PET/CT).<br /></br>Take-home message: “Neck mass + age &gt;40 = FNA first.”</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Common possibilities at this site/age:<br /></br>• Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma from oropharynx.<br /></br>• Primary salivary gland tumors (mucoepidermoid, Warthin, adenoid cystic).<br /></br>• Lymphoma.<br /></br>All require cytologic confirmation.</p></br><p>Diagnostics &amp; Management Algorithm<br /></br>1. FNA (office-based, &lt;1 mm needle, minimal risk).<br /></br>2. If positive or nondiagnostic → core biopsy or excisional biopsy.<br /></br>3. Imaging (CT neck with contrast or MRI) only after tissue dx for staging or surgical planning.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“&gt;40? Feel a lump? Grab a needle first.”<br /></br>OR<br /></br>“F-N-A: Find Neoplasm ASAP.”</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. PPD skin test: Useful for tuberculosis or atypical mycobacterial lymphadenitis (classically in children with posterior triangle nodes). Lacks constitutional or exposure clues; also TB nodes typically fluctuating/necrotic.<br /></br>b. Kveim test: Obsolete test for sarcoidosis; patient has no bilateral hilar adenopathy, uveitis, or erythema nodosum.<br /></br>c. CT scan of the chest: Appropriate only after malignancy is confirmed to stage aerodigestive tract tumors; initial priority is tissue diagnosis.<br /></br>d. MRI of the brain and neck: High-resolution imaging for perineural spread or skull-base lesions, but unnecessary before confirming what the mass is.<br /></br>e. Fine-needle aspiration: Provides immediate cytology, guides further work-up, low morbidity—correct.</p>
27. A 57-year-old woman with mixed connective tissue disease comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. She has a 6-month history of fatigue and progressive shortness of breath on exertion that improves within a few minutes of rest. She occasionally has a nonproductive cough when lying down. She has not noticed any wheezing. Her medications include prednisone and azathioprine. She works in an office as a secretary. She has never smoked cigarettes. She is 158 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 70 kg (28 kg/m2). Her temperature is 37 °C (98.6 °F), pulse is 68/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 120/72 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 97 percent at rest. Dry crackles are heard at both lung bases; they do not clear completely with coughing. Pedal pulses are 2+, and there is trace ankle edema bilaterally. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Anticyclic citrullinated peptide antibody testing  </br>b. Pulmonary function testing  </br>c. Exercise stress testing  </br>d. Polysomnography  </br>e. Cardiac catheterization</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>B. Pulmonary function testing (spirometry with lung volumes and DLCO)</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Known mixed connective tissue disease (high-risk condition for interstitial lung disease [ILD] and pulmonary arterial hypertension).<br /></br>  – 6-month history of progressive exertional dyspnea and dry, nonproductive “velcro”-like crackles at the lung bases.<br /></br>  – Normal resting oxygen saturation (early ILD often desaturates only with exertion).<br /></br>  – No wheezing, smoking history, or cardiac‐type chest pain.  </p></br><p>• Pertinent negatives (help rule out distractors)<br /></br>  – No orthopnea/PND or S3 → less suggestive of left-sided heart failure.<br /></br>  – No daytime somnolence, snoring, or obesity class II/III → less suggestive of OSA.<br /></br>  – Already has an established autoimmune diagnosis; no features suggesting a NEW rheumatologic work-up.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach: “Autoimmune disease + progressive exertional dyspnea + inspiratory crackles → think ILD or pulmonary HTN → screen with PFT first.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise approach to dyspnea in a CTD patient:</br>1. Screen non-invasively for lung involvement → Pulmonary function tests (PFTs).<br /></br>   • Restrictive physiology (↓FVC, normal or high FEV1/FVC) + ↓DLCO → ILD.<br /></br>   • Isolated ↓DLCO with near-normal spirometry → early pulmonary arterial hypertension.<br /></br>2. Abnormal PFTs are then followed by high-resolution CT, echocardiography, or right-heart cath depending on pattern.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In connective tissue diseases, routine PFTs are the initial test to detect subclinical or early symptomatic lung involvement before proceeding to imaging or invasive studies.</p></br><p>Documentation point (real patient): “Plan—Obtain full PFT with spirometry, lung volumes, and DLCO to evaluate for restrictive disease or pulmonary vascular process given new exertional dyspnea.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD) combines features of SLE, systemic sclerosis, and polymyositis; ~40–60 % develop lung complications.<br /></br>• Immune-mediated inflammation → interstitial fibrosis → restrictive defect, ↓gas-exchange surface area, ↓DLCO → exertional desaturation and “velcro” crackles.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Key Diagnostic / Management Points</br>First-line diagnostic test: Complete PFT.<br /></br>If restrictive pattern or ↓DLCO → next step is high-resolution CT chest ± rheumatology/pulmonology referral.<br /></br>Early detection allows initiation/adjustment of immunosuppression (e.g., mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide) or antifibrotics.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“CTD lungs need a PFT” – any Connective Tissue Disease with Dyspnea → do Pulmonary Function Test first.</p></br><p>Visual: Imagine a stethoscope hearing “VEL-cro” sounds at the Bases and immediately morphing into a Spirometry graph.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Anticyclic citrullinated peptide antibody testing<br /></br>   • Used to diagnose rheumatoid arthritis, not to evaluate dyspnea in someone with an already established CTD.</p></br><p>c. Exercise stress testing<br /></br>   • Assesses ischemic heart disease; dyspnea here lacks exertional chest pain/risk factors, and lung findings point away from primary cardiac ischemia.</p></br><p>d. Polysomnography<br /></br>   • Diagnoses obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, daytime somnolence, obesity). Dyspnea that improves with rest is not typical of OSA; plus lung exam would be normal in OSA.</p></br><p>e. Cardiac catheterization<br /></br>   • Right-heart cath is the gold standard for pulmonary hypertension, but it is invasive and not first line. PFTs (and echocardiogram) are screening tests before considering cath.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>Progressive exertional dyspnea with inspiratory crackles in a patient with mixed connective tissue disease warrants pulmonary function testing as the initial diagnostic step to screen for interstitial lung disease or pulmonary vascular disease.</p>
28. A 62-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 4-week history of intermittent epigastric pain, nausea, and fatigue. He has not had any other symptoms. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. The abdomen is flat and nontender. Test of the stool for occult blood is positive. His hemoglobin concentration is 10 g/dL, and hematocrit is 30 percent. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy shows nonspecific gastritis with a few gastric ulcerations. Biopsy specimens of the gastric mucosa show mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) lymphoma. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Antibiotic therapy</br>b. Chemotherapy only</br>c. Chemotherapy and radiation therapy</br>d. External beam radiotherapy</br>e. Subtotal gastrectomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Antibiotic therapy (H. pylori eradication regimen)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “gastric ulcerations,” “biopsy … MALT lymphoma,” “no prior illness/medications.”<br /></br>• Pertinent positive: Low-grade extranodal marginal-zone lymphoma of the stomach (aka gastric MALT).<br /></br>• Testable association: &gt;90 % of gastric MALT lymphomas are driven by chronic Helicobacter pylori infection.<br /></br>• Exam pattern: Whenever you see “MALT lymphoma” + “stomach,” think “treat the bug before treating the tumor.”</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach: 1) Recognize the histologic diagnosis. 2) Recall the step-wise management algorithm (eradicate H. pylori → restage → escalate only if persistent disease). 3) Resist the impulse to jump straight to chemo/radiation/surgery.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. The vast majority of stage I/II gastric MALT lymphomas regress completely after H. pylori eradication.<br /></br>2. Therefore, first-line therapy is a 10–14-day regimen: proton-pump inhibitor + clarithromycin + amoxicillin (or metronidazole).<br /></br>3. Restage with repeat endoscopy/biopsies ~3 months later; proceed to radiation or systemic therapy only if the lymphoma persists or if H. pylori testing is negative.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Gastric MALT = antibiotic-curable lymphoma.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls if this were a real patient: confirm H. pylori status (serology, stool antigen, or rapid urease test); prescribe triple therapy; arrange follow-up endoscopy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Chronic H. pylori infection recruits antigen-dependent B-cell proliferation within the gastric mucosa.<br /></br>• Over time, clonal expansion → extranodal marginal-zone lymphoma (MALT).<br /></br>• Removal of the antigenic drive (eradication of H. pylori) induces apoptosis and tumor regression.</p></br><p>Risk factors: chronic gastritis, autoimmune disorders (e.g., Sjögren, Hashimoto), age &gt;50 yr.</p></br><p>Clinical manifestations: often subtle—dyspepsia, occult GI bleeding, microcytic anemia.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. Step 1: H. pylori eradication—PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin (or metronidazole) × 14 days; confirm cure with stool antigen or urea breath test &gt;4 wks later.<br /></br>2. Step 2 (if needed):<br /></br>   • Persistent localized disease → involved-field radiation (30 Gy) OR rituximab-based chemo.<br /></br>   • Disseminated/high-grade transformation → systemic immunochemotherapy (e.g., R-CHOP).<br /></br>3. Surgery (subtotal gastrectomy) is largely historical and reserved for complications such as perforation or uncontrollable bleeding.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“MALT = Microbe-Associated Lymphoid Tumor → kill the microbe first.”<br /></br>Visual: Picture a pepperoni pizza (H. pylori) sitting on a Malt beer (MALT lymphoma); remove the pizza, the beer disappears.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Chemotherapy only – Appropriate for disseminated or antibiotic-refractory cases, not the initial step for a newly diagnosed, presumably early-stage gastric MALT.<br /></br>c. Chemotherapy and radiation therapy – Over-treatment at presentation; reserved for persistent or high-grade disease.<br /></br>d. External beam radiotherapy – Excellent local control after failed eradication, but not first-line when H. pylori is likely present.<br /></br>e. Subtotal gastrectomy – Rarely indicated today; morbidity outweighs benefit given high remission rates with medical therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>First treat the infection (H. pylori) → many gastric MALT lymphomas remit completely, sparing the patient from chemo, radiation, or surgery.</p>
29. A 27-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. Six months ago, she began treatment with a proton pump inhibitor for heartburn. Despite increasing the dosage to the maximum amount, her heartburn has not been relieved. She otherwise feels well and has had no weight loss. Examination today shows no abnormalities. Test of the stool for occult blood is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. CT scan of the chest</br>b. Esophageal acid infusion test</br>c. 24-Hour esophageal pH monitoring</br>d. Addition of an H2-receptor blocking agent</br>e. Switch to a different proton pump inhibitor</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. 24-Hour esophageal pH monitoring  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Chronic “heartburn” (typical GERD symptom) for ≥6 mo<br /></br>• Already on a proton-pump inhibitor (PPI) at maximal dose with no improvement → “PPI-refractory” symptoms<br /></br>• Age &lt;50 y, no dysphagia, odynophagia, GI bleeding, weight loss, vomiting → NO alarm features → upper endoscopy not mandated as first step<br /></br>• Normal physical exam, stool guaiac negative → low pre-test probability of peptic ulcer disease or malignancy  </p></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>1. GERD symptoms → start empiric PPI.<br /></br>2.
