Giardia lamblia  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Timeline &amp; chronicity: Symptoms began 1 month ago and are ongoing → points toward a chronic infectious or malabsorption process rather than an acute food-borne toxin.<br /></br>• Stool quality: “Semiliquid,” increased frequency, no blood or pus → non-inflammatory/watery diarrhea pattern.<br /></br>• Associated features: Abdominal bloating, anorexia, weight loss → red-flag for malabsorption. “Frothy, foul-smelling, greasy” stools are classic but not always specified on exams; “bloating” is a key clue.<br /></br>• Lack of systemic signs: No fever, no abdominal pain, no rectal bleeding → argues against invasive/bloody pathogens (e.g., Shigella, EHEC, Campylobacter, Yersinia).<br /></br>• Travel exposure: Fresh-water ingestion is common while swimming, camping, or scuba diving in developing regions; boyfriend may have avoided contaminated water → explains why only she is ill.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Decide inflammatory vs non-inflammatory diarrhea (blood, WBCs, fever?).<br /></br>2. Acute (&lt;14 days) vs persistent/chronic (&gt;14 days).<br /></br>3. Overlay epidemiology (travel, water, daycare, immunocompromise).<br /></br>Persistent, non-inflammatory, post-travel diarrhea → think Giardia, Cryptosporidium, Cyclospora. Giardia is most common and classically causes bloating/weight loss.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Giardia lamblia is a flagellated protozoan acquired from ingestion of cysts in contaminated water. Trophozoites adhere to the duodenal/jejunal mucosa, causing:<br /></br>• Villous atrophy &amp; brush-border enzyme deficiency → malabsorption → weight loss.<br /></br>• Lactose intolerance &amp; gas production → bloating.  </p></br><p>Key takeaway: Chronic watery diarrhea with malabsorption after freshwater exposure = Giardia.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient): duration/frequency of stools, travel/water history, weight change, absence of blood/fever, stool O&amp;P order, empiric metronidazole plan, counseling on water purification.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Ingested cyst → trophozoite in small bowel → mechanical irritation + enzyme impairment.<br /></br>• Incubation: ~1 week; symptoms can persist for weeks-months.<br /></br>• Immunity: IgA deficiency predisposes to severe/prolonged disease.<br /></br>• Complications: Fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, weight loss, failure to thrive in children.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment<br /></br>Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Stool ova &amp; parasites (O&amp;P) or stool antigen assay (higher sensitivity).<br /></br>2. If repeatedly negative but suspicion high → duodenal aspirate or string test.</p></br><p>Treatment (first-line)<br /></br>• Metronidazole 250 mg PO TID × 5–7 days (tinidazole or nitazoxanide are alternatives).<br /></br>• Treat close contacts if symptomatic; advise on boiling/filtration of water.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>• “Giardia the Backpacker”: think of a camper drinking mountain-stream water → greasy stools &amp; gas.<br /></br>• G-bloating, G-lamping (camping), G-iardia.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Escherichia coli O157:H7 (EHEC)<br /></br>• Produces shiga-like toxin → bloody diarrhea, severe abdominal pain, often afebrile; risk of hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS). Chronic bloating/weight loss is unusual.</p></br><p>c. Rotavirus<br /></br>• Most common severe diarrhea in infants/young children; short incubation; profuse watery stools for 3–8 days. Adults are usually asymptomatic or mildly affected; chronic course unlikely.</p></br><p>d. Vibrio cholerae<br /></br>• Acute onset of copious “rice-water” stools leading to rapid dehydration. Requires large inoculum unless gastric acidity is reduced; disease lasts days, not weeks, and weight loss is due to acute fluid loss, not malabsorption.</p></br><p>e. Yersinia enterocolitica<br /></br>• Invasive organism → febrile, bloody diarrhea with RLQ pain mimicking appendicitis; may cause reactive arthritis or erythema nodosum. Prolonged watery malabsorption picture is atypical.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Persistent watery diarrhea with bloating and weight loss after freshwater exposure in the absence of fever or blood is classic for Giardia lamblia infection.</p>
44. A male newborn is cyanotic immediately after delivery. He was born at term to a healthy 27-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 2, after an uncomplicated pregnancy and vaginal delivery. Examination shows no respiratory effort and poor muscle tone. His pulse is 60/min. Positive pressure ventilation and aggressive stimulation are begun. Examination 5 minutes later shows active movement of the extremities, grimacing in response to pain, acrocyanosis, and a strong cry. His pulse is now 120/min. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Observation only</br>b. Measurement of serum hemoglobin concentration</br>c. Echocardiography</br>d. Intravenous administration of 10 percent dextrose in water</br>e. Continued positive pressure ventilation</br>f. Intubation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Observation only  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Cyanotic immediately after delivery … no respiratory effort … pulse 60/min” → Newborn resuscitation algorithm is triggered (HR &lt; 100, absent respirations).<br /></br>• “Positive-pressure ventilation and aggressive stimulation are begun” → First-line intervention.<br /></br>• 5-minute reassessment:<br /></br>  – Active extremity movement &amp; strong cry → effective respirations.<br /></br>  – Pulse 120/min → HR &gt; 100 (normal).<br /></br>  – Color: acrocyanosis (blue hands/feet, pink trunk) → common, benign in the first 24 h.<br /></br>These are normal post-resuscitation findings; no ongoing distress or hemodynamic instability is present.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Apply the Neonatal Resuscitation Program (NRP) flowchart: Airway → Breathing → Circulation → Drugs.<br /></br>2. Reassess HR and respirations every 30 s; if HR &gt; 100 and adequate breathing, stop PPV and observe.<br /></br>3. Isolated acrocyanosis does NOT equal pathologic cyanosis.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-by-step:<br /></br>1. Initial instability (apnea, HR 60) warranted PPV.<br /></br>2. After intervention, newborn now has:</br>   • Adequate ventilation (strong cry)<br /></br>   • HR &gt; 100<br /></br>   • Only acrocyanosis (acceptable)<br /></br>3. No indication for further invasive therapy.<br /></br>Take-home: Once the physiological goals of NRP are met (HR &gt; 100, spontaneous breathing, good tone), the correct next step is routine post-delivery observation.</p></br><p>What would be documented: time and duration of PPV, HR trend, Apgar scores, final clinical status, plan for standard nursery care.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Primary apnea at birth can occur from transient perinatal respiratory depression.<br /></br>• PPV recruits alveoli and restores oxygenation; once fetal circulation transitions, central cyanosis resolves.<br /></br>• Peripheral vasoconstriction persists → acrocyanosis (blue hands/feet) but well-oxygenated core.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls<br /></br>• No labs or imaging are indicated when the newborn stabilizes quickly and completely.<br /></br>• Continue routine newborn care (thermoregulation, glucose monitoring per nursery protocol, vitamin K, eye prophylaxis).</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tip<br /></br>“100-Breathe, 60-Compress”:<br /></br>• HR &lt; 100 → Breathe (PPV)<br /></br>• HR &lt; 60 → Compress (chest compressions)<br /></br>• HR &gt; 100 with good respirations → Observe  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Serum hemoglobin concentration – Would be considered if persistent central cyanosis or pallor suggested anemia; cyanosis resolved.<br /></br>c. Echocardiography – Indicated for ongoing central cyanosis or murmur suggestive of congenital heart disease; not needed when child is now pink centrally.<br /></br>d. IV 10% dextrose – Used for hypoglycemia (jitteriness, lethargy); no symptoms or risk factors provided.<br /></br>e. Continued positive-pressure ventilation – Unnecessary; newborn is breathing and HR is 120. Excess PPV risks barotrauma.<br /></br>f. Intubation – Reserved for failed bag-mask ventilation, meconium with poor tone, or need for prolonged ventilation; criteria not met.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Core takeaway: After successful initial resuscitation, a term newborn with normal heart rate and spontaneous respirations requires only routine observation; isolated acrocyanosis is benign.</p>
45. A 37-year-old premenopausal woman undergoes a modified radical mastectomy with transverse rectus abdominis musculocutaneous (TRAM) flap reconstruction for a 4-cm infiltrating ductal carcinoma in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast. The axillary dissection yields two positive lymph nodes of 22 that were removed. The tumor is progesterone-receptor positive with a low S phase. Which of the following factors is most predictive of this patient’s survival?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Age</br>b. Lymph node involvement</br>c. Progesterone-receptor status</br>d. S-phase concentration</br>e. Tumor size</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Lymph node involvement  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Large invasive tumor (4-cm infiltrating ductal carcinoma) in a premenopausal woman → automatically high-risk disease.<br /></br>• Axillary dissection shows 2/22 positive nodes. Whether—and how many—axillary nodes contain metastases is the single most powerful prognostic datum for early-stage, non-metastatic breast cancer.<br /></br>• Other details (progesterone-receptor positive, low S-phase fraction) help guide therapy, but do not outweigh nodal status for long-term survival estimates.  </p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive Approach<br /></br>USMLE and shelf questions on breast cancer prognosis almost always hinge on:<br /></br>1. Axillary lymph-node status (number of involved nodes).<br /></br>2. Tumor size.<br /></br>3. Histologic grade &amp; receptor status (ER/PR, HER2) for therapy prediction.<br /></br>Train yourself to scan for the node count first—if any are positive, that usually trumps other listed factors for survival probability.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Survival in breast cancer is determined primarily by the extent of systemic spread.<br /></br>2. Axillary nodes are the first echelon; involvement indicates hematogenous/lymphatic dissemination potential.<br /></br>3. Each additional positive node incrementally worsens 5- and 10-year survival curves (e.g., 0 nodes ≈ 85–90 % 5-year; ≥4 nodes &lt;50 %).<br /></br>Take-home: Nodal status is the best single surrogate for occult metastatic burden.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Post-op note must include pathologic stage (pT, pN, pM) with exact node count and size of largest deposit, because that drives adjuvant chemo ± endocrine therapy decisions.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Breast cancer spreads first to intramammary &amp; axillary nodes via lymphatics.<br /></br>• Micrometastases in nodes herald the ability of tumor cells to survive outside the breast micro-environment, correlating with higher recurrence and mortality.<br /></br>• Hormone-receptor positivity reflects tumor biology (endocrine-responsive) but not necessarily tumor burden.  </p></br><p>Treatment/Diagnostic Framework<br /></br>1. Stage I–II with positive nodes → adjuvant chemotherapy (e.g., dose-dense AC-T) plus endocrine therapy if ER/PR+.<br /></br>2. Consider radiation to chest wall and regional nodes if ≥4 positive nodes or large tumor.<br /></br>3. Genomic assays (Oncotype DX) supplement node-negative cases, less so when nodes are already positive.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Nodes dictate Prognosis; Receptors dictate Therapy.”<br /></br>Mnemonic: N-S-T:<br /></br>• N = Nodes → Survival<br /></br>• S = Size → Stage adjunct<br /></br>• T = (ER/PR) Therapy choice  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Age – Younger (&lt;35) can have more aggressive disease, but age is not as predictive as nodal status for survival.<br /></br>c. Progesterone-receptor status – Predicts benefit from tamoxifen/AI, not survival independent of treatment.<br /></br>d. S-phase concentration – High S-phase (or high Ki-67) suggests aggressive tumor but still less prognostic than node status.<br /></br>e. Tumor size – Important (part of TNM), yet node involvement has a stronger independent impact on survival curves, especially once nodes are positive.  </p></br><p>Core Takeaway<br /></br>For invasive breast cancer without distant metastases, the NUMBER of positive axillary lymph nodes is the most powerful single predictor of long-term survival.</p>
46. An 87-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of difficulty starting his urinary stream and occasional postvoid dribbling. He typically arises to void two times nightly. He has a 30-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus complicated by orthostatic hypotension secondary to autonomic dysfunction. Current medications include lisinopril and glyburide. His blood pressure is 140/80 mm Hg while supine and 100/60 mm Hg while standing. Examination shows an enlarged prostate. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient's urinary symptoms?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Amlodipine</br>b. Doxazosin</br>c. Finasteride</br>d. Metoprolol</br>e. Oxybutynin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Finasteride  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Cardinal complaint: “difficulty starting urinary stream, post-void dribbling, nocturia” → classic lower-urinary-tract symptoms (LUTS) of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH).<br /></br>• Physical clue: “enlarged prostate” on exam confirms obstructive etiology.<br /></br>• Comorbidity: Long-standing diabetes with autonomic neuropathy → baseline orthostatic hypotension (BP drops from 140/80 supine to 100/60 standing).<br /></br>• Medication selection pivot: Anything that further impairs α-adrenergic tone will aggravate his dizziness/falls risk.</p></br><p>Exam-strategy framework (“3-step BPH check”):<br /></br>1. Verify LUTS + enlarged prostate → BPH very likely.<br /></br>2. Look for red-flag complications (recurrent UTI, retention, hydronephrosis) → none; so medical therapy first.<br /></br>3. Screen for contraindications to α-blockers (orthostatic hypotension, severe CAD on multiple vasodilators) → present → choose 5-α-reductase inhibitor.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>First-line BPH drugs:<br /></br>• α1-blockers (tamsulosin, terazosin, doxazosin) → quickest symptom relief (days) but lower blood-pressure by inhibiting peripheral vascular α1-receptors.<br /></br>• 5-α-reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride) → shrink prostate (↓DHT) with slower onset (3–6 mo) and no hemodynamic effect.</p></br><p>Because this patient already has symptomatic orthostatic hypotension from diabetic autonomic neuropathy, an α-blocker would worsen dizziness and falls. Finasteride provides BPH relief without additional vasodilation, making it the safest pharmacologic option.</p></br><p>Take-home: In BPH + orthostatic hypotension (or polypharmacy antihypertensives), skip α-blockers and use finasteride (± later add α-blocker if well-tolerated). Document counseling on delayed onset and sexual side effects (↓libido, erectile dysfunction, gynecomastia).</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• BPH driven by dihydrotestosterone (DHT)-mediated stromal and epithelial proliferation in the peri-urethral (transitional) zone → bladder outlet obstruction → hesitancy, weak stream, dribbling, nocturia, incomplete emptying.<br /></br>• 5-α-reductase converts testosterone → DHT; inhibiting the enzyme shrinks prostatic volume ~20–30 % over 6 months and lowers PSA ~50 %.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment Algorithm (simplified)<br /></br>1. Mild symptoms → watchful waiting, behavioral changes.<br /></br>2. Moderate–severe →<br /></br>   • No hypotension: α1-blocker ± finasteride.<br /></br>   • Hypotension / orthostasis: Begin finasteride; can add tamsulosin (uro-selective) cautiously later.<br /></br>3. Refractory or complications → TURP or laser therapy.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“5 to survive a fall”: If you want your 85-year-old patient to “survive a fall,” give the 5-α drug (Finasteride) instead of the α-blocker.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Amlodipine – Dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker used for hypertension/angina; no mechanism to relieve prostatic obstruction; adds vasodilation → worsens orthostasis.<br /></br>b. Doxazosin – Non-selective α1-blocker does treat BPH but is notorious for first-dose syncope and orthostatic hypotension; contraindicated here.<br /></br>d. Metoprolol – β1-selective blocker; lowers heart rate/BP, no benefit for urinary obstruction, could exacerbate orthostatic symptoms.<br /></br>e. Oxybutynin – Antimuscarinic for overactive bladder/urge incontinence. In BPH it can precipitate acute urinary retention and causes anticholinergic side effects, especially problematic in the elderly.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Choose finasteride for symptomatic BPH in patients who cannot tolerate α-blockers due to orthostatic hypotension or other vasodilatory-sensitive conditions; remember onset is delayed but hemodynamics stay stable.</p>
47. An asymptomatic 37-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. She has a 19-year history of hypertension that has been difficult to control with medication. She has measured her blood pressure at home every morning for the past 3 weeks; readings have ranged between 148/92 mm Hg and 166/96 mm Hg. She currently takes no medications. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. She exercises four times weekly. She is employed as a police officer and describes her work as stressful. She is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. Her blood pressure is 160/94 mm Hg in both arms while sitting and 162/92 mm Hg in the right arm while standing. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Serum  </br>Na+  141 mEq/L  </br>K+  3.2 mEq/L  </br>Cl−  103 mEq/L  </br>HCO3−  30 mEq/L  </br>Urea nitrogen  15 mg/dL  </br>Creatinine  0.9 mg/dL  </br>Aldosterone:renin ratio  42  </br>Urine aldosterone  34 µg/24 h (N=3–20)  </br>An MRI of the abdomen shows no abnormalities. The most appropriate next step in management is to add which of the following to this patient's regimen?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Diltiazem  </br>b. Doxazosin  </br>c. Lisinopril  </br>d. Losartan  </br>e. Spironolactone  </br>f. Triamterene-hydrochlorothiazide</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>Spironolactone  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Refractory hypertension beginning at a young age (19-year history, patient now 37).  </li></br><li>Unexplained hypokalemia (serum K⁺ 3.2 mEq/L) with metabolic alkalosis (HCO₃⁻ 30 mEq/L).  </li></br><li>Aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR) markedly elevated (42; &gt;20 is suspicious).  </li></br><li>24-hour urine aldosterone elevated (34 µg/24 h; nl 3–20).  </li></br><li>Normal abdominal MRI → no discrete unilateral adenoma.</li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern: High aldosterone + low renin + hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis = primary aldosteronism (Conn syndrome). Imaging negative ⇒ most likely bilateral adrenal hyperplasia.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>• Young HTN + low K⁺ → think secondary causes.<br /></br>• Order ARR; if elevated, confirm with 24-h urine aldosterone or saline-suppression test; then imaging.<br /></br>• If unilateral lesion → surgery; if bilateral or non-localizing → mineralocorticoid receptor (MR) blockade.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Goal: Block aldosterone’s action.<br /></br>First-line medical therapy for bilateral hyperplasia = MR antagonists (spironolactone or eplerenone). They:</p></br><p>• Lower BP and correct hypokalemia.<br /></br>• Directly inhibit aldosterone effects in distal nephron (Na⁺ retention, K⁺ secretion, H⁺ secretion).</p></br><p>Documentation points if real patient: confirmatory study results, imaging interpretation, diagnosis of bilateral primary aldosteronism, counseling on lifelong MR antagonist therapy, K⁺ monitoring plan.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Resistant HTN + hypokalemia + high ARR → treat with MR antagonist when surgery is not an option.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Autonomous aldosterone secretion → ↑Na⁺/water retention → HTN, ↓renin (feedback), ↑K⁺ wasting → hypokalemia, ↑H⁺ loss → metabolic alkalosis.<br /></br>• Bilateral adrenal hyperplasia (~60%) vs. unilateral adenoma (~35%).  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Treatment Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>Screen: ARR &gt;20 with aldosterone &gt;15 ng/dL.  </li></br><li>Confirm: saline, fludrocortisone, oral salt load, or 24-h urine aldosterone.  </li></br><li>Localize: CT/MRI ± adrenal venous sampling.<br /></br>   • Unilateral adenoma → laparoscopic adrenalectomy.<br /></br>   • Bilateral hyperplasia or non-localizing → MR antagonist:<br /></br>     – Spironolactone (cheap, androgen/ progesterone side-effects).<br /></br>     – Eplerenone (more selective, fewer side-effects).<br /></br>   • Monitor K⁺ and creatinine.  </li></br></ol></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Conn = CONserve Na⁺” ⇒ you need to “SPIRal down” aldosterone with SPIRonolactone.<br /></br>Rhyme: “Low renin, high aldo, potassium falls below, give Spiro to slow the flow.”</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Diltiazem (non-DHP CCB): Lowers BP but does nothing for aldosterone-driven hypokalemia; not first-line for primary aldosteronism.  </p></br><p>b. Doxazosin (α₁-blocker): Used for BPH/HTN; no effect on aldosterone; would not correct electrolyte abnormality.  </p></br><p>c. Lisinopril (ACE inhibitor) &amp; d. Losartan (ARB): Increase renin and lower angiotensin II, but aldosterone secretion in primary aldosteronism is autonomous (renin-independent), so partial effect only; MR antagonist is superior.  </p></br><p>f. Triamterene-HCTZ: Triamterene is a potassium-sparing Na⁺ channel blocker and would help K⁺, but HCTZ stimulates renin and can worsen hypokalemia; overall less effective than direct MR blockade.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Key Exam Take-Home  </p></br><p>Resistant HTN + hypokalemia + high ARR = primary aldosteronism.<br /></br>• Unilateral mass → surgery.<br /></br>• Bilateral / imaging-negative → spironolactone (or eplerenone).</p>
48. A 52-year-old woman with systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) comes to the physician because she has had an increasingly painful ulcer on her left small toe for the past month. The ulcer was caused by minor trauma. She has not had fever or chills. A 10-day course of oral ciprofloxacin and daily application of topical antibiotic cream have not resolved the ulcer. Despite acetaminophen and ibuprofen therapy, she rates her pain as a 7 on a 10-point scale. Her other medications are prednisone and hydroxychloroquine. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 84/min and regular, and blood pressure is 145/85 mm Hg. Examination shows skin changes over the face and hands consistent with scleroderma. The left small toe is erythematous and edematous. There is a 1-cm, shaggy, fibrinous, full-thickness ulcer on the dorsum of the proximal interphalangeal joint; there is crepitus on palpation of the joint. The bone is visible. Cellulitis extends along the dorsum of the foot. The posterior tibial pulse is palpable. Culture of the ulcer grows methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy</br>b. Oral methotrexate therapy</br>c. High-dose oral prednisone and dicloxacillin therapy</br>d. Local debridement and topical mupirocin ointment</br>e. Amputation of the toe</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Amputation of the toe  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that narrow the differential:<br /></br>• Systemic sclerosis → chronic digital ischemia/vasculopathy → poor wound healing and impaired antibiotic penetration.<br /></br>• “Increasingly painful ulcer … 1 month … bone is visible” → full-thickness ulcer with exposed phalanx = very high pre-test probability of osteomyelitis.<br /></br>• “Crepitus on palpation” + erythema/edema/cellulitis tracking up the foot → gas-producing, aggressive soft-tissue infection.<br /></br>• Failure of 10 days of oral antibiotics and topical therapy → conservative care has failed.<br /></br>• MRSA recovered from culture → virulent organism, but the prime issue is devitalized bone, not simply choice of drug.<br /></br>• Palpable posterior tibial pulse → macro-perfusion intact, so revascularization is not the barrier; the barrier is devitalized, infected tissue.  </p></br><p>High-yield pattern:<br /></br>Visible/probe-to-bone in an ischemic toe ulcer + signs of spreading infection = osteomyelitis until proven otherwise. In a vasculopathic digit, the definitive treatment is surgical resection (often amputation).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Ask “Is there exposed bone?” If yes, assume osteomyelitis.<br /></br>2. Decide whether antibiotics can reach the site (perfusion) and whether bone is salvageable. Chronic ischemia + scleroderma → poor host bed.<br /></br>3. If the wound is not salvageable or infection threatens spread, choose amputation.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic to the correct choice:<br /></br>1. Exposed bone + positive clinical findings = osteomyelitis (probe-to-bone has ≈89 % specificity).<br /></br>2. Osteomyelitis in a poorly perfused digit is unlikely to be cured with antibiotics alone (requires ≥6 wk IV therapy, high failure rate in scleroderma vasculopathy).<br /></br>3. Crepitus and proximal cellulitis indicate aggressive infection that can progress to necrotizing fasciitis or sepsis.<br /></br>4. Rapid source control is paramount → surgical removal of all infected/devitalized tissue. For a small toe this is best accomplished by toe amputation.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: “Bone seen = bone gone” in an ischemic, infected toe. Early amputation prevents limb- or life-threatening spread.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real practice):<br /></br>• Describe ulcer depth, exposed bone, crepitus, and extent of cellulitis.<br /></br>• Assessment: infected digital ulcer with suspected osteomyelitis, systemic sclerosis vasculopathy.<br /></br>• Plan: Surgical consult for toe amputation, peri-operative IV antibiotics (e.g., vancomycin) pending sensitivities.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Systemic sclerosis digital ulcers:<br /></br>• Pathogenesis: Intimal proliferation &amp; fibrosis of small arteries → chronic Raynaud ischemia → minor trauma → full-thickness ulcer.<br /></br>• Poor perfusion → low tissue pO₂, impaired neutrophil function, limited drug delivery → high risk of chronic infection and osteomyelitis.  </p></br><p>Osteomyelitis in foot ulcers:<br /></br>• Pathogens: S. aureus (MSSA/MRSA) most common; polymicrobial with chronic or neuropathic ulcers.<br /></br>• Diagnosis: Exposed bone or positive probe-to-bone + inflammatory signs; MRI is confirmatory but unnecessary when bone is visible.<br /></br>• Treatment:<br /></br>  – Adequate perfusion &amp; salvageable tissue → 4–6 wk pathogen-directed IV antibiotics ± debridement.<br /></br>  – Poor perfusion/necrotic bone or failure of antibiotics → surgical resection / amputation.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Tests Review  </p></br><p>Not needed here: imaging, hyperbaric O₂, immunosuppressive escalation. Surgical source control is definitive. Peri-op IV antibiotics (e.g., vancomycin for MRSA) are adjunctive.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic: “IF BONE IS SHOWN, IT’S GOTTA BE GONE.”<br /></br>• Bone seen/probed ⇒ suspect osteomyelitis ⇒ consider amputation/debridement if perfusion is poor.  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a skeleton toe sticking out of the ulcer shouting “Cut me off before the germs march north!”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy<br /></br>• Used as adjunct for chronic diabetic foot ulcers without exposed bone or for radiation necrosis. Does not provide source control for established osteomyelitis with crepitus.  </p></br><p>B. Oral methotrexate therapy<br /></br>• Immunosuppressant for systemic sclerosis arthritis/skin; would WORSEN infection. No role in treating MRSA osteomyelitis.  </p></br><p>C. High-dose oral prednisone and dicloxacillin therapy<br /></br>• High-dose steroids further suppress immunity; dicloxacillin covers MSSA, not MRSA, and cannot penetrate ischemic bone well.  </p></br><p>D. Local debridement and topical mupirocin ointment<br /></br>• Superficial measure unsuitable for full-thickness ulcer with exposed bone &amp; gas-forming infection. Fails to eradicate osteomyelitis.  </p></br><p>E. Amputation of the toe (Correct)<br /></br>• Provides rapid, definitive source control for MRSA osteomyelitis in an ischemic, unsalvageable digit, preventing proximal spread and systemic complications.</p>
49. A 32-year-old man comes to the physician because of severe throbbing headaches that have been increasing in frequency over the past 3 months. He has a 10-year history of similar headaches that typically last 6 to 12 hours and are associated with nausea, vomiting, and photophobia. The headaches used to occur approximately every 6 months but now occur twice weekly. He is unable to identify any precipitating factors. Over-the-counter medications have been ineffective. He is otherwise healthy and active. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min, and blood pressure is 120/75 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate prophylaxis for his headaches?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Carbamazepine</br>b. Cyproheptadine</br>c. Ergotamine</br>d. Propranolol</br>e. Sumatriptan</br>f. Tramadol</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Propranolol  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pattern-recognition clues<br /></br>   • “Severe throbbing headaches” + “nausea, vomiting, photophobia” → classic description of migraine without aura.<br /></br>   • Duration 6–12 h, completely normal neurologic exam between attacks → supports benign primary headache.  </li></br><li>Disability &amp; frequency<br /></br>   • Formerly q 6 months, now twice weekly (≈8/mo).<br /></br>   • Over-the-counter agents no longer work.<br /></br>   • ≥4 attacks per month and functional impairment ⇒ guideline trigger for prophylactic therapy rather than relying only on abortive drugs.  </li></br><li>No red-flag features (eg, focal neurologic deficit, positional change, papilledema, infection, trauma), so evaluation focuses on migraine management, not imaging.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Approach framework for similar questions<br /></br>A. Confirm primary headache type (migraine vs cluster vs tension).<br /></br>B. Check frequency/severity → decide acute vs prophylactic therapy.<br /></br>C. Recall first-line preventive drug classes (β-blockers, antiepileptics, antidepressants, CGRP mAbs, onabotulinum) and pick one that is listed.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why Propranolol?)  </p></br><p>• β-blockers (propranolol, metoprolol, timolol) are first-line prophylactic agents endorsed by AAN/AHS guidelines for migraine prevention.<br /></br>• They reduce attack frequency likely via modulation of central adrenergic tone and cerebral vascular reactivity.<br /></br>• Daily dosing → takes 4–6 weeks for full effect; titrate to HR 50-60/min if tolerated.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Recurrent, disabling migraines warrant daily prophylaxis, and a non-selective β-blocker is guideline-supported first choice.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient)<br /></br>– Headache diary review: 8 migraines/mo, disabling, OTC failure.<br /></br>– Plan: Start propranolol 40 mg BID, counsel on side effects (fatigue, bradycardia, depression, bronchospasm).<br /></br>– Follow-up in 6 weeks with diary to assess ≥50% reduction in frequency.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Migraine = neurovascular disorder triggered by cortical spreading depression → release of vasoactive peptides (CGRP, substance P) → sterile neurogenic inflammation &amp; vasodilation of intracranial vessels → trigeminovascular pain. Preventive drugs blunt this cascade by:<br /></br>• β-blockers: dampen adrenergic excitability &amp; stabilize vascular tone.<br /></br>• Others: antiepileptics (topiramate, valproate) inhibit neuronal firing; TCAs enhance serotonergic tone; CGRP mAbs neutralize ligand or receptor.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>Indications for preventive therapy<br /></br>1. ≥4 migraine days/month or ≥3 days with severe disability.<br /></br>2. Attacks ≥12 h or significantly impair QoL.<br /></br>3. Contraindication/intolerance to acute meds.  </p></br><p>First-line preventive options<br /></br>• β-blockers: Propranolol, Metoprolol, Timolol.<br /></br>• Antiepileptics: Topiramate, Valproate.<br /></br>• Antidepressants: Amitriptyline, Venlafaxine.<br /></br>• CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab) if refractory.<br /></br>Avoid propranolol in asthma, severe depression, brady-arrhythmia.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“PREVENT migraines with ‘PRO-MIGrain BLOCKERS’”<br /></br>PROpranolol, Metoprolol, “IMItrex” (sumatriptan) for acute, not preventive → keeps acute vs prophylaxis straight.  </p></br><p>Or visualize a “β-BLOCK” barricading the head of a throbbing cartoon brain, preventing more migraine monsters from entering.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Carbamazepine<br /></br>   • First-line for trigeminal neuralgia &amp; focal seizures, not migraine prevention. Would expect brief electric-shock facial pain, not long throbbing headaches.  </p></br><p>b. Cyproheptadine<br /></br>   • Serotonin antagonist antihistamine used occasionally for pediatric or cyclic vomiting/serotonin syndrome management, not adult first-line prophylaxis.  </p></br><p>c. Ergotamine<br /></br>   • Ergot alkaloid used ACUTELY to abort migraine/cluster headaches; not taken daily due to risk of ergotism, vasospasm, medication-overuse headaches.  </p></br><p>e. Sumatriptan<br /></br>   • 5-HT1B/1D agonist for ACUTE abortion (works best if taken at onset). Not effective as daily prophylaxis.  </p></br><p>f. Tramadol<br /></br>   • Opioid analgesic; opioids are avoided in migraine because of dependence risk, medication-overuse headache, and lack of preventive efficacy.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only propranolol among the options is guideline-recommended for long-term migraine prevention in an otherwise healthy adult.</p>
50. A 37-year-old man with hepatitis C and HIV infection comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of edema and generalized fatigue. He has not had fever, vomiting, or any other symptoms. He is taking antiretroviral therapy. One month ago his CD4+ T-lymphocyte count was 240/mm3 (N≥500). He was treated for Mycobacterium avium-intracellulare complex infection 2 years ago. His blood pressure is 150/96 mm Hg. Examination shows pitting edema from the midcalves to the feet bilaterally. Laboratory studies show:</br></br>Serum  </br>Urea nitrogen    20 mg/dL  </br>Creatinine       1.8 mg/dL  </br></br>Urine  </br>Blood            negative  </br>Protein          4+  </br></br>Microscopic examination of urine shows no cells or casts. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Urine cytology  </br>b. Discontinuation of antiretroviral therapy  </br>c. Prednisone therapy  </br>d. Renal biopsy  </br>e. Hemodialysis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Renal biopsy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key findings: 37-year-old man, long-standing HIV + hepatitis C, new edema, HTN (150/96 mm Hg), serum creatinine 1.8 mg/dL, nephrotic-range proteinuria (4+ on dipstick ≈ ≥3.5 g/24 h), bland urine sediment (no cells/casts).<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no hematuria, no active urinary sediment, no systemic symptoms to suggest infection or malignancy.<br /></br>• Pattern: Adult-onset nephrotic syndrome without an immediately obvious single etiology (e.g., long-standing diabetes). In adults, most causes (FSGS, membranous nephropathy, membranoproliferative GN, HIV-associated nephropathy, etc.) require histologic confirmation to guide therapy.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Recognize nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria &gt; 3.5 g/d, hypoalbuminemia, edema).<br /></br>2. Ask: “Is there a pre-existing diagnosis that makes the pathology obvious?” (Diabetes &gt;5 y, minimal-change in a child, etc.)<br /></br>3. If not, renal biopsy is almost always the next diagnostic step because treatment differs by lesion.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• HIV infection → classically gives collapsing focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) known as HIV-associated nephropathy.<br /></br>• Hepatitis C infection → often causes membranoproliferative GN or cryoglobulinemic GN.<br /></br>• Steroids, antivirals, or immunosuppressants are chosen based on the specific glomerular lesion. Therefore, the most appropriate immediate step is to obtain tissue diagnosis with a percutaneous renal biopsy.</p></br><p>Take-home message: “Adult nephrotic syndrome of unclear etiology → renal biopsy before definitive therapy.”</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): nephrotic-range proteinuria, rising creatinine, hypertension, plan for image-guided percutaneous kidney biopsy, counseling on bleeding risk and post-procedure monitoring.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• HIV-associated nephropathy: direct infection of renal epithelial cells → collapsing FSGS pattern, heavy proteinuria, rapid progression if untreated; improved with antiretroviral therapy (ART) + ACE inhibitors.<br /></br>• Hepatitis C: immune complex deposition → MPGN ± cryoglobulins; may respond to direct-acting antivirals ± immunosuppression.<br /></br>• Biopsy distinguishes these and other possibilities (minimal change, amyloidosis, membranous, etc.).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm<br /></br>1. Confirm nephrotic-range proteinuria (spot protein/creatinine or 24-h collection).<br /></br>2. Renal ultrasound (to confirm two kidneys, size, r/o obstruction).<br /></br>3. Percutaneous renal biopsy (gold standard for histology).<br /></br>4. Tailor therapy:<br /></br>   • Collapsing FSGS (HIVAN) → continue ART, ACE/ARB, ± steroids if non-collapsing FSGS.<br /></br>   • MPGN/cryoglobulinemia → antiviral therapy for HCV, rituximab ± plasma exchange if severe.<br /></br>   • Membranous → evaluate for secondary causes, consider immunosuppression based on risk.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Biopsy Before Big-gun therapy in adult Nephrotic” (4 B’s: Biopsy, Before, Big-gun).<br /></br>Or: “Adults need a look, kids get a trial” – adults with nephrotic syndrome generally need a look (biopsy) while children often get empiric steroids for minimal-change disease.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Urine cytology – Used for painless hematuria/bladder cancer work-up; does not evaluate glomerular disease. Sediment is bland, not hematuric.<br /></br>B. Discontinuation of antiretroviral therapy – ART slows HIVAN progression; stopping it worsens renal and HIV outcomes.<br /></br>C. Prednisone therapy – Empiric steroids without histologic confirmation risks unnecessary immunosuppression; different lesions require different regimens or none at all.<br /></br>E. Hemodialysis – Indicated for refractory volume overload, severe hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, or symptomatic uremia. Creatinine 1.8 and mild symptoms do not yet meet criteria.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In an adult with new nephrotic-range proteinuria of uncertain cause, renal biopsy is the essential next step to establish the specific glomerular pathology and guide targeted therapy.</p>
51. A 14-year-old girl is brought to the office by her parents because of a 4-month history of “antisocial behavior.” During this period, she has spent less time with her girlfriends and has spent most of her time with her 16-year-old boyfriend. Three months ago, she quit the school volleyball team and will not tell her parents why. Her parents say she has become unwilling to discuss many aspects of her life with them, including her boyfriend and her sexual activity. She receives grades of B’s and C’s in school, but her parents worry that her grades will worsen. They report that she eats and sleeps well. During the examination, the patient is silent but polite. When her parents ask her to look at them, she refuses. When interviewed alone, the patient says she finds her parents “overbearing” and “intrusive.” She says she quit the volleyball team because she would rather devote that time to her boyfriend and her increasingly challenging studies. She says she is “madly in love” with her boyfriend; with the exception of class and study time, she wants to spend as much time with him as possible. She reports that they have been intimate but that she will not have sexual intercourse until the age of 17 years. She keeps in touch with her friends through texting and social media. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Adjustment disorder</br>b. Conduct disorder</br>c. Dependent personality disorder</br>d. Oppositional defiant disorder</br>e. Normal development</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Normal development  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point to “normal adolescent development”:<br /></br>• Age 14 – mid-adolescence is when individuation from parents and prioritization of romantic/peer relationships intensifies.<br /></br>• Spends more time with 16-year-old boyfriend and less with parents – typical shift of attachment figures from family to peers/partners.<br /></br>• Quit extracurricular activity to study and be with boyfriend – changing interests is common.<br /></br>• Grades still B’s–C’s; eating and sleeping well – no major functional decline.<br /></br>• Silent but polite; not overtly hostile or destructive.<br /></br>• Keeps contact with friends via social media – still socially connected.<br /></br>• Perceives parents as “overbearing” – classic teen–parent friction.  </p></br><p>Approach framework for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Ask: Is there clinically significant impairment (school failure, risky behavior, aggression, legal trouble, self-harm)?<br /></br>2. Evaluate duration &amp; context: transient changes &lt;6 mo in mid-adolescence often benign.<br /></br>3. Screen differential for externalizing disorders (conduct/ODD) or internalizing (depression). If criteria not met → reassure.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify developmental stage: 14 y = mid-adolescence (Erikson “identity vs role confusion”).<br /></br>2. Recognize expected behaviors: decreased parent communication, increased peer/romantic focus, experimentation with autonomy.<br /></br>3. Rule out pathology: No rule-violating behavior, physical aggression, deceit, property destruction, or pervasive defiance. Grades/ADLs intact.<br /></br>4. Conclusion: Behavior falls within normative spectrum; provide anticipatory guidance and encourage open, non-judgmental communication about relationships and sexual health.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Mid-adolescent distancing from parents, mood lability, and prioritizing peers/partners are normal unless accompanied by significant impairment or dangerous behaviors.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real clinic):<br /></br>• Assess mood, suicidality, substance use, sexual activity, contraception intent.<br /></br>• Note social/academic functioning and parental concerns.<br /></br>• Provide confidential counseling per state minor consent laws.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology / Developmental Review  </p></br><p>• Neural &amp; hormonal changes (↑ limbic activation, still-maturing prefrontal cortex) drive sensation-seeking and emotional intensity.<br /></br>• Erikson: resolving personal identity while renegotiating family boundaries.<br /></br>• Autonomy-seeking, privacy, and romantic attachment are evolutionarily conserved adolescent tasks.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>• No lab/imaging needed.<br /></br>• Offer routine adolescent care: HEADSS assessment (Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide).<br /></br>• Provide contraception counseling well before planned sexual debut.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“14 = SHIFT”:<br /></br>S – Separates from parents<br /></br>H – Hangs with partner/peers<br /></br>I – Identity exploration<br /></br>F – Function intact (school, sleep)<br /></br>T – Typical teen behavior  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Adjustment disorder – Requires an identifiable stressor (e.g., parental divorce) leading to emotional/behavioral symptoms causing marked distress or impairment within 3 mo. Here, no major stressor and no impairment.  </p></br><p>B. Conduct disorder – Pattern of violating rights/societal norms (theft, cruelty, fighting, truancy). Patient is polite, no antisocial acts; only spending time with boyfriend.  </p></br><p>C. Dependent personality disorder – Pervasive need to be taken care of, difficulty making decisions, clinging, fear of separation. Developmentally inappropriate dx before age 18, and patient shows autonomy, not dependence.  </p></br><p>D. Oppositional defiant disorder – Angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant toward authority, vindictiveness for ≥6 mo. This patient is not argumentative; she is simply private.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: A 14-year-old who withdraws from parents and invests heavily in a romantic relationship without functional impairment is exhibiting normal adolescent development.</p>
52. An asymptomatic 72-year-old woman comes to the physician for an annual health maintenance examination in the fall. She takes no medications. At the age of 65 years, bone densitometry showed normal findings. At her last visit 1 year ago, a Pap smear, mammography, and sigmoidoscopy showed no abnormalities; her serum cholesterol concentration was 190 mg/dL, and serum glucose concentration was 80 mg/dL. She has been married for 50 years and remains sexually active. She does not smoke and drinks one to two glasses of wine three times weekly. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 63 kg (140 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Her blood pressure is 140/85 mm Hg. Physical examination, including pelvic examination, shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Measurement of fasting serum glucose concentration</br>b. Serum lipid studies</br>c. ECG</br>d. Influenza virus vaccination</br>e. Sigmoidoscopy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Influenza virus vaccination  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Season &amp; timing: “in the fall” – prime time to offer the annual influenza vaccine.<br /></br>• Age: 72 years. Adults ≥65 y have the highest morbidity &amp; mortality from influenza → vaccination is high-priority preventive care.<br /></br>• Asymptomatic, taking no meds: visit is purely preventive, so think of universal immunizations first.<br /></br>• Prior screening already up to date: lipids, glucose, sigmoidoscopy, Pap, mammogram, DXA – all recently normal.<br /></br>• No special cardiac or metabolic risks revealed that would change screening intervals.  </p></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>1. Ask: “Is there a universal annual immunization due?”<br /></br>2. Confirm other screenings are not yet due by USPSTF/CDC interval.<br /></br>3. Select the single intervention that clearly improves mortality in this age group.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Annual inactivated influenza vaccination is recommended for every person ≥6 months of age. The benefit is greatest in adults ≥65 y, who account for &gt;70 % of influenza-related deaths. Giving the vaccine now (fall) maximizes protective titers during peak winter circulation.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: For any preventive-care question set in the autumn, always check if the patient—especially an older adult—has received this season’s flu shot. It trumps other tests that are not yet due or lack proven mortality benefit.  </p></br><p>What to document in the chart<br /></br>• “Discussed and administered inactivated influenza vaccine for 2024–2025 season; no contraindications.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Influenza viruses undergo frequent antigenic drift, so antibodies from past seasons wane.<br /></br>• Elderly patients have decreased cell-mediated immunity, leading to higher rates of pneumonia, hospitalization, and death.<br /></br>• Vaccination reduces hospitalization by ~40 % and death by ~50 % in seniors.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Preventive Algorithm<br /></br>First-line: Annual inactivated (IIV) or recombinant (RIV) vaccine; high-dose or adjuvanted formulations preferred ≥65 y.<br /></br>CI: Severe anaphylaxis to a vaccine component. Egg allergy is no longer a contraindication.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Fall = Flu” – any preventive care stem that mentions autumn should trigger the reflex: offer influenza vaccine unless already given.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Fasting serum glucose<br /></br>• USPSTF: screen q3 y in adults 40–70 y who are overweight/obese. She is 72 y but normal-weight (BMI 23) and had a normal value last year. Not indicated now.  </p></br><p>b. Serum lipid studies<br /></br>• Women 45–75 y: repeat q5 y if prior normal. Her cholesterol was checked 1 y ago (190 mg/dL). Too soon.  </p></br><p>c. ECG<br /></br>• Routine resting ECG in asymptomatic adults offers no mortality benefit and is not recommended by USPSTF.  </p></br><p>e. Sigmoidoscopy<br /></br>• Acceptable colorectal cancer screening options:<br /></br>  – Colonoscopy q10 y<br /></br>  – Sigmoidoscopy q5 y (with FIT q3 y) until age 75.<br /></br>She had sigmoidoscopy 1 y ago; next is due in 4 y.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: None of these options confer an immediate mortality-reducing benefit today, whereas influenza vaccination clearly does.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In any elderly patient presenting for fall-season health maintenance, the single most appropriate next step—unless already done that year—is administration of the influenza vaccine.</p>
53. A 3-year-old boy is admitted to the hospital because of a 5-day history of fever and cough. Since the age of 6 months, he has been treated for recurrent episodes of acute otitis media and has been hospitalized twice for treatment of pneumonia and once for treatment of viral meningitis. He was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. His immunizations are up-to-date. He has no known drug allergies. He currently receives no medications or herbal or vitamin supplements. His maternal uncle died of sepsis at the age of 6 years. The patient has two healthy sisters and a 3-month-old brother who has no history of infection. The patient is at the 10th percentile for height, weight, and head circumference. His temperature is 39°C (102.2°F). Examination shows no lymphadenopathy and absence of tonsils. Crackles are heard over the left lung base.</br>Laboratory studies show:</br>Total B lymphocytes        1 percent</br>Total T lymphocytes        91 percent</br>Serum</br>IgA                        8 mg/dL</br>IgG                        60 mg/dL</br>IgM                        10 mg/dL</br>A PPD skin test and HIV antibody test are negative. X-rays of the chest are shown. In addition to intravenous antibiotic therapy for treatment of this patient's current symptoms, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Subcutaneous granulocyte colony-stimulating factor therapy</br>b. Subcutaneous interferon gamma-1b therapy</br>c. Complement replacement therapy</br>d. Intravenous immune globulin therapy</br>e. Bone marrow transplant</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Intravenous immune globulin (IVIG) therapy  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 3-year-old BOY; maternal uncle died of sepsis  → X-linked pattern<br /></br>   • Recurrent, severe bacterial infections beginning at 6 mo (after loss of maternal IgG)<br /></br>   • Absent tonsils &amp; no palpable lymph nodes → lack of mature B-cell tissue<br /></br>   • Flow cytometry: B cells 1 % (markedly low); T cells 91 % (normal)<br /></br>   • Quantitative Igs: IgG 60 mg/dL (↓), IgA 8 mg/dL (↓), IgM 10 mg/dL (↓)  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>   “Boy + infections after 6 mo + no tonsils + pan-hypogammaglobulinemia + near-absent CD19/20 B cells” = Bruton (X-linked) agammaglobulinemia.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • Normal PPD and HIV test → rules out HIV/SCID–like T-cell defect.<br /></br>   • No albinism, periodontitis, or abscesses (rules out CGD, Chediak–Higashi, etc.).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive takeaway: When you see sinopulmonary infections in a boy after 6 mo, ALWAYS check for B-cell number &amp; immunoglobulins; absence of tonsils/lymph nodes is the buzzword for Bruton.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Stepwise thought process<br /></br>1. Identify immunologic category (B-cell vs T-cell vs phagocyte vs complement).<br /></br>2. Labs show isolated B-cell absence + low all Igs = pure B-cell maturation defect.<br /></br>3. Definitive treatment = supply the missing antibodies → IVIG every 3–4 weeks.<br /></br>4. Bone-marrow transplant is unnecessary because T-cells are intact; condition is manageable with IVIG + prompt antibiotics.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Bruton agammaglobulinemia is treated with chronic IVIG to prevent encapsulated bacterial infections and reduce mortality.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): diagnosis, genetics counseling discussion, plan for monthly IVIG, vaccination modifications (no live vaccines), infection-prevention plan.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Gene: BTK (Bruton tyrosine kinase) on X-chromosome<br /></br>• Defect: Blocks B-cell maturation at pre-B stage → absent/↓ mature B cells &amp; immunoglobulins of all classes.<br /></br>• Clinical: Recurrent otitis, pneumonia, sinusitis, meningitis with encapsulated bacteria (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae) beginning at 3–6 mo; absent tonsils/lymph nodes; normal growth until infections interfere.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Workflow  </p></br><ol></br><li>Acute episode: broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for pneumonia.  </li></br><li>Long-term:<br /></br>   • IVIG 400–600 mg/kg q3–4 weeks (or subcutaneous preparations)<br /></br>   • Avoid live vaccines (e.g., MMR, varicella, intranasal influenza, rotavirus).<br /></br>   • Educate family about early treatment of infections; prophylactic antibiotics sometimes used.  </li></br><li>Follow-up labs: trough IgG levels prior to each IVIG infusion, pulmonary function tests / CT to monitor bronchiectasis.  </li></br></ol></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Bruton’s Boys get Bugs after Baby IgG is Gone; give them ‘B-borrowed’ IgG (IVIG).”<br /></br>• Boys (X-linked)<br /></br>• Bugs (encapsulated bacteria)<br /></br>• Baby IgG gone at 6 mo<br /></br>• Borrowed IgG = IVIG  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) – Used for severe/chronic neutropenia; this child’s neutrophil function/count isn’t mentioned and recurrent viral/pyogenic infections with low immunoglobulins point away from neutropenia.  </p></br><p>B. Interferon-γ (IFN-γ-1b) – Adjunct therapy for chronic granulomatous disease (CGD), a phagocytic oxidative burst defect presenting with catalase-positive abscesses and granulomas; B-cell numbers and immunoglobulins are normal in CGD.  </p></br><p>C. Complement replacement – Rarely used; deficiencies (C1q, C2, C3, MAC) present with meningococcal, lupus-like, or angioedema phenotypes, not persistent pan-hypogammaglobulinemia or absent tonsils.  </p></br><p>E. Bone marrow transplant – Indicated for combined immunodeficiencies (SCID, Wiskott–Aldrich, severe phagocytic disorders). In Bruton, T-cell compartment is intact; IVIG suffices, making transplant unnecessary and overly aggressive.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>Bruton (X-linked) agammaglobulinemia = near-absent B cells + very low IgG/A/M + male onset at 6 mo + absent tonsils → treat with lifelong IVIG to provide the missing antibodies and prevent life-threatening infections.</p>
54. A 25-year-old woman comes to the emergency department 45 minutes after the onset of shortness of breath, marked malaise, weakness, and fatigue. She felt well until 24 hours ago when she developed muscle soreness after an unusually strenuous physical workout; she took ibuprofen for pain 1 hour before the dyspnea began. She has a history of frequent migraines that have been prevented by taking metoprolol during the past month. Her blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Examination shows facial flushing and mild periorbital edema. Multiple wheezes are heard throughout the lung fields. During the examination, the patient's dyspnea becomes more severe. Her respirations are 30/min, and blood pressure is now 90/50 mm Hg. Her inspiratory ratio is approximately 1:1. There is circumoral cyanosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Diphenhydramine</br>b. Epinephrine</br>c. Hydroxyzine</br>d. Methylprednisolone</br>e. Ranitidine</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>B. Epinephrine</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Time course: Seconds–minutes (began 45 min ago and is rapidly worsening) → favors an acute hypersensitivity reaction.<br /></br>• Multisystem involvement:<br /></br>  – Respiratory: severe dyspnea, diffuse wheezing, I:E ≈ 1:1 (impending airway compromise)<br /></br>  – Skin/Mucosa: facial flushing, periorbital edema, circumoral cyanosis<br /></br>  – Cardiovascular: falling BP 100/70 → 90/50 mm Hg<br /></br>Together = classic triad of anaphylaxis (airway/respiratory, skin/mucosa, hemodynamic instability).</p></br><p>• Precipitant: NSAID (ibuprofen) ingestion 1 h earlier; NSAIDs can trigger IgE-independent mast-cell degranulation identical to anaphylaxis.</p></br><p>Key cognitive steps:<br /></br>1. “ABC” scan → airway &amp; breathing are threatened (wheezing, I:E 1:1, cyanosis).<br /></br>2. Recognize ≥ 2 organ systems involved after exposure to a likely allergen → diagnostic of anaphylaxis (no lab needed).<br /></br>3. First, fastest, most effective drug = intramuscular epinephrine.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>• Algorithm (2023 AAAAI/ACLS):<br /></br>  1. Call for help; place patient supine, high-flow O₂.<br /></br>  2. IM epinephrine 0.3–0.5 mg of 1:1000 in the mid-anterolateral thigh. Repeat every 5–15 min PRN.<br /></br>  3. Large-bore IV, fluid bolus, adjunctive H₁/H₂ blockers, corticosteroids, inhaled β₂-agonist only after epi.<br /></br>• Metoprolol: β-blockers can blunt response, but you still give epinephrine first; if refractory, give glucagon (β-independent inotropy).</p></br><p>Take-home message: In any suspected anaphylaxis with airway or blood-pressure involvement, epinephrine IM is the life-saving first step—never delay for antihistamines or steroids.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real practice)<br /></br>• Time, dose, and route of epi; vitals before/after; airway status; response; potential need for repeat dose or intubation.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• NSAID → direct COX-1 inhibition → ↑ leukotriene synthesis &amp; non-IgE mast-cell degranulation → histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes → bronchospasm, vasodilation (hypotension), vascular permeability (angioedema).<br /></br>• Epinephrine: α₁ vasoconstriction (↑BP, ↓mucosal edema), β₁ inotropy/chronotropy, β₂ bronchodilation &amp; mast-cell stabilization.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</br>• Immediate: IM epinephrine.<br /></br>• Adjuncts after stabilization:<br /></br>  – H₁ blocker (diphenhydramine)<br /></br>  – H₂ blocker (ranitidine or famotidine)<br /></br>  – Corticosteroid (methylprednisolone) to prevent biphasic reaction<br /></br>  – Albuterol neb for persistent wheeze<br /></br>• Refractory hypotension on β-blocker: IV glucagon 1–5 mg bolus then drip.<br /></br>• Observe ≥ 4–6 h (longer if severe) for biphasic reaction.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“EPI First, the rest can wait”<br /></br>• EPI:<br /></br>  E – Edema of airway fixed<br /></br>  P – Pressure up<br /></br>  I – Improve wheeze<br /></br>Visual: picture a fire alarm (anaphylaxis) → hit the “E-button” (epinephrine) before calling maintenance (antihistamines/steroids).</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Diphenhydramine (H₁ blocker) – helpful for urticaria/pruritus but onset 30–60 min, NO reversal of bronchospasm or hypotension. Too slow for life-threatening anaphylaxis.</p></br><p>C. Hydroxyzine – another H₁ antihistamine; oral/IM, slower onset than diphenhydramine; same limitations.</p></br><p>D. Methylprednisolone – Steroids reduce risk of late-phase/biphasic reactions; onset ≥ 4–6 h, useless for immediate airway or BP compromise.</p></br><p>E. Ranitidine – H₂ blocker; adjunct to improve cutaneous symptoms when combined with H₁ blocker; no benefit acutely for airway/BP.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: choosing antihistamine or steroid first because they “treat allergy.” On exams and in life, anaphylaxis = epinephrine first, every time.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Core Takeaway</br>Anaphylaxis = multisystem allergic reaction with airway and/or hemodynamic compromise. IM epinephrine is the only intervention that rapidly reverses both respiratory and cardiovascular collapse—administer immediately; everything else is adjunctive.</p>
55. A previously healthy 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-day history of fever and an area of swelling and redness on her right forearm. Six hours ago, she noticed red streaks extending from her forearm to her elbow. She has not traveled, and there is no history of trauma. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F). Epitrochlear lymph nodes are enlarged. Examination of the distal radial aspect of the right upper extremity shows an 8 × 13-cm warm, erythematous area of edema that is tender to palpation; there is a warm, tender red streak extending from this area to the elbow. There is no wound, ulcer, fluctuance, or palpable cord. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Deep venous thrombosis</br>b. Lymphangitis</br>c. Lymphedema</br>d. Superficial thrombophlebitis</br>e. Thoracic outlet obstruction</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>b. Lymphangitis  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Red, warm, tender “streak” running proximally from an erythematous skin focus = classic buzz-phrase for lymphatic channel infection/ inflammation (“ascending streaking”).<br /></br>• Fever (100 °F) &amp; tender regional lymphadenopathy (epitrochlear nodes) → systemic response to infection.<br /></br>• No trauma, bite, or wound is required; a microscopic break in the skin is often enough for group A strep or Staph aureus to seed the lymphatics.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no fluctuance (so not an abscess), no palpable cord (argues against superficial thrombophlebitis), no diffuse arm swelling (argues against DVT or lymphedema), no neurologic or vascular compromise (rules out thoracic outlet).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Identify the anatomic structure involved (lymphatic channel because it follows drainage path and ends in regional node).<br /></br>2. Look for systemic signs of infection (fever, LAD).<br /></br>3. Remember the pattern phrase “red streaking → think lymphangitis.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-by-step link to answer:<br /></br>1. Localized cellulitis-appearing lesion on distal forearm.<br /></br>2. Linear, tender erythema extending proximally along lymphatic drainage.<br /></br>3. Enlarged, tender draining lymph node.<br /></br>4. Mild fever → bacteremia risk.<br /></br>These four findings together = acute bacterial lymphangitis.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Red linear streaks + regional LAD + cellulitis signs = lymphangitis caused most commonly by Streptococcus pyogenes; treat promptly with systemic antibiotics (e.g., IV cefazolin or nafcillin; clindamycin if MRSA concern). Document vitals, extent of streaking, node size, and start IV antibiotics.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Inoculation of lymphatic channels by β-hemolytic streptococci (&gt;70%) or Staph aureus.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Bacterial proliferation in superficial lymphatics → inflammation → erythematous linear track toward regional node.<br /></br>• Clinical: Fever, chills, malaise possible; streaking erythema, tender LAD. Untreated can progress to sepsis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Empiric IV antibiotics covering streptococci ± MSSA:<br /></br>   – 1st-gen cephalosporin (cefazolin) or antistaphylococcal penicillin (nafcillin/oxacillin).<br /></br>   – Add vancomycin or clindamycin if MRSA prevalence high.<br /></br>2. Elevate limb, mark leading edge to monitor spread.<br /></br>3. Blood cultures if febrile/toxic. Imaging only if abscess suspected (ultrasound).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “L-for-Line” → Lymphangitis causes a LINE of redness.<br /></br>• Picture a red lightning bolt racing up the arm = infected lymph flow.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Deep venous thrombosis<br /></br>   – Presents with diffuse swelling, pain, often discoloration, usually calf or entire arm; risk factors (central line, cancer, OCP, trauma). No linear streaking or LAD.  </p></br><p>c. Lymphedema<br /></br>   – Chronic, painless, non-erythematous swelling; peau d’orange skin; no fever, redness, or tenderness.  </p></br><p>d. Superficial thrombophlebitis<br /></br>   – Local pain, erythema, palpable “cord” along a superficial vein; usually around IV sites or varicose veins; no systemic fever or nodal enlargement, and the erythema follows the vein (cord), not diffuse cell-mediated streaking.  </p></br><p>e. Thoracic outlet obstruction<br /></br>   – Neurovascular compression causing paresthesias, weakness, or venous congestion in entire limb; chronic, not infectious; no cellulitis, no red streaks, no fever.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway: Linear, tender red streaks leading to an enlarged draining lymph node in a febrile patient = acute bacterial lymphangitis—treat promptly with systemic antistreptococcal antibiotics.</p>
56. A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 20 minutes after being involved in a motor vehicle collision. He was the unrestrained driver. On arrival, he is agitated and incoherent. His pulse is 120/min, respirations are 10/min and labored, and palpable systolic blood pressure is 60 mm Hg. After endotracheal intubation and resuscitation with 2 L of lactated Ringer solution, his pulse and blood pressure remain unchanged; the ventilatory rate is 8/min. Examination shows a 4 x 6-cm ecchymosis over the left sixth and seventh intercostal spaces at the midaxillary line. He groans on palpation of the left chest. Breath sounds are equal bilaterally. There is abdominal distention. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. CT scan of the abdomen</br>b. CT scan of the chest</br>c. Aortography</br>d. Exploratory laparotomy</br>e. Exploratory left thoracotomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Exploratory laparotomy  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Hemodynamic status<br /></br>   • Palpable systolic BP ≈ 60 mm Hg + pulse 120/min ⇒ class IV hemorrhagic shock.<br /></br>   • Still hypotensive after 2 L crystalloid ⇒ ongoing bleeding that needs surgical control.</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Source of bleeding—what clues point where?<br /></br>   • Abdomen: “Abdominal distention” = blood or visceral injury inside the peritoneal cavity.<br /></br>   • Chest: Equal breath sounds; no tracheal deviation or massive dullness ⇢ tension pneumo or large hemothorax unlikely.<br /></br>   • Ecchymosis over left 6th–7th intercostal spaces (“seat-belt sign”) often overlies spleen → classic marker of occult abdominal injury in blunt trauma.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Unstable patient + suspected intra-abdominal bleed = OR now.<br /></br>   • FAST or DPL can be used when the abdominal exam is equivocal, but if the patient is crashing and the abdomen is obviously the culprit (distention, seat-belt sign), guidelines allow skipping diagnostics and proceeding directly to laparotomy.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive framework for future questions:<br /></br>Ask two rapid questions:<br /></br>   1. Is the patient hemodynamically stable?<br /></br>   2. If unstable, can I identify an immediately treatable source without imaging?<br /></br>If “unstable + probable abdominal source” → straight to exploratory laparotomy.  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Identify shock (BP 60, HR 120).<br /></br>2. Give fluids → no response → hemorrhagic shock.<br /></br>3. Look for bleeding site: chest clear, pelvis not mentioned, abdomen distended.<br /></br>4. In hypotensive trauma, CT requires stability and time; ultrasound or DPL are options but are bypassed when physical exam is strongly positive.<br /></br>5. Therefore, definitive step = exploratory laparotomy for hemorrhage control.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway:<br /></br>Unstable blunt trauma patient with obvious abdominal findings goes directly to the OR—diagnosis and treatment are achieved simultaneously with exploratory laparotomy.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl:<br /></br>• “Patient remains hypotensive after 2 L LR; abdomen distended and tender. Decision made for emergent exploratory laparotomy for presumed intra-abdominal hemorrhage. Risks, benefits, alternatives discussed briefly given emergent situation.”  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Blunt abdominal trauma (BAT) → rapid deceleration/ compression forces → solid-organ laceration (spleen &gt; liver). Free intraperitoneal blood causes distention and third-spacing, leading to hypovolemic shock. Crystalloid temporarily restores preload but definitive control of hemorrhage (packing, splenectomy, hepatorrhaphy, etc.) is required.  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm (High-Yield)<br /></br>Hemodynamically unstable BAT:<br /></br>• Positive FAST or obvious abdominal signs → emergent laparotomy.<br /></br>• Equivocal abdomen → FAST then DPL if needed.<br /></br>Hemodynamically stable BAT:<br /></br>• CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast to grade injury and decide on non-operative vs operative management.  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“FAST if they last; Cut if they crash.”<br /></br>   • If a trauma patient can “last” (stable) → FAST/CT.<br /></br>   • If they “crash” (unstable) with belly signs → straight to the “cut” (laparotomy).  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. CT scan of the abdomen<br /></br>   • Requires hemodynamic stability and 10–15 min in scanner. Unstable patients often arrest en route.  </p></br><p>B. CT scan of the chest<br /></br>   • Chosen if concern for aortic injury or pulmonary/mediastinal pathology in a stable patient. Equal breath sounds + abdominal distention make chest CT low-yield.  </p></br><p>C. Aortography<br /></br>   • Used when suspecting traumatic aortic transection (widened mediastinum, chest pain). None of those findings are present, and patient is too unstable.  </p></br><p>E. Exploratory left thoracotomy<br /></br>   • Indicated for massive hemothorax (&gt;1500 mL initial chest-tube output) or penetrating cardiac box injury. Breath sounds are equal, no evidence of massive intrathoracic bleed.  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a hypotensive blunt-trauma patient with abdominal distention, imaging can wait—take the patient directly to exploratory laparotomy to identify and control intra-abdominal hemorrhage.</p>
57. A 52-year-old man, who remains in the hospital 1 week after undergoing cadaveric renal transplantation, has been crying and feeling hopeless during the past 3 days. He says he worries that he will never get better. Two years ago, he was involved in a motor vehicle collision and required splenectomy. After the procedure, rapid-onset sepsis and end-stage renal disease developed. He had been receiving dialysis since then. The transplant has not been functioning well, and he has required emergency dialysis for the past 6 days. His current medications include prednisone, mycophenolate mofetil, and thyroglobulin. His temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 74/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 126/84 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a well-healing surgical incision over the abdomen. On mental status examination, he is sad and frequently crying, although he says he looks forward to visits from his family. He is fully oriented. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Electroconvulsive therapy</br>b. Escitalopram therapy</br>c. Lorazepam therapy</br>d. Psychodynamic psychotherapy</br>e. Supportive psychotherapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Supportive psychotherapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• “Crying and feeling hopeless during the past 3 days” – mood symptoms are acute and short-lived.<br /></br>• Still “looks forward to visits from his family” – preserved interest/pleasure → no anhedonia.<br /></br>• No neurovegetative symptoms (sleep, appetite, energy, concentration, suicidal ideation) listed.<br /></br>• Enormous current stressor (poorly functioning new kidney transplant while still hospitalized).<br /></br>• Medications include high-dose corticosteroid (can affect mood, but again only 3 days).<br /></br>• No past psychiatric history mentioned.  </p></br><p>Approach framework:<br /></br>1. Apply DSM-5 criteria for Major Depressive Disorder (≥5 SIGECAPS symptoms, ≥2 weeks).<br /></br>2. If criteria NOT met but the patient is distressed in response to an identifiable stressor &lt;3 months → think Adjustment Disorder with depressed mood / normal situational distress.<br /></br>3. Initial management of adjustment-related distress in medically ill inpatients = brief, reality-oriented supportive psychotherapy + monitor.  </p></br><p>This question tests recognizing that every sad patient in the hospital does NOT automatically warrant antidepressants or ECT—timing and diagnostic criteria matter.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-by-step:<br /></br>1. Duration only 3 days → too short for MDD or persistent depressive disorder.<br /></br>2. Stressor clearly identified (renal graft malfunction) → situational/adjustment reaction.<br /></br>3. Symptoms are mild-to-moderate (no suicidality, psychosis, functional collapse).<br /></br>4.
