Best next step: non-pharmacologic, bedside supportive psychotherapy that focuses on:<br /></br>   – Normalizing emotions (“Anyone in your situation would feel discouraged”)<br /></br>   – Mobilizing coping skills and social supports (family visits, chaplain service, transplant team reassurance)<br /></br>   – Short-term problem solving (how dialysis will proceed, expectations for the graft).  </p></br><p>Take-home: In acute situational sadness without full depressive syndrome, offer emotional support and observation first.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): mood, affect, stressor identified, absence of suicidal ideation, plan for follow-up and supportive sessions.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Adjustment Disorder<br /></br>• Trigger: identifiable stressor (medical illness, relationship, financial).<br /></br>• Onset: within 3 months of stressor, lasts ≤6 months after it ends.<br /></br>• Symptoms: emotional (depressed mood, anxiety) and/or behavioral out of proportion to stressor but not meeting criteria for another disorder.<br /></br>• Mechanism: maladaptive stress response; no specific neurochemical deficit proven.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Management ladder:<br /></br>1. Supportive psychotherapy / brief CBT-style counseling.<br /></br>2. If symptoms persist &gt;2 weeks despite support, or escalate to full MDD criteria, add SSRI (e.g., escitalopram).<br /></br>3. Pharmacotherapy earlier only if severe functional impairment or suicidality.<br /></br>4. ECT reserved for severe, refractory, psychotic, or life-threatening depression.  </p></br><p>No labs or imaging needed specifically for adjustment disorder; continue routine transplant labs.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“3-3-6 Rule” for Adjustment Disorder:<br /></br>• Starts within 3 months of stressor<br /></br>• Lasts no more than 6 months after stressor ends<br /></br>Support First, SSRI Second. (“S-before-S” → Supportive before SSRI)  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Electroconvulsive therapy – Indicated for severe depression with suicidality, psychotic features, catatonia, or when rapid response is required. None apply; duration far too short.  </p></br><p>B. Escitalopram therapy – SSRIs are first-line for MDD or persistent adjustment symptoms &gt;2 weeks affecting function; premature here and may add unnecessary drug interactions (with transplant meds).  </p></br><p>C. Lorazepam therapy – Benzodiazepines treat acute anxiety, agitation, or insomnia; they do not address depressive feelings and carry delirium/respiratory depression risks in inpatients.  </p></br><p>D. Psychodynamic psychotherapy – Long-term exploration of unconscious conflicts; not useful for acute situational distress in a hospitalized patient.  </p></br><p>E. Supportive psychotherapy – Correct. Matches acuity, severity, and setting; focuses on reassurance, education, coping skills, and mobilizing resources.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Acute sadness &lt;2 weeks in response to a major medical stressor = adjustment-related distress. Provide supportive psychotherapy and monitor; reserve medications or ECT for persistent or severe depressive syndromes.</p>
58. A 16-year-old girl is brought to the office by her parents because of a 3-month history of depressed mood, decreased energy, and decreased confidence. Her parents say they are perplexed by their daughter’s recent lack of motivation. The patient’s grades have declined from A’s and B’s to mostly C’s during this time. She says she no longer cares about being admitted to a competitive college and feels hopeless about the future. She aspires to be a writer and has been upset that she was not chosen to be the editor of her school newspaper when the position was filled 4 months ago. She and her parents immigrated to the USA from Korea 9 years ago. The patient has no history of serious medical or psychiatric illness and takes no medications. Her maternal aunt and grandmother have anxiety disorders. The patient does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she has a depressed mood and restricted affect. She reports passive suicidal ideation but no active suicide plans or attempts. She reports no hallucinations. There is no evidence of delusions. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Acculturation problem</br>b. Adjustment disorder</br>c. Major depressive disorder</br>d. Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia)</br>e. Phase of life problem</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>c. Major depressive disorder (MDD)</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues: 3-month duration, depressed mood, markedly ↓ energy/motivation, hopelessness about future, academic decline, passive suicidal ideation, no psychosis.</br>• SIGECAPS screen:</br>  – Sleep/Appetite unknown</br>  – Interest ↓ (“no longer cares”)</br>  – Guilt/worthlessness ↓ confidence, hopeless</br>  – Energy ↓</br>  – Concentration ↓ (falling grades)</br>  – Suicidal ideation (passive)</br>  – Psychomotor / Sleep not specified but ≥5 total criteria if either is present; ≥4 clearly present in vignette + depressed mood ⇒ diagnostic threshold likely met.</br>• Stressor (not chosen as newspaper editor) occurred 4 months ago, but symptoms have persisted &gt;2 weeks and fulfill full MDD criteria, so no longer classified as an adjustment disorder.</br>Approach tip: When a stressor is present, first ask “Do the symptoms fulfill criteria for a full mood disorder?” If yes → diagnose the mood disorder (MDD) rather than adjustment disorder.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Establish temporal criterion (≥2 weeks) and symptom count (≥5 of 9 SIGECAPS with depressed mood/anhedonia).</br>2. Exclude other mood disorders:</br>   • Persistent depressive disorder needs ≥2 years.</br>   • Bipolar ruled out (no manic/hypomanic history).</br>3. Evaluate suicide risk (passive ideation only, no plan).</br>Take-home: The presence of a stressor does not preclude MDD if full criteria are met.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Multifactorial: genetic vulnerability (family anxiety/mood disorders), psychosocial stressor, monoamine dysregulation (↓ serotonin, norepinephrine, dopamine), HPA-axis hyperactivity.</br>• Typical onset in adolescence; females affected 2:1.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</br>1. First-line: SSRI (e.g., fluoxetine, escitalopram) + evidence-based psychotherapy (CBT or interpersonal therapy).</br>2. Safety: screen for active suicidal intent/plan; create safety plan; involve parents.</br>3. Baseline lab work (TSH, CBC, CMP) only if clinically indicated.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>SIGECAPS mnemonic:</br>Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicide.</br>“2 blue weeks + 5 SIGECAPS = MDD.”</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Acculturation problem: Usually manifests soon after immigration with language/cultural conflict; she immigrated 9 years ago and symptoms started only 3 months ago.</br>b. Adjustment disorder: Symptom onset within 3 months of identifiable stressor, but criteria for another disorder (MDD) must NOT be met. Here they are met.</br>d. Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia): Requires ≥2 years of subthreshold depression; her symptoms are only 3 months.</br>e. Phase-of-life problem: Normal situational distress without functional impairment; she has clear functional decline (grades, motivation) and meets MDD criteria.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Core Takeaway</br>Even when a clear precipitating event exists, diagnose major depressive disorder if the patient meets full SIGECAPS criteria for ≥2 weeks and exhibits functional impairment.</p>
59. A 37-year-old woman is hospitalized because of a 1-year history of recurrent abdominal pain and diarrhea; exploratory laparoscopy on two occasions showed no abnormalities. A stool chart shows more than 10 painful bowel movements daily for 2 weeks; the pain was relieved with defecation. Physical examination and rectosigmoidoscopy with biopsy show no abnormalities. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate is within the reference range. Test of the stool is negative for occult blood, ova, and parasites and positive for phenolphthalein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Anxiety disorder due to a general medical condition</br>b. Conversion disorder</br>c. Factitious disorder</br>d. Hypochondriasis</br>e. Somatization disorder</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Factitious disorder  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Recurrent abdominal pain/diarrhea with completely normal objective evaluations (two negative laparoscopies, normal rectosigmoidoscopy, normal ESR, negative O&amp;P).<br /></br>  – Stool positive for phenolphthalein → an ex-laxative ingredient; proves the patient is ingesting a substance that causes symptoms.<br /></br>  – No mention of obvious external incentives (money, time off work, avoiding legal trouble).<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: high stool frequency, pain relieved by defecation, positive phenolphthalein.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no weight-loss incentive, no benefit such as disability payments, no abnormal inflammatory markers, no structural bowel disease.<br /></br>Pattern recognition: Intentional production of physical symptoms in order to adopt the patient role → classic factitious disorder.  </p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Determine whether symptoms are produced intentionally. (Yes → laxative found.)<br /></br>2. If intentional, ask: “Is there obvious secondary/external gain?”<br /></br>   – If yes → malingering.<br /></br>   – If no, the goal is psychological (to be a patient) → factitious disorder.<br /></br>3. If not intentional, consider somatoform spectrum (somatization, hypochondriasis, conversion, etc.).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>The core concept: Laxative abuse documented by phenolphthalein in stool = deliberate symptom fabrication without external reward. That fulfills DSM-5 criteria for factitious disorder imposed on self (formerly “Munchausen syndrome”).  </p></br><p>Documentation in a real chart would include: “Objective stool test positive for phenolphthalein indicating surreptitious laxative ingestion; pattern consistent with factitious disorder.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Factitious disorder<br /></br>• Etiology: Underlying psychological need to assume the sick role, often linked to childhood illness, healthcare employment, or feelings of inadequacy.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Conscious fabrication or induction of illness (self-harm, medication abuse).<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations: Dramatic presentation, frequent hospitalizations, eagerness for procedures, discrepancies between objective findings and reported symptoms.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostic Work-up<br /></br>1. Confirm fabrication objectively (e.g., toxicology, chart review for previous incongruities).<br /></br>2. Establish one primary physician to minimize unnecessary procedures.<br /></br>3. Maintain a non-confrontational, empathic stance; set clear boundaries.<br /></br>4. Psychiatric consultation for long-term psychotherapy.<br /></br>(No specific pharmacotherapy unless comorbid mood or anxiety disorder present.)  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Factitious = Fake for Feelings.”<br /></br>• Fake symptoms, but the payoff is internal emotional need (feelings of being cared for).<br /></br>• Malingering = “Money” (external gain).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Anxiety disorder due to a general medical condition<br /></br>   – Would require a verifiable medical illness producing the anxiety; not the case here.  </p></br><p>b. Conversion disorder<br /></br>   – Presents with neurologic or sensory deficits incompatible with known physiology (paralysis, blindness). GI symptoms alone do not fit.  </p></br><p>d. Hypochondriasis (Illness Anxiety Disorder)<br /></br>   – Excessive worry about having an illness; symptoms aren’t deliberately produced and labs are usually sought for reassurance. No evidence of feigned diarrhea.  </p></br><p>e. Somatization disorder (Somatic Symptom Disorder)<br /></br>   – Multiple physical complaints across ≥4 organ systems beginning before age 30, unconscious production. Here only GI tract is involved and intentional induction is proven.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: confusing factitious disorder with malingering. Always look for external incentives; absence of such points to factitious disorder.</p>
60. A 25-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of episodes of dizziness. She says that during the episodes, she feels as though the room is spinning. She also has had ringing in her ears, hearing loss, and a sensation of fullness in her right ear during this time. The episodes occur suddenly, last 30 minutes, and resolve spontaneously. Four months ago, she had a 2-week period of similar episodes that did not involve hearing loss; those episodes resolved spontaneously. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. On examination, Weber test lateralizes to the left. Rinne test shows that air conduction is greater than bone conduction bilaterally. Hallpike maneuver is negative. Audiometry shows low-frequency, sensorineural hearing loss on the right. Vestibular testing shows no abnormalities. Rapid plasma reagin is negative. An MRI of the brain shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Amoxicillin therapy</br>b. Canalith repositioning maneuver</br>c. High-sodium diet</br>d. Meclizine therapy</br>e. Methylprednisolone therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (per key): d. Meclizine therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases<br /></br>  – “Feels as though the room is spinning” → true vertigo (vestibular)<br /></br>  – Episodes “last 30 minutes and resolve spontaneously” → minutes-to-hours duration (not seconds, not days)<br /></br>  – “Ringing in her ears, hearing loss, and a sensation of fullness in her right ear” → classic aural symptoms<br /></br>  – Weber lateralizes to the opposite (left) ear &amp; Rinne AC&gt;BC → right-sided sensorineural loss<br /></br>  – Audiometry: low-frequency sensorineural loss on the symptomatic side → typical of Ménière disease<br /></br>  – Negative Hallpike maneuver → argues against benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV)<br /></br>• Pattern to recognize: Recurrent, spontaneous vertigo lasting ≥20 min + unilateral sensorineural hearing loss + tinnitus/aural fullness = Ménière disease (endolymphatic hydrops).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>When faced with vertigo questions, first sort by episode duration (seconds = BPPV, minutes–hours = Ménière, days = vestibular neuritis/labyrinthitis) and look for hearing loss (present only in Ménière or labyrinthitis).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify syndrome → Ménière disease.<br /></br>2. Decide on phase of care → patient is currently symptomatic with spinning attacks.<br /></br>3. Acute attack management → vestibular suppressants/antiemetics (first-line = oral antihistamine such as meclizine or dimenhydrinate; benzodiazepines second-line).<br /></br>4. Long-term prevention (not asked here) → low-sodium diet (&lt;2 g/day), limit caffeine/ETOH/nicotine, ± thiazide diuretic; refractory cases → intratympanic gentamicin or surgery.  </p></br><p>Take-home: In Ménière’s, give an antihistamine (meclizine) for acute relief; use salt restriction/diuretics for prophylaxis.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record episode characteristics, otologic exam, vestibular tests, patient counsel on diet/trigger avoidance, medication given, and follow-up for hearing evaluation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Underlying problem: Endolymphatic hydrops → excess volume/pressure of endolymph in the membranous labyrinth.<br /></br>• Produces fluctuating low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, aural fullness, and episodic vertigo as endolymphatic pressure distorts semicircular canals and cochlea.<br /></br>• Usually unilateral but may become bilateral over time.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>Acute attack<br /></br>• Meclizine (H1 antihistamine with central anticholinergic effect) 25–50 mg PO q4–6 h prn.<br /></br>• Adjuncts: promethazine or ondansetron for nausea; diazepam/lorazepam if severe.  </p></br><p>Chronic prophylaxis<br /></br>• Low-sodium diet (&lt;2 g Na/day) + avoid caffeine/alcohol/nicotine.<br /></br>• Thiazide diuretic (eg, hydrochlorothiazide-triamterene).<br /></br>• Refractory: intratympanic gentamicin → ablate vestibular hair cells; endolymphatic sac decompression or labyrinthectomy if hearing already poor.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“MEMory aid for Ménière”:<br /></br>M – Minutes-long attacks<br /></br>E – Endolymph excess<br /></br>M – Meclizine for acute spells<br /></br>I – Impaired hearing (low-freq)<br /></br>E – Ear fullness/tinnitus<br /></br>R – Restrict salt for prevention<br /></br>E – Episodes Recur  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Amoxicillin therapy<br /></br> • Would be used for acute otitis media or bacterial labyrinthitis. Those present with fever, otalgia, bulging TM; vertigo is usually constant, not episodic. No infectious signs here.  </p></br><p>b. Canalith repositioning maneuver (Epley)<br /></br> • First-line for BPPV (brief, seconds-long vertigo triggered by head movement, positive Dix-Hallpike, no hearing loss). Hallpike is negative and hearing loss present → rules out BPPV.  </p></br><p>c. High-sodium diet<br /></br> • Opposite of proven therapy. High salt increases endolymphatic pressure and worsens Ménière symptoms. Low-sodium diet is preventive.  </p></br><p>e. Methylprednisolone therapy<br /></br> • Can shorten course of vestibular neuritis or sudden idiopathic sensorineural hearing loss. Vestibular neuritis causes continuous vertigo lasting days without hearing loss; this patient has episodic vertigo with hearing loss.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core takeaway: Recurrent vertigo lasting ≥20 min plus unilateral tinnitus/hearing loss = Ménière disease; treat an acute spell with vestibular suppressants such as meclizine, and prevent future episodes with salt restriction and diuretics.</p>
61. A 6-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of hair loss and a lump on his scalp. His mother says that she first noticed the lump 5 days ago and it appears to be increasing in size. There is no history of serious illness, and he takes no medications. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F). A photograph of the lesion is shown. A tender 2-cm occipital lymph node is palpated. Which of the following is the most likely causal organism?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Group A streptococcus  </br>b. Herpes simplex virus  </br>c. Mycobacterium scrofulaceum  </br>d. Staphylococcus aureus  </br>e. Trichophyton tonsurans</br></br></br><p>Correct answer provided: e. Trichophyton tonsurans  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 6-year-old (pre-pubertal) boy → highest-risk age group for tinea capitis<br /></br>  – “Hair loss” with an enlarging “lump” on the scalp → patchy alopecia + inflammatory boggy plaque (kerion) typical of inflammatory tinea capitis<br /></br>  – Tender occipital lymph node → posterior cervical/occipital LAD frequently accompanies tinea capitis<br /></br>  – Afebrile and otherwise well → favors superficial dermatophyte infection rather than invasive bacterial disease<br /></br>  – Photograph (not shown to you) in the NBME stem displays: scaly circular patch, broken hairs, possible pustular/boggy swelling.  </p></br><p>Approach / Metacognition<br /></br>1. Any scalp lesion + alopecia → first ask “fungal (tinea capitis) or bacterial abscess/impetigo?”<br /></br>2. Look for broken “black-dot” hairs, peripheral scale (“ring”), and regional LAD → points to dermatophyte.<br /></br>3. Remember: in U.S. the most common organism is Trichophyton tonsurans (anthropophilic; spread by hats, combs, classmates).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>The question asks for the causal ORGANISM, not the diagnosis or treatment.<br /></br>• Diagnosis: Inflammatory tinea capitis (kerion).<br /></br>• Etiology: Dermatophytes infect keratinized hair shafts. In North America, 90%+ cases = T. tonsurans.<br /></br>Take-home: Any child with patchy alopecia ± LAD → think T. tonsurans until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient) would include: location/size of alopecic patch, scale, bogginess, presence of broken hairs, regional lymph nodes, KOH microscopy or fungal culture planned, and plan for systemic antifungal therapy.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Dermatophytes (Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton) digest keratin via keratinases.<br /></br>• In scalp infections, they invade hair shaft → hairs break at scalp surface (“black dots”) → alopecia.<br /></br>• An intense inflammatory response can create a boggy exudative mass (kerion).<br /></br>• Occipital/cervical LAD is a reactive response to scalp infection.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls<br /></br>1. Work-up<br /></br>   – Woods lamp: T. tonsurans usually does NOT fluoresce (unlike Microsporum canis).<br /></br>   – KOH prep or fungal culture confirms.<br /></br>2. Treatment (must be systemic—topicals don’t penetrate hair shaft):<br /></br>   – 1st line: Oral griseofulvin (microsize 20–25 mg/kg/d) for 6–8 weeks.<br /></br>   – Alternative: Oral terbinafine, itraconazole, fluconazole.<br /></br>   – Adjunct: Selenium sulfide or ketoconazole shampoo 2×/wk to reduce spore shedding.<br /></br>   – Treat close contacts or decontaminate combs/hats.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “T” for “Top of the head”: Trichophyton tonsurans = #1 cause of scalp tinea in kids.<br /></br>• Kerion = “Kerosene can burn your scalp” → boggy, tender, purulent mass from vigorous immune response.<br /></br>• Remember: Treat from the “inside (systemic)” because fungus is INSIDE the hair.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Group A Streptococcus (Strep pyogenes)<br /></br>   – Causes non-bullous impetigo or cellulitis → honey-crusted lesions, erythema, pain; no patchy alopecia or broken hairs.  </p></br><p>b. Herpes simplex virus<br /></br>   – Scalp HSV would produce grouped vesicles on an erythematous base → painful, often recurrent. Does not cause regional alopecia or kerion appearance.  </p></br><p>c. Mycobacterium scrofulaceum<br /></br>   – Atypical mycobacterial cervical lymphadenitis (“scrofula”) manifests as isolated neck mass, not scalp lesion with hair loss.  </p></br><p>d. Staphylococcus aureus<br /></br>   – Causes folliculitis, furuncles, carbuncles → pustules or abscesses, but hair shafts typically remain; alopecia patches with scaling are uncommon. Also shows systemic signs if severe.  </p></br><p>e. Trichophyton tonsurans (CORRECT)<br /></br>   – Classic for tinea capitis in children: patchy alopecia, scaling, broken hairs, kerion, occipital LAD.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Patchy scalp alopecia with broken hairs ± boggy inflammatory plaque and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy in a child = tinea capitis, most commonly caused by Trichophyton tonsurans. Treat with oral griseofulvin or terbinafine.</p>
62. A 15-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a painful lump in his right thigh for 3 weeks. Two months ago, he was kicked in the right thigh during a soccer game and had a bruise over the right femur. He has had no fever. Examination shows a mass in the distal anterior right thigh; the area is warm and tender to deep palpation. The circumference of the right thigh is larger than the left. Flexion and extension of the right knee are limited. An x-ray of the right femur and knee shows evidence of bone destruction with irregular, ragged, radiolucent defects and new bone formation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Ultrasonography of the distal thigh</br>b. Bone densitometry</br>c. Fine-needle aspiration of the knee joint for culture</br>d. Open biopsy of the bony lesion</br>e. No further diagnostic studies necessary</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Open biopsy of the bony lesion  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues in the stem<br /></br>• Patient: 15-year-old boy (peak age for primary malignant bone tumors).<br /></br>• Symptoms: “Painful lump,” progressive for 3 wk, warmth, tenderness, ↑ thigh circumference, limited knee ROM → points to an expanding, aggressive local process.<br /></br>• History of “bruise” 2 mo earlier: Minor trauma often draws attention to a pre-existing lesion; trauma itself rarely causes destructive bone changes.<br /></br>• Imaging: “Bone destruction with irregular, ragged, radiolucent defects and new bone formation” → classic aggressive (malignant) bone lesion pattern (e.g., osteosarcoma or Ewing sarcoma).<br /></br>• No fever → makes osteomyelitis/septic arthritis less likely.</p></br><p>Pattern recognition: Teenager + metaphyseal lesion of a long bone + aggressive periosteal reaction (“new bone formation”) → think primary malignant bone tumor; most common = osteogenic sarcoma.<br /></br>Metacognitive approach: 1) Identify patient demographic, 2) localize pathology (bone vs joint vs soft tissue), 3) categorize radiograph (benign vs aggressive), 4) ask “Do I need tissue to confirm?” → yes.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why biopsy is required<br /></br>1. Malignant bone tumors are diagnosed definitively only by histology.<br /></br>2. Treatment (limb-sparing resection ± chemotherapy) hinges on tumor type and grade.<br /></br>3. Open (incisional) biopsy performed by an orthopedic oncologist allows adequate tissue, orientation, and planning of future resection margins; fine-needle or joint aspiration is inadequate and risks contaminating surrounding compartments.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Any aggressive bone lesion on x-ray mandates tissue diagnosis, best obtained with a carefully planned open biopsy.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): size, location, neurovascular status, baseline ROM, neuro exam, planned biopsy tract.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Osteogenic sarcoma<br /></br>• Etiology: malignant proliferation of osteoblasts producing osteoid.<br /></br>• Risk factors: adolescence (growth spurt), Paget disease, prior radiation, familial Rb (RB1 mutation).<br /></br>• Typical site: metaphysis of long bones around knee (distal femur &gt; proximal tibia).<br /></br>• X-ray: mixed lytic/sclerotic lesion, cortical destruction, sunburst pattern, Codman triangle (periosteal lift).  </p></br><p>Ewing sarcoma (2nd most common)<br /></br>• Small round blue-cell tumor (t(11;22) EWS-FLI1).<br /></br>• Diaphyseal lesion, onion-skin periosteal reaction, systemic signs occasionally (fever, leukocytosis).</p></br><p>Either way → biopsy for diagnosis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>X-ray suggests aggressive lesion.  </li></br><li>MRI of entire bone (local staging) → evaluates soft-tissue extension, neurovascular involvement.  </li></br><li>Open biopsy (step being asked).  </li></br><li>Once pathology confirmed → metastatic work-up (CT chest, bone scan/PET).  </li></br><li>Neoadjuvant chemotherapy + limb-sparing wide resection ± endoprosthesis (osteosarcoma).  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Bad Bone in a Teen? Get the Scene.”<br /></br>B – Benign vs malignant? (ragged edges = malignant)<br /></br>O – Obtain MRI for mapping<br /></br>N – Needle NOT enough → need open biopsy<br /></br>E – Examine histology before Everything else (chemo, excision)</p></br><p>Alternate mnemonic: “O.S. = Open Specimen.” (Osteosarcoma → Open biopsy).</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Ultrasonography of the distal thigh<br /></br>• Good for cystic vs solid soft-tissue masses, not for intra-osseous lesions; would not provide tissue diagnosis or staging information.</p></br><p>b. Bone densitometry<br /></br>• Measures bone mineral density; used for osteoporosis screening, not for focal destructive lesions.</p></br><p>c. Fine-needle aspiration of the knee joint for culture<br /></br>• Targets joint fluid, appropriate for suspected septic arthritis (fever, acute pain, effusion). Lesion is intramedullary bone, patient afebrile; culture will be negative and diagnostic delay dangerous.</p></br><p>e. No further diagnostic studies necessary<br /></br>• Never appropriate when imaging shows an aggressive bone lesion; delaying biopsy risks metastasis and worse prognosis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Aggressive bone lesions in adolescents require prompt, definitive tissue diagnosis. An open (incisional) biopsy—planned by the surgical team that will perform definitive resection—is the gold-standard first step after x-ray.</p>
63. A 48-year-old man, who was admitted to the hospital 3 days ago for treatment of multiple injuries sustained during a motor vehicle collision, suddenly develops left-sided substernal chest pain. On admission, a right chest tube was placed for treatment of a hemopneumothorax and multiple right-sided rib fractures. He also sustained a right femur fracture, which was surgically repaired 2 days ago, and a grade II liver laceration, which was treated nonoperatively. Vital signs now are temperature 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse 120/min and irregularly irregular, respirations 26/min, and blood pressure 90/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 100 percent. The patient appears anxious, and his breathing is labored. Auscultation of the lungs discloses mildly diminished breath sounds on the right but no wheezes or crackles. Abdominal examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate next step in evaluation of this patient?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Chest x-ray</br>b. Complete blood count</br>c. Duplex ultrasonography of the lower extremities</br>d. ECG</br>e. Echocardiography</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. ECG  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Time course &amp; setting<br /></br>   • Day 3 after significant blunt‐force trauma (rib fractures, hemopneumothorax, femur fracture, liver lac).<br /></br>   • Post-operative day 2 after open reduction/internal fixation of the femur.  </li></br><li>Presenting symptoms &amp; vitals<br /></br>   • Sudden, left-sided substernal chest pain.<br /></br>   • Tachycardia 120/min that is “irregularly irregular” → classic description of new-onset atrial fibrillation (AF).<br /></br>   • Hypotension (90/50 mm Hg).<br /></br>   • Normothermic, mildly tachypneic; SpO₂ 100 % on room air.  </li></br><li>Physical findings<br /></br>   • No new findings to suggest worsening pneumothorax (breath sounds only “mildly diminished” on side of known chest tube).<br /></br>   • No crackles or wheezes to suggest CHF or pneumonia.<br /></br>   • No abdominal tenderness to suggest abdominal catastrophe.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Key cognitive steps<br /></br>• Any patient with acute chest pain and hemodynamic instability → first, rule out life-threatening cardiopulmonary causes.<br /></br>• The combination of chest pain + new arrhythmia + hypotension makes myocardial ischemia/contusion or a primary arrhythmia (e.g., AF with rapid ventricular response) the top concern.<br /></br>• The single fastest, lowest-risk bedside test that immediately clarifies rhythm and can show ischemic changes is an ECG.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>“Unstable vitals + chest discomfort on the floor/ICU” → Step 1 in virtually every ACLS or trauma algorithm is to put on the monitor and obtain a 12-lead ECG; it guides every subsequent diagnostic or therapeutic decision.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>What is the core concept?<br /></br>   • Rapid identification of a potentially lethal arrhythmia or post-traumatic myocardial injury requires an ECG before other tests.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Why not jump to imaging for PE or pneumothorax?<br /></br>   • Those processes rarely give an “irregularly irregular” rhythm; an ECG is quicker, noninvasive, and can be obtained within 1–2 minutes at the bedside, making it the correct immediate step.<br /></br>   • If the ECG is non-diagnostic, further testing (CT angiography, CXR, echo, etc.) can follow.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Documentation pearl<br /></br>• Record time, rhythm strip, 12-lead tracing, vitals, pain score, and any ACLS interventions ordered (e.g., IV β-blocker, rate control, synchronized cardioversion if unstable).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>A. Post-traumatic myocardial contusion<br /></br>   • Blunt chest trauma can bruise the myocardium → ectopy, AF, ventricular arrhythmias.<br /></br>B. Stress-induced or ischemic AF<br /></br>   • Pain, hypovolemia, catecholamine surge, or occult ischemia can precipitate AF.<br /></br>C. Tachyarrhythmia-mediated hypotension<br /></br>   • Rapid ventricular rates shorten diastole → ↓LV filling → ↓stroke volume → hypotension.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>Immediate<br /></br>   a. 12-lead ECG + cardiac monitor (correct answer).<br /></br>   b. If patient remains unstable, proceed with ACLS tachycardia algorithm (synchronized cardioversion for AF with RVR + hypotension).  </li></br><li>Next steps guided by ECG<br /></br>   • ST-segment changes? → obtain troponins, consider cardiac contusion vs coronary injury, manage per cardiology.<br /></br>   • Pure AF with RVR and no ST changes? → rate control (IV metoprolol or diltiazem) unless contraindicated; consider chemical/electrical cardioversion if unstable.  </li></br><li>Ancillary tests<br /></br>   • Cardiac enzymes every 6–8 h ×3.<br /></br>   • CXR to confirm chest-tube position and exclude late hemothorax.<br /></br>   • Echocardiography if concern for wall-motion abnormality, tamponade, or EF assessment.<br /></br>   • CT pulmonary angiography only if ECG and enzymes are non-diagnostic and PE remains a concern.  </li></br></ol></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“CHEST” algorithm for in-hospital chest pain (especially in trauma ICU):<br /></br>C – Check ECG FIRST<br /></br>H – Hemodynamics (IV access, fluids, pressors)<br /></br>E – Enzymes (troponin)<br /></br>S – Simple imaging (CXR)<br /></br>T – Targeted advanced tests (echo, CT)  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Chest x-ray<br /></br>   • Good for pneumothorax, hemothorax, mediastinal shift, hardware position.<br /></br>   • Does not immediately clarify an arrhythmia or myocardial ischemia. Slower than an ECG.  </p></br><p>b. Complete blood count<br /></br>   • Helpful for anemia or infection but provides no emergent information for acute chest pain + arrhythmia.  </p></br><p>c. Duplex ultrasonography of the lower extremities<br /></br>   • Screens for DVT → potential PE source, but (1) not immediate, (2) PE does not usually cause irregularly irregular rhythm, (3) he is currently unstable.  </p></br><p>e. Echocardiography<br /></br>   • Excellent for tamponade, wall-motion abnormalities, or EF but requires equipment/personnel and takes longer than a bedside ECG. Generally performed after rhythm/ischemia are assessed on ECG.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Take-Home Message<br /></br>In any hospitalized patient with sudden chest pain and hemodynamic compromise, the very first bedside test should be a 12-lead ECG to identify life-threatening arrhythmias or ischemia. Everything else hinges on those results.</p>
64. A 76-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 5-week history of shortness of breath. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. His pulse is 110/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 118/68 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 88 percent. Examination shows jugular venous distention to above the mandible bilaterally. The distention increases with inspiration. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Acute myocardial infarction</br>b. Constrictive pericarditis</br>c. Left ventricular heart failure</br>d. Left ventricular heart failure and pulmonary edema</br>e. Pulmonary edema</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Constrictive pericarditis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key findings<br /></br>   • 5-week progressive dyspnea – subacute, suggests a chronic rather than an acute ischemic process.<br /></br>   • Jugular venous distention (JVD) “to above the mandible” – markedly elevated right-sided filling pressures.<br /></br>   • JVD “increases with inspiration” – this is the classic Kussmaul sign.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>How the clues narrow the differential<br /></br>   • Kussmaul sign = paradoxical rise (or failure to fall) of JVP with inspiration → impaired right-ventricular filling in diastole.<br /></br>   • High-yield causes: constrictive pericarditis, restrictive cardiomyopathy, right-sided myocardial infarction, massive PE, severe tricuspid disease, RA tumors.<br /></br>   • Of the answer choices, only constrictive pericarditis fits that physiology.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No pulmonary crackles, orthopnea, pink frothy sputum → argues against pulmonary edema from LV failure.<br /></br>   • No crushing chest pain or diaphoresis → less consistent with an acute MI.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   • Step 1: Identify the “weird” physical exam finding (Kussmaul sign).<br /></br>   • Step 2: Recall the short list of etiologies that cause impaired RV filling.<br /></br>   • Step 3: Match to answer set → constrictive pericarditis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Constrictive pericarditis: a rigid, often calcified pericardium prevents normal ventricular expansion during diastole. Inspiration increases venous return, but the stiff pericardium blocks RV expansion, so venous blood backs up into the neck veins → Kussmaul sign.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Kussmaul sign = think “CONSTRICTION or RESTRICTION” of the right ventricle.  </p></br><p>EMR documentation (if real patient): “Marked JVD to angle of mandible with Kussmaul physiology; concern for constrictive pericarditis vs restrictive cardiomyopathy; plan for echocardiography and CT chest to evaluate pericardial thickness &amp; calcification.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Etiologies<br /></br>• Idiopathic or post-viral fibrosis<br /></br>• Prior cardiac surgery or radiation<br /></br>• Tuberculosis worldwide<br /></br>• Connective-tissue disorders  </p></br><p>Hemodynamics<br /></br>• Equalization of diastolic pressures in all chambers<br /></br>• Rapid early diastolic filling followed by abrupt halt → pericardial “knock” on auscultation<br /></br>• Kussmaul sign, prominent y-descent on JVP tracing  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Tests<br /></br>   • Transthoracic echo: thickened pericardium, septal bounce.<br /></br>   • CT/MRI: pericardial calcification or thickening (&gt;4 mm).<br /></br>   • Cardiac catheterization: “square-root” sign (dip-and-plateau) in ventricular pressure tracings.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>   • Symptomatic: diuretics for volume overload.<br /></br>   • Definitive: surgical pericardiectomy.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Kussmaul = KUSS-maul:<br /></br>  K = Knock (pericardial)<br /></br>  U = Unyielding pericardium<br /></br>  S = Stiff right ventricle<br /></br>  S = Square-root sign on cath”</p></br><p>Visual: Picture the jugular vein as a balloon “kissing” (Kuss-) the rigid pericardial “wall” that won’t let the heart expand.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Acute myocardial infarction – Presents with acute chest pain, ECG changes, ± cardiogenic shock. Kussmaul sign is uncommon unless right-ventricular infarct, which typically presents suddenly, not over 5 weeks.  </p></br><p>C. Left ventricular heart failure – Causes pulmonary congestion (crackles, orthopnea), S3 gallop, NOT Kussmaul sign. JVD may appear only if right heart fails secondarily.  </p></br><p>D. Left ventricular failure with pulmonary edema – Would have obvious rales, frothy sputum, hypoxia primarily from alveolar flooding; again, JVP usually decreases on inspiration (normal physiology).  </p></br><p>E. Pulmonary edema (non-cardiogenic or cardiogenic) – Produces diffuse crackles and hypoxia but does not create a paradoxical inspiratory rise in JVP.  </p></br><p>Key distinction: Only processes that impede right-sided diastolic filling give you Kussmaul physiology; left-sided diseases give you pulmonary findings instead.</p>
65. A 49-year-old man comes to the clinic for an initial health maintenance examination. He has not been evaluated by a physician during the past 20 years. Medical history is unremarkable. His only medication is as-needed acetaminophen. Family history is remarkable for the death of his maternal grandmother at age 82 years and of his father at age 58 years from ruptured aortic aneurysms. The patient's brother underwent elective repair of an aortic aneurysm at age 57 years. The patient has smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 24 years. He drinks four alcoholic beverages and several cups of coffee daily. He does not exercise regularly. He is 183 cm (6 ft) tall and weighs 107 kg (235 lb); BMI is 32 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 92/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 144/86 mm Hg. Physical examination discloses central obesity. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following interventions is most likely to have the greatest benefit for this patient based on his personal risk factors?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Doxycycline therapy</br>b. Elimination of caffeine from the patient's diet</br>c. Metoprolol therapy</br>d. Smoking cessation program</br>e. Weekly exercise regimen and weight-loss program</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Smoking-cessation program</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should jump out on a quick scan:<br /></br>• 49-y/o man who 1) has smoked 12.5 pack-years, 2) has a strong family history of aortic aneurysm rupture in first-degree relatives, 3) is overweight/obese, sedentary, and mildly hypertensive.<br /></br>• Question stem: “Which intervention is most likely to have the greatest benefit for this patient based on his personal risk factors?” → they are asking for the single intervention that will confer the largest reduction in future morbidity/mortality for <em>this</em> patient given his risk profile (high risk for abdominal aortic aneurysm [AAA]).</p></br><p>Cognitive “test-taking” framework:<br /></br>1. Identify the disease the test writers are targeting → AAA.<br /></br>2. Recall modifiable risk factors for AAA development/growth/rupture → the <em>strongest</em> is cigarette smoking (dose-dependent).<br /></br>3. Ask which offered intervention directly addresses that highest-impact risk factor.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Cigarette smoking is the most powerful modifiable risk factor for the formation, expansion, and rupture of abdominal aortic aneurysms. Smoking cessation markedly slows aneurysm growth rate and reduces rupture risk, in many studies conferring a larger relative-risk reduction than BP control, lipid management, or weight loss.  </p></br><p>• This patient already carries non-modifiable high-risk features (family history of fatal aneurysm), so eliminating the major modifiable driver (smoking) yields the greatest absolute benefit.</p></br><p>Core takeaway:<br /></br>For patients at risk for AAA, smoking cessation is the single most effective intervention to lower incidence, slow expansion, and decrease rupture-related mortality.</p></br><p>Real-world charting pearls<br /></br>• Document pack-year history, counseling on cessation, resources provided (pharmacologic aids, quitline referral).<br /></br>• Plan for future AAA screening with abdominal ultrasound at age 65 if still smoking or earlier given family history (USPSTF).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Smoking → ↑ oxidative stress &amp; elastase activity, ↑ matrix metalloproteinases → degradation of elastin &amp; collagen in aortic media → progressive dilatation.<br /></br>• Family history suggests possible genetic susceptibility (e.g., variants affecting extracellular matrix repair).<br /></br>• Hypertension and male sex also contribute, but smoking has the largest attributable risk.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Prevention Nuggets  </p></br><ol></br><li>Smoking cessation: counseling + pharmacotherapy (varenicline &gt; bupropion &gt; NRT in effectiveness).  </li></br><li>Surveillance U/S when size &gt;3.0 cm; elective repair when &gt;5.5 cm or rapid expansion.  </li></br><li>Control HTN &amp; lipids, but data show no clear pharmacologic agent that halts AAA growth besides nicotine abstinence.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“MELT the cigarette to SHRINK the aneurysm”<br /></br>M – Most<br /></br>E – Effective<br /></br>L – Lifestyle change is<br /></br>T – Tobacco cessation</p></br><p>Think: “No smoke, no poke (of the aneurysm)!”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Doxycycline therapy<br /></br>• Investigational MMP inhibitor; not standard care, no proven outcome benefit in humans.</p></br><p>b. Elimination of caffeine<br /></br>• Caffeine may transiently raise BP but is <em>not</em> an established independent risk factor for AAA incidence or rupture.</p></br><p>c. Metoprolol therapy<br /></br>• β-blockers are helpful in thoracic aneurysms due to Marfan syndrome (reduce shear stress), but they have not shown meaningful effect on AAA growth or rupture; his BP is only mildly elevated.</p></br><p>e. Weekly exercise &amp; weight-loss program<br /></br>• Beneficial for overall cardiovascular health; however, obesity is a much weaker risk factor for AAA compared with continued smoking. Absolute risk-reduction from smoking cessation dwarfs weight-loss benefits in this context.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In patients with strong familial predisposition to abdominal aortic aneurysm, quitting cigarettes is the single most powerful modifiable step to prevent aneurysm formation, slow enlargement, and cut rupture-related mortality.</p>
66. A 34-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-year history of episodes of nasal congestion and clear discharge, frequent sneezing, and moderate “sinus pressure.” Her symptoms occur throughout the year. She has no history of asthma, hives, generalized swelling, or known allergies. She takes no medications. Her parents are allergic to cats. The patient has never had a pet. She has never smoked cigarettes. She lives on the fourth floor of a large apartment complex. She appears in no distress. Her temperature is 36.7°C (98°F), pulse is 68/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 113/67 mm Hg. Examination shows pale, edematous nasal mucosa; no other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for this patient?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Immunotherapy</br>b. Moving to a newly constructed apartment complex</br>c. Moving to an apartment on a lower floor</br>d. Reupholstering of bedroom furniture</br>e. Use of pillow and mattress covers</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Use of pillow and mattress covers  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases &amp; clues<br /></br>   • “2-year history … throughout the year” → perennial, not seasonal.<br /></br>   • “Nasal congestion, clear discharge, sneezing, ‘sinus pressure’ ” → classic allergic rhinitis.<br /></br>   • “Pale, edematous nasal mucosa” → physical exam hallmark of allergic rhinitis.<br /></br>   • No pets, never smoked, lives on 4th floor of large apartment → indoor, high-rise environment.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • No spring/fall predominance → pollen less likely.<br /></br>   • No pet exposure → cat dander unlikely.<br /></br>   • High-rise living → dust-mite load in bedding &amp; carpets common; cockroach allergen more a problem on lower floors.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   a. Identify chronic rhinitis pattern (perennial vs seasonal).<br /></br>   b. Associate indoor perennial triggers (dust mites, mold, cockroach).<br /></br>   c. Ask: “What is the single highest-yield environmental control for that trigger?” → dust-mite impermeable encasements on bedding.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• First-line management of allergic rhinitis = (1) allergen avoidance/environmental control → (2) pharmacotherapy (intranasal steroid, oral/intranasal antihistamine) → (3) immunotherapy if refractory.<br /></br>• In perennial disease due to dust mites, the greatest allergen reservoir is the bedroom (pillows, mattress, box spring).<br /></br>• Impermeable covers plus washing bedding ≥130 °F weekly drops dust-mite antigen exposure by &gt;90 %, decreasing symptoms and medication need.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In perennial allergic rhinitis suspected to be dust-mite driven, encasing pillows and mattresses is the most evidence-based initial environmental intervention.  </p></br><p>Clinical documentation (real patient): “Counseled patient on dust-mite avoidance measures: impermeable pillow/mattress encasements, weekly hot-water laundering of bedding, removal of bedroom carpets; will start intranasal fluticasone if symptoms persist.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Allergic rhinitis = type I IgE-mediated hypersensitivity to inhaled allergens.<br /></br>• Dust mites (Dermatophagoides spp.) thrive in warm, humid indoor textiles; shed fecal pellets that become airborne with movement of bedding.<br /></br>• Chronic exposure → Th2 skewed immune response, IgE production, mast-cell degranulation → histamine, leukotrienes → sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal edema.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Environmental control measures for dust-mite allergy (high-yield list):<br /></br>1. Zippered, allergen-impermeable covers on pillows, mattress, box spring.<br /></br>2. Wash bedding weekly in hot water (&gt;130 °F).<br /></br>3. Reduce indoor humidity (&lt;50 %).<br /></br>4. Remove carpets/heavy drapes, especially in bedroom.<br /></br>5. HEPA vacuum and air filters as adjuncts.  </p></br><p>Pharmacologic step-up (if needed):<br /></br>• 1st line: Intranasal corticosteroid (eg, fluticasone) daily.<br /></br>• Additive: 2nd-gen nonsedating antihistamine (eg, cetirizine) or intranasal antihistamine (azelastine).<br /></br>• Refractory: Leukotriene receptor antagonist (montelukast) or allergen-specific subcutaneous/sublingual immunotherapy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Dusty Mites Sleep Tight”<br /></br>• Dust-Mites → Bedroom focus<br /></br>• Sleep → pillows &amp; mattress<br /></br>• Tight zippered covers keep mites in the dark  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture a giant dust mite locked inside a zippered pillow, unable to escape.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Immunotherapy<br /></br>   • Used when maximal avoidance + meds fail or when single known allergen (eg, grass pollen) identified. Initial step is avoidance, not desensitization.  </p></br><p>b. Moving to a newly constructed apartment complex<br /></br>   • Dust mites inhabit all indoor dwellings regardless of building age; construction change alone won’t reduce exposure.  </p></br><p>c. Moving to an apartment on a lower floor<br /></br>   • Lower floors are actually associated with more cockroach allergen, not dust mites; her problem is perennial without cockroach risk factors.  </p></br><p>d. Reupholstering bedroom furniture<br /></br>   • Upholstered items harbor dust but primary reservoir remains pillows/mattress; reupholstering is costly and less effective than simple encasements.  </p></br><p>e. Use of pillow and mattress covers – Correct as explained above.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────</p>
67. A 67-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife because of a 3-day history of fever and cough productive of blood-tinged, green sputum. She says he was reluctant to come to the hospital and she had to insist. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus. He does not take any medications. On questioning about his alcohol use, he says he sometimes drinks a little too much, especially when he is traveling for his job. He has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 30 years. On arrival, he appears slightly disheveled and smells strongly of cigarette smoke. He coughs frequently during the examination. His temperature is 38.9°C (102°F), pulse is 96/min and regular, and blood pressure is 110/65 mm Hg. Rhonchi are heard throughout the lung fields, most prominently on the right. A grade 2/6 systolic murmur is heard best at the left sternal border. The liver is firm, and the edge is palpated 3 cm below the right costal margin. There is trace pedal edema. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit                    37 percent</br>Leukocyte count               16,500/mm3</br>Segmented neutrophils         85 percent</br>Lymphocytes                   15 percent</br>Platelet count                99,000/mm3</br>Serum</br>  Alkaline phosphatase        350 U/L</br>  AST                         95 U/L</br>  ALT                         97 U/L</br>A chest x-ray shows an infiltrate in the middle of the right lung with a dense area of consolidation. Results of a sputum culture are pending. In addition to antibiotic therapy, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Blood cultures</br>b. Sputum culture for fungi</br>c. Sputum culture for tuberculosis</br>d. CT scan–guided needle aspiration of the area of consolidation</br>e. Thoracentesis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Blood cultures  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point to community-acquired pneumonia serious enough to warrant hospitalization:<br /></br>• 3-day fever (102 °F), cough with purulent/bloody sputum → infectious process in lung.<br /></br>• Chest-x-ray: right-middle-lobe infiltrate with dense consolidation → classic lobar pneumonia pattern.<br /></br>• Leukocytosis (16 500/mm³, 85 % neutrophils) → bacterial.<br /></br>• Comorbidities that raise mortality risk: age &gt;65 y, diabetes, heavy alcohol use, possible chronic liver disease (firm liver edge, low platelets, ↑AST/ALT/ALKP).<br /></br>• Thrombocytopenia, mild hypotension (110/65) → systemic inflammatory response; sepsis a concern.  </p></br><p>Framework to approach CAP questions:<br /></br>1. Recognize CAP (≤48 h after admission).<br /></br>2. Decide site of care (outpt, ward, ICU) with CURB-65 / Pneumonia Severity Index.<br /></br>3. BEFORE first antibiotic dose, obtain recommended cultures in patients admitted to hospital or with severe disease.  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>IDSA/ATS CAP guidelines: All patients treated in the hospital for CAP should have:<br /></br>• TWO sets of blood cultures (aerobic + anaerobic) obtained from separate venipuncture sites BEFORE antibiotics, because:<br /></br>  – Up to 10 % of hospitalized CAP patients are bacteremic.<br /></br>  – Identifies pathogens not recovered from sputum (e.g., Streptococcus pneumoniae, Klebsiella, Staph aureus).<br /></br>  – Guides narrowing of therapy and duration.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In any inpatient CAP or suspected sepsis, first step after drawing labs = obtain blood cultures prior to starting antimicrobials. Document in the chart: “Two peripheral blood culture sets obtained prior to first antibiotic dose.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Bacterial pneumonia → alveolar exudate and consolidation. Bacteremia results from translocation of organisms (especially S. pneumoniae, Klebsiella, H. influenzae, S. aureus) into the bloodstream, causing sepsis and metastatic infection (e.g., meningitis, endocarditis). Alcohol misuse and diabetes impair macrophage/neutrophil function, increasing risk of invasive disease.  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls  </p></br><ol></br><li>Diagnostic tests for hospitalized CAP:<br /></br>   • Blood cultures (2 sets).<br /></br>   • Sputum Gram stain + culture (before antibiotics if possible).<br /></br>   • Legionella &amp; pneumococcal urine antigens if severe.  </li></br><li>Empiric antibiotics for ward CAP: IV ceftriaxone + azithromycin OR IV respiratory fluoroquinolone (e.g., levofloxacin). Adjust when cultures return.  </li></br><li>Monitor for complications: sepsis, empyema, ARDS.  </li></br></ol></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tip  </p></br><p>“B for Bed-bound” – Any CAP patient sick enough for a hospital BED gets two B-lood cultures Before the first antibiotic.  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Sputum culture for fungi<br /></br>   • Indicated in immunocompromised hosts (HIV with CD4 &lt;200, neutropenia, prolonged steroids, transplant). Nothing in vignette suggests this.  </p></br><p>c. Sputum culture for tuberculosis<br /></br>   • TB presents subacutely (weeks-months), constitutional symptoms, upper-lobe cavitation or miliary pattern. Fast onset, lobar consolidation favors typical bacteria.  </p></br><p>d. CT-guided needle aspiration<br /></br>   • Used to sample a peripheral mass/nodule suspicious for malignancy or abscess not accessible by bronchoscopy; not first-line for routine lobar pneumonia.  </p></br><p>e. Thoracentesis<br /></br>   • Only if there is a pleural effusion to evaluate for empyema or parapneumonic effusion. No effusion described on exam or x-ray.  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Hospitalized community-acquired pneumonia requires two blood cultures drawn before empiric antibiotics—essential for identifying bacteremia and tailoring therapy.</p>
68. A 52-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of progressive shortness of breath with exertion. He reports shortness of breath after climbing one flight of stairs and occasional whistling breath sounds. He has no history of serious illness. He has smoked one and one-half packs of cigarettes daily for 36 years. His pulse is 88/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 160/88 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98 percent at rest. Jugular venous pressure is within normal limits. Breath sounds are distant. There is no peripheral edema. Pulmonary function tests show a FEV1 of 60 percent of predicted; the FEV1:FVC ratio is 50 percent of predicted. In addition to recommending smoking cessation, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Oral glucocorticosteroid therapy  </br>b. Oral leukotriene inhibitor therapy  </br>c. Oral theophylline therapy  </br>d. Long-acting inhaled bronchodilator therapy  </br>e. Oxygen via nasal cannula during sleep</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Long-acting inhaled bronchodilator therapy (eg, tiotropium [LAMA] or salmeterol/formoterol [LABA])</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• 52-year-old, 36-pack-year smoker → classic risk for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).<br /></br>• Progressive exertional dyspnea, occasional “whistling” → COPD symptom pattern.<br /></br>• PFTs: FEV₁ 60 % predicted and FEV₁/FVC 50 % predicted (&lt;70 %) → obstructive defect, GOLD stage II (moderate).<br /></br>• Normal JVP, clear extremities → no cor pulmonale or right-sided heart failure.<br /></br>• O₂ sat 98 % at rest → no resting hypoxemia.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach (test-taking framework):<br /></br>1. Identify obstruction (↓FEV₁/FVC) → think COPD vs asthma.<br /></br>2. Stage COPD by FEV₁ % predicted → dictates chronic maintenance therapy.<br /></br>3. Check for hypoxemia (PaO₂/SaO₂) → determines need for long-term O₂.<br /></br>4. Match guideline step-up therapy (GOLD).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>GOLD guidelines for stable COPD:<br /></br>• All patients → smoking cessation, vaccination, short-acting bronchodilator (SABA) as needed.<br /></br>• GOLD stage II (50–79 % predicted) OR pts with bothersome symptoms → add long-acting bronchodilator (LABA or LAMA).<br /></br>• Inhaled glucocorticoid (ICS) is added only with severe airflow limitation (FEV₁ &lt;50 %) and/or frequent exacerbations.<br /></br>• Oral steroids are reserved for acute exacerbations, not maintenance.<br /></br>• Long-term O₂ only if PaO₂ ≤55 mm Hg or SaO₂ ≤88 % (or cor pulmonale/erythrocytosis).  </p></br><p>Therefore, after smoking cessation counseling the next evidence-based step is a long-acting inhaled bronchodilator.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Moderate COPD without resting hypoxemia = add LABA or LAMA.</p></br><p>Documentation items: symptom burden, PFT results, smoking status, counseling provided, prescription for inhaled long-acting bronchodilator, follow-up plan.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Chronic cigarette smoke → oxidative stress → neutrophil elastase &amp; protease-mediated alveolar wall destruction (emphysema) + mucus hypersecretion &amp; airway inflammation (chronic bronchitis) → airflow limitation that is not fully reversible. Bronchodilators relax smooth muscle &amp; reduce air-trapping, improving exercise tolerance.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>First-line long-acting agents:<br /></br>• LAMA: tiotropium, umeclidinium (↓exacerbations, improves FEV₁).<br /></br>• LABA: salmeterol, formoterol, indacaterol.<br /></br>Combination LABA/LAMA often superior to either alone for symptom control.</p></br><p>Pulmonary rehab is recommended starting at stage II as well.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“COPD STEPS” mnemonic:<br /></br>S – Smoking cessation, SABA prn (all)<br /></br>T – Tiotropium / LABA (stage Two)<br /></br>E – Exacerbation prevention (vaccines, rehab)<br /></br>P – Pulmonary steroids (inhaled) if FEV₁ &lt;50 % or frequent attacks<br /></br>S – Supplemental O₂ when Sat ≤88 %</p></br><p>Visual hook: Think of climbing a staircase—Stage II patients take the “long step” (long-acting bronchodilator) before they climb to steroids or oxygen on the higher steps.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Oral glucocorticosteroid therapy<br /></br>   • Used for acute exacerbations only; chronic oral steroids cause systemic side effects and are not maintenance therapy for stable COPD.</p></br><p>b. Oral leukotriene inhibitor<br /></br>   • Montelukast/zafirlukast help asthma, not evidence-based for COPD symptom control.</p></br><p>c. Oral theophylline<br /></br>   • Narrow therapeutic window, serious toxicity; reserved for refractory patients who cannot use inhaled meds.</p></br><p>e. Oxygen via nasal cannula during sleep<br /></br>   • Indicated only when PaO₂ ≤55 mm Hg or SaO₂ ≤88 % (or cor pulmonale/polycythemia). This patient’s resting Sat 98% rules it out.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</p>
69. A 62-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of increased flatulence. During this time, she also has had mild abdominal cramps often followed by loose stools. She has not had any weight changes. She has a low-fiber diet and does not drink milk. She drinks three to four cans of diet cola daily and chews sugar-free gum. Examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show no abnormalities. Ingestion of which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's flatulence?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Aspartame</br>b. Galactose</br>c. Gluten</br>d. Sorbitol</br>e. Sucrose</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Sorbitol  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key phrases<br /></br>   • “1-year history of increased flatulence … mild abdominal cramps … loose stools” → chronic, painless, osmotic‐type GI symptoms.<br /></br>   • “Sugar-free gum” and “three to four cans of diet cola daily” → products that often contain sugar alcohols (e.g., sorbitol, mannitol, xylitol).<br /></br>   • “No weight changes, normal exam &amp; labs” → argues against malabsorption syndromes that cause nutritional deficiencies (e.g., celiac, pancreatic insufficiency).  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pertinent positives &amp; negatives<br /></br>   • Positives: Gas, bloating, diarrhea after ingesting specific processed foods.<br /></br>   • Negatives: No blood, no weight loss, normal labs → points away from inflammatory or malabsorptive disease.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>Ask yourself: “Is there a common, benign dietary trigger that is (a) poorly absorbed, (b) fermented by colonic bacteria, and (c) present in ‘diet’ or ‘sugar-free’ products?” → think sugar alcohols such as sorbitol.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Chronic bloating + loose stools → consider osmotic vs secretory diarrhea.<br /></br>2. Symptoms occur with specific foods (sugar-free gum/soda) → osmotic.<br /></br>3. Culprit substances in these products = sugar alcohols.<br /></br>4. Sorbitol is the classic USMLE favorite → choose it.  </p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>Sugar alcohols (especially sorbitol) are poorly absorbed in the small intestine, draw fluid osmotically, and are fermented by colonic bacteria, producing gas and diarrhea.  </p></br><p>Documentation note (real life)<br /></br>• Diet history reveals high intake of sorbitol-containing products.<br /></br>• Impression: Diet-induced osmotic diarrhea/flatulence (sorbitol).<br /></br>• Plan: Trial of eliminating sugar-free gum and diet sodas; counsel on reading labels.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Sorbitol is a six-carbon sugar alcohol.<br /></br>• Absorption: Slow, carrier-independent diffusion → large fraction reaches colon.<br /></br>• In colon: Fermented by bacteria → CO₂, H₂, short-chain fatty acids → gas &amp; cramps.<br /></br>• Osmotic effect: Unabsorbed sorbitol retains water → loose stools.<br /></br>Risk factors: High intake in “sugar-free” candies, gums, diet drinks, some diabetic foods.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>• Diagnosis is clinical: resolution of symptoms after stopping sorbitol confirms.<br /></br>• No labs or imaging needed if classic presentation without alarm features.<br /></br>• If unsure, can perform stool osmotic gap or elimination diet trial.<br /></br>• Management: Eliminate or drastically reduce sorbitol-containing products.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Sorbitol = Sorb-bloat-all”<br /></br>Think: “Sorbitol sits &amp; ferments → Sor(b)-gas-itol.”<br /></br>Remember the triad: Sugar-free product + Bloating + Osmotic diarrhea → Sorbitol.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Aspartame<br /></br>• Dipeptide methyl ester completely hydrolyzed to aspartate, phenylalanine, methanol → rapidly absorbed → DOES NOT reach colon in significant amounts → minimal gas/diarrhea.  </p></br><p>B. Galactose<br /></br>• Monosaccharide actively absorbed with glucose via SGLT1. Malabsorption would present early in life (galactosemia) with serious systemic illness, not isolated flatulence in an adult.  </p></br><p>C. Gluten<br /></br>• Would suggest celiac disease: weight loss, iron-deficiency anemia, dermatitis herpetiformis, positive tTG or EMA antibodies, villous atrophy on biopsy. Normal labs and no weight loss make this unlikely.  </p></br><p>E. Sucrose<br /></br>• Disaccharide hydrolyzed by brush-border sucrase; rapidly absorbed. Isolated sucrase deficiency is rare and presents in infancy with watery diarrhea upon sucrose intake, not a new problem at age 62.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only sorbitol is both (1) commonly found in the foods she consumes and (2) poorly absorbed, leading to gas and diarrhea.</p>
70. A 5-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother for a well-child examination. The family recently moved to the area from another state; the child's last examination was 6 months ago. The mother says the boy has been generally healthy. Vital signs are temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse 100/min, respirations 20/min, and blood pressure 100/60 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a protuberant umbilicus that enlarges when the boy coughs. Palpation of the umbilicus discloses a 2-cm umbilical ring with no incarcerated tissue. Without treatment, which of the following is the most likely outcome of the child's condition?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Incarceration of the right colon</br>b. Necrosis of the overlying skin</br>c. Progressive enlargement of the umbilical ring</br>d. Spontaneous closure of the umbilical ring</br>e. Volvulus of the small intestine</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>C. Progressive enlargement of the umbilical ring  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “5-year-old,” “protuberant umbilicus that enlarges when he coughs,” “2-cm umbilical ring,” “no incarcerated tissue.”<br /></br>• These describe a classic reducible umbilical hernia.<br /></br>• Size matters: defects &lt;1–1.5 cm almost always close; ≥1.5–2 cm rarely close after age 2.<br /></br>• Age matters: by 3–4 years &gt;90 % of small hernias have closed. Persistence at age 5 with a 2-cm ring indicates unlikely spontaneous closure and a tendency to enlarge.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Identify defect (umbilical vs. epigastric vs. inguinal).<br /></br>2. Note size and age.<br /></br>3. Apply natural-history rule: small + young → likely close; large or &gt;4 y → likely persist/enlarge.<br /></br>4. Scan for red flags (pain, discoloration, tender mass = incarceration/strangulation) – absent here.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Natural history drives management:<br /></br>• &lt;1–1.5 cm defect, age ≤3 y: observe.<br /></br>• Persistent defect at age ≥4–5 y, or &gt;1.5 cm at any age, or symptoms/incarceration: elective surgical repair to prevent progressive enlargement and cosmetic issues.<br /></br>Therefore, if the hernia is not repaired, the ring slowly widens as intra-abdominal pressure chronically pushes bowel/omentum through it.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Failure of the umbilical ring fascial layers to fuse after cord separation.<br /></br>• Intra-abdominal pressure (crying, coughing, valsalva) pushes peritoneum ± bowel through defect, forming the hernia sac.<br /></br>• Larger defects exert more tension on surrounding fascia, preventing secondary closure and favoring further widening over time.  </p></br><p>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>• No imaging needed in an uncomplicated, easily reducible umbilical hernia.<br /></br>• Elective surgical repair (primary fascial closure) is recommended:<br /></br>  – Persistent defect after 4–5 y,<br /></br>  – Size &gt;1.5 cm,<br /></br>  – Symptoms, incarceration, or cosmetic concerns.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“1–1.5, 3–5, or it won’t survive”:<br /></br>• &lt;1–1.5 cm closes by age 3.<br /></br>• If still open at age 5 or ≥1.5 cm → likely stays open (progresses).  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Incarceration of the right colon – Incarceration in umbilical hernias is extremely uncommon (&lt;2 %). When it occurs, it is usually omentum or small bowel, not specifically the right colon. The vignette also notes a soft, reducible hernia.<br /></br>B. Necrosis of the overlying skin – Skin compromise occurs only with strangulated hernias, which present with severe pain, erythema, and fixed, non-reducible mass—none of which are present.<br /></br>D. Spontaneous closure of the umbilical ring – Likely only in smaller (&lt;1–1.5 cm) defects and in children &lt;3–4 years. A 2-cm defect persisting to age 5 has passed the window for spontaneous closure.<br /></br>E. Volvulus of the small intestine – Volvulus is associated with malrotation or internal hernias (e.g., paraduodenal) rather than simple umbilical hernias.  </p></br><p>Take-home Message<br /></br>A reducible umbilical hernia &gt;1.5 cm persisting beyond early childhood will not close by itself; instead, the defect progressively enlarges, so elective surgical repair is advised.</p>
71. A 26-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 1-month history of low back pain that has acutely worsened during the past 24 hours. He rates his current pain as a 6 on a 10-point scale. He is in the military, and the pain originally began when he injured his back during a deployment to the Middle East. He was prescribed diazepam and hydrocodone-acetaminophen at that time. During the past 24 hours, the patient reports taking 20 combination tablets containing hydrocodone and acetaminophen, 10 diazepam tablets, and 40 acetaminophen tablets, but his back pain has persisted. Additional medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no other medications. The patient appears to be in moderate distress. Vital signs are temperature 37°C (98.6°F), pulse 88/min, respirations 16/min, and blood pressure 126/84 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 100 percent. Examination of the back discloses tenderness and muscle spasm to the left of the lumbar spine. The remainder of the physical examination, including neurologic examination, discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy at this time?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Acetylcysteine</br>b. Flumazenil</br>c. Lipid emulsion</br>d. Naloxone</br>e. Sodium bicarbonate</br>f. No pharmacotherapy is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Acetylcysteine (N-acetylcysteine, NAC)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis – pulling out the clues  </p></br><ol></br><li>Massive recent ingestion:<br /></br>   • 20 hydrocodone-acetaminophen combination tablets<br /></br>   • 40 “plain” acetaminophen tablets<br /></br>   • Total ≈ 19 g of acetaminophen (toxic threshold ≈ 7.5 g in adults).  </li></br><li>Time course: All taken “during the past 24 hours.” Early toxicity (first 24 h) is often asymptomatic or mild (nausea, malaise) → normal vitals &amp; exam do NOT exclude danger.  </li></br><li>No opioid or benzodiazepine toxicity signs:<br /></br>   • RR 16/min, O2 sat 100 %, no miosis → naloxone unnecessary.<br /></br>   • Awake, not obtunded → flumazenil unnecessary (and dangerous).  </li></br><li>Therefore the only life-threatening issue present but still silent is potential hepatic failure from acetaminophen.
