Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Cryptococcal antigen titer</br>b. Toxoplasma antibody titer</br>c. Lumbar puncture</br>d. Stereotactic brain biopsy</br>e. External-beam radiation therapy to the brain</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Stereotactic brain biopsy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Previously healthy 52-year-old with new-onset generalized tonic-clonic seizure → focal structural brain process must be excluded.<br /></br>• Subacute personality change over 3 weeks → frontal/temporal lobe lesion.<br /></br>• CT chest normal → no obvious systemic primary malignancy/metastasis.<br /></br>• Head CT (image not shown to you during the exam) is the central clue; NBME typically shows a solitary enhancing mass with surrounding edema.<br /></br>• No fever, immunosuppression, or HIV risk factors → infectious etiologies such as cryptococcus or toxoplasma are much less likely.<br /></br>Approach framework:<br /></br>1. New focal neurologic event/seizure → neuroimaging first (done).<br /></br>2. Solitary brain lesion in immunocompetent pt → obtain tissue diagnosis before treatment.<br /></br>3. Avoid LP when a mass is present (risk transtentorial herniation).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic to the correct answer:<br /></br>1. CT confirms an intracranial mass.<br /></br>2. Differential = primary brain tumor (e.g., glioblastoma, meningioma), primary CNS lymphoma, abscess, less likely metastasis (normal chest CT, no other cancer hx).<br /></br>3. Management algorithm:<br /></br>   • If lesion is resectable and surgical excision is planned, you proceed directly to craniotomy.<br /></br>   • If lesion is deep, multifocal, or location makes open resection hazardous, first obtain tissue via stereotactic needle biopsy.<br /></br>   • Histology guides definitive therapy (resection, radiation, chemo, steroids).<br /></br>Therefore, stereotactic brain biopsy is the most appropriate next step.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Never treat a solitary brain mass empirically; establish a tissue diagnosis first, unless an obvious, safely resectable mass is being removed entirely.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls: neurologic exam findings, seizure characteristics, imaging results, and informed consent for stereotactic biopsy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Gliomas (especially glioblastoma multiforme) are the most common primary malignant brain tumors in adults; they present with seizures or focal deficits plus personality change if frontal lobe involved.<br /></br>• Primary CNS lymphoma is associated with immunosuppression but can occur sporadically.<br /></br>• Imaging: both can appear as ring-enhancing lesions with vasogenic edema. Only pathology differentiates.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls  </p></br><p>Diagnostic hierarchy for a solitary mass in an immunocompetent adult:<br /></br>1. MRI with gadolinium (better anatomic detail).<br /></br>2. Stereotactic biopsy if lesion not obviously resectable or diagnosis uncertain.<br /></br>3. Avoid lumbar puncture until mass effect is excluded/treated.  </p></br><p>Therapy depends on pathology:<br /></br>• High-grade glioma → maximal safe resection + radiation + temozolomide.<br /></br>• CNS lymphoma → high-dose methotrexate ± radiation.<br /></br>Starting radiation (choice E) before biopsy can obscure histology and is therefore incorrect.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Get the Bx before the Beam”<br /></br>Bx = Biopsy; Beam = Radiation. For a brain mass, always biopsy first unless the surgeon is removing the whole thing.  </p></br><p>“LP LAST when there’s a mass.” Do not lumbar-tap a patient with an intracranial space-occupying lesion.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Cryptococcal antigen titer – Used in HIV/AIDS or transplant patients with meningitis symptoms (fever, headache, ↑ICP). No immunosuppression, no meningeal signs.  </p></br><p>B. Toxoplasma antibody titer – Multiple ring-enhancing lesions in HIV with CD4 &lt; 100. This patient is immunocompetent with (presumably) a single lesion.  </p></br><p>C. Lumbar puncture – Contraindicated when an intracranial mass is present; risk of herniation, and CSF rarely establishes tumor diagnosis.  </p></br><p>E. External-beam radiation therapy – Definitive or adjuvant treatment for certain tumors but should never precede tissue diagnosis (would also confound pathology).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In an immunocompetent adult with a solitary brain lesion, the next step is to secure a tissue diagnosis with stereotactic brain biopsy before instituting therapy.</p>
87. A 67-year-old man comes to the emergency department 1 hour after the onset of vertigo, nausea, and imbalance. He has a 20-year history of poorly controlled hypertension. His pulse is 70/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 210/115 mm Hg. Examination shows a small right pupil, mild right ptosis, and nystagmus. Neurologic examination shows weakness of the right palate. Sensation to pinprick is decreased over the right side of the face and left extremities. There is incoordination on finger-nose testing and heel-knee-shin testing on the right. Which of the following arteries is most likely to be occluded?	Correct answer: L</br></br>a. Anterior spinal</br>b. Basilar</br>c. Left anterior cerebral</br>d. Left internal carotid</br>e. Left middle cerebral</br>f. Left posterior cerebral</br>g. Left vertebral</br>h. Posterior spinal</br>i. Right anterior cerebral</br>j. Right internal carotid</br>k. Right middle cerebral</br>l. Right vertebral</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>l. Right vertebral artery</p></br><p>════════════════════════════════</br>Vignette Analysis (How to spot it fast)</br>• Sudden-onset vertigo, nausea, nystagmus → brain-stem or cerebellar stroke.<br /></br>• Ipsilateral (right-sided) findings<br /></br>  – Small pupil + mild ptosis = partial Horner syndrome (descending sympathetic tract).<br /></br>  – Facial loss of pain / temperature (spinal trigeminal nucleus).<br /></br>  – Palatal weakness / dysphagia (nucleus ambiguus).<br /></br>  – Cerebellar ataxia (inferior cerebellar peduncle).<br /></br>• Contralateral findings<br /></br>  – Loss of pain / temperature in left body (spinothalamic tract).</p></br><p>Pattern = “ipsilateral face + Horner + cerebellar signs with contralateral body sensory loss” → Lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome.</p></br><p>Cognitive roadmap for exams:<br /></br>1. Localize brain-stem vs cortex (look for crossed findings).<br /></br>2. Medulla vs pons vs midbrain (palate/ambiguus → medulla).<br /></br>3. Medial vs lateral (ataxia + Horner + trigeminal face sensory = lateral).<br /></br>4. Feed vessel: PICA or vertebral. When PICA not listed, pick vertebral on the same side.</p></br><p>════════════════════════════════</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>• Lateral medulla is perfused by the posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA), a branch of the vertebral artery.<br /></br>• Proximal vertebral occlusion or the PICA itself gives identical syndrome; answer set only offers “right vertebral,” so that is correct.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome = ipsilateral Horner, cerebellar ataxia, nucleus ambiguus signs, ipsi face &amp; contra body pain/temp loss → occlusion of PICA/vertebral.</p></br><p>Documentation points if real patient: abrupt neuro deficit, exact time-last-known-well, NIH stroke scale, BP, contraindications to thrombolysis, emergent CT/MRI plan.</p></br><p>════════════════════════════════</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>Vertebral → PICA → lateral medulla structures:<br /></br>• Vestibular nuclei → vertigo, nystagmus.<br /></br>• Inferior cerebellar peduncle → ataxia, dysmetria.<br /></br>• Spinal trigeminal nucleus → ipsilateral face pain/temp loss.<br /></br>• Spinothalamic tract → contralateral body pain/temp loss.<br /></br>• Nucleus ambiguus (CN IX, X) → dysphagia, hoarseness, palate weakness.<br /></br>• Descending sympathetic fibers → ipsilateral Horner (ptosis, miosis).</p></br><p>════════════════════════════════</br>Management &amp; Diagnostics</br>• Emergent non-contrast head CT to exclude hemorrhage.<br /></br>• If &lt;4.5 h and no contraindications → IV alteplase (tPA).<br /></br>• Strict BP management, secondary prevention: antiplatelet (aspirin), statin, aggressive HTN control, smoking cessation.<br /></br>• Swallow evaluation to prevent aspiration (nucleus ambiguus involvement).<br /></br>• MRI diffusion-weighted imaging confirms lateral medullary infarct.</p></br><p>════════════════════════════════</br>Memorization Tips</br>“WALLenberg = Wall is Lateral”<br /></br>DON’T PICA HORSE THAT CAN’T EAT:<br /></br>• PICA stroke → Hoarseness &amp; dysphagia (nucleus ambiguus).<br /></br>Horner’s for one-sided small pupil; spinothalamic crossover for body opposite.</p></br><p>════════════════════════════════</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Anterior spinal – Medial medullary syndrome: contralateral hemiparesis + proprioceptive loss + ipsilateral tongue weakness. No Horner or facial sensory loss.<br /></br>b. Basilar – “Locked-in” (quadriplegia, preserved consciousness, vertical eye movement only).<br /></br>c. Left anterior cerebral – Cortical leg-dominant weakness/sensory loss, personality change, urinary incontinence. No crossed signs.<br /></br>d. Left internal carotid – Large hemispheric cortical deficits, possible retinal ischemia.<br /></br>e. Left middle cerebral – Contralateral face/arm weakness, aphasia or hemi-neglect.<br /></br>f. Left posterior cerebral – Contralateral homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing, possible thalamic syndrome.<br /></br>g. Left vertebral – Would produce left-sided Wallenberg features; vignette is right-sided.<br /></br>h. Posterior spinal – Dorsal column loss (vibration/proprioception), not this pattern.<br /></br>i. Right anterior cerebral – Leg-predominant cortical findings, not brain-stem.<br /></br>j. Right internal carotid – Cortical deficits as above.<br /></br>k. Right middle cerebral – Face/arm motor-sensory + aphasia or neglect, not crossed pattern.</p></br><p>════════════════════════════════</br>Key Take-Home</br>Crossed neuro deficits (ipsilateral face/cranial nerve signs + contralateral body signs) localize to brain-stem. Add Horner + dysphagia + ataxia → lateral medulla = vertebral/PICA occlusion.</p>
88. A 62-year-old man comes to the physician for a routine examination. His paternal grandfather received the diagnosis of dementia, Alzheimer type, at the age of 65 years. The patient says he is "not forgetting things" but wonders whether he might have inherited the disorder. He has major depressive disorder treated with citalopram, hypertension treated with hydrochlorothiazide, and hyperlipidemia treated with atorvastatin. He sustained minor injuries in a motor vehicle collision at the age of 11 years but has no other history of trauma. Today, he appears well. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 74/min and regular, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 140/80 mm Hg. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following factors presents the greatest risk for development of dementia in this patient?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Advancing age</br>b. Closed head injury</br>c. Family history of dementia</br>d. Hyperlipidemia</br>e. Hypertension</br>f. Major depressive disorder</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Advancing age  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “62-year-old man … paternal grandfather … Alzheimer type at age 65.” – The stem purposely highlights a family history to lure you toward a genetic answer.<br /></br>• “He is ‘not forgetting things’ ” – No current cognitive decline; question is strictly about future risk.<br /></br>• List of comorbidities (HTN, hyperlipidemia, MDD, remote head injury) matches the answer options; you must rank their relative contributions to dementia risk.<br /></br>• Key task: Identify the single most powerful, non-modifiable risk factor for all-cause dementia (including Alzheimer disease).</p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>Step 1 – Ask, “Is the question about diagnosis, management, or epidemiology?”  → Epidemiology (risk factors).<br /></br>Step 2 – Recall the hierarchy of dementia risk factors.<br /></br>Step 3 – Match the strongest risk factor to the options provided.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Age is by far the largest contributor. Incidence of Alzheimer disease doubles approximately every 5 years after age 65.<br /></br>2. A first-degree relative with late-onset Alzheimer disease (e.g., parent) does increase risk, but the magnitude is less than that conferred by simply getting older.<br /></br>3. Vascular risk factors (HTN, hyperlipidemia) and depression are important and modifiable, yet their effect sizes (&lt;2-fold) are dwarfed by age (&gt;10-fold from age 60 to 85).<br /></br>4. Remote mild traumatic brain injury has only a small association unless repetitive or severe.</p></br><p>Take-home message: In population studies, chronological age overwhelms all other single variables as the dominant risk factor for dementia.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• With advancing age there is cumulative amyloid-β deposition, tau hyper-phosphorylation, mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and microvascular ischemic change—setting the stage for Alzheimer disease and other dementias.<br /></br>• Genetic factors (eg, APOE-ε4) modulate risk but still act on the background of age-related neurobiologic change.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>B. Closed head injury – Moderate/severe or repetitive traumatic brain injury (e.g., professional boxing) increases later dementia risk, but a single minor accident in childhood is a relatively weak predictor compared with age.  </p></br><p>C. Family history of dementia – First-degree relative ≈2- to 3-fold risk. Still lower than the effect of simply living into the 80s.  </p></br><p>D. Hyperlipidemia – Contributes to vascular dementia; risk can be mitigated with statins (he is already on atorvastatin). Effect size modest.  </p></br><p>E. Hypertension – Chronic mid-life HTN is a recognized risk, but control lowers that risk and again the impact is smaller than age.  </p></br><p>F. Major depressive disorder – Late-life depression is associated with increased dementia incidence, yet it is considered a secondary, modifiable factor.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: Over-valuing family history or vascular comorbidities while forgetting that age alone is the strongest predictor.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Father Time is the father of Alzheimer’s.” – Picture an old clock (age) raining β-amyloid plaques onto a brain.<br /></br>Mnemonic: AGE<br /></br>A – Age is<br /></br>G – Greatest<br /></br>E – Etiologic factor for dementia.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>If this were a real patient<br /></br>• Document risk discussion, emphasize modifiable factors (BP, lipids, physical activity, cognitive engagement).<br /></br>• No current indication for genetic testing given late-onset family history and lack of symptoms.</p>
89. A 2-month-old girl is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. She was born at 39 weeks' gestation following an uncomplicated pregnancy and a cesarean delivery for breech presentation. Apgar scores were 9 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes, respectively. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 75/50 mm Hg. Abduction of the left hip is limited. Manipulation of the hip with and without pressure elicits a palpable clunk. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Follow-up examination in 2 weeks</br>b. Follow-up examination after use of triple diapers for 1 month</br>c. X-rays of the hips</br>d. Ultrasonography of the hips</br>e. No management is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Ultrasonography of the hips  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Age: 2-month-old (cartilaginous femoral heads—not yet ossified).<br /></br>• Limited abduction of one hip.<br /></br>• Palpable “clunk” during Barlow/Ortolani maneuvers = positive exam for developmental dysplasia of the hip (DDH).<br /></br>• Normal perinatal course (no birth hypoxia to confound findings).  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: breech presentation (major DDH risk factor), asymmetric abduction, positive Ortolani/Barlow.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no fever, trauma, or systemic illness.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for future questions:<br /></br>1. Identify risk factors (breech, family history, female, first-born).<br /></br>2. Look for physical-exam red flags (clunk, asymm abduction, leg-length discrepancy, asymmetric gluteal folds).<br /></br>3. Decide imaging based on age:<br /></br>   • ≤4-6 mo → hip ultrasonography.<br /></br>   • &gt;4-6 mo → anteroposterior pelvic x-ray (ossific nucleus visible).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why ultrasound?<br /></br>• At 2 months the femoral head/acetabulum are mostly cartilage → not radiopaque.<br /></br>• Ultrasound provides dynamic, radiation-free visualization of cartilaginous structures and hip stability.<br /></br>• Early confirmation guides timely treatment (Pavlik harness).  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In any infant &lt;4–6 months with positive physical findings or risk factors for DDH, order bilateral hip ultrasonography.  </p></br><p>Documentation you would enter:<br /></br>“Left hip with limited abduction and positive Ortolani clunk. Plan: bilateral hip ultrasound to evaluate for DDH; pending results, likely Pavlik harness referral. Educated parents about importance of early treatment.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>DDH = abnormal laxity or shallow acetabulum → femoral head subluxation/dislocation.<br /></br>Etiology: breech positioning (mechanical), hormonal laxity, genetic predisposition.<br /></br>Untreated → premature osteoarthritis, gait abnormalities, limb-length discrepancy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>Imaging as above.  </li></br><li>If confirmed and age ≤6 mo → Pavlik harness (flexion-abduction splint) 6–12 weeks.  </li></br><li>Failure or age 6–18 mo → closed reduction + spica cast.  </li></br><li></br><blockquote></br><p>18 mo or persistent instability → open reduction, osteotomies.  </p></br></blockquote></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Quick Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Ultrasound UNDER 4” (months).<br /></br>“X-ray AFTER ossification” (around 4–6 mo).<br /></br>Risk factors = the 4 F’s: Female, First-born, Footling (breech), Family history.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Follow-up in 2 weeks<br /></br>• Observation alone acceptable only when the physical exam is normal but risk factors present. A positive clunk mandates imaging now.  </p></br><p>B. Triple diapers &amp; follow-up<br /></br>• Old, ineffective practice; does not reliably maintain abduction and delays definitive care.  </p></br><p>C. X-rays of the hips<br /></br>• Before 4–6 months, ossification center not visible → high false-negative rate.  </p></br><p>E. No management<br /></br>• Would miss early treatment window, risking permanent disability.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Concept: Any infant &lt;4–6 months with abnormal hip exam needs an immediate ultrasound to confirm DDH and expedite treatment with a Pavlik harness.</p>
90. A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 20 minutes after collapsing at a local restaurant. He was waiting to get his coat when he passed out. He did not lose bowel or bladder control and was awake and oriented when the paramedics arrived. He has coronary artery disease and sustained a myocardial infarction 3 years ago. His current medications include enalapril, metoprolol, aspirin, and pravastatin. Examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies, including measurement of cardiac enzyme activities, are within normal limits. A CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities. An ECG is shown. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Hyperkalemia</br>b. Premature atrial contractions that are aberrantly conducted</br>c. Premature junctional contractions that are aberrantly conducted</br>d. Subendocardial myocardial infarction</br>e. Ventricular tachycardia</br>f. Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Ventricular tachycardia (VT)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Situation/trigger: Sudden collapse while standing in a public place → classic presentation of syncope from a transient drop in cerebral perfusion.  </li></br><li>Past history: Coronary artery disease (CAD) and prior myocardial infarction (MI) → scarred myocardium is a prime substrate for re-entry VT.  </li></br><li>Rapid recovery: Awake and oriented on paramedic arrival → favors an arrhythmic syncope rather than prolonged seizure, stroke, or major bleed.  </li></br><li>Normal neuroimaging &amp; cardiac enzymes → rules out intracranial catastrophe and acute MI as immediate precipitants.  </li></br><li>ECG provided (not shown to you) is described in the answer key as diagnostic for VT. Hallmarks usually tested:<br /></br>   • Regular, wide QRS complexes (&gt;120 ms)<br /></br>   • AV dissociation, fusion or capture beats, monophasic or bidirectional complexes<br /></br>   • No preceding P waves linked to the QRS complexes  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>When you see (A) a patient with structural heart disease, (B) sudden unexplained syncope, and (C) a wide-complex tachycardia on ECG, think “malignant ventricular arrhythmia” first. On exams, treat any wide-complex tachycardia in a patient with CAD as VT until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step 1: Determine rhythm morphology. Wide QRS &gt;120 ms = ventricular origin or supraventricular with aberrancy.<br /></br>Step 2: Look for VT clues: history of MI, age &gt;60, no clearly discernible P waves, extreme axis, capture beats.<br /></br>Step 3: Syncope + VT → transient drop in cardiac output.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Post-MI ventricular scar creates re-entrant circuits → episodes of sustained or non-sustained VT → syncope or sudden cardiac death.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): description of syncope, vitals, ECG interpretation (“monomorphic VT at 160/min; wide QRS 180 ms”), risk factors, decision for telemetry, potential ICD (implantable cardioverter defibrillator) placement.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Ischemic scar tissue forms lines of conduction block.<br /></br>• Surviving myocytes adjacent to scar create slow-conduction channels → re-entry loop → monomorphic VT.<br /></br>• Output drops because ventricles do not fill properly during rapid rhythm, cerebral perfusion fails → syncope.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Immediate (ED):<br /></br>• If unstable (hypotension, chest pain, altered mental status) → synchronized cardioversion.<br /></br>• If stable → IV amiodarone or lidocaine, prepare for cardioversion.  </p></br><p>Long-term:<br /></br>• ICD placement (class I indication: VT with prior MI).<br /></br>• Beta-blocker and amiodarone for suppression.<br /></br>• Catheter ablation in recurrent cases.</p></br><p>Additional work-up: Echocardiogram for ejection fraction; coronary evaluation if ischemia suspected.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“So-SCAR” for post-MI VT causes<br /></br>S: Scar re-entry<br /></br>o: old MI<br /></br>S: Sudden Syncope<br /></br>C: CAD history<br /></br>A: AV dissociation on ECG<br /></br>R: Regular, wide QRS rhythm  </p></br><p>“Wide + Old MI = Assume VT.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Hyperkalemia<br /></br>   • ECG shows peaked T waves, prolonged PR, widening QRS that progresses to sine-wave—not a regular monomorphic tachycardia. Patient also lacks renal failure or medication trigger.  </p></br><p>b. Premature atrial contractions (PACs) with aberrancy<br /></br>   • PACs cause isolated early beats, often followed by compensatory pause, not a sustained wide-complex tachycardia sufficient to cause syncope.  </p></br><p>c. Premature junctional contractions with aberrancy<br /></br>   • Similar to PACs, isolated ectopic beats; do not produce a regular sustained rhythm.  </p></br><p>d. Subendocardial (NSTEMI) MI<br /></br>   • Troponin “within normal limits.” Even if very early, NSTEMI produces chest pain more than sudden transient syncope; ECG would show ST depressions, not wide tachycardia.  </p></br><p>f. Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome<br /></br>   • Baseline ECG shows short PR and delta wave; tachyarrhythmias are usually orthodromic AVRT (narrow) or antidromic (wide but in younger patients with no CAD). Age 72 with prior MI and no delta wave makes WPW highly unlikely.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only VT explains (1) wide-complex regular tachycardia, (2) CAD scar substrate, (3) abrupt syncope.</p>
91. A full-term 8-hour-old male newborn regurgitates his first feeding. He drools constantly and has not urinated or had a bowel movement. His pulse is 136/min, and respirations are 68/min. There are no intercostal retractions. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial diagnostic procedure?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Air-contrast study of the esophagus</br>b. pH study of the esophagus</br>c. Sweat chloride test</br>d. X-ray of the chest</br>e. Insertion of a radiopaque nasogastric tube</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Insertion of a radiopaque nasogastric tube</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• “Full-term 8-hour-old male newborn … regurgitates his first feeding” + “drools constantly” – Newborn who cannot handle oral secretions should immediately raise suspicion for esophageal obstruction.<br /></br>• “Has not urinated or had a bowel movement” – Nonspecific at 8 h of life but reinforces that nothing is moving distally.<br /></br>• Normal lung exam (no retractions) makes primary pulmonary disease less likely.<br /></br>Key diagnostic pattern: Excessive drooling + immediate regurgitation of first feed in a neonate = think esophageal atresia (EA) ± tracheoesophageal fistula (TEF).</p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Identify red-flag symptom in neonate (copious secretions, choking).<br /></br>2. First, rule out mechanical obstruction at the bedside (simple, non-invasive): try to pass NG/OG tube.<br /></br>3. If tube cannot be advanced ≥10–15 cm, suspect EA/TEF → confirm with radiograph showing coiled tube in proximal pouch.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-by-step logic:</br>1. EA/TEF is the most common congenital GI obstruction presenting immediately after birth.<br /></br>2. The safest, fastest, least invasive way to test for EA is to pass a nasogastric (or orogastric) tube.<br /></br>   • If it stops at ~10 cm (the proximal blind pouch), you have your answer.<br /></br>3. Use a radiopaque tube so that a single chest/abdomen radiograph can simultaneously confirm its position and look for distal bowel gas (indicates a distal TEF).</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In any neonate with excessive drooling or immediate regurgitation, your very first diagnostic maneuver is to attempt to pass a radiopaque NG/OG tube.</p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real patient: inability to advance tube, length at which it stops, radiograph confirming coiled tube, airway status, plan for surgical repair.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Embryology: Failure of the primitive foregut to fully separate into trachea and esophagus → EA +/- fistula.<br /></br>• Most common variant (≈85%): Proximal EA with distal TEF (type C).<br /></br>• Clinical triad: Drooling, choking/cyanosis with feeds, inability to pass NG tube.<br /></br>• Associated anomalies: VACTERL (Vertebral, Anal, Cardiac, TEF, Renal, Limb).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Management</br>Diagnostics:<br /></br>1. Bedside attempt to pass radiopaque NG/OG tube (initial step).<br /></br>2. Chest &amp; abdominal radiograph: coiled tube in upper pouch ± air in stomach/intestines (air = distal fistula).<br /></br>3. Echo, renal US, spine films for associated anomalies once diagnosis made.</p></br><p>Definitive treatment: Prompt surgical repair (primary end-to-end anastomosis ± fistula ligation). Pre-op support: Replogle tube to continuous suction, elevate head, keep NPO, IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics if aspiration suspected.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Drool, Choke, Tube Won’t Go” → EA/TEF.<br /></br>“Pass the Tube Before the Picture”: Always attempt to place a radiopaque tube first; then take the picture to see where it coils.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>A. Air-contrast study of the esophagus – Requires instilling contrast into an atretic esophagus → high aspiration risk; not first-line.<br /></br>B. pH study of the esophagus – Evaluates GERD, irrelevant acutely, time-consuming.<br /></br>C. Sweat chloride test – Screens for cystic fibrosis; CF can cause meconium ileus, not immediate regurgitation/drooling at birth.<br /></br>D. X-ray of the chest (plain) – Could incidentally show the coiled tube only after you’ve placed it. Without the radiopaque tube, sensitivity is low; the essential first action is the bedside tube placement.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>In a neonate with copious secretions and immediate post-feed regurgitation, the very first diagnostic maneuver is to pass a radiopaque nasogastric/orogastric tube to evaluate for esophageal atresia with or without a tracheoesophageal fistula.</p>
92. A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-day history of frequent diarrhea associated with painful straining, abdominal cramps, and intermittent passage of bright red blood. Her symptoms began during a trip to Mexico. She is otherwise in good health and takes no medications. Her temperature is 38.2°C (100.7°F), pulse is 92/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 130/75 mm Hg. There is mild lower abdominal tenderness without peritoneal signs. A scant amount of loose brown stool present in the rectal vault tests positive for occult blood. Which of the following is the most likely causal organism?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Ascaris lumbricoides</br>b. Campylobacter jejuni</br>c. Cryptosporidium parvum</br>d. Giardia lamblia</br>e. Vibrio cholerae</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Campylobacter jejuni  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key words &amp; clues<br /></br>• “Trip to Mexico” – classic setting for a travel-associated enteric infection.<br /></br>• “Frequent diarrhea … abdominal cramps … intermittent passage of bright-red blood” – BLOODY (inflammatory) diarrhea.<br /></br>• Fever 38.2 °C – systemic inflammatory response.<br /></br>• Occult blood positive stool – supports invasive mucosal damage.<br /></br>• No antibiotics or other risk factors for C difficile; no chronic disease.  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: bloody diarrhea, fever, abdominal cramps, recent travel.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no massive watery output (rules out cholera), no greasy/foul stools or malabsorption (rules out Giardia), no prolonged immunocompromise (rules out Cryptosporidium), no eosinophilia/obstruction (rules out Ascaris).  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: Short incubation (2-5 days) after ingesting under-cooked poultry or contaminated water → fever, crampy abdominal pain that can mimic appendicitis, and inflammatory (± bloody) diarrhea. This pattern screams Campylobacter, the most common cause of bacterial dysentery in travelers from the USA to Latin America.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>Step 1 – Classify diarrhea as watery vs inflammatory.<br /></br>Step 2 – Ask “Did the vignette give fever or blood?” If yes → invasive organisms (Campylobacter, Shigella, Salmonella non-typhoidal, enterohemorrhagic E coli, Entamoeba).<br /></br>Step 3 – Use setting &amp; buzzwords (travel + poultry) to choose specific bug.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><ol></br><li>Travel exposure ⇒ possible food-borne pathogens.  </li></br><li>Fever + blood ⇒ invasive organism damaging colonic mucosa.  </li></br><li>Among answer choices, only Campylobacter produces this classic triad in an otherwise healthy traveler.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: Campylobacter jejuni = most common bacterial cause of inflammatory diarrhea in travelers; think “bloody stool + fever + crampy belly after eating poultry or unpasteurized milk.”  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real life): duration/onset, stool frequency/character, fever, travel/food history, hydration status, abdominal exam, stool cultures if severe/prolonged, consider empiric azithromycin if high-risk.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Gram-negative, curved, motile rod; microaerophilic.<br /></br>• Reservoir: poultry, cattle; transmission via under-cooked chicken, unpasteurized milk, contaminated water.<br /></br>• Invades small &amp; large bowel mucosa → inflammatory infiltrate, crypt abscesses, ulceration → blood &amp; leukocytes in stool.<br /></br>• Complications: Guillain-Barré syndrome (molecular mimicry), reactive arthritis, HUS (rare).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>Diagnostics: Stool culture on selective Campy agar at 42 °C (likes the “hot camp fire”). Fecal leukocytes/lactoferrin often positive.  </p></br><p>Treatment: Usually self-limited (≈5–7 days).<br /></br>Indications for antibiotics (azithromycin 3 days or fluoroquinolone where resistance low):<br /></br>• Severe disease (high fever, dysentery, sepsis)<br /></br>• Immunocompromised, pregnancy, worsening symptoms.<br /></br>Supportive care: oral rehydration, anti-motility agents avoided if dysentery.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Camping trip with under-cooked chicken around a 42 °C campfire gives you Camp-ylo-bloody diarrhea.”<br /></br>• 42 °C = growth temperature.<br /></br>• Chicken = main source.<br /></br>• Camp-ylo “camp” = traveler.<br /></br>Another: “Bloody C.A.M.P.” →<br /></br>C = Cramps<br /></br>A = Abdominal pain<br /></br>M = Mexico travel<br /></br>P = Poultry source / Positive blood in stool  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Ascaris lumbricoides – Intestinal roundworm; causes eosinophilia, pulmonary (Löffler) phase, possible obstruction, NOT acute febrile bloody diarrhea.  </p></br><p>C. Cryptosporidium parvum – Watery, non-bloody diarrhea, especially in HIV/AIDS; acid-fast oocysts in stool; incubation longer, no high fever or blood.  </p></br><p>D. Giardia lamblia – Foul-smelling, greasy steatorrhea, bloating; afebrile; source is mountain streams/day-care, not typically bloody.  </p></br><p>E. Vibrio cholerae – Profuse rice-water watery diarrhea without blood or leukocytes; usually no abdominal pain or fever.  </p></br><p>Key difference: Only Campylobacter among these choices produces acute febrile BLOODY diarrhea following travel.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>End-of-Question Pearls  </p></br><ol></br><li>Inflammatory (bloody) traveler’s diarrhea = Campylobacter until proven otherwise.  </li></br><li>Short incubation (2–5 days) vs longer (Giardia ≥1 week).  </li></br><li>Remember post-infectious sequelae: Guillain-Barré &amp; reactive arthritis.</li></br></ol>
93. An 80-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of increasing somnolence over the past 2 days. Her pulse is 90/min while supine and 110/min while standing, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg while supine and 100/80 mm Hg while standing. Examination shows no abnormalities except for dry skin and mucous membranes.</br>Laboratory studies show:</br>Serum</br>Na+               137 mEq/L</br>Cl-               100 mEq/L</br>K+                4.3 mEq/L</br>HCO3-             19 mEq/L</br>Urea nitrogen     30 mg/dL</br>Glucose           900 mg/dL</br>Creatinine        1.6 mg/dL</br>Arterial pH       7.38</br>Which of the following is the most appropriate initial therapy?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Intravenous bicarbonate</br>b. Intravenous insulin</br>c. Intravenous potassium</br>d. Intravenous 0.9 percent saline</br>e. Oral hypoglycemic agent</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Intravenous 0.9 percent saline (normal saline)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Elderly (80 y/o) with 2-day progressive somnolence<br /></br>   • Marked orthostatic tachycardia/hypotension → intravascular volume depletion<br /></br>   • Very high serum glucose (900 mg/dL) with only mild ↓HCO₃⁻ (19 mEq/L) and near-normal pH (7.38) → severe hyperglycemia without significant ketoacidosis = Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS).<br /></br>   • Dry skin/mucous membranes, BUN/Cr both slightly ↑ → prerenal azotemia from dehydration.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Corrected Na⁺ (exam favorite):<br /></br>   Na⁺(corr) = Na⁺(meas) + 1.6[(glucose-100)/100]<br /></br>   = 137 + 1.6(8) ≈ 150 mEq/L → actually hypernatremic once glucose effect removed, underscoring profound free-water loss.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern: Elderly T2DM + very high glucose + neurologic change + severe dehydration − acidosis/ketones → think “HHS → Start with FLUIDS.”</p></br><p>Metacognitive framework for similar questions:<br /></br>• Ask: “What kills first?” In HHS/DKA it is volume depletion and shock, not the hyperglycemia itself.<br /></br>• Sequence to remember: F-I-K = Fluids → Insulin → K⁺ replacement.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning &amp; Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>First priority in HHS is rapid restoration of intravascular volume with isotonic crystalloid. This improves perfusion, lowers serum osmolality, and reduces counter-regulatory hormones; only after adequate fluids is insulin started. Skipping this step risks vascular collapse when insulin drives glucose (and water) into cells.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): mental status, orthostatic vitals, estimated fluid deficit, IV fluid type/rate ordered, monitoring plan (hourly vitals, chem panel, finger-stick glucose).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>• HHS = relative insulin deficiency → severe hyperglycemia → osmotic diuresis → &gt;8–10 L water loss → hyperosmolarity (&gt;320 mOsm/kg) → mental status changes.<br /></br>• Unlike DKA, there is still enough insulin to inhibit lipolysis → minimal ketoacid production, so pH usually &gt;7.30 and HCO₃⁻ &gt;18 mEq/L.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment Algorithm (Step 2 style)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Fluids<br /></br>   • 0.9 % saline 1–2 L in first hour, then 250–500 mL/hr; switch to 0.45 % if Na⁺ becomes high.  </li></br><li>Insulin drip (0.1 U/kg/hr) after initial fluid bolus and once K⁺ ≥3.3.  </li></br><li>Potassium replacement: begin when K⁺ falls to &lt;5.0 because insulin will shift K⁺ intracellularly.  </li></br><li>Treat precipitating factor (infection, MI, etc.).  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“HI-HOS!”<br /></br>H – Hydration first (normal saline)<br /></br>I – Insulin after fluids<br /></br>H – Hypo/Hyper-kalemia watch (replace K⁺)<br /></br>O – Osmolality drives neuro sx<br /></br>S – Search for Source of stress (infection, MI)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Intravenous bicarbonate<br /></br>   • Indicated only if pH &lt;6.9. Her pH is normal (7.38). Giving bicarb can worsen hypokalemia and delay recovery.</p></br><p>b. Intravenous insulin<br /></br>   • Needed, but not first. Insulin before volume repletion can precipitate vascular collapse and severe hypotension by pulling water into cells and dropping osmolality too fast.</p></br><p>c. Intravenous potassium<br /></br>   • Serum K⁺ is 4.3 (normal). Replacement is required after insulin started or if K⁺ &lt;3.3. Giving it now delays essential volume resuscitation.</p></br><p>e. Oral hypoglycemic agent<br /></br>   • Contraindicated in any acute decompensation; onset is slow and patient is obtunded.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In HHS (and DKA), stabilize circulation first with aggressive isotonic saline—everything else comes after.</p>
94. A 19-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by friends because of increasing agitation and confusion during the past 2 hours. On arrival, he is mute and unresponsive to questions. His friends say that he drinks alcohol heavily and uses various illicit drugs on the weekends. His temperature is 39.6°C (103.2°F), pulse is 130/min, and blood pressure is 144/92 mm Hg. He is diaphoretic and grinding his teeth. The pupils are 4 mm. Neurologic examination shows tremulousness and poor coordination of the upper and lower extremities. On mental status examination, he exhibits psychomotor agitation and glances about the room in a confused and anxious manner. The most likely diagnosis is intoxication with which of the following substances?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Alcohol</br>b. Alprazolam</br>c. Cannabis</br>d. Ecstasy (3,4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine)</br>e. Heroin</br>f. Nitrous oxide</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Ecstasy (3,4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine, MDMA)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>Key phrases that rule‐in MDMA intoxication<br /></br>• Young party-age male with “various illicit drugs on the weekends.” ⟶ Typical MDMA demographic (club/rave scene).<br /></br>• Hyperthermia 39.6 °C, tachycardia, mild hypertension, diaphoresis ⟶ Powerful serotonergic/adrenergic stimulation seen with MDMA and other amphetamines.<br /></br>• Bruxism (“grinding his teeth”) ⟶ Classic buzz-word for MDMA.<br /></br>• Psychomotor agitation, anxiety, visual scanning of the room (“glances about”) ⟶ Stimulant + hallucinogen properties of MDMA.<br /></br>• Tremor, ataxia ⟶ Serotonergic excess (serotonin-syndrome-like neuromuscular findings).</p></br><p>Pertinent negatives that make other options unlikely: no respiratory depression (heroin), no sedation (alprazolam, alcohol), no red conjunctiva or increased appetite (cannabis), no major anesthetic euphoria/dissociation (nitrous oxide).</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>When you see the triad “party drug + hyperthermia + bruxism” think MDMA first. Build your differential around autonomic findings (temp, pulse, BP) and pupil size (often mid-dilated but not pinpoint).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify autonomic hyperactivity (T &gt; 40 °C, HR &gt; 120).<br /></br>2. Look for stimulant vs depressant signs: agitation &amp; sweating → stimulant.<br /></br>3. Add unique clue (bruxism) → MDMA.<br /></br>Take-home message: MDMA behaves like “serotonin-amped amphetamine.” Its red-flag toxicity is life-threatening hyperthermia and hyponatremia.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): drug history, vital-sign trends, neuro exam, core temp q15 min, labs for sodium/CK, initiate external cooling, consider benzodiazepines and cyproheptadine if serotonin syndrome suspected.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>MDMA increases synaptic serotonin massively (also dopamine &amp; norepinephrine) by reversing reuptake transporters.<br /></br>• Serotonin excess → hyperthermia, tremor, mental status changes.<br /></br>• Catecholamine surge → tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis.<br /></br>• Increased ADH secretion + polydipsia → risk of severe hyponatremia, seizures.<br /></br>• Bruxism mechanism: dopaminergic overstimulation of jaw muscles.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management Snapshot<br /></br>1. Rapid external cooling (ice packs, mist-fan).<br /></br>2. IV crystalloids; monitor sodium, treat hyponatremia cautiously with hypertonic saline if symptomatic.<br /></br>3. Benzodiazepines for agitation; consider cyproheptadine for serotonin-syndrome picture.<br /></br>4. Admit &amp; trend CK (rhabdomyolysis risk) and renal function.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “E-xtreme Temp, E-mpathy, E-rratic jaw”: Ecstasy.<br /></br>• Mouthguard at a rave? → MDMA bruxism.<br /></br>• MDMA = “M” for Mid-dilated pupils + Muscle clench.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Alcohol: Intoxication causes CNS depression, nystagmus, hypothermia, slurred speech—not hyperthermia or marked autonomic activation.<br /></br>b. Alprazolam (benzodiazepine): Produces sedation, ataxia, and respiratory depression; vitals usually normal or low.<br /></br>c. Cannabis: Mild tachycardia, conjunctival injection, increased appetite, dry mouth; no severe hyperthermia, diaphoresis, or bruxism.<br /></br>e. Heroin (opioid): Pinpoint pupils, bradypnea, hypotension, sedation. This patient is agitated with mid-sized pupils.<br /></br>f. Nitrous oxide: Brief euphoria, dizziness, gait disturbance; vitals largely unchanged, no hyperthermia or significant autonomic storm.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Hyperthermia + tachycardia + diaphoresis + bruxism in a young partygoer = MDMA intoxication. Recognize early; treat aggressively to prevent serotonin-syndrome–like complications and hyponatremic seizures.</p>
95. A 57-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 3-week history of constant, severe, nonradiating, midthoracic back pain. He has not had recent trauma to the area. Six weeks ago, he had an episode of Staphylococcus aureus sepsis that resolved with antibiotic therapy. He has end-stage renal disease secondary to hypertension treated with minoxidil and metoprolol; he also receives dialysis by way of tunnel dialysis catheter. His temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), and blood pressure is 140/88 mm Hg. Examination shows tenderness to palpation over T7 and T8. Laboratory studies show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 50 mm/h. A T1-weighted MRI of the spine shows decreased signal intensity in the vertebral bodies of T7 and T8 and no abnormalities in the epidural space. In addition to changing the patient's hemodialysis catheter, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. DEXA scan</br>b. Intravenous antibiotic therapy</br>c. Oral benzodiazepine therapy</br>d. Oral nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug therapy</br>e. Physical therapy</br>f. Spinal traction</br>g. Surgical intervention</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (per key):</br>B. Intravenous antibiotic therapy</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key positives<br /></br>  – 3-week course of constant, focal, non-radiating midthoracic pain → suggests structural/infectious process rather than mechanical muscle strain.<br /></br>  – Prior episode of Staphylococcus aureus sepsis 6 weeks ago + indwelling tunneled dialysis catheter (bacteremia risk).<br /></br>  – Low-grade fever (100 °F) and markedly elevated ESR (50 mm/h) → inflammatory / infectious etiology.<br /></br>  – MRI T1: ↓ signal intensity in T7–T8 vertebral bodies, no epidural collection → classic for vertebral osteomyelitis (spondylitis) without epidural abscess or cord compression.<br /></br>  – Point tenderness over same levels supports infection within the vertebral bodies themselves.</p></br><p>• Pertinent negatives<br /></br>  – No neurologic deficits; no epidural mass seen → immediate surgery not required.<br /></br>  – No mention of osteoporosis risk factors warranting DEXA; pain is constant (not mechanical) → PT/NSAIDs traction inappropriate.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see focal spinal pain + bacteremia risk factor + ↑ESR, jump to “vertebral osteomyelitis.” Next step after establishing diagnosis on MRI is IV antibiotics for at least 6 weeks, adjusted to cultures. Surgery only if neuro compromise or failed medical therapy.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise approach:</br>1. Identify source: dialysis catheter with recent S. aureus bacteremia.<br /></br>2. Confirm infection: MRI already done → vertebral infection established.<br /></br>3. Manage source: question stem already plans catheter change.<br /></br>4. Eradicate infection: prolonged IV antibiotics that cover MSSA/MRSA until sensitivities known (e.g., vancomycin ± ceftriaxone/nafcillin).<br /></br>5. Monitor ESR/CRP and clinical status; repeat imaging if symptoms persist or worsen.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Vertebral osteomyelitis almost always demands weeks of parenteral antibiotics once imaging proves infection and there are no surgical indications.</p></br><p>Documentation in real life would include: neuro exam, blood cultures, targeted antibiotic regimen, plan for 6-week course with ESR/CRP trend, catheter exchange.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Hematogenous seeding of vertebral end arteries → infection begins in vertebral body, often spreads to adjacent disk (spondylodiscitis).<br /></br>• Most common bug overall and especially in dialysis patients: Staphylococcus aureus.<br /></br>• Elevated ESR/CRP nearly universal; WBC can be normal.<br /></br>• MRI (T1 ↓ signal, T2 ↑ signal, enhancement post-contrast) is gold standard.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</br>1. Obtain two sets of blood cultures before first antibiotic dose if not already done.<br /></br>2. Empiric IV therapy: vancomycin (covers MRSA) + third- or fourth-generation cephalosporin if gram-negatives a concern; narrow once sensitivities return.<br /></br>3. Duration: typically 6–8 weeks IV; may transition to high-bioavailability oral in select patients after improvement.<br /></br>4. Surgical consult only if: neurologic deficits, spinal instability, epidural abscess, large paravertebral abscess, deformity, failure of medical therapy.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“BAD VERTEBRAE” mnemonic for vertebral osteomyelitis red flags:</br>• Bacteremia history (IV lines, endocarditis)<br /></br>• Age &gt;50<br /></br>• Diabetes/dialysis<br /></br>• Vertebral tenderness focal<br /></br>• ESR ↑<br /></br>• Recent infection (skin, UTI)<br /></br>• T1 low signal on MRI<br /></br>• Epidural abscess risk<br /></br>• Back pain constant<br /></br>• Evaluate with MRI</p></br><p>Treatment memory aid: “6 weeks of Vanc keeps the spine intact.”</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. DEXA scan – Screens for osteoporosis; would not explain fever, ↑ESR, MRI findings.<br /></br>c. Oral benzodiazepine therapy – For muscle spasm or anxiety, not infectious bone disease.<br /></br>d. Oral NSAID therapy – Symptomatic only; infection would progress.<br /></br>e. Physical therapy – Could worsen pain, delays definitive care; PT is for mechanical back pain after infection resolved.<br /></br>f. Spinal traction – Used for radiculopathy due to disc disease, not osteomyelitis.<br /></br>g. Surgical intervention – Indicated only with neurologic deficits, spinal instability, or abscess compressing cord; none present.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>Focal back pain plus bacteremia risk and MRI evidence of vertebral body signal change = vertebral osteomyelitis. The cornerstone of therapy is prolonged intravenous antibiotics (usually ≥6 weeks) after removing/ exchanging any infected hardware or catheters.</p>
96. An 18-month-old girl is brought to the physician because she has not used her right arm since falling while running hand-in-hand with her older sister 2 hours ago. She holds her right arm at her side with the forearm pronated; she is unable to fully supinate or flex her forearm. There is no obvious swelling or point tenderness. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Rest, ice, and elevation of the elbow  </br>b. Supination of the forearm  </br>c. Use of a sling and swathe  </br>d. Application of a figure-of-8 strap  </br>e. Application of a posterior splint</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Supination of the forearm  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases &amp; clues<br /></br>• 18-month-old (classic age 1–4 yr)<br /></br>• Pulled while “running hand-in-hand” → axial traction on the arm<br /></br>• Holds arm slightly flexed, forearm pronated, won’t use it<br /></br>• No swelling, deformity, or point tenderness  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: traction mechanism, pronated “guarding” position, toddler.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no bony tenderness, no edema, no bruising → makes fracture or true elbow dislocation unlikely.  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: This is the textbook presentation of radial head subluxation (“nursemaid’s elbow”).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Ask “How old?” – toddlers → think nursemaid’s elbow.<br /></br>2. Ask “Mechanism?” – sudden pull.<br /></br>3. Look at the arm position – pronated, slightly flexed, hanging.<br /></br>4. Absence of swelling/pain on palpation → favors ligamentous rather than bony injury.<br /></br>Once these three boxes are checked, go straight to reduction maneuver; imaging is unnecessary unless reduction fails or signs of fracture appear.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Core concept: Subluxation of the radial head from under the annular ligament is reduced in the office by either<br /></br>• Supination of the forearm while flexing the elbow (classically tested), or<br /></br>• Hyper-pronation maneuver (in practice, equal or higher success).  </p></br><p>Why immediate reduction? Restoring alignment of the annular ligament around the radial head instantly relieves pain; most children resume use within minutes, confirming the diagnosis.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real world): mechanism, exam (no swelling/tenderness, neurovascular intact), reduction technique used, audible/ palpable click, immediate return of function, parental counseling on avoidance of traction.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Toddler + pulled arm + pronated guarding position → do a rapid bedside reduction (supinate ± flex).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Axial traction on an extended, pronated forearm stretches/tears the annular ligament.<br /></br>• Radial head slips distally → trapped outside the ligament → pain with supination/flexion.<br /></br>• No intra-articular effusion; periosteum and bone remain intact, so no visible swelling.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: walking while hand-held, caregivers lifting child by the wrist/forearm.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>Clinical dx. X-ray only if atypical (swelling, ecchymosis, inability to palpate radial pulse).  </li></br><li>Bedside reduction:<br /></br>   a. Supination-flexion (answer choice B): support elbow, pressure on radial head, supinate then flex.<br /></br>   b. Hyper-pronation alternative.  </li></br><li>Reassess: child usually reaches for toys within minutes.  </li></br><li>Educate caregivers (lift child under the arms, not by hands).  </li></br></ol></br><p>No splints, slings, figure-of-8 straps, or RICE needed after successful reduction.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“SUbluxation → SUpinate.”<br /></br>Picture a nursemaid turning a doorknob (supination) to let the child back in.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Rest, ice, elevation of the elbow<br /></br>• Appropriate for soft-tissue contusion or minor sprain. Would not immediately restore motion; child would remain unwilling to supinate.  </p></br><p>C. Sling and swathe<br /></br>• Used to immobilize shoulder/elbow after clavicle, proximal humerus, or scapular fractures; fractures show focal tenderness/swelling which are absent here.  </p></br><p>D. Figure-of-8 strap<br /></br>• Specifically for clavicle fractures (keeps shoulders retracted). Clavicle injury presents with crepitus, palpable step-off, localized pain.  </p></br><p>E. Posterior splint<br /></br>• Indicated for supracondylar humerus fracture, elbow dislocation, or severe sprain—conditions that produce marked swelling, deformity, and often neurovascular concerns.  </p></br><p>Each incorrect option involves immobilization rather than immediate reduction, and none addresses the underlying annular-ligament entrapment present in nursemaid’s elbow.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Toddler pulled by the arm + pronated, motionless forearm = radial head subluxation. Immediate, office-based supination (or hyper-pronation) reduces the injury and restores normal use within minutes.</p>
97. A 42-year-old man comes for a routine health maintenance examination. There is no family history of coronary artery disease, and he does not smoke. His weight is appropriate for his height. His blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Serum lipid studies show a total cholesterol concentration of 190 mg/dL, HDL-cholesterol concentration of 40 mg/dL, and triglyceride concentration of 150 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Recommend the Step 2 National Cholesterol Education Program diet</br>b. Measure serum LDL-cholesterol concentration now</br>c. Measure total serum cholesterol concentration in 5 years</br>d. Prescribe prophylactic aspirin</br>e. Begin treatment with lovastatin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Measure total serum cholesterol concentration in 5 years  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key positives: 42 y o man, normotensive (120/80), nonsmoker, normal weight, no family history of premature CAD.<br /></br>• Lipid panel: Total-C 190 mg/dL (desirable &lt; 200), HDL 40 mg/dL (borderline but still ≥40), TG 150 mg/dL (normal &lt; 150–199).<br /></br>• No additional major ASCVD (atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease) risk factors are present.  </p></br><p>Framework to approach similar questions:<br /></br>1. Identify ASCVD risk factors (age, sex, diabetes, HTN, smoking, family history, LDL level).<br /></br>2. Stratify patient as high, moderate, or low risk.<br /></br>3. Apply population-based screening interval guidelines (USPSTF/NCEP-ATP III/ACC-AHA).  </p></br><p>Here, the patient is LOW risk → screen every 5 years starting at age 20.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Does he already meet treatment or diet-modification thresholds? No—his total cholesterol &lt;200 mg/dL and LDL is presumed “optimal” (&lt;100) because total-C is 190, HDL 40.<br /></br>2. Does he have any risk factors that shorten the screening interval (e.g., diabetes, HTN, smoking, family history)? No.<br /></br>3. Therefore the appropriate evidence-based action is routine re-screening in 5 years.<br /></br>Take-home message: Low-risk adults with a normal lipid profile are re-evaluated every 5 years.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Low ASCVD risk; fasting lipid panel repeated in 5 years per USPSTF guidelines.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Atherogenesis is driven by apo-B–containing lipoproteins (mainly LDL).<br /></br>• LDL level is the primary target because it is most strongly correlated with plaque burden.<br /></br>• HDL ≥40 mg/dL is considered acceptable for men; &lt;40 is an independent risk factor.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>Screening algorithm (USPSTF / ACC-AHA):<br /></br>• Age 20–45 men (20–55 women) WITHOUT risk factors → lipid panel every 5 years.<br /></br>• If total-C &gt;200 mg/dL or HDL &lt;40 mg/dL → obtain full fasting lipoprotein profile (LDL).<br /></br>• If risk factors present → consider earlier, more frequent screening and possible statin.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“20-25-5 rule”:<br /></br>• Start at 20 y o, reassess every 5 years, unless age 25 + risk factors → check sooner.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Recommend Step 2 NCEP diet<br /></br>   • Indicated if LDL ≥130 mg/dL or total-C ≥200 mg/dL, or if moderate-high ASCVD risk. Not needed here.  </p></br><p>B. Measure LDL-cholesterol now<br /></br>   • Guidelines permit skipping the fasting LDL if total-C &lt;200 &amp; HDL ≥40 in low-risk patients. Adds cost without altering management.  </p></br><p>D. Prescribe prophylactic aspirin<br /></br>   • USPSTF endorses aspirin for MEN 45–79 when MI benefit &gt; GI bleed risk. At age 42 with low ASCVD risk, benefit does not yet outweigh harm.  </p></br><p>E. Begin treatment with lovastatin (a statin)<br /></br>   • Statin therapy is for patients with clinical ASCVD, LDL ≥190, diabetes (age 40-75 with LDL 70-189), or calculated 10-year ASCVD risk ≥7.5%. None apply.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A healthy, low-risk 42-year-old man with a desirable total cholesterol needs no immediate intervention—just repeat lipid screening in 5 years.</p>
98. A 47-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 20 minutes after being involved in a head-on motor vehicle collision. He was an unrestrained passenger of a car traveling at 30 mph. At the scene, he was conscious and followed commands. His pulse was 110/min, and blood pressure was 90/55 mm Hg. En route to the hospital, he was resuscitated with 1 L of crystalloid solution. On arrival, he is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. He has shortness of breath and chest pain. A nasogastric tube is inserted. His blood pressure is 130/70 mm Hg. Examination shows abrasions over the left anterior hemithorax. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Heart sounds are normal. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. A portable x-ray of the chest shows fluid at the base of the left lung field and a fluid density at the left pleural apex. The nasogastric tube is deviated to the right in the upper mediastinal region. His blood pressure suddenly decreases to 80/40 mm Hg. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's hypotension?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Aortic transection</br>b. Cardiac contusion</br>c. Liver laceration</br>d. Pericardial tamponade</br>e. Spinal shock</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Aortic transection  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should jump off the page:<br /></br>• High-energy, head-on MVC with rapid deceleration → classic mechanism for blunt aortic injury.<br /></br>• Initially stable but now acutely hypotensive despite adequate crystalloid → suggests ongoing intrathoracic hemorrhage.<br /></br>• CXR findings:<br /></br>  – “Fluid at the base of the left lung field” = left-sided hemothorax.<br /></br>  – “Fluid density at the left pleural apex” (so-called apical cap) = blood layering over the lung apex, highly suggestive of aortic rupture.<br /></br>  – Nasogastric tube deviated to the right in the upper mediastinum = mediastinal shift from a large left-sided hematoma, another radiographic sign of aortic transection.<br /></br>• Lungs are clear to auscultation and heart sounds normal → argues against pulmonary contusion, pneumothorax, or tamponade.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Identify the mechanism (high-speed deceleration).<br /></br>2. Look for “red-flag” CXR signs (widened mediastinum, apical cap, NG/tracheal deviation, left hemothorax).<br /></br>3. Correlate with sudden hemodynamic collapse → think major vascular injury first.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why answer A is correct)  </p></br><p>Blunt injury to the descending thoracic aorta at the isthmus can partially tear the aortic wall. A contained hematoma may keep the patient temporarily stable; expansion or free rupture into the left pleural space causes rapid hypotension and shock.<br /></br>• The constellation of left hemothorax + apical cap + mediastinal deviation is pathognomonic for traumatic aortic transection.<br /></br>• Hypotension results from massive intrathoracic blood loss, not from pump failure or neurogenic mechanisms.<br /></br>Take-home: In any high-speed deceleration crash, if you see unilateral (usually left) hemothorax plus mediastinal abnormalities on CXR, assume aortic injury until proven otherwise and proceed to immediate vascular control (e.g., thoracotomy or endovascular repair) once patient is stabilized.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient):<br /></br>– Mechanism of injury, initial vitals, resuscitation fluids given.<br /></br>– CXR interpretation: mediastinal width, apical cap, NG tube deviation, hemothorax.<br /></br>– Hemodynamic trend (initially 90/55 → after fluids 130/70 → sudden 80/40).<br /></br>– Plan for emergent thoracic surgical / endovascular intervention.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Sudden deceleration → shearing at the ligamentum arteriosum fixes the aortic arch while the descending aorta remains mobile → tear at the aortic isthmus.<br /></br>• Three outcomes: complete transection (instant death), partial tear with pseudoaneurysm, or contained hematoma that may rupture minutes to hours later (as in this vignette).<br /></br>• Blood accumulates preferentially in the left pleural space producing hemothorax and mediastinal widening.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot  </p></br><ol></br><li>High clinical suspicion → maintain SBP 100–120 mm Hg (esmolol ± nicardipine) to limit shear stress.  </li></br><li>Rapid imaging: CT angiography of chest if stable; if unstable with classic signs, go straight to OR.  </li></br><li>Definitive repair: endovascular stent-graft (TEVAR) or open repair via left thoracotomy.  </li></br><li>Simultaneously drain left hemothorax with chest tube to improve ventilation and guide ongoing blood loss.  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “BAD CAP” for blunt Aortic Disruption CXR signs:<br /></br>B – Blood (left hemothorax)<br /></br>A – Apical Cap<br /></br>D – Deviation of trachea/NG tube to right<br /></br>C – Contour of aorta blurred (mediastinal widening)<br /></br>A – Above clavicle pleural stripe (apical cap again)<br /></br>P – Pleural effusion on left  </p></br><p>Visualize: Picture a “cap” of blood sitting on the left lung apex that pushes everything to the right—think of it as an angry red beret signaling aortic rupture.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Cardiac contusion<br /></br>• Would present with arrhythmias, chest pain, possibly pump failure; CXR typically normal or shows rib/ sternal fractures, not unilateral hemothorax with apical cap.<br /></br>• Hypotension from contusion is due to decreased contractility, not massive blood loss.  </p></br><p>C. Liver laceration<br /></br>• Causes intraperitoneal hemorrhage—patient would have abdominal tenderness, distension, FAST positive, not left-sided pleural effusion or mediastinal deviation.  </p></br><p>D. Pericardial tamponade<br /></br>• Beck’s triad: hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds. Here heart sounds are normal and neck veins are not described as distended; CXR would show an enlarged, “water-bottle” cardiac silhouette, not apical cap/left hemothorax.  </p></br><p>E. Spinal shock<br /></br>• Causes hypotension with BRADYCARDIA from sympathetic loss; this patient is TACHYCARDIC (110/min). No neurologic deficits are described.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Message  </p></br><p>In high-speed deceleration trauma, sudden hypotension plus CXR showing left hemothorax, apical cap, and mediastinal deviation should immediately trigger concern for an aortic transection—an immediately life-threatening injury requiring rapid vascular control.</p>
99. An 18-year-old woman comes to the physician because of intermittent vaginal bleeding and abdominal pain over the past 4 days. Menses occur at irregular 28- to 40-day intervals. She cannot remember the date of her last menstrual period. She is sexually active with one partner, and they use condoms for contraception. Pelvic examination shows normal external genitalia and a normal-appearing cervix. There is blood coming from the cervical os. The uterus is slightly enlarged. Serum 3-hCG concentration is 7000 mIU/mL. Pelvic ultrasonography shows a small amount of fluid in the uterus only but no evidence of a gestational sac. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Ectopic pregnancy</br>b. Endometrial polyp</br>c. Endometritis</br>d. Granulosa cell tumor</br>e. Hydatidiform mole</br>f. Leiomyomata uteri</br>g. Normal pregnancy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Ectopic pregnancy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 18-year-old with vaginal bleeding + lower abdominal pain → classic presentation of early pregnancy complications.<br /></br>  – Serum β-hCG = 7,000 mIU/mL.<br /></br>  – Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) shows NO intra-uterine gestational sac; only a small amount of fluid.<br /></br>  – Uterus “slightly enlarged” (consistent with hormonal stimulation but not a visible intra-uterine pregnancy).<br /></br>• Discriminatory zone concept<br /></br>  – With modern high-resolution TVUS, an intra-uterine gestational sac should be visualized when β-hCG ≥1500–2000 mIU/mL.<br /></br>  – β-hCG 7,000 far exceeds this threshold yet the uterus is empty → pregnancy is almost certainly extra-uterine → ectopic until proven otherwise.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives<br /></br>  – No uterine mass or heterogeneous echo pattern to suggest mole.<br /></br>  – No history suggesting PID or endometritis.<br /></br>  – No heavy menses or infertility issues suggesting fibroids or polyp.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive takeaway: On exam day, the “empty uterus + β-hCG above discriminatory zone” pattern should immediately trigger the diagnosis of ectopic pregnancy and the next steps of management (repeat β-hCG/TVUS in 48 h vs methotrexate vs surgical approach).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Confirm pregnancy (β-hCG positive).<br /></br>2. Compare β-hCG to discriminatory zone.<br /></br>3. Look for intra-uterine gestational sac on TVUS.<br /></br>4. If β-hCG ≥1500–2000 and no sac → ectopic pregnancy likely (&gt;95% PPV).<br /></br>Core concept: The discriminatory zone is a quick, single-best test for ruling in/ruling out ectopic pregnancy in early pregnancy bleeding.  </p></br><p>Documentation you’d need: β-hCG value, TVUS findings, hemodynamic status, Rh status, and counseling regarding ectopic pregnancy management.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Most ectopic pregnancies (≈95%) implant in the ampulla of the fallopian tube.<br /></br>• Risk factors: previous PID, tubal surgery, smoking, DES exposure, but ~50% have no identifiable risk factor (as in this vignette).<br /></br>• Embryo cannot develop normally → trophoblastic invasion causes localized pain, bleeding, possible rupture → life-threatening hemorrhage.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>Initial:<br /></br>  – Hemodynamic stabilization if unstable.<br /></br>  – Quantitative β-hCG repeat in 48 h (in ectopic it rises slower than the expected ≥53% every 48 h).<br /></br>  – Decide between methotrexate vs laparoscopic salpingostomy/salpingectomy based on size, rupture, β-hCG level, and contraindications.<br /></br>Always give Rho(D) immune globulin if patient is Rh-negative.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Tips<br /></br>“High β, no sac? Think tube attack!” – If β-hCG is High and there’s No Sack, suspect an ectopic in the tube.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>B. Endometrial polyp – Causes painless intermenstrual bleeding; ultrasound would show an intra-uterine focal mass, and β-hCG would be negative.<br /></br>C. Endometritis – Presents with fever, uterine tenderness, postpartum or post-procedure; β-hCG not elevated to pregnancy range.<br /></br>D. Granulosa cell tumor – Ovarian neoplasm secreting estrogen; presents with heavy bleeding or precocious puberty, not positive β-hCG.<br /></br>E. Hydatidiform mole – β-hCG typically &gt;100,000; TVUS shows “snowstorm/cluster of grapes” with enlarged uterus, hyperemesis, early-onset preeclampsia. None seen here.<br /></br>F. Leiomyomata uteri (fibroids) – May enlarge uterus and cause heavy menses but do not elevate β-hCG and would be visualized as hypoechoic masses on ultrasound.<br /></br>G. Normal early pregnancy – At β-hCG 7,000 a gestational sac, yolk sac, or fetal pole should be visible. Absence rules this out.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Big Takeaway<br /></br>Empty uterus on TVUS + β-hCG above discriminatory zone = ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise.</p>
100. A 25-year-old primigravid woman at 27 weeks' gestation is brought to the emergency department because of severe contractions for 6 hours. She says the contractions have not resolved despite changing positions and increasing her fluid intake. She also noted a small amount of vaginal spotting the last time she voided. External fetal monitoring shows contractions every 2 to 3 minutes and a fetal heart rate of 130/min with no decelerations. The cervix is 1 cm dilated and 10 percent effaced. The fetal presenting part is high. During the next hour, she receives oral hydration. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation. The fundal height is 27 cm. The cervix is now 1 to 2 cm dilated and 70 percent effaced; the vertex is at -2 station. Vaginal culture for group B streptococcus is positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Observation only</br>b. Oral amoxicillin prophylaxis</br>c. Intramuscular administration of betamethasone</br>d. Cervical cerclage</br>e. Cesarean delivery</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Intramuscular administration of betamethasone  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • “25-year-old primigravid … 27 weeks’ gestation”  → Preterm period (&lt;37 wk).<br /></br>   • “Severe contractions for 6 hours … contractions every 2-3 min” → Uterine activity.<br /></br>   • Cervical change from 1 cm/10 % effaced to 1-2 cm/70 % effaced within 1 h → Objective evidence of cervical change = active preterm labor, not Braxton-Hicks.<br /></br>   • FHR 130/min, no decels → Reassuring tracing (so immediate delivery not required).<br /></br>   • GBS positive vaginal culture → Colonized patient, but intrapartum antibiotics are given only if delivery is imminent.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives: gestational age 27 wk + contractions + progressive cervical change.<br /></br>   Pertinent negatives: afebrile, no ruptured membranes, no fetal distress → we have time to intervene to improve neonatal outcome.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>• “&lt;37 wk + contractions + cervical change” = Preterm labor.<br /></br>• Next ask: GA 24-34 wk? If YES → Give antenatal corticosteroids.<br /></br>• Also consider tocolysis &amp; magnesium (&lt;32 wk) to buy 48 h for steroids to work, but the absolute first evidence-based intervention is steroids.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Diagnose preterm labor (uterine contractions + cervical change before 37 wk).<br /></br>2. Determine GA window 24-34 wk → candidate for betamethasone 12 mg IM q24 h ×2 (or dexamethasone 6 mg IM q12 h ×4).<br /></br>3. Plan adjuncts: tocolytic (eg, indomethacin or nifedipine) and magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection (&lt;32 wk), plus intrapartum GBS prophylaxis if delivery ensues.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In any patient 24-34 wk with preterm labor, antenatal corticosteroids are the single most important, time-sensitive intervention to reduce neonatal respiratory distress syndrome (RDS), intraventricular hemorrhage (IVH), and neonatal mortality.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls: “Preterm labor diagnosed at 27 wk. Betamethasone 12 mg IM given for fetal lung maturation.
