Plan to initiate tocolysis, MgSO₄ for neuroprotection, and intrapartum penicillin G if delivery occurs.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Fetal lungs start producing surfactant (lecithin) ~24 wk but reach critical maturation 34-36 wk.<br /></br>• Maternal glucocorticoids ↑ surfactant protein synthesis, enhance alveolar fluid clearance, and decrease alveolar septal collagen → ↓ risk of RDS.<br /></br>• Also stabilizes cerebral vasculature → ↓ IVH; improves gut integrity → ↓ necrotizing enterocolitis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>First-line (24-34 wk):<br /></br>1. Antenatal corticosteroid: betamethasone 12 mg IM ×2 doses 24 h apart (or dexamethasone regimen).<br /></br>2. Tocolytic to delay delivery ≤48 h (indomethacin 24-32 wk; nifedipine 32-34 wk).<br /></br>3. Magnesium sulfate (&lt;32 wk) for cerebral palsy risk reduction.<br /></br>4. GBS prophylaxis with IV penicillin/ampicillin only once patient is in true labor or ROM; no role for oral outpatient therapy.<br /></br>5. If PPROM, add latency antibiotics; if chorioamnionitis, deliver regardless of GA.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“BLuF 24-34”:<br /></br>B – Betamethasone<br /></br>L – Lungs mature<br /></br>u – (use)<br /></br>F – Forty-eight hours of Tocolysis  </p></br><p>Or visualize a “BETAbaby surfboard” riding a “24-34 wk wave” to remember surfactant benefit.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Observation only<br /></br>   • Misses the window to improve lung maturity; neonatal RDS risk remains high.  </p></br><p>B. Oral amoxicillin prophylaxis<br /></br>   • GBS colonization is managed with IV intrapartum prophylaxis, not outpatient oral therapy. No evidence it prevents neonatal sepsis when given this way.  </p></br><p>D. Cervical cerclage<br /></br>   • Used electively for cervical insufficiency (painless dilation) before viability, not during active labor when cervix is already 1-2 cm dilated and contracting. Contraindicated in contractions or ROM.  </p></br><p>E. Cesarean delivery<br /></br>   • No obstetric indication: vertex presentation, reassuring FHR, extreme prematurity. Cesarean does not treat preterm labor and would expose mother and fetus to unnecessary risk.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Preterm labor at 27 weeks requires immediate administration of antenatal corticosteroids—betamethasone IM—to accelerate fetal lung maturation and improve neonatal outcomes.</p>
101. A 1-year-old boy is brought to the physician 24 hours after the onset of lethargy. He has a 2-day history of fever, vomiting, and decreased appetite. He was recently adopted from Russia. He attends day care five times weekly. His temperature is 39.4°C (103°F). Examination shows neck stiffness and decreased skin turgor. Which of the following vaccines is most likely to have prevented this patient’s condition?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Diphtheria-tetanus-acellular pertussis</br>b. Haemophilus influenzae type b</br>c. Hepatitis A</br>d. Hepatitis B</br>e. Inactivated poliovirus</br>f. Influenza virus</br>g. Measles-mumps-rubella</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B — Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) conjugate vaccine  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Age = 1 year (peak age for Hib disease if unvaccinated)<br /></br>• 2-day prodrome of fever, vomiting → now lethargy &amp; neck stiffness → classic acute bacterial meningitis presentation.<br /></br>• Recently adopted from Russia + day-care attendance → high likelihood of incomplete U.S. immunizations and rapid spread of encapsulated organisms.<br /></br>• Neck stiffness = meningeal irritation; decreased skin turgor = dehydration secondary to vomiting/fever.  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: “Toddler with meningitis who may be undervaccinated” = think Hib first; Strep pneumo &amp; N. meningitidis are still possible, but those organisms are not uniquely vaccine-preventable in this specific age range like Hib is (there is no infant pneumococcal conjugate in answer set; meningococcal vaccine is given later).  </p></br><p>Test-taking framework:<br /></br>1. Identify syndrome (meningitis).<br /></br>2. Ask “Which vaccine on the list most dramatically reduced this disease in 6-18 mo children?” → Hib.<br /></br>3. Corroborate with risk factor (foreign adoption, day care).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Before widespread Hib conjugate vaccination (first dose at 2 mo, series completed by 12–15 mo), Hib was the leading cause of bacterial meningitis in 6 mo–3 yr children.<br /></br>• Encapsulated Hib invades the bloodstream → hematogenous seeding of meninges.<br /></br>• Conjugate vaccine (type-b capsular polyribosylribitol phosphate linked to a protein carrier) induces T-cell–dependent memory in infants → high IgG against capsule, preventing invasive disease.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: An undervaccinated toddler with meningitis = “Hib until proven otherwise”; think of the Hib conjugate vaccine as the single most transformative shot for pediatric meningitis in the past 30 years.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In real life you would document immunization history, rapid IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone + vancomycin), fluid resuscitation, and consider dexamethasone if Hib suspected.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Hib virulence factor: polyribosylribitol phosphate capsule → resists phagocytosis &amp; complement.<br /></br>Risk factors: age 6 mo–3 yr, lack of vaccination, crowding (day care), complement deficiency (esp. C3, properdin).<br /></br>Clinical spectrum: meningitis, epiglottitis, pneumonia, septic arthritis.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics (FYI)  </p></br><p>• Empiric therapy for suspected bacterial meningitis in 1-23 mo: IV vancomycin + 3rd-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime).<br /></br>• Dexamethasone started before/with first antibiotic dose ↓ hearing loss in proven Hib meningitis.<br /></br>• Confirm with blood &amp; CSF cultures; PCR if partially treated.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid  </p></br><p>“HiB for Heads in Babies”<br /></br>• H = Haemophilus<br /></br>• i = invasive<br /></br>• B = type b<br /></br>→ Prevents invasive HEAD infections (meningitis, epiglottitis) in BABIES (6 mo–3 yr).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. DTaP (diphtheria, tetanus, acellular pertussis)<br /></br>• Diphtheria: pharyngitis with pseudomembrane, myocarditis.<br /></br>• Tetanus: muscle rigidity/spasm.<br /></br>• Pertussis: paroxysmal coughing.<br /></br>None cause acute bacterial meningitis.  </p></br><p>C. Hepatitis A vaccine<br /></br>• Prevents acute viral hepatitis (jaundice, ↑ALT) typically after contaminated food; no neck stiffness.  </p></br><p>D. Hepatitis B vaccine<br /></br>• Prevents chronic hepatitis, cirrhosis, HCC; again, not meningitis.  </p></br><p>E. Inactivated poliovirus vaccine<br /></br>• Prevents paralytic poliomyelitis (flaccid asymmetric paralysis). Meningeal signs uncommon.  </p></br><p>F. Influenza vaccine<br /></br>• Influenza can predispose to secondary bacterial pneumonia but is not itself a common cause of meningitis in toddlers.  </p></br><p>G. Measles-mumps-rubella (MMR) vaccine<br /></br>• Measles: cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, rash.<br /></br>• Mumps: parotitis, orchitis, aseptic (viral) meningitis but MUCH rarer; bacterial purulent meningitis with neck stiffness and high fever is classic Hib in this age.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In an undervaccinated 6-18-month-old with acute purulent meningitis, think Haemophilus influenzae type b. The Hib conjugate vaccine, started at 2 months of age, virtually eliminated this disease in the U.S.</p>
102. A 4-year-old girl is brought to the physician because of cough and a 2.3-kg (5-lb) weight loss during the past 2 months. There is no history of serious illness, and she takes no medications. Immunizations are up-to-date. She is at the 50th percentile for height and 25th percentile for weight. Occasional crackles are heard over the right middle lung field. Intradermal skin testing with PPD, tetanus toxoid, and antigens for Candida albicans and Trichophyton tonsurans is nonreactive at 72 hours. Her leukocyte count is 5100/mm³. Nucleic acid hybridization testing of gastric aspirates shows Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's findings?	Correct answer: G</br></br>a. Antibody deficiency</br>b. Complement deficiency</br>c. Impaired chemotaxis</br>d. Impaired respiratory burst</br>e. Neutropenia</br>f. Splenic dysfunction</br>g. T-lymphocyte dysfunction</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>g. T-lymphocyte dysfunction  </p></br><p>————————————————————————————————————<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives<br /></br>• Chronic constitutional symptoms (2-month cough, 5-lb weight loss) → think tuberculosis (TB).<br /></br>• Gastric aspirate nucleic-acid probe confirms Mycobacterium tuberculosis.<br /></br>• Skin-test panel (PPD, tetanus, Candida, Trichophyton) is completely non-reactive (“anergy”). These are all delayed-type (type IV) hypersensitivity tests that require intact CD4⁺ Th1-cell function.<br /></br>• Normal total WBC count (5100/mm³) and no history of severe bacterial infections.  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>• No mention of recurrent pyogenic infections, encapsulated-organism sepsis, or sinopulmonary disease that would point to antibody, complement, or splenic problems.<br /></br>• No history of abscesses with catalase-positive organisms (CGD) or delayed umbilical cord separation (LAD).  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>“Disseminated or severe mycobacterial infection + anergic skin tests” = impaired cell-mediated immunity, specifically T-cell/IL-12–IFN-γ axis.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Identify the infectious organism.<br /></br>2. Decide which arm of immunity controls that organism. (Intracellular pathogens → T-cell/macrophage immunity.)<br /></br>3. Use the control antigens in the skin test as an internal check: anergy = T-cell problem.  </p></br><p>————————————————————————————————————<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise linkage<br /></br>1. Mycobacteria live inside macrophages → require Th1 cells to secrete IFN-γ for macrophage activation.<br /></br>2. PPD reaction is also a Th1-mediated delayed-type hypersensitivity. Lack of induration at 72 h = missing Th1 response.<br /></br>3. Therefore the child’s inability to mount both protective immunity to TB and a DTH skin response is best explained by T-lymphocyte dysfunction.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Type IV hypersensitivity tests double as in-vivo screens for cellular (T-cell) immunity. Anergy in the face of intracellular infection = T-cell defect.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl<br /></br>“Patient demonstrates anergy to control antigens, highly suspicious for defective cell-mediated immunity; recommend quantitative T-cell function studies (e.g., flow cytometry for CD3/CD4, IL-12R/IFN-γ pathway assays).”  </p></br><p>————————————————————————————————————<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Th1 pathway: APC presents antigen → IL-12 drives naïve CD4⁺ → Th1 → IFN-γ → activates macrophages to kill intracellular bugs.<br /></br>• Defects:<br /></br>  – Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) – absent T &amp; B cells.<br /></br>  – IL-12-receptor or IFN-γ–receptor deficiency – selective susceptibility to mycobacteria.<br /></br>  – Advanced HIV – CD4⁺ depletion.  </p></br><p>————————————————————————————————————<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Immediate care<br /></br>• Standard 4-drug anti-TB regimen (isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) with directly observed therapy.  </p></br><p>Further work-up<br /></br>• Flow cytometry for CD3, CD4, CD8 counts.<br /></br>• Mitogen-induced lymphocyte proliferation assay.<br /></br>• IL-12/IFN-γ pathway testing if suspicion for Mendelian susceptibility to mycobacterial disease.  </p></br><p>————————————————————————————————————<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Tips  </p></br><p>“PPD Positive = Properly Primed Delayed response (T-cells).”<br /></br>“ANergy = Absent (T-cell) Army.”<br /></br>Think “IFN-γ = Intracellular Foe Neutralizer.”  </p></br><p>————————————————————————————————————<br /></br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Antibody deficiency – Recurrent sinopulmonary infections, encapsulated bacteria (Strep pneumo, H. flu). PPD may remain positive; doesn’t explain anergy to Candida/tetanus.  </p></br><p>b. Complement deficiency – Late components (C5-C9) → Neisseria; early components (C1q, C2, C4) → lupus-like; normal PPD.  </p></br><p>c. Impaired chemotaxis (Leukocyte adhesion deficiency) – Recurrent skin infections, poor wound healing, delayed umbilical cord separation, marked neutrophilia—not present here.  </p></br><p>d. Impaired respiratory burst (Chronic granulomatous disease) – Recurrent catalase-positive bacterial/fungal infections (Staph, Serratia, Aspergillus), normal PPD or exaggerated; DHR/NTB abnormal.  </p></br><p>e. Neutropenia – Profound bacterial/fungal infections, low absolute neutrophil count (&lt;1500/µL); child’s count normal.  </p></br><p>f. Splenic dysfunction – Encapsulated bacteria sepsis (Strep pneumo, H. flu, Neisseria), Howell-Jolly bodies on smear, normal DTH responses.  </p></br><p>————————————————————————————————————<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>An anergic response to PPD and other control antigens in the setting of culture-proven tuberculosis is classic for defective cell-mediated (T-lymphocyte) immunity.</p>
103. An asymptomatic 27-year-old man comes to the physician 1 month after he received the diagnosis of HIV infection. He takes no medications. He has been sexually active with multiple male partners. Examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>CD4+ T-lymphocyte count   479/mm3 (Normal≥500)</br>Serum</br>  Cytomegalovirus (CMV) antibody   negative</br>  Hepatitis B surface antibody     negative</br>  Hepatitis C antibody             negative</br>  Toxoplasma gondii antibody       negative</br>Plasma HIV viral load      2000 copies/mL</br>Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy during the next year?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Influenza virus and 23-valent pneumococcal vaccines</br>b. Influenza virus and 23-valent pneumococcal vaccines and CMV prophylaxis</br>c. Influenza virus vaccine and T. gondii prophylaxis</br>d. Influenza virus, 23-valent pneumococcal, and hepatitis B vaccines</br>e. Prophylaxis for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and T. gondii</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Influenza virus, 23-valent pneumococcal, and hepatitis B vaccines  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Newly diagnosed, untreated HIV-positive man<br /></br>• CD4 count 479/mm³ → well above the cut-offs (200 and 100) that trigger opportunistic‐infection prophylaxis<br /></br>• Serologies show NO immunity to hepatitis B (HBsAb-negative)<br /></br>Key “pertinent negatives”: CMV-, Toxoplasma-, Hep B-, Hep C- antibodies all negative → no chronic co-infections, no latent IgG that would alter prophylaxis decisions.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework for any new HIV patient:<br /></br>1. Verify CD4 count → decide on OI prophylaxis thresholds.<br /></br>2. Check baseline serologies/immunity → update childhood vaccines, give adult boosters, add special vaccines (HBV, PCV/PPSV, meningococcal, HPV).<br /></br>3. Start ART as soon as possible (not specifically asked).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Opportunistic-infection (OI) prophylaxis:<br /></br>   • PCP with TMP-SMX starts at CD4 &lt;200 or history of oropharyngeal candidiasis.<br /></br>   • Toxoplasma prophylaxis (also TMP-SMX) requires BOTH CD4 &lt;100 AND positive Toxo IgG.<br /></br>   • CMV prophylaxis is not used routinely; valganciclovir reserved for transplant patients, not ambulatory HIV care.<br /></br>   → No OI prophylaxis needed now.  </p></br><ol></br><li>Vaccination needs in HIV (regardless of CD4, if no contraindication):<br /></br>   • Annual inactivated influenza vaccine.<br /></br>   • Pneumococcal sequence: PCV13 once, then 23-valent PPSV23 ≥8 wk later (exam answers often lump as “23-valent pneumococcal vaccine”).<br /></br>   • Hepatitis B vaccine series if HBsAb negative.<br /></br>   • Others: HPV up to age 45, meningococcal ACYW135 + booster q5yr, hepatitis A if risk—but these options were not offered.  </li></br></ol></br><p>The only answer that supplies ALL mandatory immunizations the patient currently lacks is option D.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In HIV patients with CD4 &gt;200, focus first on catch-up vaccines; begin OI prophylaxis only when CD4 thresholds are crossed or specific IgG is positive.  </p></br><p>Documentation note: Record vaccine administration, counsel on ART initiation, schedule repeat CD4/viral load in 3 mo.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)<br /></br>• HIV progressively depletes CD4 T cells, increasing infection risk in a stepwise fashion.<br /></br>• Vaccines protect against common pathogens that are more severe in HIV (influenza, pneumococcus) and against co-infecting viruses that accelerate disease (HBV).<br /></br>• OI prophylaxis targets pathogens whose incidence explodes below specific CD4 counts because cell-mediated immunity is crucial for control (PCP &lt;200, Toxo &lt;100, MAC &lt;50).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Influenza + PPSV23 only<br /></br>   – Omits hepatitis B vaccine despite documented lack of immunity.<br /></br>B. Influenza + PPSV23 + CMV prophylaxis<br /></br>   – Unnecessary valganciclovir; CMV disease risk rises only when CD4 &lt;50. Adds drug toxicity (myelosuppression) without benefit.<br /></br>C. Influenza + Toxoplasma prophylaxis<br /></br>   – Misses pneumococcal and hepatitis B vaccination. Toxo prophylaxis inappropriate (CD4 &gt;100 and Toxo serology negative).<br /></br>E. PCP + Toxo prophylaxis<br /></br>   – Neither indicated at CD4 479; also ignores essential vaccinations.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“HIP” for baseline HIV vaccines:<br /></br>H – Hepatitis B series if not immune<br /></br>I – Influenza (inactivated) every year<br /></br>P – Pneumococcal sequence (PCV13 → PPSV23)  </p></br><p>For OI prophylaxis numbers:<br /></br>• “2-1-5” rule: &lt;200 PCP, &lt;100 Toxo, &lt;50 MAC/CMV.  </p></br><p>Keep these cut-offs and the HIP mnemonic on your white-board for quick test recall.</p>
104. A 24-year-old woman with sickle cell disease is admitted to the hospital for management of severe abdominal pain and diffuse muscle and bone pain of the arms and legs associated with a sickle cell crisis. Her pain is similar to that of her previous crises. Her medications are acetaminophen-hydrocodone and folic acid supplementation. She appears to be in moderate discomfort. Her temperature is 36.7°C (98.1°F), pulse is 94/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 116/68 mm Hg. The abdomen is diffusely tender; there is no guarding or rebound. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Her hemoglobin concentration is 6.8 g/dL, and leukocyte count is 14,000/mm3. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 94 percent. Administration of intravenous fluids and hydromorphone is begun. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Oral administration of hydroxyurea</br>b. Oxygen supplementation by nasal cannula</br>c. Subcutaneous administration of erythropoietin</br>d. Intravenous administration of heparin</br>e. Intravenous administration of piperacillin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Oxygen supplementation by nasal cannula  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Known sickle cell disease + “severe abdominal, muscle, and bone pain” = classic vaso-occlusive (pain) crisis.<br /></br>• Afebrile (36.7 °C) and hemodynamically stable → infection or sepsis less likely.<br /></br>• O2 saturation 94 % on room air → technically “normal,” but NOT optimal for Hb S patients (sickling starts with even mild hypoxemia).<br /></br>• Already receiving the two cornerstones of acute crisis care: 1) aggressive IV fluids, 2) potent opioid analgesia (hydromorphone).<br /></br>• Question asks for the most appropriate NEXT step, i.e., what additional acute-phase measure is needed right now.</p></br><p>High-yield pattern: Acute vaso-occlusive crisis management always follows the mnemonic “HIP FLO”:<br /></br>H – Hydration (IV fluids)<br /></br>I – Infection evaluation/antibiotics if indicated<br /></br>P – Pain control (opioids)<br /></br>F – Folic acid (chronic)<br /></br>L – Labs/Look for triggers (O2 sat, retic count, CBC)<br /></br>O – Oxygen (supplement to keep SpO2 &gt; 95-98 %)</p></br><p>They have provided H &amp; P; the next letter in the acute sequence is O → give supplemental oxygen.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: In any sickle cell stem, immediately check the saturation. If it’s &lt;95 %, choose oxygen unless something life-threatening (acute chest, sepsis) is screaming at you.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thinking:<br /></br>1. Identify crisis → treat precipitants (hypovolemia, hypoxia, infection).<br /></br>2. Hydration and opioids already started.<br /></br>3. Remaining reversible precipitant = hypoxia. Even 94 % means some Hb is deoxygenated; polymerization of Hb S occurs exponentially as O2 drops.<br /></br>4. Therefore administer supplemental O2 by nasal cannula to raise SpO2 to ≥ 95-98 % and blunt ongoing sickling.<br /></br>Take-home message: In vaso-occlusive crisis, give oxygen whenever saturation is not near 100 %.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Placed on 2 L NC O2; SpO2 improved from 94 % to 99 %.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Deoxygenated Hb S polymerizes → RBC sickling → microvascular occlusion → ischemic pain.<br /></br>• Polymerization threshold drops sharply once Hb is &lt;100 % saturated; even small SpO2 dips enlarge the fraction of deoxy-Hb.<br /></br>• Supplemental O2 shifts Hb toward the oxygenated state, decreasing further sickling and limiting crisis severity.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Acute vaso-occlusive crisis algorithm (ABCs + HIP FLO):<br /></br>1. Airway/Breathing: Oxygen if SpO2 &lt;95 %.<br /></br>2. Circulation: IVF (usually D5½NS or NS).<br /></br>3. Pain: IV opioids (morphine, hydromorphone).<br /></br>4. ± Empiric antibiotics if fever, infiltrate on CXR, or indwelling device.<br /></br>5. Blood transfusion/exchange if: acute chest syndrome, stroke, splenic sequestration, or Hb &lt;5-6 g/dL with symptoms.<br /></br>6. Adjuncts: incentive spirometry, folic acid, consider hydroxyurea later for prevention.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “SICKLE”:<br /></br>S – Supplemental O2<br /></br>I – IV fluids<br /></br>C – Control pain<br /></br>K – Keep looking for infection<br /></br>L – Labs/Look for triggers<br /></br>E – Ensure follow-up (hydroxyurEa for prophylaxis)</p></br><p>Visual: Picture a traffic light—Green (100 % sat) keeps the red sickled cars flowing. Yellow (94 %) turns to Red (crisis) unless you add O2.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Oral hydroxyurea<br /></br>• Disease-modifying agent that ↑ HbF to prevent FUTURE crises. Takes weeks to months; not useful acutely.</p></br><p>c. Subcutaneous erythropoietin<br /></br>• SCD patients already have chronically elevated endogenous EPO due to anemia; giving more does not help acute crisis and won’t correct hemolysis.</p></br><p>d. Intravenous heparin<br /></br>• Used for thromboembolism. Vaso-occlusion in SCD is due to sickled RBCs, not fibrin clots; heparin does not relieve pain crisis.</p></br><p>e. Intravenous piperacillin<br /></br>• Broad-spectrum antibiotic appropriate for febrile neutropenia or sepsis. This patient is afebrile and shows no infection focus; leukocytosis is common in stress crises.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Any degree of hypoxemia perpetuates sickling. After fluids and opioids, the next acute intervention is supplemental oxygen to push SpO2 above 95–98 %.</p>
105. A 62-year-old man comes to the physician because he has been "slowing down" during the past 6 months. He has noticed that his gait is slower, he has difficulty rising from a chair, and his handwriting is smaller. He has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, insomnia, gastroparesis, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Medications are metformin, metoclopramide, lisinopril, atorvastatin, and zolpidem. His blood pressure is 140/80 mm Hg; other vital signs are within normal limits. During the examination, there is decreased facial expression and reduced eye blinking. Tone is increased in the axial and limb muscles. Muscle strength is 5/5 throughout, and deep tendon reflexes are 1+ bilaterally. Sensation to vibration is decreased over the toes bilaterally. His gait is shuffling, and there is decreased arm swing. Which of the following medications is most likely worsening this patient's neurologic condition?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Atorvastatin</br>b. Lisinopril</br>c. Metformin</br>d. Metoclopramide</br>e. Zolpidem</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Metoclopramide  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases pointing to parkinsonism: “slowing down,” small handwriting (micrographia), hypomimia (↓ facial expression), hypophonia, shuffling gait with ↓ arm swing, axial-limb rigidity, bradykinesia, and postural instability (difficulty rising).<br /></br>• Strength is preserved (5/5) and reflexes are low-normal → suggests extrapyramidal disorder rather than corticospinal disease.<br /></br>• Peripheral sensory loss (↓ vibration in toes) is explained by long-standing diabetes and is NOT part of the central syndrome.<br /></br>• Medication list is the essential clue: only one agent is a potent central dopamine-2 (D2) receptor blocker—metoclopramide.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>1. Recognize the parkinsonism pattern (bradykinesia + rigidity ± tremor).<br /></br>2. Scan medication list for dopamine antagonists (typical/atypical antipsychotics, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, valbenazine, some antiemetics).<br /></br>3. Elderly patients and females are highest risk for drug-induced parkinsonism (DIP). Discontinue the culprit or add benztropine/amantadine if drug cannot be stopped.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning &amp; Core Takeaway<br /></br>Drug-induced parkinsonism results from dopamine blockade in the nigrostriatal pathway. Metoclopramide is commonly prescribed for diabetic gastroparesis and is a frequent, often overlooked, cause. Stopping the drug usually reverses symptoms within weeks to months.  </p></br><p>Real-world documentation points:<br /></br>• Onset and progression of symptoms<br /></br>• Complete neurological exam documenting bradykinesia and rigidity<br /></br>• Medication reconciliation noting start date/dose of metoclopramide<br /></br>• Plan to discontinue or taper metoclopramide, consider alternative prokinetic (eg, erythromycin), and discuss risk/benefit with patient  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Metoclopramide: D2 antagonist in area postrema (antiemetic) and GI tract (↑ ACh, ↑ peristalsis). Central D2 blockade in substantia nigra lowers dopaminergic tone → parkinsonism, akathisia, acute dystonia, tardive dyskinesia.<br /></br>• DIP typically lacks the resting tremor seen in idiopathic Parkinson disease (IPD) and is more symmetrical.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Algorithm<br /></br>1. Identify and stop offending agent.<br /></br>2. If symptoms persist or drug cannot be stopped, add anticholinergic (benztropine, trihexyphenidyl) or amantadine.<br /></br>3. Avoid levodopa; generally ineffective while dopamine blockade remains.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Metoclopra-MIDE makes movements MINIMIZE” → metoclopramide can cause micrographia and bradykinesia.<br /></br>Other high-yield D2 blockers causing EPS: “HAPPI”<br /></br>• Haloperidol<br /></br>• Antipsychotics (typical &gt; atypical)<br /></br>• Prochlorperazine<br /></br>• Promethazine<br /></br>• MetoclopraIde  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Atorvastatin – May cause myopathy or hepatotoxicity, not parkinsonism.<br /></br>b. Lisinopril – ACE inhibitor; adverse effects include cough, hyperkalemia, angioedema. No dopaminergic action.<br /></br>c. Metformin – Can cause B12-deficiency neuropathy (explains vibration loss) but not central parkinsonism.<br /></br>e. Zolpidem – GABA-A agonist used for insomnia; produces sedation, not extrapyramidal symptoms.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: attributing new parkinsonian signs in an older diabetic to neurodegeneration when they are often medication-induced and reversible.</p>
106. A 13-year-old girl with neurofibromatosis 2 is brought to the office for a follow-up examination. She feels well. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. In addition to audiometry, which of the following is most appropriate to include in the annual screening of this patient?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Dilated eye examination</br>b. ECG</br>c. MRI of the brain</br>d. Skin examination</br>e. Visual evoked potential testing</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. MRI of the brain  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key information: 13-year-old, known neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2), feels well, normal physical exam. The stem asks for ANNUAL screening in addition to audiometry.<br /></br>• NF2 red flags: bilateral vestibular schwannomas, multiple meningiomas, ependymomas. These are frequently asymptomatic until large. Early detection changes management (surgical debulking, bevacizumab, radiosurgery).<br /></br>• “Feels well/normal exam” tells you you’re looking for surveillance, not problem-driven imaging.<br /></br>Testing strategy: When a question says “patient with NF2 + asymptomatic + routine surveillance,” immediately think “brain and spine MRI every year” because tumor burden drives morbidity.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: For phakomatoses, memorize the surveillance chart.<br /></br>• NF1 → eyes &amp; skin (annual ophtho for optic glioma), BP, scoliosis check.<br /></br>• NF2 → EARS &amp; MRI (audiogram ± BAER, brain &amp; spine MRI).<br /></br>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify the syndrome (NF2).<br /></br>2. Recall its hallmark lesions (vestibular schwannomas, meningiomas).<br /></br>3. Recognize that audiometry alone will miss intracranial and intradural tumors before they affect hearing.<br /></br>4. Guideline: baseline and then annual MRI of the brain (and often spine) beginning in late childhood/early adolescence.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: NF2 = bilateral vestibular schwannomas → audiometry + yearly contrast-enhanced brain MRI (± spine) even when asymptomatic.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Annual NF2 surveillance per guidelines—ordered contrast MRI brain (± whole-spine), completed audiogram today, no new neuro deficits.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Gene: NF2 (merlin/schwannomin) tumor-suppressor gene on chromosome 22.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Loss of merlin → unchecked Schwann cell, meningeal, and ependymal cell proliferation.<br /></br>• Clinical spectrum:<br /></br>  – Bilateral vestibular schwannomas (≈95% by age 30).<br /></br>  – Multiple meningiomas (50%–75%).<br /></br>  – Spinal ependymomas &amp; schwannomas.<br /></br>  – Cataracts (posterior subcapsular) in ~60%, but ocular lesions do NOT carry same morbidity as CNS tumors; thus imaging prioritized.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Algorithm<br /></br>1. Annual audiogram (± auditory brain-stem response).<br /></br>2. Annual contrast MRI brain; MRI spine every 1–3 years or if symptoms.<br /></br>3. Ophthalmologic exam baseline, then every 1–2 yrs (not annually mandated like NF1).<br /></br>4. Early surgery/radiosurgery or bevacizumab for growing vestibular schwannomas; monitor meningiomas/ependymomas similarly.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“NF TWO = TWO ears (hearing test) + TWO MRIs (brain ± spine).”  </p></br><p>───────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Dilated eye examination – Important for NF2 cataracts, but evidence-based guidelines recommend every 1–2 years, not necessarily <strong>annual</strong> and not as critical as MRI for life-threatening tumors. NF1, not NF2, mandates yearly ophtho for optic pathway gliomas.  </p></br><p>b. ECG – NF2 is not associated with cardiomyopathy or arrhythmia; no screening indication.  </p></br><p>d. Skin examination – Essential in NF1 (cafè-au-lait spots, cutaneous neurofibromas, melanoma risk), but cutaneous lesions are minimal in NF2; not part of core surveillance.  </p></br><p>e. Visual evoked potential testing – Used for optic nerve disorders (e.g., optic neuritis, NF1 optic gliomas). NF2 primarily affects Schwann and meningeal cells; VEP is low-yield.  </p></br><p>Common trap: conflating NF1 (skin, eyes) with NF2 (ears, CNS tumors). Recall:<br /></br>• NF1 – chromosome 17, “1 looks like café spot,” cutaneous.<br /></br>• NF2 – chromosome 22, “22 looks like two ears,” vestibular schwannomas.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In NF2, life-limiting morbidity comes from intracranial and intraspinal tumors; therefore, routine annual contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain (and periodic spine) is mandatory surveillance alongside audiometry.</p>
107. A 3-year-old girl is brought to the clinic because of a 3-day history of low-grade fever and sore throat. She also has a 12-hour history of rash that began on her hands; the rash is not itchy or painful. She has no history of serious illness and receives no medications. Her temperature is 38.4°C (101.2°F), pulse is 132/min, respirations are 34/min, and blood pressure is 93/48 mm Hg. Examination shows multiple ulcers on the soft palate and posterior aspect of the pharynx. The lungs are clear to auscultation. A grade 2/6 ejection murmur is heard on cardiac examination; S2 varies with inspiration. The abdomen is soft and nontender. The liver edge is palpated 2 cm below the right costal margin. There are multiple vesicles over the palms and digits. A dose of acetaminophen is administered. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Acyclovir therapy</br>b. Blood culture</br>c. Echocardiography</br>d. Throat culture</br>e. Observation only</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Observation only  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key findings<br /></br>   • Age 3 years → common age for viral exanthems<br /></br>   • 3 days of low-grade fever + sore throat<br /></br>   • Multiple ulcers on the soft palate &amp; posterior pharynx (herpangina pattern)<br /></br>   • Vesicular rash on palms &amp; digits, non-pruritic, non-tender<br /></br>   • No systemic toxicity; normal lung &amp; abdominal exams<br /></br>   • Innocent-sounding, grade 2/6 midsystolic murmur with normal physiologic S2 splitting  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>   Oral ulcers in the posterior oropharynx + vesicles on the hands = Hand-Foot-Mouth Disease (HFMD) caused by Coxsackie A (an enterovirus).<br /></br>   – Posterior oral lesions (herpangina) distinguish Coxsackie from HSV gingivostomatitis (anterior mouth, lips, gingiva).<br /></br>   – Palmar/plantar lesions distinguish it from group A strep pharyngitis (no vesicles).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No exudative tonsils, sandpaper rash, or strawberry tongue → argues against GAS pharyngitis.<br /></br>   • No high fever, drooling, or stridor → rules out epiglottitis.<br /></br>   • No vesicles on lips/gingiva, no tender cervical nodes → argues against HSV.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>   When a febrile toddler presents with posterior oral ulcers + acral vesicles, jump to “Coxsackie A → supportive care.” Resist the urge to treat or test further unless red-flag signs (toxicity, dehydration, neurologic symptoms) are present.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why “Observation only”)  </p></br><p>• HFMD is a self-limited viral illness.<br /></br>• There is no antiviral proven to shorten Coxsackie A disease.<br /></br>• Management = antipyretics, oral hydration, return precautions.<br /></br>• The benign flow murmur and mild hepatomegaly are common incidental findings in healthy children and do not alter acute management.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Most pediatric viral exanthems, including HFMD, require only symptomatic care.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls if this were a real encounter:<br /></br>   – “Education given: viral etiology expected to resolve in 7–10 days; advised fluids, acetaminophen/ibuprofen, watch for signs of dehydration or neurologic change; return if symptoms worsen.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Brief Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Coxsackie A16, A6, or enterovirus 71.<br /></br>• Transmission: fecal-oral &amp; respiratory droplets; late summer/early fall peaks.<br /></br>• Incubation ≈ 4 days → prodrome of fever/malaise → herpangina (vesicles on posterior oropharynx) + 24 h later, vesicular/erythematous lesions on palms, soles, buttocks.<br /></br>• Complications are rare (aseptic meningitis, encephalitis).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>First-line:<br /></br>   – Supportive care only (fluids, antipyretics).<br /></br>No role for:<br /></br>   – Acyclovir (ineffective vs enteroviruses).<br /></br>   – Antibiotics.<br /></br>   – Routine viral or throat cultures (results do not change management).<br /></br>   – Echocardiography unless murmur is pathologic (loud &gt;3/6, diastolic, fixed split S2, cyanosis, symptoms).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Coxa-‘ski’ A hits the mouth first, then the hands &amp; feet glide.”<br /></br>• Coxsackie A = herpangina + hand/foot lesions → supportive skate (care).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Acyclovir therapy<br /></br>   – Used for HSV gingivostomatitis (anterior oral ulcers, bleeding gums, tender submandibular nodes, no acral rash). Coxsackie viruses lack thymidine kinase → acyclovir ineffective.  </p></br><p>b. Blood culture<br /></br>   – Indicated for suspected bacteremia/sepsis (high fever, ill appearance, hemodynamic instability). Child is mildly febrile &amp; well appearing.  </p></br><p>c. Echocardiography<br /></br>   – Necessary for pathologic murmurs (holosystolic, diastolic, loud, fixed S2, cyanosis). This is a benign flow murmur (grade 2/6, varies with inspiration).  </p></br><p>d. Throat culture<br /></br>   – Detects group A Streptococcus (exudative pharyngitis, palatal petechiae, scarlatiniform rash). Vesicular lesions &amp; posterior ulcers argue against GAS. A positive culture would not explain the rash pattern.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Core Concept:<br /></br>Hand-Foot-Mouth Disease (Coxsackie A) in a well child = reassure, hydrate, and observe.</p>
108. A previously healthy 7-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 3-week history of pain in his left shoulder and arm. He has difficulty raising his left arm over his head. The pain is worse with motion and at night; it often awakens him from sleep. Over-the-counter medications have not relieved the pain. There is no history of trauma. Examination of the left shoulder shows diffuse tenderness of the greater tuberosity involving the entire humeral head. Passive range of motion of the left shoulder is limited to 90 degrees of abduction and 70 degrees of forward flexion. Extension and flexion of the left wrist are full. Muscle strength and sensation are intact. Radial pulses are palpable bilaterally. X-rays of the left upper extremity are shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. MRI of the left upper extremity</br>b. Corticosteroid therapy</br>c. Electromyography</br>d. Arteriography of the left upper extremity</br>e. Amputation of the left upper extremity</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. MRI of the left upper extremity  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 7-year-old boy (malignant primary bone tumors peak in childhood/adolescence).<br /></br>  – Deep, aching shoulder pain for 3 weeks, worse at night, awakens patient (classic red flag for bone malignancy; benign causes or inflammatory arthritis are usually activity-related and improve with NSAIDs).<br /></br>  – No trauma; OTC analgesics ineffective (night-time bone pain that is NOT relieved by NSAIDs makes osteoid osteoma unlikely and raises suspicion for sarcoma).<br /></br>  – Diffuse bony tenderness over the proximal humerus and markedly limited passive ROM (suggests the lesion is in bone, not muscle or nerve).<br /></br>  – X-ray (image not shown here) already obtained → the question is asking what to do NEXT.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Recognize “night pain + lytic or sclerotic bone lesion on x-ray in a child” → think malignant bone tumor.<br /></br>2. After initial plain film, local staging with MRI comes before biopsy or definitive therapy.<br /></br>3. Choose the option that best provides local anatomic detail without jumping prematurely to treatment.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Goal at this step: local staging. MRI is the modality of choice because it<br /></br>  – Defines the intramedullary extent (“skip” lesions).<br /></br>  – Maps soft-tissue and neurovascular involvement for limb-sparing surgery planning.<br /></br>  – Guides the orthopedic oncologist to the safest biopsy trajectory (biopsy must be placed within the future resection field).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: For any suspicious primary bone tumor seen on x-ray, obtain an MRI of the entire bone and adjacent joints before biopsy or treatment.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): Impression of malignant bone tumor, request MRI with contrast of the entire humerus and shoulder girdle, and place oncology/orthopedic referral.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Osteosarcoma<br /></br>  – Most common primary malignant bone tumor in children/teens.<br /></br>  – Metaphyseal region of long bones (distal femur &gt; proximal tibia &gt; proximal humerus).<br /></br>  – X-ray: “sunburst,” mixed lytic/sclerotic, Codman triangle.<br /></br>• Ewing sarcoma<br /></br>  – Diaphyseal, onion-skin periosteal reaction.<br /></br>• Both present with progressive bone pain (often night pain) ± swelling; systemic symptoms are uncommon.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Workflow<br /></br>1. Plain radiograph → suggests malignancy.<br /></br>2. MRI of entire bone &amp; adjacent joints (local staging).<br /></br>3. Chest CT (pulmonary mets) and bone scan/PET (distant staging).<br /></br>4. Image-guided biopsy.<br /></br>5. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy → limb-sparing resection if feasible. (Amputation only if major neurovascular invasion or non-resectable lesion.)  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“XRAY shows a scary bone lesion?<br /></br>Move Right Immediately (MRI) before you Make Right Incisions (biopsy/surgery).”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>B. Corticosteroid therapy<br /></br>• Would be considered for inflammatory arthritides (e.g., juvenile idiopathic arthritis) but those improve with NSAIDs and present with joint, not bony, tenderness. Steroids without a tissue diagnosis could mask malignancy.  </p></br><p>C. Electromyography<br /></br>• Used to evaluate neuromuscular disorders or radiculopathies. This patient has normal strength and sensation; pain source is osseous.  </p></br><p>D. Arteriography of the left upper extremity<br /></br>• Reserved for suspected vascular injury or pre-op planning for large soft-tissue sarcomas after MRI. Not the first next step.  </p></br><p>E. Amputation of the left upper extremity<br /></br>• Modern management of extremity sarcomas aims for limb-sparing resection after neoadjuvant chemo/radiation whenever possible. Amputation is never the immediate next step without full staging.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Big Picture<br /></br>When a child’s x-ray raises concern for a primary bone tumor, remember:<br /></br>Plain film → MRI for local staging → systemic staging → biopsy → multimodal therapy.</p>
109. A 62-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of increasingly severe left knee pain and a “giving-way” sensation while walking. The pain occurs in the front of the knee and is exacerbated by walking up stairs or sitting in a car for prolonged periods of time. There is no history of trauma. Five years ago, she underwent a discectomy for herniation of L3–4. She is 158 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 113 kg (250 lb); BMI is 46 kg/m2. Examination of the left knee shows no joint effusion. There is tenderness to palpation along the patella. Muscle strength in both lower extremities is 5/5. Sensation to light touch is decreased over the left hallux. Pedal pulses are 2+ bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Anterior cruciate ligament tear</br>b. Chondromalacia patella</br>c. Lumbar radiculopathy</br>d. Medial meniscus tear</br>e. Prepatellar bursitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Chondromalacia patella (patellofemoral pain syndrome)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis – How the clues point to chondromalacia patella  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pain location: “front of the knee” = anterior knee pain → classic for patellofemoral pathology.  </li></br><li>Aggravating factors:<br /></br>   • Walking up or down stairs (increases patellofemoral compressive force).<br /></br>   • Prolonged sitting with flexed knees (the “movie-theatre sign”).  </li></br><li>“Giving-way” sensation: usually reflects pain-inhibition of the quadriceps, common in patellofemoral pain.  </li></br><li>Exam:<br /></br>   • No effusion (argues against internal derangements such as meniscal tear or acute ligament rupture).<br /></br>   • Focal tenderness over the patella (not over joint line).  </li></br><li>Risk factors: Female sex, obesity (BMI 46), possible quadriceps deconditioning or malalignment.  </li></br><li>Absence of traumatic event.</li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive framework: Any middle-aged or young patient with insidious, anterior knee pain that is worse with stairs, squatting, or prolonged sitting → first think “patellofemoral pain syndrome / chondromalacia patella.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>A. Localize pain (anterior vs medial vs lateral).<br /></br>B. Look for swelling/effusion (present in internal derangements).<br /></br>C. Determine precipitating movements (stairs, sitting → patellofemoral; twisting → meniscus; direct kneeling → bursitis).<br /></br>D. Check trauma history (present in ACL/MCL, absent here).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Patellofemoral pain syndrome presents with anterior knee pain exacerbated by activities that load the patellofemoral joint in flexion.</p></br><p>Documentation bullets for a real chart: Location/quality of pain, exacerbating/relieving factors, absence of effusion, patellar tenderness, stability tests, neurovascular status, BMI/obesity.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Chondromalacia patella = softening and degeneration of the articular cartilage on the underside of the patella → inflammation and pain as patella tracks in trochlear groove. Predisposed by:<br /></br>• Overuse (running, stairs)<br /></br>• Malalignment (valgus, vastus medialis obliquus weakness)<br /></br>• Obesity (↑ joint load)<br /></br>• Female &gt; male (wider Q-angle)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Diagnosis is clinical; imaging only if atypical findings.<br /></br>First-line management:<br /></br>1. Activity modification (avoid deep knee flexion on stairs, squats).<br /></br>2. NSAIDs for pain.<br /></br>3. Physical therapy focusing on:<br /></br>   • Quadriceps (vastus medialis) and hip abductor strengthening.<br /></br>   • Stretching hamstrings/calves.<br /></br>4. Weight loss.<br /></br>Second-line: Patellar taping/bracing → rarely arthroscopic debridement.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Movie-theatre knee”: Pain in the front of the knee after sitting through a long film = patellofemoral pain.<br /></br>“Step-pain in the kneecap” → Stairs + Patella = chondromalacia.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Anterior cruciate ligament tear<br /></br>   • Usually acute, traumatic (pivot or deceleration) with immediate swelling/hemarthrosis, instability (“pop”).<br /></br>   • Positive Lachman/pivot-shift. None of these present.</p></br><p>c. Lumbar radiculopathy (e.g., L5)<br /></br>   • Dermatomal pain radiating from back to leg, often neuropathic. Knee pain is referred, not activity-dependent.<br /></br>   • Motor/sensory deficits predominate; knee palpation benign. Her mild hallux sensory loss is residual from prior L3-4 surgery and does not explain knee-specific, activity-related pain.</p></br><p>d. Medial meniscus tear<br /></br>   • Often twisting injury, medial joint-line tenderness, effusion, +McMurray/apley tests, pain with deep squatting.<br /></br>   • Vignette lacks trauma, has patellar (not joint-line) tenderness, and no effusion.</p></br><p>e. Prepatellar bursitis (“housemaid’s knee”)<br /></br>   • Anterior knee swelling over the patella, focal fluctuance, often after repetitive kneeling or trauma.<br /></br>   • Physical exam here shows no swelling/effusion, only tenderness. Pain pattern (stairs, sitting) less typical.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Anterior knee pain worsened by stairs or prolonged sitting, with patellar tenderness and no effusion, in an obese female with no trauma = chondromalacia patella (patellofemoral pain syndrome).</p>
110. A 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-day history of temperatures to 39.4°C (103°F). She has not had any other symptoms. She underwent splenectomy at the age of 17 years because of injuries she sustained in a motor vehicle collision. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Her father died of alcoholic hepatitis and cirrhosis. She is sexually active with multiple partners and does not use condoms. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 15 years. She occasionally drinks alcohol. She has an 8-year history of frequent crack cocaine use and daily intravenous heroin use. She appears ill. Her temperature is 39.8°C (103.7°F), pulse is 120/min and regular, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 80/60 mm Hg. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit           33 percent</br>Leukocyte count      21,000/mm³</br>Segmented neutrophils 85 percent</br>Bands                9 percent</br>Lymphocytes          6 percent</br>Platelet count       490,000/mm³</br>Urine</br>  RBC                10/hpf</br>  WBC                2/hpf</br>  Bacteria           negative</br>Serum studies show no abnormalities. Results of blood cultures are pending. A chest x-ray and transthoracic echocardiography show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial antibiotic therapy for this patient?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a.    Highly active antiretroviral therapy</br>b.    Oral levofloxacin</br>c.    Intramuscular ceftriaxone and oral doxycycline</br>d.    Intravenous ceftriaxone and azithromycin</br>e.    Intravenous vancomycin and gentamicin</br>f.    No antibiotic therapy is indicated at this time</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Intravenous vancomycin and gentamicin  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Fever to 39.8 °C, tachycardia, hypotension → septic shock picture.<br /></br>• IV heroin and crack use → very high risk for Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia/endocarditis (especially MRSA).<br /></br>• Prior splenectomy → poor clearance of encapsulated organisms (eg, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, Haemophilus influenzae) → need early, broad, parenteral coverage.<br /></br>• Normal chest x-ray; sterile urine; no obvious localized infection → blood-borne source most likely.<br /></br>• TTE “normal” does NOT rule out infective endocarditis in IV drug users (sensitivity &lt;60 %).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Recognize sepsis/shock → ABCs, cultures, high-dose IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 h.<br /></br>2. Overlay patient-specific risk factors (IVDU → MRSA; asplenia → encapsulated; young woman with no implanted devices → native valve).<br /></br>3. Choose an empiric regimen that covers BOTH resistant gram-positive cocci and aerobic gram-negative bacilli until cultures return.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise link to the answer<br /></br>1. Need intravenous (not oral/IM) therapy because patient is hypotensive and septic.<br /></br>2. Must cover MRSA and viridans/enterococci (gram-positive) → vancomycin.<br /></br>3. Must simultaneously cover gram-negative aerobes (including Pseudomonas) → gentamicin (an aminoglycoside).<br /></br>4. Combination provides the classic empiric regimen for suspected native-valve infective endocarditis in an IVDU with pending cultures.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>In any septic, hemodynamically unstable IV drug user with no clear focus, assume occult S. aureus bacteremia/endocarditis and give broad IV coverage (vanco + gent) immediately.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life)<br /></br>• Sepsis bundle time-stamps (cultures drawn, antibiotics given)<br /></br>• Hemodynamic status &amp; fluid resuscitation<br /></br>• Risk factors for resistant organisms (IVDU, splenectomy)<br /></br>• Plan for repeat echo with TEE once stabilized  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Infective endocarditis in IVDU<br /></br>• Etiology: S. aureus &gt; Pseudomonas, Candida, enterococci.<br /></br>• Vegetations often on the tricuspid valve.<br /></br>• Presentation: fever, septic pulmonary emboli, shock.<br /></br>Asplenia<br /></br>• Loss of splenic macrophages → ↓opsonization of encapsulated bacteria → fulminant bacteremia.<br /></br>• Vaccinate and give prompt empiric IV antibiotics at first fever.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm  </p></br><p>Immediate management<br /></br>1. 2 sets blood cultures from separate sites.<br /></br>2. Broad IV antibiotics (vancomycin + gentamicin).<br /></br>3. Aggressive IV fluids ± vasopressors.<br /></br>4. Transesophageal echo (TEE) once stable; adjust antibiotics when cultures return.  </p></br><p>First-line vs second-line regimens<br /></br>• If prosthetic valve or recent health-care exposure, add cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam for better gram-negative coverage.<br /></br>• If cultures grow MSSA, narrow to nafcillin/oxacillin + gentamicin for synergy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Tips  </p></br><p>“Van-Gent covers Vegetations”<br /></br>• Vancomycin (gram-positives, esp MRSA)<br /></br>• Gentamicin (gram-negatives, synergy with vanco for enterococci)  </p></br><p>Think “IVDU = IV VanGent.” If the patient is septic and an IV drug user, start IV Vancomycin + Gentamicin until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Highly active antiretroviral therapy – No indication of HIV; HAART is not an acute therapy for sepsis.<br /></br>b. Oral levofloxacin – Oral route, single-agent fluoroquinolone inadequate for septic shock and MRSA.<br /></br>c. IM ceftriaxone + oral doxycycline – Empiric therapy for uncomplicated gonorrhea/chlamydia; useless in septic shock.<br /></br>d. IV ceftriaxone + azithromycin – Standard inpatient regimen for community-acquired pneumonia; chest x-ray is clear and MRSA not covered.<br /></br>f. No antibiotics – Contraindicated; delaying antibiotics in septic shock markedly increases mortality.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom line: Septic, asplenic IV drug user → give immediate IV vancomycin + gentamicin for broad empiric coverage of MRSA, enterococci, and aerobic gram-negatives until culture results dictate narrowing.</p>
111. A 52-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 6-hour history of thirst and fatigue and a 2-hour history of light-headedness. He has been working outside during the past 2 days in temperatures reaching 90.0°F. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. On arrival, he is lethargic and disoriented. His temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 150/min and irregular, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 78/50 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation; no bruits are heard. No murmurs are noted on cardiac examination. The abdomen is nontender. ECG is shown. In addition to administration of 0.9 percent saline, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Administration of amiodarone</br>b. Administration of diltiazem</br>c. Administration of labetalol</br>d. Carotid massage</br>e. Electrical cardioversion</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>E. Electrical cardioversion</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key words/phrases:</br>  – “Pulse 150/min and irregular” → rapid, irregularly irregular rhythm ⇒ atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (AF + RVR).</br>  – “Blood pressure 78/50 mm Hg … lethargic and disoriented” → hemodynamic instability (hypotension, altered mental status).</br>  – Working outdoors in 90 °F with thirst → severe volume depletion explaining why even a ‘usual’ AF + RVR has produced shock, but the tachyarrhythmia itself is now the immediate threat.</br>• Pertinent positives: irregular tachycardia, hypotension, AMS.</br>• Pertinent negatives: no murmurs/bruits, clear lungs, indicates no primary structural/valvular or pulmonary cause of instability.</p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Identify rhythm.<br /></br>2. Assess stability (hypotension, chest pain, HF, AMS).<br /></br>3. Unstable = immediate synchronized DC cardioversion for any tachyarrhythmia (except pulseless VT/VF → defibrillation).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process:</br>1. ECG + exam → AF + RVR.</br>2. Patient is unstable (SBP &lt;90, AMS).</br>3. ACLS tachycardia algorithm: Unstable supraventricular tachyarrhythmia → synchronized electrical cardioversion.</br>4. Give IVF concurrently to correct volume loss, but definitive, time-critical therapy is shock.</p></br><p>Core takeaway:<br /></br>“Unstable tachyarrhythmia? Shock first, ask questions later.”</p></br><p>Documentation pearls: rhythm strip, BP/mental status, indication for synchronized cardioversion, joules used, patient response, sedation given.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>Atrial fibrillation causes chaotic atrial activity (multiple re-entry circuits around pulmonary veins) → ventricles receive irregular impulses via AV node → RVR. Loss of atrial kick + shortened diastole drops cardiac output. In dehydration, preload is already low; rate-dependent CO fall precipitates hypotensive shock.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics / Treatment Algorithm</br>• Immediate: ABCs, IV access, 0.9 % saline.</br>• Unstable: synchronized cardioversion (100–200 J biphasic).<br /></br>• Stable: rate control (diltiazem, β-blocker) and anticoagulation considerations.</br>• After stabilization, evaluate precipitating factors (electrolytes, thyroid, infection, volume status).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“FAST” for tachycardia algorithm:<br /></br>F – Find rhythm.<br /></br>A – Assess stability.<br /></br>S – Shock if unstable.<br /></br>T – Treat medically if stable.</p></br><p>Or simply: “If they’re crashing, start smashing (the shock button).”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong</br>A. Amiodarone – Used for rhythm control in stable AF when β-blocker/CCB contraindicated or for VT; slower onset, not first-line in unstable patients.</p></br><p>B. Diltiazem – Excellent rate control if STABLE. Will further lower BP in this hypotensive patient and wastes precious time.</p></br><p>C. Labetalol – Similar issue: rate control only in stable patients; β-blockade acutely worsens hypotension.</p></br><p>D. Carotid massage – Vagal maneuver for AV-node dependent, regular tachycardias (AVNRT, AVRT). AF is irregular and not terminated by vagal maneuvers.</p></br><p>E. Electrical cardioversion – Correct because it rapidly restores sinus rhythm and cardiac output in an unstable AF + RVR.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Exam Pearls</br>• Irregularly irregular + narrow complex = AF.</br>• Unstable tachyarrhythmia (regardless of origin) → synchronized cardioversion.</br>• Volume depletion often unmasks or worsens AF; always correct underlying triggers after stabilization.</p>
112. A 62-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by paramedics 30 minutes after the onset of sharp, anterior chest pain. She is otherwise asymptomatic. Administration of nitroglycerin en route to the hospital has not relieved her symptoms. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and peripheral artery disease. She underwent laparoscopic cholecystectomy 1 year ago. Her medications are insulin, ramipril, hydrochlorothiazide, atorvastatin, and aspirin. Her pulse is 96/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 124/76 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation. A normal S1 and S2 are heard. Peripheral pulses are decreased bilaterally. An ECG shows a left bundle branch block; review of her medical records shows that a routine ECG 1 year ago showed no abnormalities. Her serum troponin I and myoglobin concentrations are within the reference ranges. The patient is admitted to the hospital. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Exercise stress test</br>b. Cardiac stress scintigraphy</br>c. Positron emission tomography</br>d. Electrophysiologic studies</br>e. Cardiac catheterization</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E.  Cardiac catheterization (emergent coronary angiography ± percutaneous coronary intervention)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should immediately raise concern for an ST-elevation–equivalent acute coronary syndrome (ACS):</p></br><p>• 62-year-old woman with multiple atherosclerotic risk factors (diabetes, HTN, PAD, hyperlipidemia)<br /></br>• Sudden‐onset sharp anterior chest pain 30 min ago, unrelieved by nitroglycerin<br /></br>• NEW left bundle branch block (LBBB) compared with ECG 1 year ago<br /></br>• Very early, therefore cardiac biomarkers can still be negative (troponin may take 3–4 h to rise)</p></br><p>Pattern recognition for the exam: “Ischemic chest pain + new LBBB = treat as STEMI.”</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On Step 2 CK, any patient with ongoing chest pain and either (a) ST-segment elevation or (b) new LBBB goes straight to reperfusion therapy. Do not waste time with stress testing or additional imaging.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why choice E is correct)  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify high-risk ACS: Ongoing pain + high-risk ECG change (new LBBB).<br /></br>2. New LBBB is considered a STEMI equivalent because it can obscure ST elevations yet usually reflects an occlusive proximal LAD lesion.<br /></br>3. Guideline-directed next step = immediate coronary angiography with intent to perform primary PCI (percutaneous coronary intervention) within 90 min of first medical contact.<br /></br>4. Cardiac catheterization is both diagnostic (visualizes culprit lesion) and therapeutic (angioplasty/stent). Waiting for repeat enzymes or doing stress imaging would delay life-saving reperfusion.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Reperfuse first, ask questions later when you see new LBBB + active ischemic symptoms.</p></br><p>Documentation bullet you’d record in real life: “New left bundle branch block on ECG in the setting of ongoing chest pain → STEMI equivalent. Plan: emergent activation of cath lab for primary PCI.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• LBBB occurs when an ischemic event or structural heart disease interrupts conduction in the left bundle.<br /></br>• Anterior wall transmural infarcts (usually proximal LAD) are the classic precipitant of new LBBB.<br /></br>• Because LBBB distorts the QRS and ST segments, ST elevations may be obscured; thus guidelines treat new LBBB like ST-elevation MI to avoid missed reperfusion.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic-and-Treatment Algorithm (acute chest pain)  </p></br><ol></br><li>ABCs, 12-lead ECG, troponin.  </li></br><li>If ST elevation or new LBBB → activate cath lab for emergent PCI (Goal door-to-balloon ≤90 min).<br /></br>   • If PCI unavailable within 120 min → give fibrinolysis unless contraindicated.  </li></br><li>Adjuncts: aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitor, heparin/LMWH, high-intensity statin, ± beta-blocker if stable.  </li></br><li>Serial troponins, repeat ECGs, manage complications.</li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “CHEST PAIN + NEW ‘BBB’ = ‘Bring Balloon’ (PCI).”<br /></br>Think of the left bundle as the “left main highway”—if it suddenly closes, you open the artery immediately.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Exercise stress test<br /></br>• Contraindicated in active chest pain and in patients with baseline LBBB (abnormal QRS makes interpretation unreliable).<br /></br>• Would dangerously delay reperfusion.</p></br><p>B. Cardiac stress scintigraphy (nuclear stress)<br /></br>• Also contraindicated in acute chest pain; nuclear imaging is for stable patients with indeterminate symptoms. Delays therapy.</p></br><p>C. Positron emission tomography<br /></br>• Used to assess myocardial viability or perfusion in chronic ischemic disease, not acute ACS management.</p></br><p>D. Electrophysiologic studies<br /></br>• Evaluate arrhythmia mechanisms; has no role in diagnosing coronary occlusion causing MI.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>When a patient with current ischemic chest pain develops a new LBBB, presume an acute transmural MI. Skip noninvasive tests and proceed directly to emergent cardiac catheterization for definitive diagnosis and reperfusion.</p>
113. An 82-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her daughter because of shortness of breath. Each night for the past week, the patient has awakened with shortness of breath; she has found that using three pillows instead of one allows her to breathe more easily at night. Two days ago, she had shortness of breath with exertion, and 8 hours ago, she had the sudden onset of mild shortness of breath at rest. She also has a 4-day history of swelling of her ankles and feet. Her only medication is 81-mg aspirin. On arrival, she is in mild respiratory distress but able to speak in complete sentences. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 86/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 162/88 mm Hg. Examination shows jugular venous distention. Bilateral basilar crackles are heard over the lower quarter of the lung fields. Cardiac examination shows a regular rhythm. A grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur is heard best at the apex with the patient in the left lateral decubitus position. There is 2+ pitting edema of the ankles. In addition to furosemide, administration of which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Digoxin</br>b. Dobutamine</br>c. Lisinopril</br>d. Milrinone</br>e. Nifedipine</br>f. Nitroprusside</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Lisinopril  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases:<br /></br>  – “Awakens with shortness of breath,” “needs three pillows” → orthopnea/PND → congestive heart failure (CHF).<br /></br>  – JVD, basilar crackles, 2+ pitting edema → volume overload.<br /></br>  – Holosystolic murmur at the apex (↑ in left-lateral position) → chronic mitral regurgitation, commonly secondary to LV dilation in systolic HF.<br /></br>  – BP 162/88 mm Hg → not hypotensive; therefore no evidence of cardiogenic shock.  </p></br><p>• Pertinent negatives: normal temperature (no infection), able to speak in full sentences (not impending respiratory failure), HR 86 (not tachyarrhythmia).  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Recognize acute decompensated HFrEF (Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction) with volume overload.<br /></br>2. Immediate management: loop diuretic for pulmonary congestion (furosemide already ordered).<br /></br>3. Next chronic, disease-modifying therapy unless contraindicated: ACE inhibitor (or ARB/ARNI) + guideline-directed β-blocker ± mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist. The patient is ACE-inhibitor-naïve and hypertensive, so start an ACEI now.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why Lisinopril?)<br /></br>• ACE inhibitors (e.g., lisinopril, enalapril):<br /></br>  – Afterload and preload reduction (venous &amp; arterial dilation).<br /></br>  – RAAS blockade slows adverse ventricular remodeling, decreases morbidity and all-cause mortality in HFrEF.<br /></br>  – Guideline: Initiate as soon as congestion is controlled and blood pressure permits; no need to wait for outpatient follow-up.<br /></br>• BP 162/88 provides a safety margin; kidney function is not given but assumed acceptable (always check Cr/K⁺ in real life).  </p></br><p>Take-home: In hemodynamically stable decompensated HFrEF, start or resume an ACE inhibitor in addition to loop diuretics to improve survival. Document: indication, baseline creatinine &amp; potassium, counseling about cough/angioedema, plan to uptitrate.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Systolic HF → ↓ forward flow → neurohormonal activation (RAAS, SNS) → Na⁺/H₂O retention, ventricular remodeling.<br /></br>• ACEI blocks conversion of angiotensin I → II, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone, thereby:<br /></br>  – ↓ preload/afterload → symptomatic relief.<br /></br>  – ↓ remodeling/apoptosis → mortality benefit.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Digoxin<br /></br>• Positive inotrope &amp; AV-node blocker. Added only if symptoms persist despite ACEI/β-blocker/MRA or for rate control in AF. No mortality benefit; not first-line here.  </p></br><p>b. Dobutamine<br /></br>• β1-agonist IV inotrope for severe systolic dysfunction with hypotension or cardiogenic shock (cold &amp; wet). This patient is warm (BP 162/88) and perfusing.  </p></br><p>d. Milrinone<br /></br>• IV PDE-3 inhibitor, similar indications to dobutamine (bridge therapy, refractory shock). Not needed in stable, hypertensive HF.  </p></br><p>e. Nifedipine<br /></br>• Dihydropyridine CCB; negative inotropic effect can worsen systolic HF. CCBs have no mortality benefit and are generally avoided in HFrEF (exception: amlodipine if needed for HTN).  </p></br><p>f. Nitroprusside<br /></br>• Potent arterial/venous vasodilator used in hypertensive emergencies or flash pulmonary edema with severe afterload excess. Useful if BP &gt;200 systolic with pulmonary edema; not required here once ACEI covers afterload.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“ACE-ing heart failure”:<br /></br>A – Afterload ↓<br /></br>C – Congestion ↓<br /></br>E – Extends life  </p></br><p>OR<br /></br>“Loopy first, Pril second” → In decompensated HFrEF give a Loop diuretic for lungs, then a -pril for life-saving benefit (if BP tolerates).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Summary<br /></br>The patient has volume-overloaded, hemodynamically stable systolic heart failure. Guideline-directed medical therapy mandates initiation of an ACE inhibitor—lisinopril—immediately after diuresis to improve symptoms and, most importantly, to confer a proven mortality benefit.</p>
114. A 44-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of nonproductive cough that worsens at night. She says occasionally she has coughed so hard that she vomited or had loss of urine. Between these episodes, she feels well. She has not had fever, night sweats, shortness of breath, wheezing, chills, weight loss, or cough productive of bloody sputum. She says her symptoms began as a cold with sore throat and runny nose and seemed to improve by the end of the first week, but then she began coughing. Use of over-the-counter cold medicine has not provided relief of her cough. Results of a PPD skin test 1 year ago were negative. The patient has no history of asthma, hypertension, or allergies. She takes no medications. She does not smoke cigarettes. Her three children recently had similar illnesses, but she says their symptoms resolved within 1 week. They attend school, and their vaccinations are up-to-date. The patient volunteers at a homeless shelter twice monthly. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 72/min and regular, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 122/76 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 99 percent. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Bordetella pertussis polymerase chain reaction test</br>b. Spirometry</br>c. Sputum culture for Haemophilus influenzae type b</br>d. Sputum culture for acid-fast bacillus</br>e. X-ray of the chest</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Bordetella pertussis polymerase chain reaction (PCR) test  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key time course: “cold” (catarrhal phase) for ~1 week → apparent improvement → onset of severe paroxysmal cough lasting &gt;2 weeks.<br /></br>• Cough qualities: nocturnal predominance, repetitive paroxysms, post-tussive emesis, stress urinary incontinence.<br /></br>• Systemic/respiratory negatives: afebrile, no dyspnea/wheezing, normal O2 sat and exam → argues against pneumonia, asthma, COPD.<br /></br>• Epidemiology: Adult caregiver of school-aged children (who had brief “colds”) → classic household exposure to pertussis; adult immunity wanes 5–10 y after last Tdap.<br /></br>• High-yield buzz phrase: “Coughing so hard she vomits.”  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On NBME/Step 2, a &gt;2-week paroxysmal cough with post-tussive emesis or syncope—especially after a mild URI and in someone with contact to kids—should trigger the “pertussis reflex.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Decide if immediate imaging or empiric therapy is required (No—benign vitals/exam).<br /></br>2. Identify most likely etiology: adult pertussis.<br /></br>3. Confirm diagnosis because treatment impacts patient and public health (macrolide + prophylaxis for close contacts).<br /></br>   • Two preferred tests within first 4 wks of cough:<br /></br>     – PCR of nasopharyngeal swab (rapid, sensitive)<br /></br>     – Culture (gold standard but slower)  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Prolonged paroxysmal cough with post-tussive vomiting in an adult warrants pertussis PCR to guide therapy and chemoprophylaxis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Bordetella pertussis attaches to ciliated respiratory epithelium via filamentous hemagglutinin → toxin-mediated inflammation.<br /></br>• Clinical phases:<br /></br>  1. Catarrhal (1–2 wk): nonspecific URI.<br /></br>  2. Paroxysmal (2–6 wk): severe coughing fits ± “whoop,” post-tussive emesis/syncope.<br /></br>  3. Convalescent (wks–mo): gradual resolution.<br /></br>• Adult immunity wanes; vaccinated children may have mild cases yet transmit organism.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls<br /></br>• Test choice: Nasopharyngeal PCR (≤4 wk of cough) ± culture.
