Serology useful after 4 wk.<br /></br>• First-line therapy: oral macrolide (azithromycin, clarithromycin, or erythromycin).<br /></br>• Post-exposure prophylaxis: same macrolide regimen for household/close contacts regardless of vaccination status.<br /></br>• Document in chart: suspicion for pertussis, exposure risk, test ordered, need for public-health reporting.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“ABCs of Pertussis”<br /></br>A = Adult with waning immunity<br /></br>B = Bouts of cough → Barf/Bladder leak<br /></br>C = Confirm with Culture/PCR, then give a macrolide  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>b. Spirometry<br /></br>• Cough-variant asthma causes nocturnal cough but typically chronic (&gt;8 wk) and lacks violent paroxysms with emesis. FEV₁ reversibility testing is diagnostic, not first step here.</p></br><p>c. Sputum culture for Haemophilus influenzae type b<br /></br>• H influenzae type b vaccines make adult infection rare; it causes epiglottitis/pneumonia with fever or stridor, not isolated prolonged cough.</p></br><p>d. Sputum culture for acid-fast bacilli (AFB)<br /></br>• Tuberculosis presents with &gt;3-wk cough plus constitutional symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss, hemoptysis). Afebrile patient, negative PPD last year, no risk factors → low pre-test probability.</p></br><p>e. Chest X-ray<br /></br>• Indicated if concern for pneumonia, malignancy, or complicated cough (&gt;3 wk with red flags). Normal vitals/exam and classic pertussis features make targeted microbiologic testing higher yield.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>In any patient—especially an adult caregiver—with &gt;2 weeks of paroxysmal cough causing post-tussive vomiting or incontinence after a mild URI, think B. pertussis and order a nasopharyngeal PCR to confirm and initiate macrolide therapy plus contact prophylaxis.</p>
115. An 18-year-old woman comes to the physician in the fall because of episodes of coughing and wheezing since she moved from the Midwest to New England to attend college 3 weeks ago. The episodes have been occurring several times daily and are associated with a sensation of tightness in her chest. During this period, she has had decreased stamina during exercise. She has had one episode of bronchitis each winter for 3 years. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She does not smoke cigarettes. She is not in respiratory distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Spirometry shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Allergy skin testing</br>b. Chest x-ray</br>c. CT scan of the sinuses</br>d. Exercise stress test</br>e. Inhaled albuterol therapy</br>f. Measurement of diffusion capacity for carbon monoxide</br>g. Oral azithromycin therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Inhaled albuterol therapy (short-acting β2-agonist, SABA)</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Recurrent “coughing and wheezing … several times daily” + “tightness in her chest” = classic asthma symptoms.<br /></br>• “Decreased stamina during exercise” → exercise-induced bronchoconstriction is a common asthma manifestation.<br /></br>• “Moved … 3 weeks ago” and fall season = new aeroallergens/viral triggers.<br /></br>• “No abnormalities” on baseline spirometry: asthma can be completely normal between attacks, especially in a young patient.<br /></br>Key test-taking clue: Normal spirometry does NOT exclude asthma.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: When symptoms strongly suggest asthma but spirometry is normal, the examiners want you to (1) give a bronchodilator trial or (2) order a bronchoprovocation test (e.g., methacholine). If a rapid, low-risk treatment option is listed, choose that.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1 – Identify the syndrome: Episodic, reversible lower-airway obstruction.<br /></br>Step 2 – Severity: Symptoms several times daily but no nighttime cough, no limitation of daily activities beyond stamina drop, normal exam → consistent with mild intermittent or possibly mild persistent asthma.<br /></br>Step 3 – Confirm/empirically treat: Guidelines allow an empiric SABA trial when suspicion is high and spirometry is non-diagnostic. Improvement after SABA supports the diagnosis and provides relief.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Normal spirometry does not rule out asthma. A therapeutic (diagnostic) trial with an inhaled SABA is the quickest, safest next step in a young, otherwise healthy patient.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Administered albuterol 2 puffs in office; patient reports immediate ↓ wheeze/tightness. Plan = prescribe SABA PRN, re-evaluate in 4 weeks; consider controller therapy if symptoms &gt;2 days/week.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Asthma = chronic inflammatory disease → airway hyper-responsiveness + reversible bronchoconstriction. Triggers (allergens, viral URIs, exercise, cold air) cause mast-cell mediator release and smooth-muscle contraction. Baseline PFTs may be normal; during an attack FEV1 ↓ disproportionately to FVC.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Treatment Algorithm (simplified GINA/NHLBI)<br /></br>1. Confirm/strongly suspect asthma with history → trial of SABA or bronchoprovocation.<br /></br>2. If symptoms ≤2 days/week &amp; no nighttime awakening → SABA PRN.<br /></br>3. If symptoms &gt;2 days/week → add low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS).<br /></br>4. Escalate stepwise (ICS/LABA, etc.) if control inadequate.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “SABAs are the First ‘ABA’ in AsthmA”: Short-Acting Beta-Agonist for first-line relief and as a diagnostic tool.<br /></br>• Normal spirometry? Think “Hidden Asthma” → H = Hand them albuterol.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Allergy skin testing<br /></br>• Helpful for long-term trigger identification, not for immediate diagnosis/relief. Does not address acute symptoms.</p></br><p>b. Chest x-ray<br /></br>• Indicated for red flags (fever, focal rales, hemoptysis, trauma). Her exam and history are classic for asthma, not pneumonia or structural lung disease.</p></br><p>c. CT scan of the sinuses<br /></br>• Ordered for chronic sinusitis contributing to asthma exacerbations but only after asthma is established and refractory to standard therapy.</p></br><p>d. Exercise stress test<br /></br>• Evaluates cardiac or exercise-induced ischemia, not episodic wheezing in a healthy 18-year-old.</p></br><p>f. Measurement of diffusion capacity for carbon monoxide (DLCO)<br /></br>• Useful in interstitial lung disease or emphysema; normal in asthma. Adds no value here.</p></br><p>g. Oral azithromycin therapy<br /></br>• Used for atypical pneumonia or occasionally in COPD with frequent exacerbations. No infectious signs; indiscriminate antibiotics increase resistance.</p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Students think “normal spirometry = not asthma → order more tests.” Remember, asthma is reversible; a normal office PFT doesn’t exclude it. Always consider a bronchodilator response or methacholine challenge before expensive imaging or invasive tests.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Young patient with classic episodic wheeze + normal baseline spirometry → give an inhaled SABA trial. Improvement clinches the diagnosis and treats her symptoms.</p>
116. A 2-year-old boy is admitted to the intensive care unit through the emergency department 20 minutes after his parents found him at the bottom of the backyard swimming pool. The parents report that when they pulled their son from the pool, he was not breathing and had no pulse. They are unsure of the duration of the submersion. They administered cardiopulmonary resuscitation for 10 minutes until emergency medical technicians arrived. En route to the emergency department, the patient was intubated and mechanically ventilated with 100 percent oxygen at a rate of 20 breaths per minute, after which his pulse returned at a rate of 150/min. On arrival in the emergency department, vital signs were temperature 36.5°C (97.7°F), pulse 150/min, and blood pressure 100/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 100 percent oxygen showed an oxygen saturation of 100 percent. Now, in the intensive care unit, his vital signs are unchanged. He has spontaneous respirations, and is reaching for his endotracheal tube. His parents want to know if he will survive. Which of the following factors would most strongly suggest a good prognosis in this patient?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Age of 2 years</br>b. Pulse greater than 100/min en route</br>c. Purposeful movements in the intensive care unit</br>d. Resuscitation without the use of epinephrine</br>e. Temperature greater than 35.0°C (95.0°F) in the emergency department</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Purposeful movements in the intensive care unit</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that shape prognosis after a pediatric submersion injury:<br /></br>• Submersion/drowning → global cerebral hypoxia is the major threat to survival and long-term outcome.<br /></br>• CPR lasted only 10 min before ROSC (return of spontaneous circulation) – relatively short.<br /></br>• Now, in the ICU, the child:<br /></br>  – Has spontaneous respirations (brain-stem function intact).<br /></br>  – Is “reaching for his endotracheal tube” → a deliberate, goal-directed (purposeful) movement that requires intact cerebral cortical function, not just spinal or brain-stem reflexes.  </p></br><p>For neurologic prognostication after cardiorespiratory arrest, the single strongest bedside predictor of a favorable outcome is early return of higher-cortical function (purposeful withdrawal, obeying commands, eye tracking, etc.). That is exactly what this vignette demonstrates.</p></br><p>Metacognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Identify the main organ system that will decide outcome (here, brain).<br /></br>2. Look for early signs of that organ system’s functional integrity (purposeful movement, following commands, intact pupillary reflexes).<br /></br>3. Treat “vital signs, age, isolated lab values” as supportive data, but remember they carry less prognostic weight than direct neurologic assessment.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Near-drowning → hypoxic-ischemic injury risk.<br /></br>2. Best prognostic indicators focus on neurologic recovery, not cardiovascular numbers.<br /></br>3. Purposeful movement = cortex working → implies limited hypoxic damage → highest likelihood of survival with good neurologic outcome.</p></br><p>Take-home message: After resuscitation from drowning/cardiac arrest, early purposeful or command-following movements are the most reliable clinical sign of a good prognosis.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): “Patient opens eyes to voice and purposefully pushes away ETT; localizes pain; GCS motor 5 — suggests intact cortical function; favorable neurologic prognosis discussed with family.”</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Drowning leads to hypoxemia → diffuse neuronal injury.<br /></br>• Cortical neurons are the most oxygen-sensitive; their recovery manifests as purposeful actions.<br /></br>• Brain-stem reflexes (pupils, gag) can persist even in severe cortical damage; hence they are weaker predictors than purposeful movement.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Considerations  </p></br><p>Initial management priorities (already done): airway, 100 % O₂, C-spine stabilization, rewarming, monitor for pulmonary edema/ARDS.<br /></br>Ongoing care: neuro checks, targeted temperature management (32–36 °C), seizure prophylaxis, monitor electrolytes. Prognostication usually postponed ≥24 h, but early purposeful movement is still a reassuring sign.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic for GOOD neuro prognosis after arrest: “MAPS”<br /></br>M – Moves purposefully<br /></br>A – Awakens/opens eyes to voice<br /></br>P – Pupils reactive (less predictive than M/A)<br /></br>S – Spontaneous respirations  </p></br><p>If you see “Moves purposefully,” almost always choose it as the strongest positive prognostic clue.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Age of 2 years<br /></br>• Young age does NOT independently predict outcome. Infants/toddlers actually have higher morbidity due to thinner cortex and higher water aspiration risk.  </p></br><p>b. Pulse &gt;100/min en route<br /></br>• Tachycardia reflects catecholamine surge; it tells you hemodynamics, not cerebral recovery. Not a validated prognostic marker for neurologic survival.  </p></br><p>d. Resuscitation without epinephrine<br /></br>• Absence of epinephrine usually means ROSC occurred quickly, but time to ROSC is already known (10 min). Drug use itself is not an established independent prognostic factor.  </p></br><p>e. Temperature &gt;35 °C on arrival<br /></br>• Mild hypothermia (&lt;35 °C) can be neuro-protective (“diving reflex” in cold water). A normal temperature therefore does NOT confer improved prognosis; if anything, hypothermia is sometimes associated with better neurologic outcome.  </p></br><p>Key distinguishing feature: only option C directly demonstrates higher-order cortical function, which is the crucial determinant of long-term neurologic survival after drowning.</p>
117. A 42-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by the staff of a homeless shelter after they found him unconscious outside the shelter. He has alcohol use disorder, schizophrenia, hepatitis C, and cirrhosis. He has been treated for hepatic encephalopathy and ascites. On arrival, he is responsive only to painful stimuli. He is 188 cm (6 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 19 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.7°C (99.9°F), pulse is 130/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 86/40 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 92 percent. Examination shows jaundice and conjunctival icterus. There are multiple ecchymoses over all the extremities. Abdominal examination shows ascites and a fluid wave. The patient moans on palpation of the abdomen. There is trace edema of the ankles. All extremities are warm. Urine output has been 60 mL/h during the past 4 hours. In addition to obtaining a complete blood count, serum electrolyte concentrations, and urinalysis, which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Abdominal CT scan  </br>b. Blood culture  </br>c. Culture and Gram stain of abdominal fluid  </br>d. Exploratory laparotomy  </br>e. Flat and upright abdominal x-rays  </br>f. Measurement of cell count and differential of abdominal fluid</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>f. Measurement of cell count and differential of abdominal fluid (diagnostic paracentesis)</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases &amp; clues<br /></br>• Long‐standing cirrhosis + known ascites → high risk for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP).<br /></br>• New systemic inflammatory response: T 37.7 °C, HR 130, RR 24, BP 86/40 (septic picture).<br /></br>• Altered mental status (responds only to pain) ± abdominal tenderness (“moans on palpation”).<br /></br>• Warm extremities and preserved urine output (early sepsis physiology).  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: fever, tachycardia, hypotension, abdominal pain/tenderness, encephalopathy, known ascites.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no history of trauma, surgery, or clear intra-abdominal catastrophe—points away from perforated viscus or hemorrhage.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>In any patient with cirrhosis + ascites who acutely decompensates (fever, pain, AMS), think “Rule out SBP first.” The first step in management and the diagnostic test of choice is bedside diagnostic paracentesis before antibiotics.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Cirrhotic with ascites becomes febrile/septic → SBP is most common, life-threatening, and treatable.<br /></br>2. Confirmation of SBP = polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMNs) ≥ 250 cells/mm³ in ascitic fluid, regardless of culture.<br /></br>3. Therefore the test that confirms the diagnosis is ascitic fluid cell count and differential obtained by paracentesis.<br /></br>Core takeaway: “Paracentesis with cell count ≥250 PMNs/mm³ = SBP.”  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Bedside paracentesis performed prior to antibiotics; ascitic fluid sent for cell count with differential, total protein, albumin, Gram stain &amp; culture.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Portal hypertension → ascites.<br /></br>• Bacterial translocation of gut flora (usually E. coli, Klebsiella, Streptococcus) into peritoneal fluid that has low opsonic activity.<br /></br>• Neutrophilic response within ascites causes systemic inflammatory response &amp; organ dysfunction.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: low ascitic total protein (&lt;1 g/dL), advanced Child-Pugh class, GI bleeding, prior SBP episode.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Diagnostic algorithm<br /></br>1. Immediate paracentesis in ALL hospitalized cirrhotics with GI bleed or any new pain, fever, AMS, hypotension.<br /></br>   • PMN ≥ 250/mm³ = treat as SBP.<br /></br>   • Send culture, but do NOT wait for results.  </p></br><p>Management<br /></br>• IV 3rd-generation cephalosporin (e.g., cefotaxime or ceftriaxone).<br /></br>• IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3) reduces renal failure &amp; mortality.<br /></br>• Secondary prophylaxis: oral norfloxacin or TMP-SMX indefinitely after recovery.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“3 C’s of SBP”<br /></br>Cirrhosis + Clinical change → do a Cell count (PMNs ≥ 250).  </p></br><p>OR “P-250”<br /></br>P = Paracentesis, &gt;250 PMNs → Peritonitis.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Abdominal CT scan<br /></br>   • Useful for abscess, perforation, pancreatitis; not needed to confirm SBP and would delay antibiotics.  </p></br><p>b. Blood culture<br /></br>   • May grow the organism (30-50 %), but positivity is neither sensitive nor specific for SBP and not diagnostic by itself.  </p></br><p>c. Culture and Gram stain of abdominal fluid<br /></br>   • Culture is done, but ≥50 % of SBP cases are culture-negative. Diagnosis hinges on PMN count, not culture. Gram stain is often negative.  </p></br><p>d. Exploratory laparotomy<br /></br>   • Reserved for surgically amenable pathology (perforation, peritonitis with source control); SBP is treated medically.  </p></br><p>e. Flat &amp; upright abdominal x-rays<br /></br>   • Look for free air/obstruction; cannot diagnose SBP.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a cirrhotic patient with ascites who acutely decompensates, the first and definitive diagnostic step is paracentesis with an ascitic fluid cell count and differential. A PMN count ≥ 250 cells/mm³ confirms spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and mandates immediate antibiotic therapy.</p>
118. A 26-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of a 3-month history of abdominal cramps, painful bowel movements, and worsening bloody diarrhea. During this time, she also has had fecal urgency and says she occasionally passes only mucus with some blood. She has had a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight loss during the past month despite no change in appetite. She has not had nausea or vomiting. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 57 kg (125 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37.8 °C (100 °F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 112/68 mm Hg. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Abdominal examination shows mild, diffuse tenderness; no masses are palpated. Bowel sounds are normal. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Rectal examination produces pain; there are streaks of blood on the physician’s gloved finger.  </br>Laboratory studies show:  </br>Hemoglobin                     9.5 g/dL  </br>Hematocrit                     29 percent  </br>Mean corpuscular volume        75 μm3  </br>Leukocyte count                9700/mm3  </br>Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 65 mm/h  </br>Serum  </br>  Protein, total               6.3 g/dL  </br>  Albumin                      3.5 g/dL  </br>  Ferritin                     <10 ng/mL  </br>  C-reactive protein           3.5 mg/L (N=0.08–3.1)  </br>Test of the stool for Clostridium difficile toxin is negative. A stool culture is negative. Colonoscopy shows continuous mucosal inflammation and ulceration from the rectum through the transverse colon. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Azathioprine therapy  </br>b. Infliximab therapy  </br>c. Mesalamine therapy  </br>d. Metronidazole therapy  </br>e. Total parenteral nutrition</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Mesalamine therapy  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues:<br /></br>  – 26-year-old woman with 3 months of crampy abdominal pain, fecal urgency, mucus- and blood-streaked diarrhea.<br /></br>  – Weight loss, low-grade fever, iron-deficiency anemia (microcytosis, low ferritin), ↑ESR/CRP = active inflammation.<br /></br>  – Colonoscopy: continuous inflammation beginning at the rectum and extending proximally through the transverse colon (classic for ulcerative colitis, UC).<br /></br>  – No systemic toxicity (stable vitals, no severe abdominal tenderness, no peritoneal signs). Disease extent = left-sided/extended but not pancolitis to cecum.   </p></br><p>• Pertinent negatives:<br /></br>  – No skip lesions, no ileal involvement, no perianal fistulae → rules out Crohn disease.<br /></br>  – Negative C. difficile/toxin and stool culture → infectious colitis excluded.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Identify inflammatory bowel disease pattern (continuous vs skip).<br /></br>2. Stage severity (mild-to-moderate vs severe/fulminant).<br /></br>3. Apply treatment ladder: 5-ASA → corticosteroid → immunomodulator → biologic → surgery.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Goal: induce remission of mild-to-moderate UC limited to colon. First-line induction and maintenance drug = 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA) compounds such as oral mesalamine (± rectal mesalamine suppositories/enemas for distal disease). They act topically on colonic mucosa to inhibit cyclooxygenase &amp; prostaglandin synthesis, decreasing inflammation with excellent safety profile.  </p></br><p>Take-home point: Mild–moderate UC → start 5-ASA (mesalamine or sulfasalazine). Escalate only if 5-ASA fails or disease is severe.  </p></br><p>Documentation items (real patient): number of bloody stools/day, HR/BP, abdominal exam findings, labs (CBC, CMP, CRP, stool cultures, C. diff test), colonoscopy extent and biopsies, treatment plan (start oral mesalamine 2–4 g/day, counseling, iron supplementation, follow-up).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Ulcerative colitis = idiopathic chronic inflammatory condition limited to mucosa/submucosa of colon beginning in rectum → continuous extension proximally. Driven by dysregulated immune response (Th2-type cytokine profile) in genetically susceptible host. Complications: severe hemorrhage, toxic megacolon, colorectal cancer (risk correlates with duration/extent), primary sclerosing cholangitis.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>UC step-wise algorithm:<br /></br>1. Mild–moderate: topical (rectal) ± oral 5-ASA (mesalamine, sulfasalazine, balsalazide, olsalazine).<br /></br>2. Inadequate response or moderate–severe flare: oral or IV corticosteroids (prednisone, budesonide/MMX).<br /></br>3. Steroid-dependent/refractory: immunomodulators (azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine) for maintenance, NOT induction.<br /></br>4. Biologics: anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab), vedolizumab, ustekinumab for refractory/severe disease.<br /></br>5. Curative surgery (total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis) for fulminant, dysplasia or cancer.  </p></br><p>WBC, CRP, ESR, fecal calprotectin are followed to gauge activity. Colonoscopy every 1–2 yrs starting 8 yrs after diagnosis if disease extends ≥30 cm from anus.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “UC is CONTINUOUS and CURABLE by Colectomy.”<br /></br>• “M’s for Mild UC: MesalaMine, Mouth (oral) &amp; bottom (rectal) route, Mainly mucosal.”<br /></br>• Treatment ladder mnemonic “5-CIBS:” 5-ASA → Corticosteroids → Immunomodulator → Biologic → Surgery.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Azathioprine therapy – Purine analog immunomodulator used for steroid-dependent or maintenance therapy, not first-line induction of mild disease. Slow onset (8–12 weeks), inappropriate acutely.  </p></br><p>b. Infliximab therapy – Anti-TNF biologic for moderate-severe or fistulizing disease or steroid-refractory UC. Over-treatment here; higher cost and risk of infection.  </p></br><p>d. Metronidazole therapy – Utilized in Crohn disease with perianal fistulas or anaerobic intraabdominal infection, not effective for UC mucosal inflammation.  </p></br><p>e. Total parenteral nutrition – Supportive measure in severe malnutrition or high-output fistulizing Crohn’s. This patient has normal BMI and can tolerate oral intake; TPN does not treat colonic inflammation.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Continuous rectal-to-transverse colonic inflammation with mild systemic symptoms = mild–moderate ulcerative colitis. The evidence-based first step is oral (± rectal) mesalamine, a 5-ASA agent, to induce and maintain remission.</p>
119. A 47-year-old man comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. He feels well. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. His mother was diagnosed with breast cancer at the age of 60 years; his father has hypertension and hyperlipidemia; and his maternal grandmother was diagnosed with colon cancer at the age of 47 years. The patient has smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 25 years and drinks three to four beers on weekends. He does not use illicit drugs. He is 183 cm (6 ft) tall and weighs 90 kg (199 lb); BMI is 27 kg/m². His pulse is 70/min, and blood pressure is 120/85 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for this patient regarding colon cancer screening?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Stool DNA testing now</br>b. Stool DNA testing at the age of 50 years</br>c. Colonoscopy now</br>d. Colonoscopy at the age of 50 years</br>e. Flexible sigmoidoscopy now</br>f. Flexible sigmoidoscopy at the age of 50 years</br></br></br><p>Correct answer<br /></br>D. Colonoscopy at the age of 50 years  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 47-year-old man – asymptomatic, routine visit<br /></br>• Only relative with colorectal cancer (CRC) is his maternal grandmother diagnosed at 47 y → a second-degree relative (grandparent).<br /></br>• No first-degree relative (parent, sibling, child) with CRC or advanced adenoma.<br /></br>• No personal history of inflammatory bowel disease, polyposis syndromes, or hereditary non-polyposis colon cancer (HNPCC/Lynch).  </p></br><p>Framework to approach CRC screening questions<br /></br>1. Ask: “Is the patient average-risk or increased-risk?”<br /></br>   – Increased risk = first-degree relative with CRC &lt;60 y or ≥2 first-degree relatives at any age → start at 40 y or 10 y before youngest case.<br /></br>2. If average risk → begin screening at 50 y (45 y per new ACS, but NBME/USPSTF still test 50 y).<br /></br>3. Choose a modality (colonoscopy q10 y is gold standard; others are acceptable alternatives when colonoscopy refused or unavailable).  </p></br><p>Applying to this vignette<br /></br>• Because his affected relative is a second-degree relative, he remains in the average-risk category.<br /></br>• Therefore screening begins at 50 y.<br /></br>• Colonoscopy every 10 y is the preferred test because it visualizes entire colon and allows polypectomy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify degree of relative with CRC → 2nd degree (grandmother).  </li></br><li>Assign risk level → average.  </li></br><li>Apply guideline → initiate screening at 50 y.  </li></br><li>Recommend modality → colonoscopy (most sensitive + therapeutic).  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: Only first-degree relatives influence the starting age; second- or third-degree relatives do not accelerate screening.  </p></br><p>Documentation point: In the note you would list “Average-risk for CRC; plan: screening colonoscopy at age 50, repeat q10 y if normal.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Most colorectal cancers arise from adenomatous polyps via the adenoma-carcinoma sequence (APC → KRAS → p53). Colonoscopy detects and removes precancerous lesions, interrupting this progression, which is why it is the preferred screening tool.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Algorithm  </p></br><p>Average-risk patients<br /></br>• Start at 50 y (USPSTF/NBME).<br /></br>• Modalities (choose one):<br /></br>  – Colonoscopy q10 y (best)<br /></br>  – CT colonography q5 y<br /></br>  – FIT annually<br /></br>  – FIT-DNA (stool DNA) q1-3 y<br /></br>  – Flexible sigmoidoscopy q5 y (+ FIT q3 y)  </p></br><p>Increased-risk patients<br /></br>• First-degree relative &lt;60 y or ≥2 first-degree relatives: start at 40 y or 10 y before youngest case; colonoscopy q5 y.<br /></br>• IBD, polyposis, Lynch: disease-specific protocols.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“1st-degree = 1 decade earlier (40) or 10 years before”<br /></br>“2nd-degree = standard date (50) — second place gets standard ribbon”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Stool DNA testing now – Too early; patient is only 47 y and average-risk. Start at 50 y.  </p></br><p>B. Stool DNA testing at 50 y – Age is correct, but colonoscopy is the preferred test when no contraindication; stool DNA is an alternative for those who decline colonoscopy.  </p></br><p>C. Colonoscopy now – Would be correct only if he had a first-degree relative with CRC &lt;60 y or two first-degree relatives; not warranted for second-degree relative.  </p></br><p>E. Flexible sigmoidoscopy now – Same timing error as C; also evaluates only distal colon.  </p></br><p>F. Flexible sigmoidoscopy at 50 y – Age is right, but colonoscopy visualizes the entire colon and allows polyp removal, making it the superior recommendation when feasible.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Over-reacting to any family history rather than differentiating first- vs second-degree relatives.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Summary  </p></br><p>This patient is average-risk because his only affected relative is a second-degree relative. For average-risk adults, the USPSTF and most NBME questions endorse starting colorectal cancer screening with colonoscopy at age 50 and repeating every 10 years if results are normal.</p>
120. A 72-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 3-day history of bright red rectal bleeding. She also has had moderate left-sided lower abdominal cramps that decrease in intensity shortly after the passage of bloody stools. She has no history of similar symptoms. A colonoscopy at the age of 65 years showed mild diverticular disease and no polyps or cancers. She has coronary artery disease and cardiomyopathy with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 15 percent. Her medications are an ACE inhibitor, a β-adrenergic blocking agent, oral nitrates, a statin, an aldosterone receptor antagonist, and a loop diuretic. The dose of the loop diuretic was increased 10 days ago during a routine examination because of volume overload. She appears healthy and is not in acute distress. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 90/65 mm Hg. There is no jugular venous distention with the patient laying at a 45-degree angle. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiovascular examination shows a displaced point of maximal impulse and a soft S3 gallop. Abdominal examination shows tenderness to deep palpation in the left lower quadrant with minimal guarding; there is no rebound. Bowel sounds are normal. The liver cannot be palpated. Rectal examination shows scant stool with a small amount of blood on the examination glove. There is no edema of the lower extremities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Colon cancer</br>b. Diverticulitis</br>c. Ischemic colitis</br>d. Mesenteric venous thrombosis</br>e. Sigmoid volvulus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Ischemic colitis  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Bright-red rectal bleeding + crampy, left-sided abdominal pain that improves after stool passage = classic pain-then-bleeding sequence of ischemic colitis.<br /></br>• Age 72 + extensive atherosclerotic/low-flow state (severe cardiomyopathy, EF 15 %, recent diuretic up-titration, hypotension 90/65) → markedly increases risk for watershed colonic hypoperfusion (splenic flexure, rectosigmoid junction).<br /></br>• Mild LLQ tenderness without peritoneal signs supports mucosal/submucosal injury, not full-thickness perforation.<br /></br>• Absence of fever, marked leukocytosis, obstruction, or chronic “B-symptoms” helps rule out competing diagnoses (diverticulitis, volvulus, cancer, etc.).</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>1. Identify bleeding pattern (hematochezia vs melena).<br /></br>2. Localize pain; LLQ pain + hematochezia in an older vascular patient → think ischemia.<br /></br>3. Screen for low-flow precipitants (hypotension, CHF, diuretics, dialysis).<br /></br>4. Rule out mimics by looking for their “must-have” findings (fever &amp; no bleeding = diverticulitis; obstructive picture = volvulus; occult/chronic symptoms = cancer).</p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning / Take-Home Message<br /></br>Ischemic colitis = transient hypoperfusion of watershed colon → mucosal sloughing → sudden crampy pain followed by bloody diarrhea/hematochezia. Most often seen in elderly with atherosclerosis or any recent systemic hypotensive/low-flow episode (sepsis, CHF exacerbation, vigorous diuresis, cardiac surgery, etc.). Document abdominal exam, stool appearance, hemodynamics, precipitating medications, and vascular history.</p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Non-occlusive hypoperfusion (MC), arterial/venous thrombosis, emboli.<br /></br>• Watershed zones: Splenic flexure (SMA-IMA), rectosigmoid junction (IMA-hypogastric).<br /></br>• Injury limited to mucosa/submucosa → self-limited in &gt;80 %, but can progress to gangrene/perforation if severe.</p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management<br /></br>1. Initial: CBC (mild leukocytosis), metabolic panel, lactate, stool cultures to exclude infection.<br /></br>2. Imaging: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast → segmental colonic wall thickening (“thumbprinting”), pericolonic stranding, mesenteric edema.<br /></br>3. Confirmation: Colonoscopy (after stabilization) shows pale edematous mucosa with petechial hemorrhages &amp; ulcerations.<br /></br>4. Treatment:<br /></br>   • Supportive—IV fluids, bowel rest, correct precipitating factor (reduce diuretic, improve perfusion).<br /></br>   • Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone + metronidazole) to cover translocated gut flora.<br /></br>   • Surgery only if peritonitis, necrosis, or persistent bleeding.</p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Pain then stain” mnemonic: Colonic pain followed by a blood stain  think ischemic colitis.<br /></br>Watershed = “Where two rivers meet, drought hurts first” → splenic flexure &amp; rectosigmoid junction.</p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Colon cancer<br /></br>• Bleeding is usually occult or low-grade; when brisk, pain is uncommon.<br /></br>• Presents chronically with weight loss, anemia, change in stool caliber—not an acute 3-day episode with cramps.  </p></br><p>b. Diverticulitis<br /></br>• LLQ pain + fever/leukocytosis; bleeding is rare (diverticular bleeding is painless).<br /></br>• CT would reveal inflamed diverticula, pericolic fat stranding; here we have bleeding plus minimal guarding and no systemic signs.  </p></br><p>d. Mesenteric venous thrombosis<br /></br>• Classically causes diffuse mid-abdominal pain out of proportion to exam and often involves small intestine; risk factors include hypercoagulable states, portal HTN, pancreatitis—none noted.<br /></br>• Hematochezia is less common early.  </p></br><p>e. Sigmoid volvulus<br /></br>• Presents with abdominal distension, obstipation, vomiting; x-ray “coffee bean.”<br /></br>• Bleeding is not typical, and pain is colicky without initial hematochezia.  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>In an elderly patient with vascular disease or recent hypotension/diuresis who develops sudden LLQ pain followed by hematochezia, ischemic colitis is the leading diagnosis.</p>
121. A 52-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 5-day history of continuous abdominal pain, nausea, and decreased appetite. Nine days ago, she underwent an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic cholelithiasis. She was discharged from the hospital 7 days ago. Her temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), pulse is 108/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 102/64 mm Hg. Examination shows jaundice. Abdominal examination shows right upper quadrant tenderness. Bowel sounds are present throughout all quadrants. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin               12.2 g/dL</br>Hematocrit               36 percent</br>Leukocyte count          17,200/mm3</br>Serum</br>    Urea nitrogen        48 mg/dL</br>    Glucose              186 mg/dL</br>    Creatinine           1.5 mg/dL</br>    Total bilirubin      7.2 mg/dL</br>    Alkaline phosphatase 251 U/L</br>    AST                  84 U/L</br>Ultrasonography of the abdomen shows no fluid collections. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. CT scan of the abdomen</br>b. HIDA scan</br>c. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography</br>d. Transhepatic cholangiography</br>e. Exploratory laparotomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP)</p></br><p>──────────────────────── Vignette Analysis ────────────────────────<br /></br>Key clues that should immediately narrow the differential:</p></br><ol></br><li>Timing: 9 days after laparoscopic cholecystectomy (common window for retained CBD stone or bile‐duct injury).  </li></br><li>“Continuous abdominal pain, nausea, decreased appetite” + fever (38.2 °C) + tachycardia + hypotension → systemic inflammatory response.  </li></br><li>Charcot triad present:<br /></br>   • Fever<br /></br>   • Right-upper-quadrant (RUQ) pain<br /></br>   • Jaundice<br /></br>   → Classic for acute ascending cholangitis.  </li></br><li>Lab pattern:<br /></br>   • Marked conjugated hyperbilirubinemia (total bili 7.2 mg/dL)<br /></br>   • Cholestatic enzymes elevated (alk phos 251 U/L &gt; AST 84 U/L).<br /></br>   • Leukocytosis, mild AKI (Cr 1.5 mg/dL) → septic physiology.  </li></br><li>RUQ ultrasound shows no fluid collection—rules out biloma or abscess, but cannot visualize distal CBD well post-op.</li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive framework:</br>• Post-cholecystectomy jaundice + cholestatic labs → ask “Is there obstruction?”<br /></br>• Charcot triad or Reynold pentad (add hypotension/confusion) → urgent biliary drainage.<br /></br>• ERCP is both diagnostic (defines level of obstruction/stone vs leak) and therapeutic (sphincterotomy, stone extraction, stent).</p></br><p>────────────────────── Clinical Critical Reasoning ──────────────────<br /></br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Recognize biliary obstruction with sepsis → ascending cholangitis.<br /></br>2. Initial imaging (US) negative for abscess; still need to visualize duct.<br /></br>3. Next best test must allow rapid decompression of biliary tree.<br /></br>   • ERCP: quickest endoscopic route; can cannulate, inject contrast for diagnosis, and relieve obstruction (stone retrieval, stent).<br /></br>   • Delay increases risk of septic shock.<br /></br>Take-home: In suspected cholangitis, ERCP is the test of choice and should be performed emergently.</p></br><p>Documentation note: Record sepsis screen, Charcot triad, hemodynamics, plan for emergent ERCP with IV antibiotics (eg, piperacillin-tazobactam) pre-procedure.</p></br><p>────────────────────── Pathophysiology Review ─────────────────────<br /></br>• Retained common bile duct (CBD) stone after cholecystectomy → stasis → bacterial ascent (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus) → inflammation of biliary tree.<br /></br>• Obstruction raises intraductal pressure → translocation of bacteria/endotoxin → systemic response.<br /></br>• Without drainage mortality &gt;50%; with prompt ERCP &lt;10%.</p></br><p>───────────────── Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review ──────────────<br /></br>1. Immediate resuscitation + coverage with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (pip-tazo, cefepime-metronidazole, or meropenem).<br /></br>2. ERCP within 24 h:<br /></br>   • Cholangiogram delineates obstruction.<br /></br>   • Endoscopic sphincterotomy + stone extraction or plastic/metal stent.<br /></br>3. Percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) reserved if ERCP fails or anatomy precludes (e.g., Roux-en-Y gastric bypass).<br /></br>4. Elective cholecystectomy prevents recurrence—already completed here.</p></br><p>────────────────────── Memorization Tips ───────────────────────────<br /></br>“Charcot ERCP” mnemonic:<br /></br>C – Cholangitis<br /></br>H – Has<br /></br>A – Automatic<br /></br>R – Rescue (drainage) via<br /></br>C – Cannulation (ERCP)<br /></br>O – Or (if failed) PTC<br /></br>T – Today!</p></br><p>───────────────────── Incorrect Answer Analysis ─────────────────────<br /></br>a. CT scan of the abdomen<br /></br>   • Useful for abscess, pancreatitis, leak, or perforation. Does NOT define biliary tree and cannot treat obstruction. Delays definitive therapy.</p></br><p>b. HIDA scan (cholescintigraphy)<br /></br>   • Detects cystic duct obstruction or bile leak (biloma) post-op. Patient has jaundice and cholestatic pattern, not expected with leak; no therapeutic benefit.</p></br><p>d. Transhepatic cholangiography (PTC)<br /></br>   • Alternative drainage when ERCP impossible. More invasive; higher bleeding risk. ERCP is preferred first-line in a post-cholecystectomy patient with accessible ampulla.</p></br><p>e. Exploratory laparotomy<br /></br>   • Last resort when endoscopic/percutaneous methods fail or for uncontrolled peritonitis. Unnecessary and overly invasive given endoscopic options.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core takeaway: Ascending cholangitis after cholecystectomy demands urgent ERCP for combined diagnosis and biliary decompression.</p>
122. Four days after admission to the hospital for management of cellulitis of the right lower extremity, a 70-year-old woman has an increased serum creatinine concentration. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her medications are glipizide and a multivitamin; she also has received intravenous nafcillin since admission. She is retired from the military. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination of the trunk shows no abnormalities. There is edema, erythema, and warmth of the right lower extremity. Examination of the left lower extremity shows no abnormalities. On admission, her serum creatinine concentration was 0.9 mg/dL. Today, laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 11 g/dL  </br>Hemoglobin A1c 7.5 percent  </br>Leukocyte count 15,000/mm^3  </br>  Segmented neutrophils 75 percent  </br>  Bands 1 percent  </br>  Eosinophils 8 percent  </br>  Lymphocytes 14 percent  </br>  Monocytes 2 percent  </br>Serum  </br>  K+ 5 mEq/L  </br>  Urea nitrogen 60 mg/dL  </br>  Creatinine 2.3 mg/dL  </br>  Phosphorus 4.3 mg/dL  </br>  Albumin 2 g/dL  </br>  Cholesterol 250 mg/dL  </br>Urine  </br>  pH 6.00  </br>  Protein trace  </br>  RBC 10/hpf  </br>  WBC 20-50/hpf  </br>  RBC casts 0/lpf  </br>  WBC casts 0/lpf  </br>Serum complement concentrations are within the reference ranges. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Add ciprofloxacin to the medication regimen  </br>b. Add prednisone to the medication regimen  </br>c. Discontinue nafcillin therapy  </br>d. Schedule hemodialysis  </br>e. Continue current medication regimen</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Discontinue nafcillin therapy  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Timeline: Serum creatinine rose from 0.9 → 2.3 mg/dL only 4 days after starting IV nafcillin. A rapid rise after a new drug is a red flag for drug-induced kidney injury.<br /></br>• Urinalysis:<br /></br>  – WBC 20-50/hpf (sterile pyuria)<br /></br>  – RBC 10/hpf, trace protein, NO RBC or WBC casts<br /></br>  Pattern is classic for acute interstitial nephritis (AIN), not glomerulonephritis (would have RBC casts) or ATN (would have muddy brown casts).<br /></br>• Peripheral eosinophilia (8%)—another AIN clue.<br /></br>• Normal complement—helps exclude immune-complex GN.<br /></br>Key takeaway: New beta-lactam + eosinophils + pyuria/hematuria + rising creatinine ⇒ acute interstitial nephritis.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework for similar NBME items<br /></br>1. Ask: “Any new drug, infection, hypotension, obstruction?”<br /></br>2. Correlate time course with mechanism (days-weeks for AIN, minutes-hours for contrast nephropathy, etc.).<br /></br>3. Check UA pattern: casts vs. free cells, protein level, complements.<br /></br>4. Stop the culprit drug first; only escalate (steroids, dialysis) if renal function fails to improve.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Identify kidney injury: ΔCr &gt; 0.3 mg/dL in 48 h (AKI definition) → yes.<br /></br>2. Classify AKI type:<br /></br>   • Pre-renal? — Vitals, exam, BUN/Cr (60/2.3 ≈ 26), but urine indices not supplied; no volume depletion clues.<br /></br>   • Intrinsic? — Abnormal UA with WBCs/RBCs suggests intrinsic.<br /></br>3. Sub-type intrinsic injury:<br /></br>   • ATN: muddy casts, no eosinophilia.<br /></br>   • AIN: WBCs ± eosinophilia, recent drug.<br /></br>   • GN: RBC casts, proteinuria, low complement.<br /></br>4. Match to vignette → AIN.<br /></br>5. First-line management of AIN = stop the offending drug.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Acute interstitial nephritis is a T-cell–mediated hypersensitivity to medications (β-lactams, sulfa, NSAIDs, PPIs, diuretics). Removing the trigger reverses injury in most patients.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Impression: Drug-induced acute interstitial nephritis likely secondary to nafcillin. Plan: Discontinue nafcillin, switch to alternate anti-staphylococcal agent, trend renal panel q12 h, consider renal biopsy or steroids if no improvement in 3–5 days.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Mechanism: Type IV hypersensitivity → interstitial edema, infiltration by T-cells, eosinophils.<br /></br>• Typical onset: 3–10 days (previous exposure) or 1–2 weeks (first exposure).<br /></br>• Classic triad (rarely all present): fever, rash, eosinophilia (“F-R-E”).<br /></br>• UA: sterile pyuria, WBC casts, mild protein, microscopic hematuria; eosinophiluria (Hansel stain).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. Discontinue culprit drug (main therapy).<br /></br>2. Supportive care: optimize volume, avoid further nephrotoxins.<br /></br>3. Steroids (e.g., prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg) reserved for persistent or severe AKI after drug withdrawal.<br /></br>4. Dialysis only if refractory volume overload, severe hyperkalemia, uremic complications.<br /></br>5. Renal biopsy rarely needed unless diagnosis unclear.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Tips<br /></br>“P’s cause interstitial nephritis— Pee (diuretics), Pain-free (NSAIDs), Penicillins/cephalosporins, Proton-pump inhibitors, rifamPin.”<br /></br>Think “β-Lactam = Basophils &amp; Leukocytes (eosinophils) in urine.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Add ciprofloxacin – Unnecessary; nafcillin already covers MSSA. Ciprofloxacin does not treat the renal problem and adds another nephrotoxin risk.<br /></br>b. Add prednisone – Steroids can speed recovery, but only after the offending drug has been stopped and if creatinine fails to improve. Not the first step.<br /></br>d. Schedule hemodialysis – Indicated only for life-threatening complications (K⁺ &gt; 6.5, refractory acidosis, fluid overload, uremic pericarditis/encephalopathy). Her creatinine 2.3 mg/dL with stable vitals does not meet criteria.<br /></br>e. Continue current regimen – Ignores the obvious causative agent and allows ongoing renal damage.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Concept<br /></br>In any hospitalized patient who develops unexplained AKI, always look for a recently started drug. Acute interstitial nephritis is reversible once the trigger is removed—stop the drug first.</p>
123. A 52-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 2, comes to the physician because of increasingly frequent loss of urine during the past 2 years. She often feels an overwhelming urge to void and completely saturates an absorbent pad before reaching the bathroom. The loss of urine occurs most frequently when she steps out of her car. She also awakens three to four times nightly to void. She has not had any loss of urine with coughing or sneezing. She has hypertension treated with a diuretic. Vital signs are within normal limits. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. The external genitalia, vagina, and cervix appear normal. The uterus and adnexae are normal to palpation. There is no loss of urine with Valsalva maneuver or with coughing. Her postvoid residual volume is 50 mL. Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Overactive bladder with incontinence</br>b. Overflow incontinence</br>c. Stress incontinence</br>d. Urinary tract infection</br>e. Vesicovaginal fistula</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>• Overactive bladder with incontinence (urge incontinence)</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key diagnostic clues<br /></br>1. “Overwhelming urge to void” and “completely saturates an absorbent pad before reaching the bathroom” → classic urgency + inability to defer urination.<br /></br>2. Leakage is precipitated by the “key‐in‐lock” situation (stepping out of the car) → buzz-phrase for urge incontinence.<br /></br>3. No leakage with cough/Valsalva and specifically denies loss of urine with coughing or sneezing → argues strongly against stress incontinence.<br /></br>4. Post-void residual (PVR) = 50 mL (normal &lt; 50–100 mL) → rules out overflow incontinence, which requires a high PVR.<br /></br>5. Normal pelvic exam, normal UA → excludes fistula and urinary tract infection.<br /></br>6. Nocturia (awakens 3–4×/night) → another urge incontinence feature.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>• First sort urinary incontinence questions into the “big three”: stress, urge, overflow.<br /></br>• Look for the “trigger + volume + PVR” triad:<br /></br>  – Stress: exertion trigger, small leaks, normal PVR.<br /></br>  – Urge: urgency trigger, large volume loss, normal PVR.<br /></br>  – Overflow: constant dribble, high PVR.<br /></br>• Always consider reversible causes → UA, PVR supplied in vignette.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning &amp; Core Take-away  </p></br><p>Detrusor muscle overactivity (uninhibited bladder contractions) produces urgency, frequency, nocturia, and large-volume leakage before a patient can reach the toilet. A normal PVR distinguishes it from overflow incontinence. Absence of stress maneuvers causing leakage rules out stress incontinence.</p></br><p>Document in real encounter<br /></br>• Frequency, nocturia, urgency, pad count<br /></br>• Pelvic exam findings<br /></br>• PVR result<br /></br>• UA to exclude infection<br /></br>• Discuss lifestyle factors (diuretics, caffeine)</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Urge incontinence = overactive detrusor contractions.<br /></br>Etiologies: idiopathic (most common), neurologic disease (stroke, MS, spinal cord injury), bladder outlet obstruction, aging, medications (e.g., diuretics aggravate). Detrusor contracts at low bladder volumes, leading to sudden leakage.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>First-line:<br /></br>   • Behavioral therapy: bladder training (scheduled voids with progressive delay), pelvic floor muscle training.<br /></br>   • Lifestyle: limit caffeine/alcohol, adjust timing of diuretic.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pharmacologic (if refractory to behavior):<br /></br>   • Antimuscarinics: oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin, trospium.<br /></br>   • β3-agonist: mirabegron (good if anticholinergic side-effects problematic).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Third-line: intradetrusor botulinum toxin A or sacral neuromodulation.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“OUt of control” = Overactive (Urge) →<br /></br>• O = Overwhelming urge<br /></br>• U = Urine gushes before toilet<br /></br>• t = tiny post-void residual  </p></br><p>Key-in-lock trick: “When the key’s in the door, the bladder hits the floor.” → visualize patient dancing at the door/car.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Overflow incontinence<br /></br>• Presentation: continuous dribbling, weak stream, high PVR (&gt;200 mL), often diabetic neuropathy or obstruction (BPH). None present here; PVR only 50 mL.</p></br><p>C. Stress incontinence<br /></br>• Leakage with ↑ intra-abdominal pressure (cough, sneeze, laugh), positive cough stress test, small volumes. This patient explicitly denies stress leaks and exam is negative.</p></br><p>D. Urinary tract infection<br /></br>• Would show dysuria, frequency, possibly hematuria; UA would have pyuria ≥10 WBC/hpf or nitrites/leukocyte esterase. Her UA is normal.</p></br><p>E. Vesicovaginal fistula<br /></br>• Painless continuous leakage day and night, typically after pelvic surgery or obstructed labor; pelvic exam often detects pooling/blue dye. She has intermittent urgency-related leaks and a normal exam.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Urgency, nocturia, large-volume leakage with normal PVR = overactive bladder (urge) incontinence. Recognize the pattern, rule out overflow with PVR, and start with bladder training ± antimuscarinic therapy.</p>
124. A 32-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 18 weeks' gestation comes to the office for a routine prenatal visit. She has no history of operative procedures, and pregnancy has been uncomplicated. She has hypothyroidism and asthma. Her medications are levothyroxine, fluticasone, and a prenatal vitamin. Immunizations are up-to-date. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated and ended in spontaneous vaginal delivery at term 3 years ago. She is married and works as an international airline pilot. Recently, she has made frequent trips to South America and the Pacific Islands for work. Fetal heart rate is 125/min. Examination shows a uterus consistent in size with an 18-week gestation. Fetal anatomic survey shows microcephaly and a clubbed foot. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Amphotericin B therapy</br>b. Famciclovir therapy</br>c. Folic acid supplementation</br>d. Immune globulin therapy</br>e. No pharmacotherapy is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. No pharmacotherapy is indicated  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>1. 18-week fetus with microcephaly and clubfoot (talipes equinovarus).<br /></br>2. Mother is an international airline pilot with “frequent trips to South America and the Pacific Islands.”<br /></br>3. Otherwise uncomplicated pregnancy; no mention of fever, rash, or other acute maternal illness.</p></br><p>Why they matter:<br /></br>• Microcephaly + travel to endemic regions virtually screams congenital Zika virus infection (Aedes mosquito transmission in Central/South America &amp; Pacific).<br /></br>• Clubfoot/arthrogryposis are common contracture findings reported in congenital Zika.<br /></br>• There is currently NO effective antiviral, immune globulin, or other specific medical therapy for Zika in pregnancy. Management is purely supportive (serial ultrasounds ± maternal counseling regarding pregnancy continuation).</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>1. Ask: “Which congenital infections → calcifications / microcephaly?” (CMV, toxo, Zika).<br /></br>2. Scan vignette for geographic/travel, dietary, pet, or rash clues to narrow list.<br /></br>3. Once Zika suspected → recall: no treatment, only prevention (mosquito protection, sexual abstinence/condoms, screen travelers).</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Abnormal US (microcephaly + limb deformities) at 18 wks → think congenital infection or genetic syndrome.<br /></br>2. Maternal travel to Zika-endemic areas → highest‐yield risk factor.<br /></br>3. Ask: “What therapy exists?”<br /></br>   • There is NONE; only diagnostic confirmation (maternal Zika PCR or serology, amniotic fluid PCR) and counseling.<br /></br>4. Therefore → select “No pharmacotherapy is indicated.”</p></br><p>Take-home message: For suspected congenital Zika virus, management is surveillance and counseling, not medication.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient):<br /></br>• Detailed travel history, mosquito exposure, sexual partners’ travel.<br /></br>• Ultrasound findings, Zika testing offered, counseling re prognosis and pregnancy options, plan for serial growth scans.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>• Zika virus: Flavivirus, +ssRNA, transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquito or sexually.<br /></br>• Neurotropic → infects fetal neural progenitor cells → cortical thinning, calcifications, ventriculomegaly, microcephaly.<br /></br>• Joint immobility in utero → contractures (clubfoot, arthrogryposis).<br /></br>• Maternal infection often mild (low-grade fever, maculopapular rash, conjunctivitis) or asymptomatic.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management  </p></br><p>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Maternal Zika RT-PCR (serum &amp; urine) ≤2 wks after symptom onset OR Zika IgM + PRNT &gt;2 wks.<br /></br>• Amniotic fluid RT-PCR can be offered after 15 wks.<br /></br>• Serial fetal ultrasounds q3-4 wks for growth &amp; brain anomalies.  </p></br><p>Management:<br /></br>• Supportive only.<br /></br>• Offer detailed counseling; discuss option of pregnancy termination where legal if severe findings.<br /></br>• Postnatal evaluation for congenital Zika: head circumference, neuro exam, ophthalmology, hearing screen.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“ZIKA”:<br /></br>Z – Zoonotic mosquito (Aedes)<br /></br>I – Incurable In utero (no therapy)<br /></br>K – “Kid’s” skull small (microcephaly)<br /></br>A – Arthrogryposis (limb contractures)</p></br><p>Picture a small-headed baby (“micro”) wearing tiny “Zika sneakers” (clubfeet) swatting mosquitos—reminds you of microcephaly + clubfoot from mosquito-borne Zika, no meds available.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Amphotericin B therapy<br /></br>• Used for systemic fungal infections (e.g., histoplasmosis, cryptococcosis). Congenital fungal infections rarely cause isolated microcephaly/clubfoot. Geographic travel fits endemic fungi, but US findings &amp; presentation fit Zika. Amphotericin B toxicity (nephrotoxicity) makes its empiric use untenable.</p></br><p>B. Famciclovir therapy<br /></br>• An oral prodrug of penciclovir, active vs. HSV/VZV; acyclovir is preferred in pregnancy. Congenital HSV typically presents with skin lesions, ocular/cerebral involvement, NOT isolated microcephaly and limb contractures. Travel history irrelevant.</p></br><p>C. Folic acid supplementation<br /></br>• Prevents neural tube defects (spina bifida, anencephaly) when started pre-conception. Microcephaly at 18 wks is already established; folate will not reverse it. Patient is already on prenatal vitamins.</p></br><p>D. Immune globulin therapy<br /></br>• Variants: VZV IG (post-exposure), Rh-Ig (prevent isoimmunization), CMV hyper-IG (investigational). None target Zika. Giving IG provides no fetal benefit here.</p></br><p>E. No pharmacotherapy is indicated<br /></br>• Correct for suspected congenital Zika virus infection; focus on surveillance and counseling.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</p>
125. A 28-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 12 weeks’ gestation comes to the office for her first prenatal visit. She feels well, and pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Her first pregnancy ended in cesarean delivery at 41 weeks’ gestation because of an active herpes simplex lesion. The newborn weighed 4763 g (10 lb 8 oz) at birth; growth and development have been appropriate for age. The patient has no other history of serious illness and takes no medications. She has a brother and father with type 2 diabetes mellitus. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall. She weighed 86 kg (190 lb) prior to pregnancy; BMI was 35 kg/m2. She has had a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight gain during this pregnancy. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 12-week gestation. Pelvic ultrasonography confirms gestational age. Which of the following is the most appropriate screening test for this patient at this visit?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Glucose tolerance test</br>b. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br>c. Quadruple screening</br>d. Serum herpes simplex antibody testing</br>e. Serum lipid studies</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Glucose tolerance test (early screening for gestational diabetes mellitus)</p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Pertinent positives<br /></br>  – Prior macrosomic infant: 4763 g (&gt;4500 g)<br /></br>  – Obesity: prepregnancy BMI 35 kg/m²<br /></br>  – 1st-degree relatives with type 2 DM (brother, father)<br /></br>  – Current pregnancy is still early (12 weeks) → opportunity for “early” rather than routine 24-28 wk screening.  </p></br><p>• Pertinent negatives<br /></br>  – No thyroid symptoms, no personal thyroid history<br /></br>  – No maternal age–related aneuploidy risk factors given yet (quad screen timing)<br /></br>  – Already known HSV infection; no need for serology confirmation.  </p></br><p>Mnemonic for high-risk GDM screening “BIG MOM”:<br /></br>B-MI ≥30,<br /></br>I-nfant large in prior pregnancy,<br /></br>G-lucose relatives (1º relative with DM),<br /></br>M-ultiple gestations,<br /></br>O-besity,<br /></br>M-edications (steroids, antipsychotics).<br /></br>If any item present, screen at the first prenatal visit. This patient has three.</p></br><p>Cognitive strategy: On test day, whenever you see obesity + previous macrosomic baby, immediately think “early GDM screen.”</p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify risk category: high-risk for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) per ACOG, ADA.<br /></br>2. Management algorithm:<br /></br>   • High-risk → 50-g 1-hr glucose challenge test (or 75-g 2-hr OGTT) at first visit.<br /></br>   • If negative, repeat standard screening at 24-28 wk because placental hormones later cause insulin resistance.<br /></br>3. Documentation for real patient: indication for early GDM screen, type of test ordered, patient counseling regarding diet/exercise pending results.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: High-risk women require immediate glucose screening; do not wait until the routine 24-28 week window.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Pregnancy hormones (human placental lactogen, progesterone, cortisol, growth hormone) create progressive insulin resistance.<br /></br>• Obesity and prior macrosomia indicate baseline insulin resistance, so hyperglycemia can appear earlier.<br /></br>• Early detection prevents fetal hyperinsulinemia, shoulder dystocia, stillbirth, maternal complications.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments / Diagnostics Recap<br /></br>• Screening test: non-fasting 50 g oral glucose challenge, plasma glucose measured at 1 hr.<br /></br>   – Threshold ≥130–140 mg/dL → diagnostic 3-hr 100 g OGTT.<br /></br>• If GDM confirmed: start diet/exercise, then pharmacotherapy (insulin first-line; metformin or glyburide second-line).<br /></br>• Follow-up postpartum 6-12 wk 75-g OGTT because 50% develop overt DM within 10 yr.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Screen FAST for SUGAR if she was LARGE before”: Large previous baby or Large BMI = Fast (early) glucose screen.<br /></br>Visual: Picture a stoplight at the first prenatal visit—obese mom holding a giant 10-lb baby doll; the sign says, “Test Glucose NOW.”</p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Serum TSH: Universal TSH screening is NOT recommended; reserved for symptoms, personal thyroid disease, type 1 DM, or infertility. None present.<br /></br>c. Quadruple screen: Tests for aneuploidy &amp; NTDs; performed at 15-20 weeks. Too early at 12 weeks.<br /></br>d. Serum HSV antibody: She already has a clinically confirmed HSV infection (prior active lesion). Serology would not alter management; intrapartum prophylaxis (acyclovir at 36 wk) is based on history, not titers.<br /></br>e. Lipid studies: Pregnancy physiology drastically alters lipid panels; routine lipid screening is deferred until postpartum unless extreme familial hyperlipidemia suspected.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Obesity, prior macrosomic infant, and strong family history make this patient high-risk for gestational diabetes. Therefore, an oral glucose tolerance test at her first prenatal visit is the most appropriate next step.</p>
126. One day after uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery of a healthy newborn at term, a 17-year-old patient, gravida 1, para 1, has severe right nipple pain when attempting to breast-feed. She has no history of serious illness, and her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. She is crying and frustrated. Examination of the right nipple shows erythema and no discharge. Examination of the left nipple shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for this patient?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Acetaminophen therapy before breast-feeding</br>b. Applying lidocaine over the nipples</br>c. Bottle-feeding pumped breast milk</br>d. Repositioning of the infant’s mouth</br>e. Switching to formula feeding</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Repositioning of the infant’s mouth  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Timing: “One day after uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery” → the very first attempts at breast-feeding.  </li></br><li>Chief problem: “severe right nipple pain … erythema and no discharge.”<br /></br>   • Pain + mild erythema WITHOUT fever, systemic symptoms, purulent discharge, or induration → rules out mastitis or abscess.  </li></br><li>Unilateral involvement and the mother is “crying and frustrated” → classic picture of a poor latch (improper infant positioning) that traumatizes one nipple.</li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>• In the first 48 h postpartum, always think “latch problem” before infection.<br /></br>• Ask: 1) systemic signs? 2) bilateral vs unilateral? 3) timing (&gt;3–4 d suggests engorgement/mastitis).<br /></br>If (1) absent, unilateral, and very early → fix the latch.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning / Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>Poor positioning causes the infant to pull on the nipple tip rather than draw the entire areola into the mouth, leading to focal erythema, cracking, and severe pain.<br /></br>Step-wise reasoning:<br /></br>• Severe nipple pain day 1 → think mechanical trauma.<br /></br>• Mechanical trauma → intervention must correct mechanics, i.e., help mother achieve a deep latch: infant’s mouth wide open, lips flanged, chin and nose touching breast, more areola visible above baby’s upper lip than below the lower lip.<br /></br>• Therefore, “Repositioning of the infant’s mouth” is the best answer.</p></br><p>What you would document: maternal report of pain, nipple exam findings, teaching given regarding proper latch and positioning, patient’s response, and plan for follow-up.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Nipple pain &lt;1 week postpartum = 70-90 % caused by poor latch.<br /></br>  – Compression/friction → epithelial microtrauma → erythema, cracks, bleeding.<br /></br>• Other causes (rarer this early): candidal infection (burning, shiny nipple, baby thrush), mastitis (fever, wedge-shaped erythema, 2–4 wk postpartum), engorgement (bilateral, firm breasts).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Review  </p></br><p>First-line management of latch trauma:<br /></br>1. Lactation consultation to correct positioning/latch.<br /></br>2. Continue breast-feeding; short, frequent feeds.<br /></br>3. Express a few drops of breast milk and let dry on nipple (antibacterial, promotes healing).<br /></br>4. Analgesia (acetaminophen/ibuprofen) is adjunctive, not primary.<br /></br>No imaging or labs are needed.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Day-1 Nipple Pain = Bad Latch, Fix the Catch.”<br /></br>Visualize a baby’s mouth like a Pac-Man: wide open, covers the cookie (areola), not just the chocolate chip (nipple).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Acetaminophen therapy before breast-feeding<br /></br>   • May blunt pain but does NOT correct the underlying mechanical cause; pain will persist and trauma will worsen.</p></br><p>b. Applying lidocaine over the nipples<br /></br>   • Potentially numbs infant’s oral cavity, impairs suck, and still doesn’t fix the latch.</p></br><p>c. Bottle-feeding pumped breast milk<br /></br>   • Breaks establishment of breast-feeding, increases risk of “nipple confusion,” and doesn’t solve latch technique. Reserve only if mother cannot feed directly due to severe trauma or infection.</p></br><p>e. Switching to formula feeding<br /></br>   • Unnecessary, undermines breast-feeding benefits, and fails to address the simple mechanical issue.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Early, focal nipple pain without systemic signs is almost always due to improper latch. First fix the latch → “Reposition the infant’s mouth.”</p>
127. A 24-year-old nulligravid college student comes to the emergency department because of a 20-day history of intermittent heavy vaginal bleeding with passage of clots. During this time, she also has had fatigue and light-headedness with no visual changes or headaches. She has no history of easy bruisability. Menarche was at the age of 12 years. During the past 3 years, menses have occurred at irregular 45- to 70-day intervals and last 5 to 7 days. Menses previously occurred at regular 28- to 30-day intervals and lasted 3 to 5 days. She is not sexually active. Her temperature is 37.4°C (99.3°F), pulse is 96/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 115/60 mm Hg. Physical examination, including pelvic examination, shows no petechiae or other abnormalities. Her hemoglobin concentration is 8.7 g/dL, and hematocrit is 28 percent. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's heavy menses?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Adenomyosis</br>b. Anovulation</br>c. Immune thrombocytopenic purpura</br>d. Leiomyomata uteri</br>e. von Willebrand disease</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>B. Anovulation (dysfunctional uterine bleeding/AUB-O)</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key time course: 20 days of prolonged, heavy bleeding with clots → acute abnormal uterine bleeding.<br /></br>• Cycle pattern: For the past 3 years menses come every 45-70 days (oligomenorrhea) rather than her prior predictable 28-30-day cycles → chronic menstrual irregularity.<br /></br>• Negatives: No petechiae/easy bruising (argues against platelet or von Willebrand abnormalities); pelvic exam normal (rules out uterine enlargement, masses, adenomyosis pain).<br /></br>• Demographics: 24-year-old, nulligravid, not sexually active → pregnancy-related causes excluded.  </p></br><p>Framework to approach similar questions:<br /></br>1. Ask “Is the pattern regular?”  If irregular → think ovulatory dysfunction.<br /></br>2. Look for systemic bleeding signs → if absent, coagulopathy less likely.<br /></br>3. Examine uterus on bimanual → bulky/tender? nodular?  If normal, structural PALM lesions less likely.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Oligo- and amenorrhea for years → Suggests chronic anovulation (eg, PCOS, thyroid dz, stress).<br /></br>2. During anovulatory cycles there is no corpus luteum → no progesterone withdrawal to induce a coordinated menstrual bleed.<br /></br>3. Unopposed estrogen causes the endometrium to proliferate until it outgrows its blood supply, sloughs irregularly, and bleeds heavily and unpredictably → exactly this patient’s presentation.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In women of reproductive age with irregular cycles and heavy, prolonged bleeding but a normal pelvic exam, the most common cause is anovulation (AUB-O).</p></br><p>Documentation pearls: duration/volume of bleeding, cycle history, associated pain, pregnancy test, Hb/Hct, hemodynamic status, rule-out structural lesions.</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Normal cycle: Follicular estrogen proliferation → LH surge → ovulation → corpus luteum secretes progesterone → orderly, limited endometrial growth and coordinated shedding.<br /></br>• Anovulation: No LH surge → no corpus luteum → no progesterone. Estrogen remains unopposed → continuous endometrial proliferation → fragile vessels, asynchronous stroma → unpredictable, heavy bleeding.<br /></br>Risk factors: PCOS, obesity, extreme weight changes, stress, thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, adolescence, perimenopause.</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests</br>Acute heavy bleed:</br>• High-dose combined oral contraceptive pill or high-dose IV/PO estrogen, or high-dose oral progestin to stabilize endometrium.<br /></br>• If unstable: uterine tamponade, D&amp;C, or TXA.<br /></br>Chronic management:</br>• Combined OCPs, progestin-only pills, levonorgestrel-IUD, cyclic progesterone, treat underlying PCOS (weight loss, metformin).<br /></br>Work-up:</br>• β-hCG, CBC, TSH, prolactin, androgen panel if PCOS suspected, pelvic US if structural lesion suspected.</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>• PALM-COEIN mnemonic for abnormal uterine bleeding:<br /></br>  • Structural (PALM): Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia.<br /></br>  • Non-structural (COEIN): Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction (anovulation), Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified.<br /></br>• Think “O for Out-of-order cycles” → Ovulatory dysfunction.</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Adenomyosis – Usually presents with regular, painful, heavy menses and a diffusely enlarged, tender “boggy” uterus on exam; absent here.<br /></br>C. Immune thrombocytopenic purpura – Would cause mucocutaneous bleeding (petechiae, epistaxis) and isolated low platelets; exam shows no bruising/petechiae, bleeding limited to menses, and cycles are irregular.<br /></br>D. Leiomyomata uteri (fibroids) – Heavy REGULAR menses and an enlarged, irregular, firm uterus; pelvic exam in vignette is normal.<br /></br>E. von Willebrand disease – Causes lifelong heavy but regular periods beginning at menarche plus easy bruising, epistaxis; patient had normal prior cycles until age 21 and no other bleeding signs.</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Key Take-Home</br>Irregular, infrequent cycles followed by prolonged, heavy bleeding in a woman with a normal pelvic exam = anovulatory dysfunctional uterine bleeding (AUB-O).</p>
128. A 37-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 2-week history of episodes of anxiety. The anxiety is accompanied by a rapid heartbeat and light-headedness. Pulse is 130/min, and blood pressure is 100/50 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a bilateral hand tremor. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Advising the patient to increase her fluid and salt intake</br>b. Citalopram and lorazepam therapy</br>c. Cognitive behavioral therapy</br>d. Propranolol therapy</br>e.
