Serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Serum thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) concentration</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:</br>• 2-week onset of “anxiety” episodes → new, medical etiologies must be excluded.<br /></br>• Autonomic findings: pulse 130/min (sinus tachycardia), BP 100/50 mm Hg (relative hypotension), bilateral hand tremor → classic sympathetic over-activity picture.<br /></br>• No psychosocial precipitants or panic symptoms (fear of dying, depersonalization, etc.) provided.</p></br><p>Pattern recognition:</br>Anxiety + tachycardia + fine tremor = think thyrotoxicosis until proved otherwise. USMLE loves to test “medical cause of psych symptoms.”</p></br><p>Test-taking framework:</br>1. Is there a physiologic explanation for the psychiatric complaint?<br /></br>2. If yes, obtain the key screening test before labeling it primary anxiety.<br /></br>   → For hyperthyroidism the single best initial test = TSH.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach:</br>1. Patient complains of anxiety → check vitals &amp; exam.<br /></br>2. Objective findings (130/min tachycardia, tremor) are disproportionate to usual generalized anxiety disorder.<br /></br>3. Hyperthyroidism or thyroid storm can masquerade as panic; easiest, cheapest screen is a serum TSH.<br /></br>4. Therefore the most appropriate NEXT STEP is diagnostic confirmation with TSH (± free T₄ if TSH suppressed).</p></br><p>Core takeaway:</br>Always rule out thyrotoxicosis (and other organic etiologies) before initiating psychiatric therapy in new-onset anxiety with sympathetic signs.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl:</br>“Plan: r/o medical causes of anxiety; order serum TSH w/reflex free T₄. Will defer anxiolytics until results available.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Hyperthyroidism → ↑β-adrenergic receptor density &amp; sensitivity → tachycardia, palpitations, tremor, anxiety, heat intolerance, weight loss.<br /></br>Etiologies include Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, thyroiditis, excess exogenous thyroid hormone.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Diagnostics:</br>• First test: TSH (low) → reflex free T₄/T₃ (high).<br /></br>• Additional labs: thyroid-stimulating IgG (Graves), ESR/CRP (thyroiditis), RAIU scan if etiology unclear.</p></br><p>Management algorithm (after confirmation):</br>1. Symptom control: non-selective β-blocker (propranolol) 20–40 mg PO q6-8h.<br /></br>2. Definitive: antithyroid drugs (methimazole or PTU in first trimester), radioactive iodine ablation, or surgery.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“THYROID” mnemonic for hyperthyroid presentation:<br /></br>Tremor, Heart racing, Yawning heat intolerance, Restlessness/anxiety, Irritable bowels (diarrhea), Ocular signs, ↓Weight.</p></br><p>Rule of thumb: “Vital-sign anxiety” (tachycardia, tremor, sweating) → Screen TSH before SSRIs/therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Increase fluid &amp; salt intake<br /></br>   • Used for neurocardiogenic syncope/POTS. Would not explain hand tremor; must first identify cause of tachycardia.</p></br><p>b. Citalopram (SSRI) + lorazepam<br /></br>   • Pharmacologic treatment for primary generalized anxiety disorder. Initiating psych meds without excluding medical causes risks mismanagement.</p></br><p>c. Cognitive behavioral therapy<br /></br>   • Excellent first-line for primary anxiety disorders but, again, premature without ruling out thyrotoxicosis.</p></br><p>d. Propranolol therapy<br /></br>   • Appropriate symptomatic treatment once hyperthyroidism is diagnosed, but choosing therapy before confirming the diagnosis violates “test → treat” sequence.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom line: New-onset anxiety plus objective adrenergic signs = Think thyroid. Order a serum TSH first.</p>
129. A 37-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after losing consciousness immediately following a scuba diving session. According to another diver, the patient had a normal descent into the water but shortly after seemed confused and made a rapid ascent. The patient uses high-quality scuba diving equipment and has experience in the sport. He has hypertension controlled with diet and exercise. He does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol. The patient is unresponsive to voice and minimally responsive to sternal rub. His temperature is 35°C (95°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 80 percent. The skin is cyanotic, and the tongue is pale. Scattered crackles are heard bilaterally. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Decompression sickness</br>b. Fat embolism</br>c. Hypothermia</br>d. Oxygen toxicity</br>e. Pneumothorax</br>f. Sinus barotrauma</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Decompression sickness  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should immediately jump out:<br /></br>1. “Rapid ascent” after an otherwise “normal descent” – classic setup for gas-bubble–related barotrauma rather than equipment failure or hypothermia.<br /></br>2. Acute neurologic deterioration (confusion → loss of consciousness) within minutes.<br /></br>3. Marked hypoxemia (SpO₂ 80%), cyanosis, bilateral crackles = acute pulmonary injury (“the chokes”).<br /></br>4. Experienced diver with “high-quality equipment”: steers you away from faulty regulator/oxygen mix and toward a physiologic complication of ascent.<br /></br>5. Timeline: symptoms began DURING/IMMEDIATELY AFTER ascent (seconds–minutes), which fits decompression sickness (“the bends”/“the chokes”) much more than fat embolism or hypothermia.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>• Whenever you see a diver + rapid ascent + acute neuro or pulmonary findings, your first two thoughts should be:<br /></br>  – Pulmonary barotrauma leading to pneumothorax or arterial gas embolism (usually unilateral breath-sound loss, hemodynamic collapse)<br /></br>  – Decompression sickness (multi-system, “bends/chokes” ± neuro findings)  </p></br><p>Systematically look for: chest asymmetry, unilateral vs bilateral lung findings, timing (seconds–minutes after ascent), and any trauma history.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why Decompression Sickness?)  </p></br><ol></br><li>On ascent, ambient pressure drops. Dissolved inert gas (mainly nitrogen) comes out of solution too rapidly if ascent is not controlled.  </li></br><li>Nitrogen bubbles lodge in:<br /></br>   • CNS/white matter → confusion, LOC, stroke-like deficits.<br /></br>   • Pulmonary vasculature → endothelial injury, ventilation–perfusion mismatch → hypoxemia, cyanosis, crackles (“the chokes”).  </li></br><li>Nothing in the stem suggests unilateral lung collapse or trauma-related fat release.  </li></br><li>Core temperature is only mildly low (35 °C) and does not explain the dramatic neurologic collapse.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home: Rapid ascent + multi-system involvement = Decompression sickness, treat with immediate 100% O₂ and transfer to hyperbaric chamber.</p></br><p>Documentation bullets if this were a real chart:<br /></br>• Onset of neuro &amp; respiratory symptoms seconds after ascent<br /></br>• Vital instability with SpO₂ 80%<br /></br>• Suspected decompression sickness → started 100% high-flow O₂, arranged emergent hyperbaric therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Supersaturation of dissolved inert gas (mostly N₂) during ascent → intravascular &amp; extravascular gas bubbles.<br /></br>• Risk modifiers: depth/time, rapid ascent, inadequate decompression stops; NOT equipment quality.<br /></br>• Clinical types:<br /></br>  – Type I (“bends”): musculoskeletal pain, pruritus.<br /></br>  – Type II (“chokes”/neurologic): pulmonary edema-like picture, cough, dyspnea, hypoxemia, CNS deficits, spinal cord injury.<br /></br>• Definitive therapy: recompression in hyperbaric chamber, which shrinks bubbles &amp; drives N₂ back into solution; adjunct 100% O₂, IV fluids.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><ol></br><li>Immediate 100% non-rebreather oxygen (decreases nitrogen partial pressure, promotes wash-out).  </li></br><li>Rapid transfer to hyperbaric recompression.  </li></br><li>Supportive: IV fluids, airway protection.  </li></br><li>Imaging is usually not required emergently but spinal MRI/CT can demonstrate gas; CXR may be done to exclude pneumothorax if unilateral breath sounds absent.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“RAPID” ascent mnemonic for Decompression sickness features:<br /></br>R – Rapid ascent<br /></br>A – Altered mental status / Ataxia<br /></br>P – Pulmonary “chokes” (crackles, cyanosis)<br /></br>I – Inert gas bubbles (N₂)<br /></br>D – Dive again in hyperbaric chamber (Definitive treatment)</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Fat embolism<br /></br>• Requires long-bone fracture or orthopedic trauma; onset 24-72 h later with petechiae. Not applicable here.  </p></br><p>C. Hypothermia<br /></br>• Core temp &lt;35 °C can cause AMS, but 35 °C is only mild. Does not explain sudden pulmonary findings or diving context.  </p></br><p>D. Oxygen toxicity<br /></br>• Occurs from breathing high partial pressures of O₂ at depth (&gt;1.4 ATA): visual field changes, seizures underwater, not post-ascent hypoxemia/cyanosis.  </p></br><p>E. Pneumothorax (pulmonary barotrauma)<br /></br>• Would present with sudden unilateral pleuritic pain, decreased/absent breath sounds on one side, possible tracheal deviation if tension; auscultation would not show bilateral crackles.  </p></br><p>F. Sinus barotrauma<br /></br>• Causes facial pain/epistaxis related to pressure changes in sinuses; no systemic hypoxemia or neurologic collapse.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>A diver who ascends too quickly and immediately develops neurologic dysfunction plus hypoxemic pulmonary findings is experiencing decompression sickness—manage with 100% oxygen and hyperbaric recompression.</p>
130. An asymptomatic 27-year-old man comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. He has a history of inguinal hernia repair during childhood. He takes no medications. His father died at the age of 42 years of an aortic root dissection, and his paternal grandmother died suddenly at the age of 46 years of unknown causes. He is 188 cm (6 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 85 kg (187 lb); BMI is 24 kg/m2. His pulse is 76/min and regular, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 130/70 mm Hg. Examination shows a high narrow palate, mild scoliosis, and a pectus excavatum deformity of the chest. His arm span is 196 cm (6 ft 5 in). The joints of the upper and lower extremities are hyperextensible bilaterally. The feet are flat. In addition to echocardiography, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Ophthalmologic examination  </br>b. Renal ultrasonography  </br>c. CT scan of the head  </br>d. Audiometry  </br>e. Upper endoscopy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Ophthalmologic examination  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives<br /></br>   • Tall (188 cm) with increased arm-span:height ratio (196 cm &gt; 188 cm)<br /></br>   • High-arched palate, pectus excavatum, mild scoliosis, joint hyperlaxity, pes planus → classic skeletal criteria for Marfan syndrome.<br /></br>   • Strong family history of early aortic catastrophe (father died of aortic root dissection at 42 yr).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • Normal blood pressure, no headache, no abdominal/flank pain → makes entities such as polycystic kidney disease or vascular Ehlers-Danlos less likely.<br /></br>   • No hearing loss, no GI bleeding, no neurologic deficits.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive framework for test day<br /></br>When a stem screams “Marfan,” immediately think of the two organ systems that must be screened even if the patient is asymptomatic:<br /></br>   1. Cardiovascular: ascending aorta/root (echo, ± MRI/CT).<br /></br>   2. Ocular: lens dislocation, myopia, retinal detachment (complete ophthalmologic exam).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why ophthalmology?<br /></br>• Up to 60–80 % of patients with fibrillin-1 (FBN1) mutations develop ectopia lentis (usually superior–temporal lens subluxation), severe myopia, or early cataract.<br /></br>• Visual complications may be silent until irreversible damage occurs; early detection allows refractive correction or prophylactic surgery.<br /></br>• International Ghent criteria for Marfan management recommend baseline slit-lamp exam at diagnosis and at least every 1–2 yr thereafter.</p></br><p>What must be documented in the chart<br /></br>• “Referred to ophthalmology for baseline evaluation of lens dislocation and retinal pathology associated with Marfan syndrome.”<br /></br>• Results of baseline transthoracic echocardiogram (already ordered).  </p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>Marfan work-up = Echo for aortic root + Eye exam for ectopia lentis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Gene: FBN1 on chromosome 15 → abnormal fibrillin-1 → defective microfibrils and dysregulated TGF-β.<br /></br>• Weak elastic tissue in aorta → root dilation → dissection, aortic regurgitation.<br /></br>• Weak zonular fibers → lens instability (up-and-out subluxation).<br /></br>• Skeletal overgrowth due to excess TGF-β signaling → long limbs, arachnodactyly, pectus deformities, scoliosis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Renal ultrasonography – Appropriate for suspected autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD: flank pain, hematuria, cysts, intracranial aneurysm risk) or Ehlers-Danlos vascular type with arterial/uterine rupture. Marfan does NOT carry a high prevalence of renal cysts.  </p></br><p>c. CT scan of the head – Used for screening berry aneurysms in ADPKD or subarachnoid hemorrhage; Marfan patients’ cerebrovascular risk is not high enough to justify routine CT/MRA screening.  </p></br><p>d. Audiometry – Screening for conductive/sensorineural hearing loss in conditions like osteogenesis imperfecta, Alport, congenital CMV, or rubella; not routinely indicated in Marfan.  </p></br><p>e. Upper endoscopy – Surveillance for Barrett esophagus, varices, familial polyposis, or Ehlers-Danlos with GI fragility, but not for Marfan.  </p></br><p>Key discriminator: Only ophthalmologic disease and aortic root disease are common, morbid, and silent in Marfan, warranting routine surveillance.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Marfan = MArFAN”<br /></br>M – Mitral/aortic root (Echo)<br /></br>A – Aorta (Echo/MRI)<br /></br>F – Fibrillin<br /></br>A – Arachno-skeleton (tall, long limbs)<br /></br>N – Needed eye exam (Nod for ophthalmology)  </p></br><p>Lens “Subluxes up like the MARs (planets) go UP” → upward/outward displacement in MARfan.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom line: In a patient with features of Marfan syndrome, once the aorta is imaged, the next indispensable evaluation is a comprehensive ophthalmologic examination.</p>
131. A study is conducted to assess the relationship between cigarette smoking and the development of lung cancer. One thousand participants who smoke cigarettes and 1000 participants who do not smoke are observed for 15 years. The results show:</br>              Lung Cancer</br>              Present   Absent   Total</br>Cigarette Smokers   30        970      1000</br>Nonsmokers          3         997      1000</br>Total               33        1967     2000</br>Based on these results, which of the following best approximates the attributable risk of cigarette smoking on the development of lung cancer?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. 0.3 percent</br>b. 2.7 percent</br>c. 10 percent</br>d. 90 percent</br>e. 99.7 percent</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>B. 2.7 percent</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key task: “best approximates the attributable risk of cigarette smoking on the development of lung cancer.”<br /></br>• Study type: Prospective cohort (1000 smokers vs 1000 nonsmokers followed for 15 years).<br /></br>• Data you need: Incidence of disease in the exposed group vs the non-exposed group.</p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar items<br /></br>1. Pull the 2 × 2 numbers out of the table.<br /></br>2. Calculate the incidence (risk) in each group.<br /></br>3. For attributable risk, subtract the two incidences (Ie – Iu).<br /></br>4. Convert to the units they want (usually %).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Incidence in smokers (exposed)<br /></br>   Ie = 30 cases ÷ 1000 people = 0.03 = 3 %<br /></br>2. Incidence in nonsmokers (unexposed)<br /></br>   Iu = 3 cases ÷ 1000 people = 0.003 = 0.3 %<br /></br>3. Attributable risk (AR)<br /></br>   AR = Ie – Iu = 0.03 – 0.003 = 0.027 = 2.7 %  </p></br><p>Take-home message: 2.7 % of the absolute risk for lung cancer in smokers can be directly “attributed” to smoking itself.</p></br><p>What you would document clinically: “Compared with never-smokers, smoking confers an additional absolute lung-cancer risk of ≈2.7 % over 15 years.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (condensed)<br /></br>• Smoking introduces polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and nitrosamines that create DNA adducts → mutations in tumor-suppressor genes (e.g., p53) and proto-oncogenes (e.g., KRAS), driving carcinogenesis.<br /></br>• Risk is dose-dependent and synergistic with other exposures (e.g., asbestos).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Nuggets<br /></br>• Primary prevention = smoking cessation; risk diminishes with time but never equals that of a lifelong nonsmoker.<br /></br>• Screening: Annual low-dose CT for ≥20 pack-year current smokers aged 50–80 per USPSTF.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“ATTRIB table: AR = In – Io (n = nicotine, o = other).”<br /></br>OR visualize a simple ruler: the extra length (risk) sticking out beyond the unexposed bar is the attributable risk.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. 0.3 %<br /></br>• That is the incidence in the unexposed group (Iu) itself, not the difference. Confusing baseline risk with excess risk.</p></br><p>C. 10 %<br /></br>• Roughly the Relative Risk (RR = 3 % / 0.3 % = 10). AR uses subtraction; RR uses division. Classic exam trap: mixing up AR with RR.</p></br><p>D. 90 %<br /></br>• Could be mistaken for “attributable risk percentage” if you did (RR – 1)/RR = (10 – 1)/10 = 0.9 = 90 %. They asked for AR (absolute difference), not AR%.</p></br><p>E. 99.7 %<br /></br>• Approximates 1 – (Ie / Iu), which is not a standard epidemiologic measure here. Extreme value flags a miscalculation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Attributable risk = incidence in exposed – incidence in unexposed. In this cohort, 3 % – 0.3 % = 2.7 %.</p>
132. A study is conducted to determine if a hemoglobin A₁c greater than 15 percent predicts diabetic retinopathy. Results show that 88 percent of all patients with diabetic retinopathy had a hemoglobin A₁c greater than 15 percent. Which of the following is the most accurate interpretation of these results?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. False-negative rate is 88 percent</br>b. False-positive rate is 88 percent</br>c. Negative predictive value is 88 percent</br>d. Positive predictive value is 88 percent</br>e. Power of the study design is 88 percent</br>f. Sensitivity is 88 percent</br>g. Specificity is 88 percent</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>f. Sensitivity is 88 percent  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrase: “88 percent of all patients with diabetic retinopathy had a hemoglobin A₁c &gt; 15 percent.”<br /></br>• The population being described = patients who already have the disease (diabetic retinopathy).<br /></br>• Proportion of these diseased patients who test positive = definition of sensitivity.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: nothing is stated about patients without retinopathy, so we cannot comment on specificity, false-positive rate, or positive predictive value (PPV).</p></br><p>Cognitive framework: When a question gives you data restricted to the “diseased” group, immediately think sensitivity (true-positive rate) or its complement, false-negative rate. If data are restricted to the “non-diseased” group, think specificity or false-positive rate. If data involve “all who tested positive” or “all who tested negative,” think PPV or NPV respectively.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify reference group (diseased).<br /></br>2. Recognize that “test positive” proportion within that group = sensitivity.<br /></br>3. 88 % therefore represents sensitivity.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Sensitivity = True Positives / (True Positives + False Negatives).</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review (brief)<br /></br>HbA₁c reflects average blood glucose over ~3 months; higher levels correlate with microvascular complications such as retinopathy. A very high threshold (&gt;15 %) would be expected to capture most severely uncontrolled diabetics, hence the high sensitivity for retinopathy in this study.</p></br><p>Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>No additional labs/imaging needed to understand the statistical concept, but in practice HbA₁c is routinely checked every 3–6 mo to monitor glycemic control, and annual dilated fundoscopic exams screen for retinopathy.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“SENSitivity Screens” → use when you don’t want to miss disease (high sensitivity catches Sick people).<br /></br>“SPecificity Spares” → high specificity rules in disease (few false positives).<br /></br>Mnemonic: “SIN → Sensitive tests, If Negative, rule OUT disease.”</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. False-negative rate = 1 – sensitivity = 12 %, not 88 %.<br /></br>b. False-positive rate refers to non-diseased patients who test positive = 1 – specificity; not provided.<br /></br>c. Negative predictive value (NPV): proportion of negative test results that are true negatives; requires data on all negative tests, not given.<br /></br>d. Positive predictive value (PPV): proportion of positive tests that are true positives; requires data on all positive tests, not given.<br /></br>e. Power: probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis; independent of any single test characteristic; 88 % figure here is unrelated.<br /></br>g. Specificity: proportion of non-diseased who test negative; again, no data on patients without retinopathy.</p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Confusing sensitivity with PPV because both involve “positives.” Remember: sensitivity’s denominator = all diseased patients; PPV’s denominator = all positive tests.</p>
133. A study is planned to compare the efficacy of two sedatives in all children who are receiving mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure in a pediatric intensive care unit. Which of the following is most likely to invalidate the results of the study?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a.  Etiology of respiratory failure</br>b.  Inclusion of children with coma</br>c.  Lack of age restriction</br>d.  Severity of respiratory failure</br>e.  Types of mechanical ventilation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Inclusion of children with coma  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases:<br /></br>• “Compare the efficacy of two sedatives” – primary outcome is the quality/depth of sedation or time to adequate sedation.<br /></br>• “All children … receiving mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure” – target population is ventilated children who require pharmacologic sedation.<br /></br>• “Most likely to invalidate the results” – asks which factor threatens the INTERNAL validity of the trial the most (i.e., makes the study incapable of measuring what it set out to measure).  </p></br><p>Pertinent positive/negative: Children who are already comatose cannot manifest (or need) drug-induced sedation, so their inclusion destroys the ability to compare the two agents. Age, etiology or severity of respiratory failure, or ventilator mode may influence dose requirements, but these can be controlled for (stratification, randomization, regression).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach: Always ask, “Can the outcome actually be measured in every subject?” If a subset cannot express or be assessed for the outcome, their enrollment introduces nondifferential misclassification and potentially nullifies the study.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Stepwise logic:<br /></br>1. Study objective = compare sedative efficacy.<br /></br>2. Essential prerequisite = subjects must be capable of demonstrating a sedative response (e.g., change in consciousness, agitation scale).<br /></br>3. Comatose children have no measurable conscious level to titrate; they are already deeply unresponsive.<br /></br>4. Including them mixes two fundamentally different physiologic states (awake vs comatose), producing outcome misclassification and bias toward the null.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Eligibility criteria must require that every participant be suitable for the intervention and that the primary outcome can be validly measured.  </p></br><p>Documentation if real patient: mental status examination (Glasgow Coma Scale), indication for sedation, current neurologic status.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)  </p></br><p>• Sedatives in PICU (midazolam, dexmedetomidine, propofol) act on CNS to induce decreased awareness and anxiolysis.<br /></br>• In coma (structural brain damage, metabolic encephalopathy), neuronal networks are already suppressed; additional sedative has little observable effect and can distort pharmacodynamic comparisons.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Points  </p></br><p>• Before randomization in any sedation trial, patients should have a baseline sedation score (eg, RASS – Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale).<br /></br>• Comatose patients score at the deepest level before drug; they cannot show incremental change, invalidating endpoints such as “time to target sedation.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“NO OUTCOME ⇒ NO VALID TRIAL” – if a subgroup cannot produce the outcome, exclude them or your conclusions collapse.<br /></br>Mnemonic: “COmA kills COmparisons.” (COmA = Cannot Observe measurable Activity).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Etiology of respiratory failure – Asthma vs pneumonia may alter ventilatory settings but does not inherently prevent assessment of sedation. Stratification or multivariate adjustment is sufficient; does not invalidate study.  </p></br><p>C. Lack of age restriction – Infants vs adolescents differ in pharmacokinetics, but age can be blocked in randomization or analyzed separately. Does not, by itself, make results meaningless.  </p></br><p>D. Severity of respiratory failure – Degree of hypoxemia/hypercapnia influences ventilatory demands, not the ability to gauge sedation. Can be controlled for analytically.  </p></br><p>E. Types of mechanical ventilation – Volume vs pressure modes influence ventilator synchrony but still allow standardized sedation scoring; variability introduces noise, not fatal bias.  </p></br><p>Key difference: only option B removes the ability to measure the primary endpoint, thereby threatening internal validity and rendering the comparison uninterpretable.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>When designing or critiquing a clinical trial, the inclusion criteria must ensure every participant can (1) receive the intervention as intended and (2) have the outcome meaningfully measured. Including patients whose baseline state precludes outcome assessment (eg, already comatose in a sedation study) invalidates results far more than other heterogeneities, which can generally be adjusted or stratified away.</p>
134. Investigators would like to assess the effectiveness of a new drug for the treatment of bacterial meningitis in humans. Previous studies have shown that the drug is effective in treating the disease in laboratory animals. The investigators design a study protocol that includes randomization of participants to an experimental group and a control group. Which of the following is the primary consideration regarding use of placebo for the control group?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Patients must be able to opt out if assigned to receive placebo</br>b. Placebo should be discontinued once verifiable results have been obtained</br>c. The placebo must undergo a phase 1 clinical trial before being used</br>d. Use of placebo must be stratified to disease severity</br>e. Placebo should not be used</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>E. Placebo should not be used</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis</br>Key phrases: </br>• “bacterial meningitis in humans” – an acute, rapidly progressive, life-threatening infection with high morbidity and mortality if not treated promptly.<br /></br>• “Previous studies have shown that the drug is effective in … laboratory animals” – the new drug looks promising, so investigators want to move to human trials.<br /></br>• “experimental group and a control group” – raises the question: What is an ethical control?<br /></br>When a proven, lifesaving therapy already exists (standard IV antibiotics ± steroids), true clinical equipoise no longer exists. Choosing a placebo arm would mean knowingly withholding effective, potentially life-saving therapy from some subjects, violating basic ethical principles (beneficence, non-maleficence, justice, and respect for persons).</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why E is correct)</br>1. Condition severity: Bacterial meningitis has a case-fatality rate up to 20% and can leave survivors with severe neurologic sequelae. Delay or omission of therapy is clearly harmful.</br>2. Existing standard of care: Third-generation cephalosporin + vancomycin (± ampicillin, steroids) improves survival. Ethical trial design must compare the new drug with standard therapy, not with placebo.</br>3. Principle of clinical equipoise: You may randomize only when the medical community is genuinely uncertain which of two (or more) active regimens is superior. Here, there is certainty that “no drug” (placebo) is worse than standard antibiotics.</br>4. Declaration of Helsinki (2013 update): Placebo-controlled trials are allowed only when no proven effective intervention exists OR when use of placebo is necessary for compelling methodological reasons AND patients will not face serious harm from lack of therapy. Neither criterion is met here.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review</br>• N. meningitidis, S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae or L. monocytogenes invade meninges → inflammation → cerebral edema, ↑ ICP, potential brain herniation and death within hours if untreated.<br /></br>• Early, appropriate antibiotics rapidly sterilize CSF and limit inflammatory response. Withholding therapy (placebo) invites irreversible injury.</p></br><p>Treatment and Diagnostic Tests Review</br>Standard of care:</br>1. Empiric IV ceftriaxone or cefotaxime + vancomycin; add ampicillin if age &gt;50 yr or immunocompromised to cover Listeria.<br /></br>2. Dexamethasone before or with first antibiotic dose to reduce neurologic complications (especially with pneumococcal meningitis).<br /></br>3. Adjust regimen once CSF culture sensitivities return.</p></br><p>If investigating a “new drug,” ethical design:</br>• Randomize to “standard therapy” vs. “standard therapy + new drug” (adjunctive) OR “new drug vs. best-available standard drug” (non-inferiority/superiority trial).<br /></br>• Primary endpoints: mortality, neurologic sequelae, time to CSF sterilization.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips</br>“No Placebo for Kill-o”: If a disease kills quickly and a therapy already works, placebo is unethical.<br /></br>Remember the three-part rule: 1) Serious disease, 2) Effective therapy exists, 3) Placebo = harm → Don’t use it.</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. “Patients must be able to opt out if assigned to receive placebo.”<br /></br>• Right to withdraw is necessary but does NOT justify exposing subjects to known, avoidable harm. Still unethical to randomize to placebo initially.</p></br><p>B. “Placebo should be discontinued once verifiable results have been obtained.”<br /></br>• Stopping placebo early lessens but does not eliminate initial unethical withholding of therapy. The problem is the very act of assigning placebo.</p></br><p>C. “The placebo must undergo a phase 1 clinical trial before being used.”<br /></br>• Placebos are inert; they do not require safety testing. More importantly, the ethical issue is withholding active treatment, not placebo safety.</p></br><p>D. “Use of placebo must be stratified to disease severity.”<br /></br>• Stratifying severity does not solve the ethical dilemma; even mild bacterial meningitis can progress rapidly. All severities require immediate antibiotics.</p></br><p>Take-Home Message</br>When an acute, life-threatening condition already has proven effective therapy, research subjects must receive at least the standard of care. Placebo controls are unethical; the comparator must be an active treatment arm.</p>
135. A previously healthy 18-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a lump in her neck that she first noticed 1 month ago. She is otherwise asymptomatic. Examination shows a 3-cm left supraclavicular lymph node that is firm and rubbery. The spleen is palpated 3 cm below the left costal margin. The remainder of examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies are most likely to show which of the following?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Decreased serum potassium concentration</br>b. Increased erythrocyte count</br>c. Increased serum calcium concentration</br>d. Increased serum lactate dehydrogenase activity</br>e. Increased serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Increased serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) activity  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues in the stem<br /></br>• “18-year-old woman”: classic age peak for Hodgkin lymphoma (HL) but can also be high-grade non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL).<br /></br>• “Painless, firm, rubbery 3-cm left supraclavicular node (Virchow node)” + splenomegaly → strongly suggest malignant lymphoma.<br /></br>• “Otherwise asymptomatic” (no fever, night sweats, weight loss) does not exclude HL; early‐stage disease is often B-symptom-negative.  </p></br><p>Approach framework<br /></br>1. Localize lesion → left supraclavicular node.<br /></br>2. Generate differential → lymphoma &gt; metastatic GI malignancy (unlikely at 18) &gt; infectious mono (but node classically posterior cervical; spleen can be large but LDH less striking).<br /></br>3. Recognize high cell-turnover malignancy → expect markers of rapid proliferation / cell lysis (↑LDH, ↑uric acid, possibly mild anemia).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why LDH is the best choice<br /></br>• LDH is released from virtually every cell; rapidly proliferating tumors (lymphomas, leukemias, germ-cell tumors) leak LDH because of high cell‐turnover and tissue necrosis.<br /></br>• In lymphoma, serum LDH correlates with tumor burden and is part of the International Prognostic Index (IPI) for NHL and is followed during therapy.<br /></br>Take-home: “Painless lymphadenopathy + splenomegaly → think lymphoma → high cell turnover → ↑LDH.”</p></br><p>What you would document<br /></br>• Location, size, consistency of node; presence of splenomegaly; absence of B symptoms; plan for excisional biopsy; staging labs (CBC, CMP, LDH, uric acid).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Lymphomas<br /></br>• Malignant transformation of B (or less commonly T) lymphocytes.<br /></br>• Rapid mitosis + apoptosis → cellular contents (LDH, uric acid) spill into serum.<br /></br>• LDH (catalyzes pyruvate ↔ lactate) serves as a surrogate for tumor burden.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic / Treatment Pearls  </p></br><p>Staging work-up<br /></br>1. Excisional lymph-node biopsy (never FNA alone).<br /></br>2. Labs: CBC, CMP, LDH, uric acid, ESR.<br /></br>3. Imaging: PET/CT for Ann Arbor staging.<br /></br>First-line therapy (classical HL): ABVD (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“L for Lymphoma, Lysis, LDH”<br /></br>Visualize a rubbery lymph node leaking “L-shaped” bubbles of LDH into the bloodstream.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Decreased serum potassium<br /></br>• Seen with hyperaldosteronism, diuretic use, vomiting/diarrhea—not linked to isolated painless lymphadenopathy.  </p></br><p>B. Increased erythrocyte count<br /></br>• Suggests polycythemia (JAK2 mutation, hypoxia, EPO‐secreting tumor). Lymphoma more often causes anemia, not polycythemia.  </p></br><p>C. Increased serum calcium<br /></br>• Hypercalcemia of malignancy typically from PTHrP-secreting solid tumors (squamous cell, renal, breast) or bone metastases; uncommon in early lymphoma, especially with normal systemic symptoms.  </p></br><p>E. Increased TSH<br /></br>• Indicates primary hypothyroidism. No thyroid enlargement, fatigue, weight gain, or menstrual changes are given. Lymphoma does not raise TSH.  </p></br><p>Key discriminator: Only ↑LDH logically follows from the high cell-turnover pathophysiology of lymphoma suggested by the vignette.</p>
136. A 37-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by a coworker 30 minutes after the sudden onset of moderately severe chest pain. His coworker reports that he has become increasingly irritable over the past week. He has no history of diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease, hyperthyroidism, mood disorder, or intravenous drug abuse. He takes no medications. He is physically fit and exercises regularly. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 160/100 mm Hg. Physical examination shows diaphoresis. He is alert, oriented, hypervigilant, and irritable. His speech is rapid and pressured. He frequently yells at staff to keep away from him because he believes they are trying to kill him. An ECG shows ST-segment elevation across the precordium. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Bipolar disorder</br>b. Complex partial seizures</br>c. Delirium</br>d. Schizoaffective disorder</br>e. Schizophrenia</br>f. Substance-induced psychotic disorder</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Substance-induced psychotic disorder  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Sudden, severe chest pain with ST-segment elevation in a 37-year-old with no classic cardiac risk factors → think vasospasm or thrombosis from sympathomimetic (eg, cocaine, amphetamines).<br /></br>• Autonomic hyperactivity: tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis.<br /></br>• Acute‐onset psychosis: hypervigilance, paranoia (“you’re trying to kill me”), irritability, rapid pressured speech.<br /></br>• Temporal relationship: Symptoms began within the past week and crescendoed over 30 min—typical of drug intoxication, not a primary psychiatric illness that requires ≥ 6 mo.<br /></br>Approach framework:<br /></br>1. Ask, “Could a drug do ALL of this?” (chest pain + psychosis + SNS surge).<br /></br>2. Look for absence of long psychiatric prodrome.<br /></br>3. Link physiologic findings to pharmacology (catecholamine excess).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Cocaine/amphetamine intoxication blocks presynaptic reuptake of NE, DA, 5-HT → massive catecholamine surge.<br /></br>  – Cardiac: Coronary vasospasm → acute STEMI in a young patient.<br /></br>  – CNS: Dopamine excess → paranoia, hallucinations, pressured speech.<br /></br>• DSM-5: Substance-induced psychotic disorder = prominent hallucinations/delusions during or soon after substance use; not better explained by a primary disorder; resolves after drug cleared.<br /></br>Take-home: “Young patient + STEMI + acute paranoia → think sympathomimetic intoxication = substance-induced psychosis.”  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real life):<br /></br>• Time course &amp; substance suspicion, vital signs, mental-status exam, ECG findings.<br /></br>• Plan for urine toxicology, cardiac enzymes, benzodiazepines, nitrates, aspirin; avoid β-blockers (risk of unopposed α-stimulation).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Cocaine/amphetamines ↑ synaptic catecholamines →<br /></br>  – Vasoconstriction (α-1) → myocardial ischemia/infarction, HTN.<br /></br>  – Tachyarrhythmias, diaphoresis.<br /></br>  – DA surge in mesolimbic pathway → psychosis.<br /></br>Risk factors: Recreational use, stress, occupational drug testing avoidance, recent irritability/insomnia.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>Acute management of suspected cocaine-associated STEMI:<br /></br>1. MONA-B minus the B<br /></br>   • Morphine &amp; benzodiazepines (↓ SNS tone)<br /></br>   • Oxygen if hypoxic<br /></br>   • Nitroglycerin &amp; aspirin<br /></br>   • NO β-blocker (may worsen vasospasm).<br /></br>2. Cardiac cath if indicated.<br /></br>3. Haloperidol or benzodiazepines for severe agitation/psychosis (monitor QT).<br /></br>Confirmatory tests: urine toxicology (cocaine metabolite benzoylecgonine).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Cocaine CHAMP”:<br /></br>• Chest pain<br /></br>• Hypertension<br /></br>• Agitation<br /></br>• MI (STEMI)<br /></br>• Paranoia/psychosis  </p></br><p>Or: “If the ECG STs and the pt sees SPies—Suspect Powder (cocaine).”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Bipolar disorder (manic episode)<br /></br>• Pressured speech &amp; irritability fit, but mania requires ≥ 1 week of elevated/expansive mood and additional DIG FAST symptoms (↓ sleep, grandiosity, etc.).<br /></br>• Does NOT explain acute STEMI or sympathetic surge.  </p></br><p>b. Complex partial seizures<br /></br>• Produce brief automatisms ± post-ictal confusion, not sustained paranoia with coherent speech or MI.  </p></br><p>c. Delirium<br /></br>• Fluctuating consciousness, inattention, disorientation. Patient is alert, oriented, and hypervigilant—not delirious.  </p></br><p>d. Schizoaffective disorder<br /></br>• Requires ≥ 2 weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms plus ≥ 6 months total illness. No chronic history; mood criteria absent.  </p></br><p>e. Schizophrenia<br /></br>• Needs ≥ 6 months of psychotic symptoms with functional decline; onset usually &lt; 30 y. No prodrome, plus physiologic signs point to drug effect.  </p></br><p>Key difference: All primary psychiatric disorders lack the acute autonomic storm and STEMI seen here.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a young, previously healthy patient with sudden chest pain, STEMI, SNS overdrive, and new-onset paranoia, think sympathomimetic intoxication → substance-induced psychotic disorder.</p>
137. A 32-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 1 hour after an episode of palpitations, sweating, and shortness of breath. The episode occurred while she was at her job working as an administrative assistant. She thought that she was having a heart attack. She reports that her company recently underwent a reorganization, and she has been under increased stress. This is her fourth visit to the emergency department during the past 3 weeks for similar symptoms. At each previous visit, physical examination and laboratory studies showed no abnormalities. Today, her pulse is 88/min, respirations are 17/min, and blood pressure is 130/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is tense and fearful. She has a linear thought process. There is no evidence of hallucinations or delusions. Serum cardiac enzyme activity is within the reference range. Her hematocrit is 39 percent. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 99 percent. An ECG shows sinus arrhythmia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Acute stress disorder</br>b. Cocaine intoxication</br>c. Myocardial infarction</br>d. Panic disorder</br>e. Thyrotoxicosis</br>f. Unstable angina pectoris</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Panic disorder  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Recurrent episodes: “fourth visit … past 3 weeks for similar symptoms.”<br /></br>• Somatic surge of autonomic symptoms: palpitations, sweating, SOB, fear of dying (“thought that she was having a heart attack”).<br /></br>• No physical cause on repeated work-ups: normal vitals, ECG = sinus arrhythmia, normal cardiac enzymes, normal SpO₂.<br /></br>• Trigger is not a specific external trauma—attacks occur abruptly while doing ordinary activities (at her desk).<br /></br>• Acute anxiety on mental-status exam (“tense and fearful”) but intact cognition, no psychosis.  </p></br><p>Approach framework for similar questions (“PANIC”)<br /></br>P – Paroxysmal episodes, Peak &lt;10 min<br /></br>A – Absence of medical explanation (normal labs/ECG)<br /></br>N – No specific trigger required (unexpected)<br /></br>I – Intense fear of dying/losing control<br /></br>C – Concern about future attacks or ED visits  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Recognize classic sympathetic storm (palpitations, diaphoresis, dyspnea).<br /></br>Step 2: Scan for red-flag organic findings (tachyarrhythmia, abnormal enzymes) → none.<br /></br>Step 3: Check chronology—recurrent, sudden, short-lived.<br /></br>Step 4: Map to DSM-5 criteria: ≥2 unexpected attacks + worry/maladaptive behavior (multiple ED trips) ≥1 month.<br /></br>Take-home: In the absence of objective cardiopulmonary or metabolic abnormalities, repeated sudden “heart-attack” feelings = panic disorder.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Thought to arise from hypersensitive fear circuitry (locus coeruleus, amygdala) and dysregulated sympathetic outflow.<br /></br>• Genetic predisposition + stress can lower the threshold.<br /></br>• Patients often develop anticipatory anxiety and avoidance behavior → functional impairment if untreated.</p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>1. First-line maintenance:<br /></br>   – SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine, fluoxetine).<br /></br>   – SNRIs (venlafaxine).<br /></br>   – Cognitive-behavioral therapy (exposure + cognitive restructuring).<br /></br>2. Acute abortive: short-term benzodiazepine (e.g., lorazepam) while waiting for SSRI to take effect.<br /></br>3. Document: number of attacks, triggers, associated functional impairment, safety plan.<br /></br>No further cardiopulmonary testing is indicated after multiple negative work-ups unless new findings emerge.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“PANIC = Palpitations, Abdominal distress, Nausea/paresthesias, Intense fear of dying/losing control, Chest pain/Chills/Choking.”<br /></br>Visual: Alarm bell in the amygdala → sympathetic lightning bolts → patient runs to the ED.</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Acute stress disorder – Requires exposure to a life-threatening trauma and prominent dissociation (flashbacks, depersonalization) within 3 days-1 month. This patient’s stress is work-related, not traumatic, and she lacks dissociative symptoms.<br /></br>B. Cocaine intoxication – Would show marked sympathetic activation (severe tachycardia, HTN, mydriasis, agitation, possibly chest pain) and usually positive drug screen. No history of cocaine use and vitals are normal.<br /></br>C. Myocardial infarction – Typically elevated troponins, ischemic ECG changes, persistent crushing chest pain. Enzymes and ECG are normal; symptoms resolved.<br /></br>E. Thyrotoxicosis – Produces chronic hyperadrenergic signs (weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, persistent tachycardia). Vitals normal, and episodes are episodic, not constant.<br /></br>F. Unstable angina pectoris – Chest pain at rest, ECG changes (ST depression/T-wave inversion), risk factors (older age, CAD). This 32-year-old woman has no pain currently, normal ECG/enzymes, and repeated negative evaluations.</p></br><p>Key takeaway: Recurrent, sudden, short-lived attacks of overwhelming physical anxiety without medical etiology = panic disorder.</p>
138. A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because she has been feeling sad and hopeless for the past 2 weeks. She reports having mood swings several times daily and crying suddenly for no reason. She has had difficulty motivating herself and can work no more than 4 hours daily as a retail salesperson before becoming tired. She has been wondering if her condition is hopeless and if she will ever feel better. One month ago, she had sinusitis that was treated with amoxicillin. At the end of treatment 3 weeks ago, weakness in her right arm developed that was attributed to a postinfectious autoimmune neuropathy. Since then, she has been receiving prednisone therapy. She has no other history of serious medical or psychiatric illness. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Muscle strength is normal in all extremities. On mental status examination, she is alert and oriented. She has a sad mood and is on the verge of crying several times. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Begin supportive group psychotherapy</br>b. Add citalopram to the medication regimen</br>c. Add clonazepam to the medication regimen</br>d. Add quetiapine to the medication regimen</br>e. Increase the dosage of prednisone</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Add citalopram to the medication regimen  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• “Feeling sad and hopeless… difficulty motivating herself… crying spells” → Core depressive symptoms.<br /></br>• Duration “past 2 weeks” → meets the ≥2-week minimum for a major depressive episode.<br /></br>• Temporal relationship: “Since then [starting prednisone 3 weeks ago], she has been feeling sad.” → Strong temporal link to glucocorticoid use.<br /></br>• No prior psychiatric history; normal physical &amp; neuro exam → points away from a primary neurologic cause.<br /></br>• Current medical need: prednisone is being used to treat an autoimmune neuropathy; no suggestion it can safely be stopped today.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Identify syndrome (depression).<br /></br>2. Search for iatrogenic causes (meds, endocrine, infection).<br /></br>3. Decide whether to remove the offending agent or treat the side effect.<br /></br>4. Pick evidence-based first-line therapy (SSRI) when the drug cannot be stopped.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><ol></br><li>Diagnose: Glucocorticoid-induced depressive episode (subtype of “substance/medication-induced depressive disorder,” DSM-5).  </li></br><li>Management algorithm:<br /></br>   • If possible, taper/discontinue steroid.<br /></br>   • If taper not feasible (active autoimmune disease), pharmacologically treat depression.<br /></br>   • First-line drug therapy for steroid-induced depression = SSRI (e.g., citalopram, sertraline, fluoxetine).  </li></br><li>Therefore, adding citalopram is the most appropriate next step.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: Long-term or even short-term systemic glucocorticoids can precipitate mood disorders. If the steroid is essential, treat the psychiatric side effect exactly as you would primary depression—with an SSRI.  </p></br><p>Documentation must include: depressive symptoms, temporal association with prednisone, risk/benefit discussion, and initiation of citalopram with follow-up plan.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Glucocorticoids increase CNS glutamate and alter serotonin/dopamine turnover, predisposing to mood changes.<br /></br>• Spectrum ranges from euphoria/mania (common at high doses) to major depression or frank psychosis.<br /></br>• Risk factors: dose ≥20–40 mg prednisone-equivalent, longer duration, personal or family psychiatric history—though any patient can be affected.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>• Pharmacologic: SSRI (citalopram 20 mg daily) is first-line; consider SNRI or bupropion if SSRI not tolerated.<br /></br>• Non-pharmacologic: psychotherapy is useful adjunct but not adequate as sole acute treatment for medication-induced major depression.<br /></br>• Monitoring: baseline PHQ-9, follow-up in 2–4 weeks, watch for suicidality, assess steroid necessity at each visit.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Pred-no-zone blues” mnemonic:<br /></br>P – Prednisone<br /></br>R – Raises<br /></br>E – Emotions (up or down)<br /></br>D – Depression treated with<br /></br>N – SSRI (citalopram, sertraline) when<br /></br>O – Off-loading the steroid is<br /></br>NO – Not possible.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Supportive group psychotherapy – Helpful adjunct but insufficient for acute major depression, especially when a clear pharmacologic trigger exists and the offending drug cannot be stopped.  </p></br><p>c. Clonazepam – Benzodiazepines treat anxiety or agitation, not core depressive symptoms; add risk of dependence and sedation.  </p></br><p>d. Quetiapine – An antipsychotic used for steroid-induced psychosis or mania, not for uncomplicated depressive episodes without psychotic features. Unnecessary side-effect burden (metabolic, extrapyramidal).  </p></br><p>e. Increase prednisone dose – Would likely worsen the mood disorder; no clinical indication to escalate.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Concept:<br /></br>When systemic steroids cause a depressive episode that cannot be managed by dose reduction, treat with a standard antidepressant—most commonly an SSRI such as citalopram.</p>
139. An otherwise healthy 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because her left eye has been red since she awoke 4 hours ago. She has had no changes in her vision and cannot recall any trauma. She takes no medications. Her blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. A photograph of the left eye is shown. Her visual acuity is 20/20 in both eyes. Pupils are equal and reactive to light. Ocular movements are full. Visual field testing shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Reassurance</br>b. Slit-lamp examination</br>c. Complete blood count</br>d. Measurement of prothrombin and partial thromboplastin times</br>e. CT scan of the head</br>f. Topical methylprednisolone therapy</br>g. Topical sulfacetamide sodium therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Reassurance  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point to a benign subconjunctival hemorrhage:<br /></br>• “Red since she awoke 4 hours ago” – sudden onset; patients often notice it in the mirror on awakening.<br /></br>• “No changes in vision” – visual acuity, fields, pupils, and extra-ocular movements are all normal.<br /></br>• “Cannot recall any trauma” – most cases are spontaneous or occur after minor Valsalva (coughing, sneezing).<br /></br>• Photo (classic bright‐red well-circumscribed patch) – blood trapped between conjunctiva &amp; sclera, sharply demarcated, not a diffuse injection.<br /></br>• Normal vital signs and no meds – rules out anticoagulant use.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach: In any “red eye” question, first triage for danger signs (pain, photophobia, corneal opacity, vision change, unequal pupils, trauma): none are present → think benign etiologies such as subconjunctival hemorrhage or episcleritis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify red eye pattern → focal, bright red blotch = subconjunctival hemorrhage.<br /></br>2. Rule out serious pathology → vision intact, painless, normal pupil reactivity → no globe injury, keratitis, uveitis or acute glaucoma.<br /></br>3. Determine management → condition is self-limited (resolves in 1–2 wks). No labs or imaging required unless hemorrhages are recurrent or patient is on anticoagulation.<br /></br>Take-home message: A single, painless, localized subconjunctival hemorrhage in an otherwise healthy patient requires only reassurance ± artificial tears for comfort.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): note absence of pain/photophobia, normal visual acuity, lack of trauma/anticoagulant use, and patient education given.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Rupture of conjunctival capillaries → blood accumulates under bulbar conjunctiva.<br /></br>• Precipitants: Valsalva, minor eye rubbing, hypertension spikes, coughing, sneezing; often idiopathic.<br /></br>• Natural history: Blood is slowly resorbed by lymphatics over 1–2 weeks, changing colors like a bruise. No lasting sequelae.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>First-line: None needed. Advise patient:<br /></br>• Expect discoloration to spread then fade over ~14 days.<br /></br>• Return if pain, vision change, recurrent episodes, or if on anticoagulants/bleeding disorders.<br /></br>Artificial tears can alleviate mild foreign-body sensation but are optional.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Bright red, no dread.” – If eye is brilliantly red but the patient has no pain, no vision change, think subconjunctival hemorrhage → reassure.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Slit-lamp examination – Necessary for painful red eye, suspected corneal abrasion, foreign body, keratitis, or uveitis. Not needed when presentation is classic and benign.  </p></br><p>c. Complete blood count – Consider with recurrent hemorrhages, systemic bruising, or anemia suspicion; single isolated event in healthy adult does not warrant.  </p></br><p>d. PT/PTT – As above, only if concern for coagulopathy or anticoagulant therapy.  </p></br><p>e. CT scan of the head – Indicated for traumatic orbital injuries, proptosis, limited EOM. Normal exam rules this out.  </p></br><p>f. Topical methylprednisolone – Steroid drops treat anterior uveitis/allergic conjunctivitis; may worsen infection and unnecessary for hemorrhage.  </p></br><p>g. Topical sulfacetamide sodium – Antibiotic for bacterial conjunctivitis (diffuse conjunctival injection + discharge), not a blood patch.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>A painless, well-circumscribed bright-red patch on the conjunctiva with normal vision is a subconjunctival hemorrhage. Management is simple reassurance; the lesion will self-resolve without intervention.</p>
140. A 43-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 10-year history of enlarging, painless growths on her eyelids. She also has a 1-year history of mild fatigue and occasional bilateral knee pain. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the eyelids is shown. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Determination of erythrocyte sedimentation rate</br>b. Fasting serum lipid studies</br>c. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br>d. Serum antinuclear antibody assay</br>e. Serum rheumatoid factor assay</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Fasting serum lipid studies  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key buzzwords &amp; findings<br /></br>   • “Enlarging, painless growths on her eyelids” present for 10 years<br /></br>   • Photograph (not shown here) in the actual exam depicts soft, yellow-to-orange, slightly raised plaques clustered near the medial canthi.<br /></br>   • No tenderness, normal vital signs, otherwise unremarkable exam.<br /></br>   → Classic description of xanthelasma (xanthelasma palpebrarum).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives that steer you away from other diagnoses<br /></br>   • No systemic inflammatory symptoms (fever, weight loss, morning stiffness &gt;1 hour).<br /></br>   • No thyroid symptoms (cold intolerance, constipation, hair loss).<br /></br>   • No photosensitivity, rash, oral ulcers, or Raynaud phenomenon.<br /></br>   • No subcutaneous nodules typical of rheumatoid arthritis (RA).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach: When you see yellow peri-orbital plaques, think “xanth-” → check lipids. The question usually asks for “next best step” or “test to confirm.” Fasting lipid panel is the high-yield answer.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-by-Step)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify lesion = xanthelasma → lipid-laden macrophages in dermis.  </li></br><li>Most common association = hyperlipidemia (especially elevated total cholesterol &amp; LDL).  </li></br><li>Confirm hyperlipidemia with a fasting serum lipid profile.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Periorbital xanthelasmata are dermatologic clues to underlying lipid disorders. Always order a fasting lipid panel.</li></br></ol></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Yellow, flat, slightly raised plaques over bilateral upper eyelids consistent with xanthelasma; plan—obtain fasting lipid profile to evaluate for hypercholesterolemia.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Xanthelasma = subtype of xanthoma localized to eyelids.<br /></br>• Pathology: Cholesterol esters accumulate within dermal macrophages (foam cells).<br /></br>• Etiologies:<br /></br>  – Primary hyperlipidemias (Type IIa familial hypercholesterolemia, Type III dysbetalipoproteinemia).<br /></br>  – Secondary hypercholesterolemia (cholestatic liver disease, hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, diabetes, medications).<br /></br>• Patients may be normolipidemic (~50 %), but guidelines still recommend lipid testing because an occult disorder is treatable and cardiovascular risk needs assessment.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Review  </p></br><p>Diagnostic algorithm:<br /></br>1. Fasting lipid panel (total cholesterol, LDL-C, HDL-C, triglycerides).<br /></br>2. If abnormal → rule out secondary causes with TSH, fasting glucose/A1c, LFTs, renal studies, etc.<br /></br>3. Tailor therapy:<br /></br>   • Lifestyle (dietary modification, exercise, weight loss).<br /></br>   • First-line drug for LDL elevation = high-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 40–80 mg).<br /></br>   • Consider ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors in familial cases.  </p></br><p>Treatment of lesion itself: largely cosmetic—surgical excision, laser ablation, or trichloroacetic acid—but addressing lipid abnormality is priority for CV risk reduction.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Xanthe-LIDS…check the LIPIDS.”<br /></br>Visual cue: Picture yellow “butter pads” on eyelids → butter = fat → think cholesterol.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. ESR – Elevated in systemic inflammation (temporal arteritis, polymyalgia rheumatica, infection). Xanthelasma is non-inflammatory; ESR adds no diagnostic value.  </p></br><p>C. Serum TSH – Hypothyroidism can cause hypercholesterolemia and sometimes xanthelasma, but TSH is a secondary work-up step after confirming dyslipidemia, not the initial “test to confirm the diagnosis” of xanthelasma.  </p></br><p>D. ANA – Screens for systemic lupus erythematosus and other connective-tissue diseases, which do not present with isolated eyelid xanthomas.  </p></br><p>E. Rheumatoid factor – Suggestive of RA; RA can produce subcutaneous nodules, dry eyes (Sjögren), or scleritis, not yellow eyelid plaques. Mild knee pain alone is nonspecific; RF would not confirm the eyelid lesions.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Students anchor on the patient’s “fatigue and knee pain” and over-order autoimmune labs. Always match the test with the most characteristic physical finding being asked about.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>Yellow, soft, painless peri-orbital plaques = xanthelasma → think lipid disorder. First step to confirm = fasting lipid profile.</p>
141. A 52-year-old man is brought to the physician because of a 2-day history of pain at the base of his scrotum, fever, and confusion. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus poorly controlled with insulin. He has no other history of serious illness. He appears lethargic. He is oriented to person but not to place or time. His temperature is 39.2°C (102.6°F), pulse is 120/min, and blood pressure is 118/78 mm Hg. Examination of the scrotum shows several bullae over the scrotum and crepitus of the perineum. The scrotum is edematous and tender. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. His leukocyte count is 16,000/mm^3 with a left shift, and serum glucose concentration is 415 mg/dL. In addition to insulin and antibiotic therapy, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Culture of bullae</br>b. CT scan of the pelvis</br>c. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy</br>d. Surgical debridement</br>e. No further management is indicated at this time</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Surgical debridement</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases that clinch the diagnosis<br /></br>   • “Pain at the base of his scrotum, fever, and confusion” → toxic‐appearing, systemic involvement.<br /></br>   • “Several bullae over the scrotum and crepitus of the perineum” → subcutaneous gas = necrotizing soft-tissue infection.<br /></br>   • Poorly controlled diabetes + leukocytosis + hyperglycemia → classic host factors for necrotizing fasciitis (especially Fournier gangrene in the perineum).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Rapid (2-day) progression, severe pain out of proportion to exam, toxic vitals (T 39.2 °C, HR 120) → hallmark of necrotizing infection.<br /></br>   • No mention of testicular tenderness or epididymal swelling → argues against epididymo-orchitis.<br /></br>   • Crepitus eliminates cellulitis alone.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>   • Any diabetic or immunocompromised patient with acute pain + bullae + crepitus → think necrotizing fasciitis.<br /></br>   • First thought: “Cut it out fast” – definitive therapy is surgical exploration and wide debridement; nothing (imaging, cultures, HBOT) should delay the OR.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Recognize Fournier gangrene (necrotizing fasciitis of scrotum/perineum).<br /></br>2. Core management triad = (a) emergent surgical debridement, (b) broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering gram-positives, gram-negatives, and anaerobes (e.g., piperacillin–tazobactam + vancomycin + clindamycin), and (c) aggressive supportive care (IV fluids, tight glucose control).<br /></br>3. Delay in debridement sharply increases mortality; therefore surgery is the next immediate step.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life)<br /></br>   • Time of diagnosis of presumed necrotizing fasciitis.<br /></br>   • Surgical team and OR activation time.<br /></br>   • Antibiotics initiated, blood cultures drawn, informed consent obtained if possible.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: polymicrobial infection (type I: aerobic + anaerobic bacteria) thriving in hypoxic subcutaneous tissue; gas production → crepitus.<br /></br>• Risk factors: diabetes, obesity, immunosuppression, recent trauma or surgery, peripheral vascular disease.<br /></br>• Rapid spread along fascial planes → thrombosis of cutaneous vessels → ischemia → bullae/skin necrosis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Immediate actions:<br /></br>   1. Hemodynamic resuscitation, correct hyperglycemia with IV insulin.<br /></br>   2. Empiric IV antibiotics: Piperacillin-tazobactam (or meropenem) + vancomycin + clindamycin (inhibits toxin production).<br /></br>   3. Emergent surgical exploration and wide excisional debridement of all necrotic tissue ± repeat “second-look” operations within 24 h.  </p></br><p>Adjuncts (ONLY after debridement begun, never instead of):<br /></br>   • CT/MRI to define extent if diagnosis uncertain.<br /></br>   • Hyperbaric O₂ therapy in specialized centers.<br /></br>   • Culture/sensitivity obtained in OR to tailor antibiotics.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Four hours for Fournier: if you even THINK Fournier gangrene, the patient needs the OR within hours.”<br /></br>Mnemonic “CUT GAS” for necrotizing fasciitis red flags:<br /></br>   C – Crepitus<br /></br>   U – Uncontrolled diabetes / immunosuppression<br /></br>   T – Tachycardia &amp; Toxic appearance<br /></br>   G – Gray (“dishwater”) drainage / bullae<br /></br>   A – Acute severe pain out of proportion<br /></br>   S – Surgical debridement lifesaving</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Culture of bullae<br /></br>   • Cultures are obtained, but bedside or OR cultures should never precede or delay emergent debridement.  </p></br><p>b. CT scan of the pelvis<br /></br>   • Imaging can delineate fascial spread when diagnosis unclear, but in a textbook presentation with crepitus and systemic toxicity it wastes lifesaving time.  </p></br><p>c. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy<br /></br>   • Possible adjunct after surgery, not a substitute for immediate debridement; evidence for mortality benefit is limited.  </p></br><p>e. No further management is indicated at this time<br /></br>   • Lethal error. Mortality of untreated necrotizing fasciitis approaches 100 %. Delay worsens prognosis dramatically.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Necrotizing fasciitis/Fournier gangrene is a surgical emergency—debride first, ask questions later.</p>
142. A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents because of a 2-day history of increasingly severe pain in his right leg with an associated limp. He has not fallen or sustained any trauma. One week ago, he was treated with amoxicillin for a middle ear infection. He has no history of serious illness and currently takes no medications. He is at the 50th percentile for height and weight. Vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, the patient holds the right hip flexed and externally rotated. Passive range of motion of the right hip is limited by pain. An x-ray of the right hip shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. CT scan of the right hip</br>b. Measurement of serum inflammatory markers</br>c. Serum rheumatoid factor assay</br>d. Technetium 99m bone scan</br>e. X-ray of the right knee</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Measurement of serum inflammatory markers (eg, ESR ± CRP ± CBC with differential)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• 4-year-old boy – typical age for transient synovitis or septic arthritis of the hip.<br /></br>• 2-day history of “increasingly severe pain … with an associated limp” – acute inflammatory process.<br /></br>• No trauma.<br /></br>• Holds hip flexed &amp; externally rotated, ↓ passive ROM because of pain – classic irritable hip presentation.<br /></br>• Hip x-ray normal – rules out fracture, SCFE, obvious Perthes lesion.<br /></br>• Afebrile now, but fever may be intermittent (not enough to rule out septic joint).  </p></br><p>Approach framework (“irritable hip” algorithm):<br /></br>1. Rule-out fracture (plain film) → already done &amp; normal.<br /></br>2. Distinguish benign transient synovitis from dangerous septic arthritis / osteomyelitis.<br /></br>   • Use Kocher criteria: Temp &gt; 38.5 °C, WBC &gt; 12 000/µL, ESR &gt; 40 mm/h (or CRP &gt; 2 mg/dL), inability to bear weight.<br /></br>   • ≥2 positive → obtain arthrocentesis ± imaging; 0–1 → likely transient synovitis, observe.<br /></br>→ Next essential data point = serum inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP, CBC).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Anytime a child presents with an acutely painful hip and a normal x-ray, immediately think “Is this septic arthritis?” and reach for the Kocher labs before ordering cross-sectional imaging.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why labs first?<br /></br>• Septic arthritis can destroy cartilage within 24–48 h → must be ruled out early.<br /></br>• ESR/CRP &amp; CBC are quick, inexpensive, non-invasive, and stratify the need for emergent hip ultrasound &amp; diagnostic arthrocentesis.<br /></br>• If markers are elevated, proceed to ultrasound-guided joint aspiration and start empiric IV antibiotics.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In a limping child with normal plain films, laboratory inflammatory markers are the pivotal next step before more expensive imaging.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): “Child with acute atraumatic hip pain; x-ray normal. Plan: order CBC with differential, ESR, CRP to evaluate for septic arthritis vs transient synovitis per Kocher criteria.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Transient synovitis: post-viral immune reaction causing sterile effusion; mild ESR/CRP elevation or normal; self-limited.<br /></br>Septic arthritis: hematogenous seeding of joint (Staph aureus most common); marked elevation ESR/CRP/WBC; requires emergent drainage &amp; IV antibiotics to prevent joint destruction.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>Plain film → if normal but pain persists →  </li></br><li>Labs (CBC, ESR, CRP).<br /></br>   • Low suspicion → likely transient synovitis → NSAIDs, close follow-up.<br /></br>   • High suspicion (fever, ↑ labs) → <br /></br>        a. Hip ultrasound for effusion<br /></br>        b. Diagnostic/therapeutic arthrocentesis<br /></br>        c. Empiric IV vancomycin + (oxacillin/cefazolin if MSSA likely) pending culture.  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“SEPTIC” mnemonic for Kocher criteria:<br /></br>S – Serum WBC &gt; 12k<br /></br>E – ESR &gt; 40<br /></br>P – Pyrexia &gt; 38.5 °C<br /></br>T – Unable To bear weight<br /></br>I – (Inflammatory) CRP &gt; 2 mg/dL<br /></br>C – Count criteria (0–1 = transient synovitis likely; 2–4 = septic arthritis likely)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. CT scan of the right hip<br /></br>   • Good for cortical bone detail/fractures or tumor, not for effusion or early infection; exposes child to radiation; skips essential screening labs.  </p></br><p>c. Serum rheumatoid factor assay<br /></br>   • Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) is chronic, not acutely painful; RF rarely positive in kids &lt;5 yrs; onset is weeks-to-months, not 2 days.  </p></br><p>d. Technetium-99m bone scan<br /></br>   • Can pick up osteomyelitis after 24–48 h, but expensive, less specific, and not first-line when septic arthritis is higher on the differential.  </p></br><p>e. X-ray of the right knee<br /></br>   • Hip pathology can refer pain to knee, but the hip film is already normal. Still would not change urgent need to rule out septic joint; labs come first.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>In an afebrile but symptomatic 4-year-old with a normal hip x-ray, the immediate next step is to obtain ESR, CRP, and CBC to apply Kocher criteria and rapidly distinguish transient synovitis from limb-threatening septic arthritis.</p>
143. A 62-year-old man with a 6-month history of poorly controlled hypertension comes to the office for a follow-up examination. His blood pressure measurements 1 year ago averaged 140/90 mm Hg; at that time, he was treated with hydrochlorothiazide. He is now taking the maximum doses of enalapril, metoprolol, and hydrochlorothiazide. He also has peripheral vascular disease. His blood pressure today is 170/110 mm Hg. Examination shows narrowing of the retinal arterioles. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination shows an S4 at the lower left sternal border. The abdomen is nontender, and a systolic bruit is heard just to the right of the umbilicus. The dorsalis pedis and posterior tibialis pulses are absent bilaterally; there is no lower extremity edema.
