Laboratory studies show:</br>Serum</br>K+ 3.6 mEq/L</br>Urea nitrogen 25 mg/dL</br>Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL</br>Urine</br>Protein 1+</br>RBC none</br>WBC none</br>Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Intravenous pyelography</br>b. Measurement of peripheral plasma renin activity</br>c. Measurement of urine catecholamine concentration</br>d. MR angiography of the renal arteries</br>e. Kidney biopsy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. MR angiography of the renal arteries  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should immediately raise suspicion for renovascular (renal-artery stenosis) hypertension:</br>1. Resistant HTN: BP 170/110 mm Hg despite maximally tolerated doses of three different antihypertensives (thiazide, ACE-I, β-blocker).<br /></br>2. Abdominal bruit to the right of the umbilicus → classic objective finding of turbulent blood flow in a stenotic renal artery.<br /></br>3. Systemic atherosclerosis: peripheral vascular disease, absent pedal pulses, retinal arteriolar narrowing, S4 (long-standing HTN).<br /></br>4. Mild, otherwise unexplained hypokalemia (K⁺ 3.6) in a patient already on an ACE-I, suggesting secondary hyperaldosteronism driven by ↑ renin release from an under-perfused kidney.<br /></br>5. Normal creatinine (1.1 mg/dL) and only trace proteinuria → renovascular disease usually produces minimal urinary abnormalities until late.</p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions:</br>• Any patient with (a) abrupt onset or rapidly worsening HTN after 55 y, (b) malignant or resistant HTN on ≥3 meds, or (c) HTN with flash pulmonary edema or unexplained hypokalemia merits evaluation for secondary causes—top of list is renovascular HTN.<br /></br>• A systolic abdominal bruit is nearly pathognomonic; pursue vascular imaging rather than more invasive or unrelated testing.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning / Step-by-Step Thought Process  </p></br><ol></br><li>Recognize the clinical pattern of secondary (renovascular) HTN.  </li></br><li>Decide between diagnostic modalities: duplex Doppler ultrasound, CT angiography (CTA), MR angiography (MRA), or catheter-based digital subtraction angiography (gold standard but invasive).  </li></br><li>In an older patient with atherosclerosis and normal kidney function, non-invasive vascular imaging (CTA or MRA) is preferred first.  </li></br><li>Because the question lists “MR angiography of the renal arteries,” that is the best next step.</li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: Resistant HTN + abdominal bruit → image renal arteries.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): note degree of BP control, medication list and doses, bruit location, renal function, plan for MRA, and counseling about contrast risks.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Atherosclerotic plaque narrows the proximal renal artery → ↓ perfusion pressure → the juxtaglomerular apparatus secretes renin → ↑ angiotensin II → systemic vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated Na⁺/water retention → secondary hyperaldosteronism → HTN ± hypokalemia.<br /></br>• Over time, chronic ischemia can cause ischemic nephropathy and atrophic kidney (“string of beads” would instead suggest fibromuscular dysplasia in younger women).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li>Confirm diagnosis with non-invasive imaging (Doppler, CTA, or MRA).  </li></br><li>If significant (&gt;70%) stenosis with refractory HTN, recurrent flash pulmonary edema, or rapidly declining renal function, proceed to revascularization (percutaneous transluminal angioplasty ± stent).  </li></br><li>Continue optimal medical therapy (ACE-I/ARB carefully, diuretics, statins, antiplatelet therapy, smoking cessation).  </li></br><li>Monitor renal function after ACE-I/ARB initiation; an acute rise in creatinine &gt;30% suggests bilateral significant stenosis.</li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“RAB” mnemonic for secondary HTN with Renal Artery Bruit:<br /></br>R – Resistant HTN on ≥3 drugs<br /></br>A – Abdominal brUit (say it as “brOO-it” so the U reminds you of Under-perfused kidney)<br /></br>B – Bruit = go get vascular imaging (CTA/MRA)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Intravenous pyelography (IVP)<br /></br>   • Visualizes collecting system, stones, masses; poor at depicting renal arteries. Not useful for renovascular HTN.  </p></br><p>b. Measurement of peripheral plasma renin activity<br /></br>   • Renin may be high, low, or normal in essential HTN; overlap is large. Renin alone cannot localize disease and is less sensitive/specific than imaging; also patient is already on ACE-I and β-blocker, altering renin levels.  </p></br><p>c. Measurement of urine catecholamine concentration<br /></br>   • Screens for pheochromocytoma (paroxysmal HTN, headaches, diaphoresis, palpitations). No paroxysms, no classic triad, presence of abdominal bruit ⇒ renovascular far more likely.  </p></br><p>e. Kidney biopsy<br /></br>   • Indicated for intrinsic renal parenchymal diseases (e.g., glomerulonephritis). Normal creatinine, bland urine sediment, trace protein, and strong bruit point away from primary renal pathology. Biopsy would not help and carries risk.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In an older patient with diffuse atherosclerosis, resistant HTN, and an abdominal bruit, the next appropriate diagnostic step is non-invasive vascular imaging—MR angiography of the renal arteries.</p>
144. Twelve hours after delivery, a newborn is cyanotic and in respiratory distress. He was born at term to a 27-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, following an uncomplicated pregnancy; he weighed 3317 g (7 lb 5 oz). Delivery was complicated by meconium aspiration, and the trachea was suctioned at birth. Apgar scores were 3, 5, and 8 at 1, 5, and 10 minutes, respectively. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 170/min, respirations are 70/min, and blood pressure is 70/50 mm Hg. Examination shows labored breathing with grunting, nasal flaring, and subcostal and intercostal retractions. The lungs are clear to auscultation. A loud S2 is heard. An X-ray of the chest shows small scattered infiltrates in the lung fields and a normal cardiac silhouette. An ECG shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's respiratory distress?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Congenital cystic adenomatoid malformation</br>b. Left-to-right cardiac shunting</br>c. Pneumothorax</br>d. Premature closure of the ductus arteriosus</br>e. Pulmonary hypertension</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Pulmonary hypertension (persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn, PPHN)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point to PPHN:<br /></br>• “Born at term … uncomplicated pregnancy” → rules out surfactant-deficient RDS (premies).<br /></br>• Meconium aspiration at birth → classic antecedent for PPHN because hypoxia/acidemia and meconium-induced inflammation cause intense pulmonary vasoconstriction.<br /></br>• On exam:  – Severe cyanosis + tachypnea + retractions.<br /></br>  – Lungs “clear to auscultation” → distress without appreciable parenchymal disease.<br /></br>  – “Loud S2” → accentuated pulmonic component, a hallmark of elevated pulmonary artery pressure.<br /></br>• CXR: “small scattered infiltrates … normal cardiac silhouette” → no large pneumonia, no cardiomegaly/CHF, no mass, no tension pneumothorax; suggests mainly a vascular problem.<br /></br>• Normal ECG → no structural heart disease.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework:<br /></br>1. In a term infant with early profound cyanosis, first decide: pulmonary parenchyma vs pulmonary blood flow vs intracardiac shunt.<br /></br>2. Clear lungs + loud S2 → think high PVR (PPHN) or cyanotic CHD with R→L flow.<br /></br>3. Normal heart size &amp; ECG makes CHD less likely; perinatal stressor (meconium) points to PPHN.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why E is correct)  </p></br><p>Pathway: Perinatal hypoxia/acidosis + meconium in small airways → pulmonary vasoconstriction fails to fall after birth → pulmonary artery pressure remains higher than systemic → right-to-left shunt across foramen ovale and/or ductus arteriosus → severe hypoxemia despite adequate ventilation → cyanosis, loud S2, clear lungs.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: A term or post-term neonate with cyanosis, clear lungs, loud single S2, and a perinatal stressor = persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): Oxygen saturations (pre- and post-ductal), arterial blood gases, echocardiogram confirming elevated PAP and R→L shunt, response to O2, nitric oxide, or hyperventilation test.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Brief Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Normal transition: first breaths ↓ pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) dramatically.<br /></br>• PPHN: anything keeping PVR high (meconium aspiration, perinatal asphyxia, sepsis, congenital diaphragmatic hernia) → persistent fetal circulation.<br /></br>• Elevated PVR → R→L shunt through PDA/PFO → systemic hypoxemia even with 100% FiO2 (“oxygenation failure”).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management / Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><ol></br><li>Immediate: 100% O2 (pulmonary vasodilator), gentle ventilation to avoid barotrauma.  </li></br><li>Target PaO2 &gt; 60–70 mm Hg, avoid acidosis (give bicarbonate if needed).  </li></br><li>Specific therapy: inhaled nitric oxide (selective pulmonary vasodilator).  </li></br><li>Adjuncts: sildenafil, prostacyclin, high-frequency oscillatory ventilation, ECMO for refractory cases.  </li></br><li>Diagnostics: pre- vs post-ductal oxygen saturation gradient &gt; 10%, echocardiogram to confirm high PAP and exclude structural heart disease.</li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“MAS = Meconium → Acidemia → (P)ulmonary hypertension.”<br /></br>Think “Loud S2 = Loud Squeezing 2 lungs (PAP high).”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>a. Congenital cystic adenomatoid malformation: Usually prenatal/early postnatal respiratory distress with a unilateral multicystic mass, mediastinal shift on CXR—not scattered infiltrates or clear lungs.  </p></br><p>b. Left-to-right cardiac shunting (e.g., VSD, PDA): Yields tachypnea ± CHF over days–weeks, NOT immediate severe cyanosis; CXR would show cardiomegaly and pulmonary over-circulation, not clear lungs. Murmur often present, not loud single S2.  </p></br><p>c. Pneumothorax: Presents with unilateral decreased breath sounds, asymmetric chest expansion, hyperresonance, possible mediastinal shift, and CXR shows air in pleural space—none of which are described.  </p></br><p>d. Premature closure of the ductus arteriosus: Causes fetal right-sided heart failure and hydrops; postnatally the ductus is normally closing. Would not cause sudden cyanosis with loud P2 in an otherwise normal term infant.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>In a term infant with perinatal stress (meconium), clear lungs, a loud S2, and refractory hypoxemia, think persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn. Treat with oxygen, avoid acidosis, and use selective pulmonary vasodilators (inhaled NO).</p>
145. A 32-year-old primigravid woman at 16 weeks’ gestation is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after the onset of heavy vaginal bleeding, palpitations, and moderate headache. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 150/100 mm Hg. She appears flushed and anxious. The uterus is consistent in size with a 20-week gestation. No fetal heart tones are heard. Urinalysis shows 2+ protein. Ultrasonography shows a uterus with a cystic appearance and 10-cm, cystic adnexal masses bilaterally. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Oral administration of misoprostol</br>b. Intramuscular administration of methotrexate</br>c. Dilation and suction evacuation</br>d. Exploratory laparotomy and bilateral ovarian cystectomies</br>e. Operative laparoscopy and bilateral ovarian cystectomies</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Dilation and suction evacuation (suction curettage/D&amp;E)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>“Primigravid … 16 weeks” + “uterus consistent with 20 weeks”<br /></br>   • Size-&gt;date discrepancy is classic for a molar pregnancy.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>“Heavy vaginal bleeding,” “no fetal heart tones,” “cystic appearance” of the uterus on US<br /></br>   • “Snow-storm” or “cluster of grapes” pattern = hydatidiform mole.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Maternal systemic findings<br /></br>   • Headache, palpitations, flushing, HR 110, BP 150/100, 2+ protein → early‐onset gestational hypertension/preeclampsia (&lt;20 weeks) strongly suggests a molar pregnancy driven by extremely high β-hCG.<br /></br>   • Bilateral 10-cm “cystic adnexal masses” = theca-lutein cysts stimulated by β-hCG.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>These clues rule in a complete hydatidiform mole and rule out viable intra-uterine pregnancy, ectopic, inevitable abortion, etc.</p></br><p>Cognitive framework for future questions:<br /></br>• Size&gt;dates + first-trimester/early second-trimester preeclampsia or hyperemesis → think mole.<br /></br>• Confirm with US → immediate uterine evacuation by suction curettage.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Goal: remove molar tissue quickly to stop hemorrhage, prevent malignant transformation (invasive mole/choriocarcinoma), and correct maternal complications.</p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify molar pregnancy (based on US + clinical findings).<br /></br>2. First-line evacuation method in a hemodynamically stable patient up to ~24 weeks = suction curettage with cervical dilation under anesthesia.<br /></br>3. Follow with serial quantitative β-hCG until undetectable for 6 months while the patient uses reliable contraception.</p></br><p>Documentation points: hemodynamic status, uterine size, β-hCG level, consent for suction curettage, Rh status and Rh immunoglobulin administration if Rh-negative.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Hydatidiform mole ⇒ immediate uterine evacuation by dilation and suction curettage.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Complete mole: fertilization of an “empty” ovum by a duplicated sperm (46,XX paternal only) → no fetal tissue, diffuse trophoblastic proliferation, massively elevated β-hCG.<br /></br>High β-hCG → </br>• theca-lutein ovarian cysts,<br /></br>• hyperemesis gravidarum,<br /></br>• early-onset preeclampsia/gestational HTN,<br /></br>• hyperthyroidism-like symptoms (β-hCG weakly stimulates TSH receptor).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><ol></br><li>Immediate: suction curettage ± IV fluids, blood products if needed.  </li></br><li>Post-evacuation:<br /></br>   • Serial quantitative β-hCG weekly until 3 negatives, then monthly for 6 months.<br /></br>   • Effective contraception (OCPs, IUD, implant) during surveillance.<br /></br>   • Chest X-ray if β-hCG plateaus/rises (evaluate for metastatic gestational trophoblastic neoplasia).  </li></br><li>Theca-lutein cysts usually regress spontaneously after β-hCG normalizes—no surgical removal is required.</li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“MOLE”:<br /></br>• M = Markedly elevated β-hCG<br /></br>• O = Ovarian theca-lutein cysts<br /></br>• L = Larger than dates uterus, “snow-storm” look<br /></br>• E = Early-onset preeclampsia → Evacuate with suction curettage.</p></br><p>Picture grapes in a vacuum hose = molar “grapes” sucked out by suction curettage.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Oral misoprostol (PGE1)<br /></br>   • Used for medical termination or missed/incomplete abortion. In molar pregnancy it is slower and carries higher risk of hemorrhage and retained molar tissue → not first-line.</p></br><p>b. Intramuscular methotrexate<br /></br>   • First-line for unruptured ectopic pregnancy and chemotherapy for persistent gestational trophoblastic neoplasia/choriocarcinoma. Initial management of a molar pregnancy is surgical evacuation, not systemic chemotherapy.</p></br><p>d. Exploratory laparotomy with bilateral ovarian cystectomies<br /></br>e. Operative laparoscopy with bilateral ovarian cystectomies<br /></br>   • Theca-lutein cysts are hormonally driven and almost always regress after molar tissue is removed. Surgery risks bleeding and ovarian damage; only indicated if cysts are torsed, ruptured, or fail to regress.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>Complete hydatidiform mole presents with size&gt;dates uterus, very high β-hCG, early preeclampsia, “snow-storm” US, and bilateral theca-lutein cysts. Immediate dilation and suction evacuation is the correct management; follow with β-hCG surveillance to detect persistent trophoblastic disease.</p>
146. A 57-year-old woman comes to the physician because of vaginal bleeding for 10 days. Her last menstrual period was 9 years ago. She is not receiving hormone replacement therapy. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 85 kg (188 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m2. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Transvaginal ultrasonography shows a 2.5-cm subserosal leiomyoma uteri and an 18-mm endometrium. There are no ovarian abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Hormone replacement therapy</br>b. Endometrial sampling</br>c. Endometrial ablation</br>d. Myomectomy</br>e. Hysterectomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Endometrial sampling (office endometrial biopsy or dilation &amp; curettage)</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases that should ring alarm bells<br /></br>   • “57-year-old … last menstrual period 9 years ago” → post-menopausal woman.<br /></br>   • “Vaginal bleeding for 10 days” → post-menopausal bleeding (PMB) until proven otherwise.<br /></br>   • “Transvaginal US … 18-mm endometrium” → markedly thickened; normal post-meno cutoff is ≤4–5 mm.<br /></br>   • BMI 29 (overweight/obese) → unopposed peripheral estrogen → risk factor for endometrial carcinoma.<br /></br>   • Small (2.5 cm) subserosal fibroid → outside uterine cavity; rarely causes PMB.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives: PMB, thick stripe, obesity.<br /></br>   Pertinent negatives: No ovarian mass, no HRT, no coagulopathy.  </p></br></li></br><li>Pattern: PMB + endometrial stripe &gt;4 mm → “must sample the endometrium first.”</li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any time you see a postmenopausal patient with ANY bleeding, mentally lock into the algorithm “TVUS → if stripe &gt;4 mm or imaging unavailable → perform endometrial biopsy.”</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why endometrial sampling is next)  </p></br><p>Step-wise algorithm:<br /></br>1. PMB → initial evaluation with transvaginal ultrasound OR endometrial biopsy.<br /></br>2. If TVUS shows endometrial thickness &gt;4 mm → proceed to biopsy to rule out hyperplasia/carcinoma.<br /></br>3. Definitive treatment (e.g., hysterectomy) is considered only after histologic diagnosis.<br /></br>Take-home: Diagnosis precedes therapy; you must rule out cancer.</p></br><p>Documentation you would enter: “Post-menopausal bleeding. Endometrial stripe 18 mm. High suspicion for hyperplasia / carcinoma. Plan: office endometrial biopsy today; counselled patient on indications and risks.”</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Estrogen from aromatization in adipose tissue (↑ BMI) without progesterone opposition → continuous endometrial proliferation → hyperplasia → carcinoma (Type I endometrioid).<br /></br>• PMB is the classic presenting symptom (≈90 % of endometrial cancer).<br /></br>• Endometrium involutes after menopause; a stripe thicker than 4–5 mm is abnormal.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>Diagnostic hierarchy for PMB:<br /></br>1. TVUS (stripe measurement) or straight to office biopsy.<br /></br>2. If biopsy inadequate or focal lesion suspected → hysteroscopy ± dilation &amp; curettage.<br /></br>3. Management depends on pathology:<br /></br>   • Hyperplasia without atypia → progestin therapy ± repeat biopsy.<br /></br>   • Hyperplasia with atypia or carcinoma → hysterectomy with staging.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“BLEED”:<br /></br>B – Bleeding post-menopause<br /></br>L – Look with ultrasound<br /></br>E – Endometrium &gt;4 mm<br /></br>E – Evaluate tissue (biopsy)<br /></br>D – Decide treatment after diagnosis</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Hormone replacement therapy – Contraindicated; exogenous estrogen/progestin can worsen unexplained PMB and obscure diagnosis.  </p></br><p>c. Endometrial ablation – Used for heavy menses in PRE-menopausal women after malignancy is excluded; never first step in PMB.  </p></br><p>d. Myomectomy – Fibroid is small, subserosal, and not the likely source of bleeding; myomectomy is for symptomatic fibroids in women desiring fertility.  </p></br><p>e. Hysterectomy – Potential definitive treatment if cancer found, but invasive surgery is not done before tissue diagnosis. First diagnose, then treat.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>Post-menopausal bleeding = malignancy until proven otherwise.<br /></br>Ultrasound stripe &gt;4 mm mandates endometrial sampling — ALWAYS get tissue before therapy.</p>
147. A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus is brought to the emergency department by her family 24 hours after the onset of vomiting. She has not taken any medications during this period and has become confused during the past 4 hours. She has not had fever or any other symptoms. Her diabetes has been complicated by neuropathy, retinopathy, gastroparesis, and nephropathy. Her prescribed medications include short- and intermediate-acting insulin and gabapentin. She also takes aspirin as needed for headache. On arrival, she is oriented to person but not to place or time. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 112/78 mm Hg. Examination, including neurologic examination, shows no other abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL</br>Leukocyte count 14,200/mm^3</br>  Segmented neutrophils 90 percent</br>  Bands 4 percent</br>  Eosinophils 1 percent</br>  Lymphocytes 3 percent</br>  Monocytes 2 percent</br>Platelet count 240,000/mm^3</br>Serum</br>  Na+ 127 mEq/L</br>  K+ 4.2 mEq/L</br>  Cl- 98 mEq/L</br>  HCO3- 22 mEq/L</br>  Urea nitrogen 36 mg/dL</br>  Glucose 890 mg/dL</br>  Creatinine 2.6 mg/dL</br>Arterial blood gas analysis on room air shows:</br>  pH 7.35</br>  Pco2 37 mm Hg</br>  Po2 100 mm Hg</br>Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's confusion?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Aspirin overdose</br>b. Hyperosmolarity</br>c. Hyponatremia</br>d. Ketoacidosis</br>e. Lactic acidosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: b. Hyperosmolarity  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Long-standing type 2 diabetes with classic microvascular complications (neuropathy, retinopathy, nephropathy) → predisposed to hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS).<br /></br>  – Precipitating stressor: 24 h of vomiting → stopped all insulin → profound hyperglycemia (glucose 890 mg/dL).<br /></br>  – Mental-status change without focal deficits = common presentation of HHS.<br /></br>  – Labs: pH 7.35, HCO₃ 22 mEq/L, anion gap 7 → no significant metabolic acidosis or ketosis.<br /></br>  – Measured Na⁺ 127 mEq/L appears low, but when corrected for hyperglycemia becomes ~140 mEq/L (ΔNa ≈ +1.6 mEq/L for every 100 mg/dL glucose over 100).<br /></br>  – Effective serum osmolality = 2 × Na(corrected) + glucose/18 ≈ 2 × 140 + 890/18 ≈ 329 mOsm/kg (normal 275-295) → markedly hyperosmolar.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Check glucose. If &gt;600 think HHS.<br /></br>2. Evaluate pH &amp; HCO₃ to rule out DKA (&lt;7.30, HCO₃ &lt;18) or lactic acidosis.<br /></br>3. Correct Na⁺, then calculate serum osmolality. If &gt;320, expect neurologic symptoms (confusion, seizures, coma).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise link to the correct answer:<br /></br>1. Extreme hyperglycemia (890 mg/dL) → free-water shift from intracellular to extracellular compartment → dilutional “pseudo-hyponatremia.”<br /></br>2. Resultant plasma hyperosmolarity (&gt;320 mOsm/kg) directly causes neuronal dehydration and cerebral dysfunction → acute confusion.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In HHS the neurologic findings correlate with serum osmolality, not acidosis or sodium concentration.  </p></br><p>Documentation items (real patient): mental status, precipitating illness, glucose level, corrected Na⁺, calculated osmolality, fluid balance, renal function, plan for IVF + insulin + electrolyte repletion.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• HHS = insulin deficiency + peripheral insulin resistance → severe hyperglycemia → osmotic diuresis → profound dehydration → elevated serum osmolality.<br /></br>• Residual insulin suppresses lipolysis → minimal ketone production, so pH usually &gt;7.30 and HCO₃ &gt;18 mEq/L (distinguishes from DKA).<br /></br>• CNS manifestations (confusion → coma) are proportional to hyperosmolarity.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests (for context)<br /></br>1. Immediate large-volume isotonic saline (restore perfusion, lower osmolality).<br /></br>2. Regular insulin infusion after initial fluids (reduce glucose gradually; avoid rapid shifts).<br /></br>3. Potassium monitoring/replacement once urine output adequate (total-body K⁺ deficit despite normal serum value).<br /></br>4. Search for precipitating cause (infection, MI, medications) via cultures, CXR, ECG, etc.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“HHS = Huge Hyperglycemia &amp; Serum-osmolality”<br /></br>• Glucose &gt;600<br /></br>• pH &gt;7.30 (no acidosis)<br /></br>• Serum osMol &gt;320 → Mental status change  </p></br><p>Think “HHS makes neurons shrink → brain Haze.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Aspirin overdose<br /></br>   • Produces early respiratory alkalosis followed by anion-gap metabolic acidosis (low pH, low pCO₂). Not seen here. No tinnitus or history of excess ASA use.  </p></br><p>c. Hyponatremia<br /></br>   • Severe neurologic symptoms usually when Na⁺ &lt;120 mEq/L or falls rapidly. After correction for hyperglycemia, this patient’s true Na⁺ ≈140 mEq/L → normonatremic.  </p></br><p>d. Ketoacidosis<br /></br>   • DKA: glucose 250-600 mg/dL, pH &lt;7.30, HCO₃ &lt;18, positive serum/urine ketones, high anion gap. None of these are present.  </p></br><p>e. Lactic acidosis<br /></br>   • Would cause anion-gap metabolic acidosis with low HCO₃ and low pH. Vitals lack hypotension or severe sepsis; pH and gap are normal.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>This patient’s confusion is best explained by severe hyperosmolarity from a hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state, not acidosis or sodium disturbance.</p>
148. Six hours after undergoing surgical excision of the distal colon for cancer, a 77-year-old man has decreased urine output. His urine output has been 10 mL/h during the past 3 hours. During the 4-hour operation, he lost 500 mL of blood and underwent transfusion of 1 unit of packed red blood cells. His initial postoperative course was uncomplicated. Two years ago, he had a myocardial infarction and underwent coronary artery bypass grafting. He has a 10-year history of hypertension well controlled with atenolol. Current medications include morphine and labetalol. He currently appears pale and diaphoretic. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 85/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 2 L/min of oxygen via nasal cannula shows an oxygen saturation of 89 percent. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. The abdomen is nondistended with mild tenderness over the incision. His hematocrit is 24 percent, serum sodium concentration is 140 mEq/L, and serum creatinine concentration is 2.1 mg/dL. The patient is switched to administration of 100 percent oxygen by a nonrebreathing face mask. The most appropriate next step in management is administration of which of the following?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Bumetanide</br>b. 5 percent Dextrose in water</br>c. Fresh frozen plasma</br>d. Furosemide</br>e. Packed red blood cells</br>f. 0.45 percent Saline</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Packed red blood cells (PRBCs)</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives<br /></br>   • Oliguria: 10 mL/h × 3 h (normal ≥ 0.5 mL/kg/h)<br /></br>   • Hypotension: 90/60 mm Hg (new compared with intra-op record)<br /></br>   • Pale, diaphoretic, O2 sat 89 % despite supplemental O2<br /></br>   • Hematocrit 24 % (markedly ↓; most elderly men start ≈ 40 %)<br /></br>   • β-blockers (atenolol, labetalol) --&gt; masks reflex tachycardia, so HR 85/min is not reassuring.<br /></br>   • Cr 2.1 mg/dL (acute prerenal azotemia from hypovolemia/poor perfusion).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No crackles, JVD, or peripheral edema → not volume overloaded.<br /></br>   • Abdomen soft, incision tender only → no peritonitis or massive intra-abdominal bleed suspected clinically.<br /></br>   • No active coagulopathy reported.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern: Post-op elderly patient with unexpected anemia + hypotension + end-organ hypoperfusion (kidney → oliguria; skin → diaphoresis; SpO2 ↓) in the setting of inadequate replacement of operative blood loss → think occult blood loss or hemodilution → treat with PRBCs.</p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>Step 1 – Check vitals/urine output.<br /></br>Step 2 – Ask “Is perfusion adequate?” (BP, urine, mental status, lactate, Cr).<br /></br>Step 3 – If not, decide whether the primary problem is pump, pipes, or blood. Here:<br /></br>   • Pump: no signs of CHF, lungs clear.<br /></br>   • Pipes: no massive vasodilation/sepsis signs.<br /></br>   • Blood: Hct 24 % + symptoms → transfuse.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Goal: Restore intravascular volume and O2-carrying capacity.<br /></br>• Elderly patient with CAD has higher transfusion threshold (often keep Hgb &gt; 9-10 g/dL) to prevent myocardial ischemia.<br /></br>• PRBCs simultaneously supply volume (each unit ≈ 300 mL) and hemoglobin; crystalloid alone would dilute Hb further.<br /></br>• Treating the underlying prerenal azotemia and oliguria requires improving renal perfusion; blood is the fastest, most targeted therapy.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In a β-blocked, post-op patient, anemia + hypotension + oliguria is prerenal hypoperfusion until proven otherwise—transfuse PRBCs.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): estimated blood loss, current hemodynamics, urine output, lab results, indication for transfusion, informed consent, and post-transfusion monitoring plan.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Acute blood loss or peri-operative hemodilution ↓ circulating red cell mass → ↓ arterial O2 content (CaO2 = 1.34 × Hgb × SaO2 + 0.003 × PaO2).<br /></br>• ↓ CaO2 → tissue hypoxia → sympathetic activation (masked by β-blockers) → renal vasoconstriction → ↓ GFR → oliguria and ↑ Cr (prerenal azotemia).<br /></br>• Elderly myocardium &amp; prior MI cannot tolerate low O2 delivery; demand ischemia may precipitate another infarct if not corrected.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Algorithm (high-yield)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Evaluate vitals + UOP.  </li></br><li>Check Hb/Hct, lactate, Cr.  </li></br><li>If Hct &lt; 30 % in symptomatic patient or &lt; 25 % in CAD, transfuse PRBCs (1 unit ↑ Hct ~3 %; Hgb ~1 g/dL).  </li></br><li>Reassess BP, UOP, mental status; consider type &amp; cross for additional units; search for bleeding source (H&amp;H q6h, exam, imaging as needed).</li></br></ol></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Post-op 3 H’s = Hypotension + Hct low + Hypoperfusion → think Hemo (blood) loss → Hang blood.”<br /></br>OR<br /></br>“Beta-blocked hearts whisper, they don’t shout.” (Don’t rely on tachycardia to detect hypovolemia in patients on β-blockers.)</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Bumetanide (loop diuretic)<br /></br>   • Would worsen hypovolemia, drop BP further. Used for volume overload, not depletion.</p></br><p>b. 5 % Dextrose in water<br /></br>   • Distributes mostly to intracellular space; provides negligible intravascular expansion and zero O2-carrying capacity.</p></br><p>c. Fresh frozen plasma<br /></br>   • Corrects coagulopathy (INR &gt; 1.5, active bleeding). No evidence of clotting factor deficiency here; does not improve CaO2.</p></br><p>d. Furosemide<br /></br>   • Same rationale as bumetanide—treats fluid overload, not deficit; would further decrease renal perfusion.</p></br><p>f. 0.45 % Saline (half-normal)<br /></br>   • Hypotonic; rapidly leaves intravascular space, risks hyponatremia and cerebral edema; still fails to raise hemoglobin.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>In a post-operative patient with hypotension, oliguria, and low hematocrit—especially if β-blocked—immediate PRBC transfusion is indicated to restore intravascular volume and oxygen delivery, reversing prerenal azotemia and preventing end-organ ischemia.</p>
149. A 27-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 4-day history of pain and fullness in his left testicle and a 2-day history of mild pain with urination. He has been sexually active with one female sexual partner for the past 2 years. Examination shows mild left testicular and scrotal tenderness with no redness or edema. Urinalysis shows 3–5 WBC/hpf but no obvious organisms. Which of the following is the most likely causal organism?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Candida albicans  </br>b. Chlamydia trachomatis  </br>c. Escherichia coli  </br>d. Trichomonas vaginalis  </br>e. Ureaplasma urealyticum</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Chlamydia trachomatis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 27-year-old man (sexually active, &lt;35 yr)<br /></br>• 4 days of unilateral testicular pain/fullness → acute epididymitis pattern<br /></br>• Mild dysuria → antecedent urethritis<br /></br>• Physical exam: tender epididymis, no overlying erythema → typical early epididymitis, not torsion, orchitis, or cellulitis.<br /></br>• UA: 3-5 WBC/hpf (pyuria) but “no organisms” on standard light microscopy → points to an intracellular pathogen that does not Gram-stain well (classically Chlamydia).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Recognize the syndrome (acute unilateral scrotal pain in a young, sexually active man → epididymitis).<br /></br>2. Recall age-based etiologies:<br /></br>   • &lt;35 yr → C. trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae<br /></br>   • ≥35 yr or urinary tract manipulation → coliforms (E. coli)<br /></br>3. Match UA finding of pyuria without organisms to Chlamydia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Epididymitis results from retrograde ascent of pathogens from the urethra.<br /></br>• C. trachomatis is the most common cause of non-gonococcal urethritis and epididymitis in sexually active men under 35.<br /></br>• Because Chlamydia is an obligate intracellular bacterium, it is not seen on routine Gram stain or standard UA “organism” assessment → explains the leukocytes with “no organisms”.<br /></br>Take-home message: In a young man with epididymitis + pyuria but no organisms on UA, think Chlamydia.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>• Sexual history, condom use, prior STIs<br /></br>• Urethral swab/first-void urine NAAT for C. trachomatis &amp; N. gonorrhoeae<br /></br>• Empiric treatment given (IM ceftriaxone + oral doxycycline)<br /></br>• Partner notification/treatment counseling  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• C. trachomatis serovars D–K infect columnar epithelium of urethra → urethritis → spread via vas deferens to epididymis → inflammation, pain, swelling.<br /></br>• Obligate intracellular lifecycle (elementary &amp; reticulate bodies) = invisible on Gram stain; detected by nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT).<br /></br>• Risk factors: age &lt;35, multiple partners, inconsistent condom use.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><ol></br><li>Empiric therapy (per CDC/IDSA):<br /></br>   • Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM ×1 (covers possible N. gonorrhoeae)<br /></br>   • Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID ×7 days (covers C. trachomatis)  </li></br><li>Alternatives if contraindications: azithromycin 1 g PO ×1 instead of doxycycline.  </li></br><li>Scrotal support, NSAIDs.  </li></br><li>NAAT of urine/urethra ± rectum/pharynx for definitive diagnosis and partner management.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Quick Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Painful epiDID-Y-mitis in a young guy? He CHLAMs up the urethra.”<br /></br>OR<br /></br>“&lt;35 = STD; ≥35 = pee (E. coli).”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Candida albicans<br /></br>• Causes balanitis or fungal orchitis mainly in diabetics or immunocompromised; UA typically shows budding yeast/pseudohyphae on KOH, not sterile pyuria.  </p></br><p>c. Escherichia coli<br /></br>• Leading cause of epididymitis in older men or those with urinary tract obstruction/instrumentation. Would often show many bacteria on UA and a positive urine culture.  </p></br><p>d. Trichomonas vaginalis<br /></br>• Protozoan causing urethritis with frothy discharge; may occasionally co-infect but far less common as sole cause of epididymitis in men. Motile trichomonads can be seen on wet mount. UA would not be “no organisms.”  </p></br><p>e. Ureaplasma urealyticum<br /></br>• Can cause non-gonococcal urethritis but is a rare cause of epididymitis. Presents similarly to Chlamydia but far less prevalent; exam writers favor C. trachomatis for classic presentation.  </p></br><p>Remember: sterile pyuria + young age + epididymal pain → think Chlamydia trachomatis.</p>
150. A 47-year-old man has had marked shortness of breath during ambulation and at rest for 24 hours. He has no chest pain. He completed a required course of chemotherapy including doxorubicin for non-Hodgkin lymphoma 4 months ago. His resting pulse has increased from 80/min to 110/min during this period. Echocardiography shows a left ventricular ejection fraction of 40 percent. An ECG shows sinus tachycardia but is otherwise normal. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Congestive heart failure</br>b. Endocarditis</br>c. Pericarditis</br>d. Pneumonia</br>e. Pulmonary embolus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Congestive heart failure (anthracycline-induced dilated cardiomyopathy)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis – How the clues point to CHF<br /></br>1. Chemotherapy with doxorubicin 4 months ago<br /></br>   • High-yield red flag: Anthracyclines (doxorubicin, daunorubicin) are classic for dose-dependent, irreversible cardiomyopathy.<br /></br>2. Progressive shortness of breath with minimal exertion and now at rest<br /></br>   • Cardinal symptom of left-sided heart failure.<br /></br>3. Resting pulse 80 → 110 bpm over same time period<br /></br>   • Reflex sympathetic tachycardia as cardiac output falls.<br /></br>4. Echocardiogram: LVEF 40 % (↓)<br /></br>   • Objective evidence of systolic dysfunction.<br /></br>5. ECG: Sinus tachycardia, otherwise normal<br /></br>   • Rules out acute ischemia or pericarditis changes, supports global rather than focal process.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>• When a post-chemo patient presents with new dyspnea, always ask “Which agents are cardiotoxic?” Anthracyclines, trastuzumab, cyclophosphamide, and radiation top the list. An echo with reduced EF = doxorubicin cardiomyopathy until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Reasoning &amp; Take-Home Message<br /></br>Anthracycline therapy → free radical generation + iron-dependent DNA damage → myocyte necrosis → dilated cardiomyopathy → systolic CHF (↓EF, tachycardia, dyspnea). The vignette gives every step of that chain, so CHF is the only answer fully explained by the data.</p></br><p>In a real encounter you would document:</br>• Onset, duration, precipitating factors of dyspnea<br /></br>• Chemo regimen &amp; cumulative doxorubicin dose<br /></br>• Vital signs, volume status, heart sounds, lung exam<br /></br>• Echo parameters (EF, LV dimensions)<br /></br>• Plan: Admit for acute decompensated HF, start IV diuretics, afterload reduction, consider beta-blocker once euvolemic, ACEi/ARNI long-term, counsel on dexrazoxane use if further anthracycline planned.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Anthracyclines intercalate DNA AND generate ROS.<br /></br>• Myocardial cells have low antioxidant defenses → lipid peroxidation, mitochondrial damage → cell death.<br /></br>• Results in a dilated, flabby LV with systolic failure.<br /></br>• Risk correlates with cumulative dose (&gt;300 mg/m²).<br /></br>• Dexrazoxane (iron chelator) scavenges free radicals; given prophylactically in high-dose regimens.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management Snapshot (high-yield)<br /></br>Acute decompensation:<br /></br>1. Loop diuretic (furosemide) IV<br /></br>2. Nitrates or nitroprusside if hypertensive congestion<br /></br>3. Oxygen, maybe BiPAP<br /></br>Chronic therapy after stabilization:<br /></br>• ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) or ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan)<br /></br>• Beta blocker (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate)<br /></br>• Mineralocorticoid antagonist (spironolactone)<br /></br>• ± ICD if EF ≤35 % after 3 mo optimized therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“DoxoRUINs the heart” – RUIN =<br /></br>R = Reactive Oxygen species<br /></br>U = ↑ Up (tachy) heartbeat (compensation)<br /></br>I = Irreversible cardiomyopathy<br /></br>N = Needs DexrazoXANE for prophylaNE</p></br><p>Visual: Picture a red (anthraCYCLINE) bicycle pump over-inflating a heart balloon until it becomes floppy and can’t pump.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>b. Endocarditis<br /></br>   • Would present with fever, murmur, positive blood cultures, possibly embolic phenomena—not isolated ↓EF.<br /></br>c. Pericarditis<br /></br>   • Classically pleuritic chest pain relieved by leaning forward, diffuse ST-segment elevations on ECG, friction rub; echo would show normal EF ± pericardial effusion.<br /></br>d. Pneumonia<br /></br>   • Dyspnea plus fever, cough, focal crackles/infiltrate on CXR; EF unaffected.<br /></br>e. Pulmonary embolus<br /></br>   • Sudden dyspnea, pleuritic pain, possible hemoptysis; echo may show RV strain, but LV EF usually normal. No chemo agent link.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Exam Pearls<br /></br>• Any new systolic HF in a patient months to years after anthracycline = dilated cardiomyopathy until proven otherwise.<br /></br>• Always correlate chemo history with organ toxicities (vincristine → neuropathy, cisplatin → nephro/oto-toxicity, bleomycin → pulmonary fibrosis, etc.).</p>
151. Patient Information  </br>Age: 68 years  </br>Gender: M, self-identified  </br>Ethnicity: African American, self-identified  </br>Site of Care: office  </br></br>History  </br>Reason for Visit/Chief Concern: routine follow-up visit for hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus  </br></br>History of Present Illness:  </br>• patient has felt well  </br>• 15-year history of hypertension  </br>• 15-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus  </br>Past Medical History:  </br>• otherwise unremarkable  </br>Medications:  </br>• valsartan  </br>• metformin  </br>• atorvastatin  </br>Vaccinations:  </br>• up-to-date except for pneumococcal, which he has never received  </br>Allergies:  </br>• no known drug allergies  </br>Psychosocial History:  </br>• does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcoholic beverages, or use illicit drugs  </br></br>Physical Examination  </br>Temp             Pulse    Resp     BP             O₂ Sat      Ht                   Wt                 BMI  </br>37.0°C (98.6°F)  82/min   14/min   136/82 mm Hg   98 percent on RA    178 cm (5 ft 10 in)  69 kg (153 lb)     22 kg/m²  </br>• Pulmonary: clear to auscultation  </br>• Cardiac: normal S₁ and S₂; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops  </br></br>Question: Which of the following is the most appropriate management regarding pneumococcal 23-valent polysaccharide vaccine (PPSV23) and pneumococcal 13-valent conjugate vaccine (PCV13) administration?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. PCV13 vaccination today, followed by PPSV23 vaccination in 1 year  </br>b. PCV13 vaccination today, with no further pneumococcal vaccination indicated  </br>c. PPSV23 vaccination today, followed by PCV13 vaccination in 1 year  </br>d. PPSV23 vaccination today, with no further pneumococcal vaccination indicated  </br>e. No pneumococcal vaccination is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. PCV13 vaccination today, followed by PPSV23 vaccination in 1 year  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Demographics: 68-year-old, community-dwelling adult; no immunocompromising condition.  </li></br><li>Comorbidities: Type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension – both are “chronic medical conditions,” but NOT conditions that alter T-cell function (so he is not considered immunocompromised for vaccine purposes).  </li></br><li>Vaccination status: “Never received” any pneumococcal vaccine → we are starting from zero.  </li></br><li>Examination/Labs: Normal, confirming he is clinically stable; visit is preventive.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>Ask two questions with any pneumococcal‐vaccine stem.<br /></br>A. Age &lt;65 or ≥65?<br /></br>B. Any high-risk / immunocompromising conditions (CSF leak, cochlear implant, asplenia, HIV, malignancy, transplant, immunosuppressive drugs)?<br /></br> • If ≥65 and NO high-risk condition → give BOTH vaccines, PCV13 1st, PPSV23 ≥1 yr later.<br /></br> • If 19–64 WITH chronic medical condition only (diabetes, heart, lung, liver, smoker) → single dose PPSV23 now, then at 65 repeat PPSV23 (≥5 yr since last).<br /></br> • If high-risk / immunocompromised (any age ≥19) → PCV13 then PPSV23 8 wks later, then PPSV23 booster 5 yrs later and again at ≥65.  </p></br><p>Our patient is in the first bolded category.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. He is already ≥65.<br /></br>2. No immunocompromising illness.<br /></br>3. Has never received either pneumococcal vaccine.<br /></br>4. ACIP/CDC schedule (still tested on NBME/Step 2) →<br /></br>   • Give a single dose of PCV13 first (conjugate vaccine induces T-cell–dependent memory).<br /></br>   • Follow with PPSV23 (polysaccharide) ≥12 months later to broaden serotype coverage.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: For ALL immunocompetent adults ≥65 with no prior pneumococcal vaccination, sequence is PCV13 → PPSV23 1 year later.  </p></br><p>Documentation if this were a real encounter:<br /></br>• “Administered PCV13 0.5 mL IM L-deltoid; patient tolerated well. Plan: PPSV23 at next annual visit (12 months).”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)  </p></br><p>• PCV13 (conjugate): Polysaccharide antigen covalently linked to diphtheria protein → engages T helper cells → high-affinity IgG, mucosal immunity, immunologic memory.<br /></br>• PPSV23 (polysaccharide only): T-cell–independent IgM response; broader serotype coverage (23), but no memory.<br /></br>• Giving PCV13 first “primes” the immune system so the subsequent PPSV23 produces a stronger, broader response.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Algorithms  </p></br><p>Not applicable labs or imaging. Vaccine schedule is the intervention.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“65 = CONjugate first, then POLy later.”<br /></br>CON (13 letters) comes before POL (23 letters) alphabetically and chronologically (≥12 months).  </p></br><p>OR visualize a 65-year-old holding a 13-balloon (PCV13) today and receiving a bouquet of 23 balloons (PPSV23) on next year’s birthday.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. PCV13 today, no further vaccination<br /></br> • Misses the additional 10 serotypes covered only by PPSV23. ACIP guideline still mandates PPSV23 for complete protection.  </p></br><p>c. PPSV23 today, followed by PCV13 in 1 year<br /></br> • Sequence reversed. If PPSV23 is given first, immune response to later PCV13 is blunted (“hyporesponsiveness”). Correct order is conjugate then polysaccharide.  </p></br><p>d. PPSV23 today, no further vaccination<br /></br> • Fails to provide T-cell–dependent immunity and misses the incremental benefit of PCV13.  </p></br><p>e. No pneumococcal vaccination indicated<br /></br> • All adults ≥65 need pneumococcal vaccination, regardless of comorbidities.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Memorizing that “diabetics get PPSV23” and forgetting the separate ≥65 algorithm. Always check AGE first!  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>For immunocompetent adults aged ≥65 who have never received pneumococcal vaccine:<br /></br>1. Administer a single dose of PCV13 now.<br /></br>2. Administer PPSV23 at least 1 year later.</p>
152. A 2-year-old girl is brought to the physician for an examination prior to entering day care.  </br>The patient has a history of stridor and expiratory wheezing after drinking cow milk.  </br>Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Administer prednisone therapy every other day prior to entering day care  </br>b. Avoid day-care placement  </br>c. Introduce gradually increasing amounts of cow’s milk to the patient’s diet prior to entering day care  </br>d. Provide the day-care center with autoinjectable epinephrine  </br>e. Provide the day-care center with over-the-counter antihistamines</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Provide the day-care center with autoinjectable epinephrine</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 2-year-old (non-verbal, cannot reliably report symptoms).<br /></br>• “Stridor and expiratory wheezing” occur after ingesting cow’s milk → upper-airway edema + bronchospasm = systemic, potentially life-threatening IgE-mediated food allergy (anaphylaxis).<br /></br>• Physical exam today is normal → patient is currently asymptomatic; the question is about prevention/safety once she is in an environment that you (the clinician) cannot directly control: day care.</p></br><p>High-yield pattern<br /></br>Food allergy with respiratory involvement → always think epinephrine as first-line, life-saving therapy for future accidental ingestions. Caregivers must have immediate access and know how/when to use it.</p></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>1. Does the history meet criteria for anaphylaxis? Any respiratory symptoms or hypotension after exposure = anaphylaxis. ✔<br /></br>2. What is the standard of care for patients at risk for future anaphylaxis? Strict avoidance + carry epinephrine autoinjector everywhere + caregiver education. ✔<br /></br>3. What do they want you to do specifically for a day-care setting? Make sure the facility has an autoinjector and training. ✔  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Stepwise thought process<br /></br>1. Identify the diagnosis: IgE-mediated cow’s-milk allergy with prior anaphylaxis.<br /></br>2. Determine potential future risk: high likelihood of inadvertent exposure in day care.<br /></br>3. Apply guideline-directed management: prescribe epinephrine autoinjector(s) and educate all caregivers, including day-care staff, in recognition and treatment of anaphylaxis.<br /></br>Core takeaway<br /></br>Any patient—especially a young child—with a history of anaphylaxis must have readily available epinephrine at all times; this necessity extends to all alternate caregivers and locations (school, day care, relatives’ homes).</p></br><p>Documentation point (real life)<br /></br>“Diagnosis: IgE-mediated cow’s-milk allergy with prior anaphylaxis. Epinephrine autoinjector (0.15 mg) prescribed; day-care staff instructed on storage and intramuscular administration to mid-lateral thigh; emergency plan provided.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>IgE antibodies specific to cow’s-milk proteins cross-link on mast cells → rapid degranulation → histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes → vascular leak (angioedema), smooth-muscle contraction (bronchospasm → wheeze, stridor), possible hypotension. Epinephrine (α1, β1, β2 agonist) reverses all major pathophysiologic mechanisms: vasoconstriction, bronchodilation, inhibition of further mediator release.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management Algorithm (Food-induced Anaphylaxis)<br /></br>1. Strict allergen avoidance; read labels.<br /></br>2. Epinephrine autoinjector (0.15 mg if &lt;25 kg; 0.3 mg if ≥25 kg). Keep two doses available.<br /></br>3. Train parents, teachers, day-care workers on early signs (hives, cough, voice change, wheeze) and immediate intramuscular injection.<br /></br>4. Call EMS; transport for observation (biphasic reactions).<br /></br>5. Adjuncts (after epinephrine): H1 blocker (diphenhydramine), H2 blocker, steroids, inhaled β-agonist if needed.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“EPI-Pen for EPI-dermis &amp; EPI-glottis”<br /></br>If a food causes Skin (urticaria) + Airway (stridor/wheeze) or Gut rapidly → carry an EPI-Pen.  </p></br><p>“Rule of 3 A’s for food allergy”<br /></br>Avoid allergen, have Autoinjector, and Alert caregivers.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Prednisone every other day<br /></br>• Chronic oral steroids do not prevent acute anaphylaxis and carry significant side-effects. Steroids are only adjunctive after epinephrine to reduce late-phase reactions.</p></br><p>B. Avoid day-care placement<br /></br>• Overly restrictive; the standard of care is safe inclusion with an emergency action plan, not isolation.</p></br><p>C. Gradually introduce cow’s milk (“oral desensitization”)<br /></br>• Not first-line outside specialized allergy centers; unsupervised exposure in a 2-year-old with prior anaphylaxis is dangerous.</p></br><p>E. Provide over-the-counter antihistamines<br /></br>• H1 blockers treat cutaneous symptoms but do not relieve airway obstruction or hypotension. Relying on antihistamines delays epinephrine and increases mortality.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A child with a history of anaphylaxis to cow’s milk must have epinephrine immediately available in all settings—including day care—because it is the only agent that rapidly reverses life-threatening airway and cardiovascular compromise.</p>
153. A 72-year-old man is admitted to the hospital 40 minutes after the onset of presyncope, fatigue, and malaise. He has myelodysplastic syndrome, IgA deficiency, and hypertension. His medications are lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide. He appears tired but is not in acute distress. His temperature is 37.7°C (99.9°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. His hemoglobin concentration is 6 g/dL. Transfusion of packed red blood cells is begun. Five minutes later, he has moderate abdominal pain, nausea, and nonproductive cough. His temperature is 38.2°C (100.7°F), pulse is 124/min and regular, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 80/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 94 percent. Examination shows no jugular venous distention. Diffuse wheezes are heard bilaterally. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. Urinary catheterization yields clear urine. The transfusion is discontinued. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate step in pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Diphenhydramine</br>b. Epinephrine</br>c. Folic acid</br>d. Furosemide</br>e. Immune globulin</br>f. Levofloxacin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Epinephrine (0.3–0.5 mg IM of 1:1,000 solution)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Timing: Symptoms begin “5 minutes” after the transfusion starts ⟹ points to an acute immunologic reaction rather than delayed hemolysis or infection.<br /></br>• Patient risk factor: “IgA deficiency” is the classic predisposition to an anaphylactic transfusion reaction because patients can form anti-IgA IgE antibodies.<br /></br>• Clinical picture: Sudden abdominal pain, nausea, cough, diffuse wheezes, tachycardia (124/min), hypotension (80/50 mm Hg), and low-grade fever. This is the typical triad of anaphylaxis during transfusion—cutaneous/respiratory/GI + hypotension.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No jugular venous distention, clear lungs previously, clear urine (rules out TACO, TRALI, or acute hemolytic reaction). Oxygen saturation 94 % rules out severe hypoxemia from TRALI.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: During any transfusion, ask “How long after the transfusion did the problem start, and what organ systems are involved?” Minutes + hypotension/wheeze in an IgA-deficient patient = anaphylaxis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process:</br>1. Stop the transfusion (already done).<br /></br>2. Recognize anaphylaxis: rapidly progressive, multisystem, IgE-mediated reaction.<br /></br>3. First-line drug = intramuscular epinephrine, which:</br>   • α1-agonism ⟹ vasoconstriction ⟹ ↑BP, ↓edema<br /></br>   • β1/β2-agonism ⟹ bronchodilation, ↑cardiac output, ↓mediator release<br /></br>4. Supportive care: airway, high-flow O2, IV fluids, adjuncts (H1/H2 blockers, corticosteroids) only after epi.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In any transfusion recipient—especially with IgA deficiency—sudden hypotension + respiratory/GI symptoms mandates immediate IM epinephrine.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls: Time transfusion stopped, vitals, drug/dose/time of epinephrine, airway status, discussion with blood bank.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Selective IgA deficiency (≤7 mg/dL serum IgA) → patients develop anti-IgA IgE antibodies.<br /></br>• Transfused blood products contain donor IgA → IgE-mediated mast-cell degranulation → histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes → vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, increased capillary permeability, GI smooth-muscle contraction.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls</br>Immediate:</br>1. IM epinephrine 0.3–0.5 mg (children 0.01 mg/kg).<br /></br>2. Large-bore IV access, 1–2 L isotonic crystalloid if hypotensive persists.<br /></br>3. Airway management, 100 % O2.<br /></br>Adjuncts (after epi):</br>• Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, ranitidine/famotidine, methylprednisolone.<br /></br>Future transfusions:</br>• Wash RBCs or use IgA-deficient donor blood; document the reaction in the transfusion record.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“ABCDE for transfusion reactions”<br /></br>A = Acute hemolysis (flank pain, red urine)<br /></br>B = Bacterial contamination (high fever, shock)<br /></br>C = Circulatory overload (TACO: HTN, JVD)<br /></br>D = Delayed hemolysis (days later ↑bilirubin)<br /></br>E = Epinephrine for anaphylaxis (minutes, IgA-deficient, hypotension + wheeze + GI)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Diphenhydramine – Helpful for mild urticarial transfusion reactions (itching, flushing, no hypotension/resp distress). Insufficient as monotherapy for full anaphylaxis with hypotension.<br /></br>C. Folic acid – Used for chronic hemolytic anemias to support erythropoiesis; irrelevant acutely.<br /></br>D. Furosemide – Treats transfusion-associated circulatory overload (dyspnea, HTN, JVD, S3). This patient is hypotensive without JVD.<br /></br>E. Immune globulin – IVIG can treat autoimmune cytopenias or prophylaxis for infections, but it neither reverses anaphylaxis nor acts quickly enough.<br /></br>F. Levofloxacin – Would be appropriate for transfusion-related sepsis (rigors, very high fever, hypotension) caused by gram-negative contamination; onset is typically &gt;30 min to several hours and patient usually has rigors and dramatic fever spikes (&gt;39 °C).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Take-Home Message</br>In any transfusion, minutes-onset hypotension plus respiratory or GI symptoms—especially in an IgA-deficient patient—equals anaphylaxis. Stop the transfusion and give IM epinephrine immediately.</p>
154. A 22-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the office in February for a follow-up examination 6 weeks after spontaneous vaginal delivery of a healthy male newborn at term. Pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated. She says she has been tearful during the past 3 weeks and has not left the house since visiting relatives 2 weeks ago. She says she loves her son and husband, but she has not enjoyed spending time with them during the past 2 weeks. She wakes up every 3 hours at night to breast-feed her infant and is unable to sleep well even when her husband offers to care for their son. She spends most of the day in bed with her infant in a crib nearby; she gets up only to feed and change him. She has no history of serious illness, takes no medications, and has no known allergies. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she has a depressed mood and flat affect. She does not have suicidal or homicidal ideation. She has not had audio or visual hallucinations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Bipolar disorder</br>b. Major depressive disorder</br>c. Postpartum psychosis</br>d. Seasonal affective disorder</br>e. Normal postpartum course</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Major depressive disorder (post-partum onset, i.e., post-partum depression)</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Time course<br /></br>   • Symptoms began ≈3 weeks ago and are still present at 6 weeks post-delivery → duration &gt; 2 weeks, well past the “post-partum blues” window (&lt; 14 days).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Key depressive features (SIGECAPS)<br /></br>   • S = Sleep disturbance: awakens every 3 h to feed but still “unable to sleep well even when husband offers to help.”<br /></br>   • I = Interest: “has not enjoyed spending time” with baby or husband (anhedonia).<br /></br>   • G/E = Guilt / low Energy are implied by tearfulness, staying in bed most of the day.<br /></br>   • C = Concentration impairment is often present when patient withdraws socially.<br /></br>   • A = Appetite change not specified, but not necessary for diagnosis.<br /></br>   • P = Psychomotor changes: remains in bed most of day.<br /></br>   • S = Suicidal ideation: absent, but not required for MDD.<br /></br>   At least 5 criteria (including depressed mood or anhedonia) present for ≥2 weeks = major depressive episode.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Lack of psychosis<br /></br>   • No hallucinations, delusions, or thoughts of harming baby/herself → rules out post-partum psychosis.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Functional impairment<br /></br>   • Leaves bed only to feed/change infant, no social interaction → meets DSM-5 impairment requirement.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>High-yield pattern: Onset within 4-6 weeks of delivery + ≥2-week major depressive episode → post-partum depression (DSM-5: “major depressive disorder, with peripartum onset”).</p></br><p>Metacognitive takeaway:<br /></br>   • Always place post-partum mood disorders on a timeline:<br /></br>     &lt; 2 wk = post-partum blues<br /></br>     &gt; 2 wk &amp; ≤1 yr = post-partum depression<br /></br>     Any time + psychosis = post-partum psychosis</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Rule out emergency (psychosis/harm to self or infant) → absent  </li></br><li>Screen for major depression criteria → fulfilled  </li></br><li>Confirm duration &gt; 2 wk → yes  </li></br><li>Assign diagnosis: Major depressive disorder, peripartum onset  </li></br></ol></br><p>Core concept: Post-partum depression is simply MDD occurring within 12 mo of delivery; management mirrors MDD but must consider breastfeeding (sertraline/paroxetine are first-line SSRIs; psychotherapy is always indicated).</p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real pt: Columbia or PHQ-9 score, absence of SI/HI, functional impairment, offer of therapy/SSRI, breastfeeding counseling, safety plan &amp; follow-up.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Multifactorial: rapid drop in estrogen/progesterone, sleep deprivation, psychosocial stressors, prior mood disorders.<br /></br>• Untreated PPD increases risk of impaired bonding, infant developmental issues, maternal suicide.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot  </p></br><p>First-line<br /></br>   1. Cognitive-behavioral therapy or interpersonal therapy<br /></br>   2. SSRI (sertraline or paroxetine preferred in lactation)<br /></br>Screen: Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale at post-partum visits.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid  </p></br><p>“Blue ≤2, Mood &gt;2”<br /></br>   • Blues last ≤2 weeks<br /></br>   • Depressed mood &gt;2 weeks → think MDD (post-partum depression)</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Bipolar disorder<br /></br>   • Requires at least one manic/hypomanic episode (elevated mood, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity). None described.</p></br><p>C. Post-partum psychosis<br /></br>   • Presents within days–weeks of delivery with delusions, hallucinations, disorganized behavior, often thoughts of harming baby/self → absent here.</p></br><p>D. Seasonal affective disorder<br /></br>   • Characterized by recurrent winter depressive episodes over ≥2 consecutive years, remitting in spring; here onset is clearly linked to childbirth, not seasonal pattern.</p></br><p>E. Normal post-partum course (“post-partum blues”)<br /></br>   • Emotional lability/tearfulness limited to &lt;14 days post-delivery and mild functional impairment; this patient’s symptoms persist 6 weeks with marked impairment → exceeds blues threshold.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>A depressive episode lasting &gt;2 weeks within the first year after childbirth, without psychotic features, is major depressive disorder with peripartum onset (post-partum depression); recognize it early to initiate therapy/SSRI and safeguard both mother and infant.</p>
155. A 16-year-old girl is brought to the office by her mother as a new patient because of weight loss. The mother states that 9 months ago, her daughter became preoccupied with her weight; she then began restricting what she ate and exercising vigorously for several hours daily. The mother adds that her daughter's performance at school has deteriorated and that she has withdrawn from her friends. According to the mother, the patient had been an excellent student and an obedient daughter, and she has had no behavioral problems until now. The patient's height is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in); her weight has decreased from 52 kg (114 lb) to 38 kg (84 lb), and her menses have ceased. Current BMI is 15 kg/m2. The patient is soft-spoken and emotionally bland. She expresses little concern about her severe weight loss and malnourished status. She states that she continues to be dissatisfied with her weight and that she would like to lose additional pounds. Temperature is 36.1°C (97.0°F), pulse is 46/min, and blood pressure is 10/60 mm Hg. Physical examination shows lanugo over the face and body. ECG is ordered. Results of serum laboratory studies are shown:</br>ALT 110 U/L</br>AST 121 U/L</br>Na+ 130 mEq/L</br>K+ 3 mEq/L</br>Cl- 93 mEq/L</br>HCO3- 34 mEq/L</br>Protein</br>Albumin 2.4 g/dL</br></br>This patient and her family should be counseled that she is at increased risk for developing which of the following?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Atrophic gastritis</br>b. Osteoporosis</br>c. Periodontal disease</br>d. Steatohepatitis</br>e. Thrombocytopenia</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Osteoporosis  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “16-year-old girl,” “preoccupied with her weight,” “restricting what she ate,” “BMI 15 kg/m²,” “amenorrhea,” “bradycardia (46/min),” “hypotension,” “lanugo,” “expresses little concern.”<br /></br>• Pattern recognition: classic picture of restricting-type anorexia nervosa (AN).<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: prolonged caloric restriction, secondary amenorrhea ≥ 3 months, low BMI, physiologic bradycardia, elevated transaminases from starvation, metabolic alkalosis &amp; hypokalemia (vomiting/laxatives), hyponatremia, hypoalbuminemia.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no binge-purge episodes described (makes bulimia unlikely).  </p></br><p>Cognitive roadmap:<br /></br>1. Identify DSM-5 criteria for AN.<br /></br>2. Recall systemic complications (cardiac, endocrine, GI, skeletal, hematologic).<br /></br>3. Choose the complication that is both common and long-term: osteoporosis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Why osteoporosis?<br /></br>• AN suppresses hypothalamic GnRH → ↓ LH/FSH → ↓ estrogen.<br /></br>• Estrogen is the principal anti-resorptive hormone in adolescents; loss of estrogen halts accrual of peak bone mass and accelerates bone resorption.<br /></br>• Malnutrition → ↓ calcium, vitamin D, IGF-1 → further impairs osteoblast activity.<br /></br>• Result: low bone mineral density (BMD); fragility fractures can occur even in teenagers.<br /></br>Take-home message: Any adolescent/young woman with ≥ 6 months of amenorrhea from AN is automatically high-risk for osteoporosis and should receive counseling, baseline DEXA once weight is restored, calcium/vit D supplementation, and weight-bearing exercise after medical stabilization.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record amenorrhea duration, vital-sign instability, degree of malnutrition (BMI), and counsel patient/parents on bone-health risks and need for BMD assessment.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>1. Energy deficit → hypothalamic suppression → functional hypothalamic amenorrhea.<br /></br>2. Low estrogen &amp; malnutrition → ↓ bone formation + ↑ bone resorption → osteoporosis.<br /></br>3. Additional endocrine changes: ↑ cortisol, ↓ thyroid hormones (low-T3 syndrome), ↓ insulin &amp; IGF-1.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>• Medical stabilization (correct electrolytes, monitor for refeeding syndrome).<br /></br>• Nutritional rehabilitation &amp; psychotherapy (family-based therapy for adolescents).<br /></br>• Bone health:<br /></br>  – DEXA scan if disease ≥ 6–12 mo or history of fracture.<br /></br>  – Calcium 1200–1500 mg/day + vitamin D 800–1000 IU/day.<br /></br>  – Weight restoration is the single most effective “treatment” for low BMD; pharmacologic therapy (e.g., bisphosphonates) reserved for severe, refractory cases because of teratogenicity concerns.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tip<br /></br>“AN = A Null estrogen → Ailing N-bones.”<br /></br>Visualize bones becoming hollow when estrogen “dries up.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Atrophic gastritis – Seen with autoimmune pernicious anemia or chronic H. pylori infection, not a recognized sequela of AN.<br /></br>c. Periodontal disease – Dental enamel erosion and parotid enlargement occur in bulimia (due to gastric acid exposure) rather than restrictive AN; periodontal disease is linked to poor oral hygiene and diabetes.<br /></br>d. Steatohepatitis – Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis is associated with obesity/metabolic syndrome, the opposite metabolic milieu. Mild transaminase elevation in AN is from starvation hepatocyte autophagy, not fatty infiltration.<br /></br>e. Thrombocytopenia – AN commonly causes mild leukopenia and normocytic anemia; isolated thrombocytopenia is uncommon and usually mild if present, whereas osteoporosis is nearly universal with prolonged disease.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Concept<br /></br>Chronic caloric restriction + amenorrhea in adolescents → functional hypothalamic hypogonadism → profound loss of bone mineral density. Early recognition and counseling are essential to prevent irreversible osteoporosis and fractures.</p>
156. A 9-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of behavioral problems at school during the past 6 months. Her teachers report that she refuses to speak or answer questions in front of the class yet answers questions when the teacher approaches her one-on-one. Her mother reports that her daughter will not answer the telephone at home and will not tell waiters what she wants in restaurants. On questioning, the patient states that she worries throughout the school day that others think she is "stupid." Her mother reports that her daughter interacts normally with her family. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. The patient is quiet and avoids direct eye contact. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Acute stress disorder</br>b. Generalized anxiety disorder</br>c. Oppositional defiant disorder</br>d. Social phobia</br>e. Specific phobia</br>f. Normal behavior</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Social phobia (now called Social Anxiety Disorder)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Recurrent, marked fear of situations in which she might be judged (speaking in class, ordering in restaurants, answering the phone).<br /></br>• Fear leads to avoidance (refuses to speak) or marked distress when endured.<br /></br>• Intact functioning in familiar settings (speaks normally at home with family) → points away from a language disorder or global mutism.<br /></br>• Duration ≥ 6 months (‘‘past 6 months’’).<br /></br>Key “buzz” phrase: worries others will think she is “stupid’’ = fear of negative evaluation, the diagnostic hallmark of social anxiety disorder.</p></br><p>Test‐taking tip: When a child shows situational silence + fear of embarrassment but normal speech at home, think social anxiety disorder (or “selective mutism’’ if the silence itself is the chief symptom; not listed here, so choose social phobia).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify anxiety trigger: performance / social interaction.<br /></br>2. Rule out trauma, defiance, global anxiety, or isolated object phobia.<br /></br>3.
