Confirm chronicity &amp; functional impairment.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Social anxiety disorder = fear of social or performance situations where scrutiny is possible, leading to avoidance and impairment.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: record specific feared situations, duration, functional impact, and lack of medical/neurologic speech disorder.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Combination of genetic predisposition, temperament (behavioral inhibition), and environmental factors.<br /></br>• Hyperactivation of amygdala &amp; insula → exaggerated fear response to social cues.<br /></br>• Onset often in late childhood/early adolescence; if untreated, risk of depression, substance use.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• Diagnosis is clinical; no labs/imaging.<br /></br>• First-line treatment:<br /></br>  – Cognitive-behavioral therapy (exposure + cognitive restructuring).<br /></br>  – SSRIs (eg, sertraline, fluoxetine) or SNRIs if CBT insufficient or unavailable.<br /></br>• For performance-only subtype (eg, public speaking), propranolol or low-dose benzodiazepine PRN.<br /></br>• Monitor for comorbid disorders (depression, other anxieties).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“SOCIAL”:<br /></br>S – Speaks softly or not at all in public<br /></br>O – Others’ judgment feared<br /></br>C – Chronic ≥6 mo<br /></br>I – Impaired social/academic functioning<br /></br>A – Avoids triggering situations<br /></br>L – Loves normal interaction at home</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Acute stress disorder – Requires exposure to a traumatic event within the last month, intrusive symptoms, and hyperarousal. None present.<br /></br>b. Generalized anxiety disorder – Excessive worry about multiple domains (school, health, family) most days ≥6 mo plus physical symptoms; here anxiety is limited to social evaluation.<br /></br>c. Oppositional defiant disorder – Pattern of angry/irritable mood and defiant behavior toward authority, not anxiety or avoidance.<br /></br>e. Specific phobia – Fear is restricted to a specific object/situation (heights, dogs). This patient fears a broad range of social situations.<br /></br>f. Normal behavior – The degree of avoidance and impairment for ≥6 months exceeds normal shyness.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A child who avoids speaking or participating in public settings because she fears negative judgment, yet functions normally with family, meets criteria for social anxiety disorder (social phobia).</p>
157. A 47-year-old man is disoriented and having auditory hallucinations 2 days after open reduction and internal fixation of a femoral fracture sustained in a motor vehicle collision. He received perioperative cefazolin, and today his only medication is morphine for pain control. On admission, his blood alcohol concentration was 120 mg/dL. He has a 25-year history of alcoholism. He has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 30 years. His temperature is 38 °C (100.4 °F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 26/min, and blood pressure is 175/95 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 94 percent. Cardiac and abdominal examinations show no abnormalities. Examination of the incision site shows no evidence of infection. On mental status examination, he says that he hears voices. Laboratory studies show a hematocrit of 44 percent and leukocyte count of 11,000/mm3. An x-ray of the chest shows no abnormalities. Further laboratory studies are most likely to show which of the following electrolyte abnormalities?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Hyperglycemia</br>b. Hyperkalemia</br>c. Hypocalcemia</br>d. Hypomagnesemia</br>e. Hyponatremia</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Hypomagnesemia   </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Timeline &amp; Symptoms<br /></br>   • Post-operative day #2 after femur ORIF<br /></br>   • Acute confusion, disorientation, vivid auditory hallucinations<br /></br>   • Autonomic hyperactivity: T 100.4 °F, HR 120, RR 26, BP 175/95  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Past History<br /></br>   • 25-year heavy alcohol use; admission BAL 120 mg/dL (≈0.12%)  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent Negatives<br /></br>   • No wound infection (rules out sepsis)<br /></br>   • CXR normal; SpO₂ 94 % (rules out hypoxia, fat embolism)<br /></br>   • Only on morphine; dose-dependent morphine delirium is much less likely this early and would not explain autonomic findings.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern Recognition → Classic alcohol-withdrawal delirium tremens (DTs): 48–96 h after last drink, presents with agitation, hallucinations, and marked sympathetic surge.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>   a. Ask: “Does this look like withdrawal, infection, metabolic, or medication side effect?”<br /></br>   b. Look for timing (post-op day 2), autonomic signs, and long alcohol history → withdrawal.<br /></br>   c. Recall high-yield electrolyte derangements in chronic alcoholics/DTs: hypomagnesemia ± hypokalemia ± hypophosphatemia.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Why Mg²⁺?<br /></br>  – Ethanol causes renal wasting of Mg²⁺ and decreases intestinal absorption.<br /></br>  – Poor nutritional intake worsens depletion.<br /></br>  – Surgical stress/volume resuscitation → further urinary losses.<br /></br>  – Low Mg²⁺ increases CNS and cardiac excitability → tremor, seizures, hallucinations, ventricular arrhythmias.<br /></br>  – Correcting Mg²⁺ is essential; refractory hypokalemia will persist until Mg²⁺ is replaced.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Always check Mg²⁺ (and K⁺, PO₄³⁻) in any alcoholic with acute mental-status changes, especially in the DT window.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): “Post-operative day #2 probable DTs. Plan: ICU, IV benzodiazepines, thiamine, glucose, replace Mg²⁺/K⁺/PO₄³⁻, telemetry.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Chronic ethanol →<br /></br>  1. ↓ Dietary intake &amp; GI malabsorption of Mg²⁺<br /></br>  2. Ethanol-induced diuresis → ↑ renal Mg²⁺ loss<br /></br>  3. Stress hormones (catecholamines, aldosterone) post-op → further Mg²⁺ wasting  </p></br><p>• Mg²⁺ deficiency →<br /></br>  – Disinhibition of NMDA receptors &amp; ↓ GABA activity → neuro-excitability (tremor, seizures, hallucinations)<br /></br>  – Parathyroid resistance → secondary hypocalcemia (may coexist)<br /></br>  – Potassium channel dysfunction → hypokalemia</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Immediate management of DTs:<br /></br>   • IV benzodiazepines (e.g., lorazepam), ICU monitoring<br /></br>   • Thiamine 100 mg IV BEFORE glucose<br /></br>   • Banana bag: MgSO₄ 2 g IV, plus KCl, PO₄ repletion as indicated  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Labs to order:<br /></br>   • Serum Mg²⁺, K⁺, PO₄³⁻, Ca²⁺, Na⁺, glucose<br /></br>   • CBC, CMP, CK if agitation severe</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“DTs = Don’t forget THe ‘MaG, K, Phos’”:<br /></br>   D – Delirium tremens<br /></br>   T – Thiamine/Trauma (post-op)<br /></br>   H – Hypo-Mg²⁺ (most common)<br /></br>   MaG – reminds you Mg<br /></br>   K – hypokalemia<br /></br>   Phos – hypophosphatemia</p></br><p>Visual: Picture a shaky “Magi-cian” withdrawing a flask—he drops his MAGnesium wand, Potassium coins, and PHOSphorescent dust.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Hyperglycemia – Stress hyperglycemia is possible post-op but does not cause hallucinations + sympathetic surge. It is not specifically linked to alcoholism/withdrawal.  </p></br><p>b. Hyperkalemia – Alcohol withdrawal and catecholamine surge typically drive K⁺ into cells; Mg²⁺ deficiency generally causes hypokalemia, not hyper-.  </p></br><p>c. Hypocalcemia – May occur if massive transfusion (citrate) or pancreatitis; no evidence here. Would cause tetany, perioral numbness, Chvostek sign rather than hallucinations/DTs.  </p></br><p>e. Hyponatremia – Beer potomania or SIADH can appear in alcoholics but would require low serum osmolality and usually presents with lethargy or seizures, not the classic DT autonomic picture. CXR normal argues against pulmonary SIADH trigger.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>In a chronic alcoholic developing delirium tremens, expect and look for hypomagnesemia (often with low K⁺ and PO₄³⁻). Prompt recognition and replacement are critical to control symptoms and prevent arrhythmias.</p>
158. A 67-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of progressive loss of peripheral vision. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with metformin. He appears to be in mild discomfort. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 84/min and regular, and blood pressure is 160/98 mm Hg. Ocular movements are intact bilaterally. The pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light. Funduscopic examination shows increased optic cupping bilaterally. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Carotid duplex ultrasonography</br>b. Fluorescein angiography</br>c. Gonioscopy</br>d. MRI of the brain and neck</br>e. Tonometry</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Tonometry (measurement of intra-ocular pressure)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “67-year-old,” “2-month history of progressive loss of peripheral vision,” “increased optic cupping bilaterally,” mild ocular discomfort, normal pupils and extra-ocular movements.<br /></br>• Pattern recognition: Gradual peripheral visual field loss + optic nerve cupping = classic presentation of chronic open-angle glaucoma.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No acute eye pain, no fixed mid-dilated pupil (rules out acute angle closure); no transient monocular vision loss (rules out carotid disease/TIA); no HA, endocrine symptoms, or bitemporal hemianopsia (makes pituitary mass less likely).  </p></br><p>Approach framework for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Identify the visual complaint (peripheral vs central, acute vs chronic).<br /></br>2. Look for optic disc findings (cupping vs edema).<br /></br>3. Ask, “What single test confirms the suspected eye pathology?” → In glaucoma, check intra-ocular pressure (IOP) with tonometry.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Chronic open-angle glaucoma is usually asymptomatic until late when patients note tunnel vision. Fundus shows optic disc “cupping” from ganglion cell axon loss.<br /></br>• The diagnostic algorithm:<br /></br>  1) Suspicion based on symptoms or fundus exam →<br /></br>  2) Confirm elevated IOP with tonometry →<br /></br>  3) Full visual-field testing, optic nerve imaging, and, if needed, gonioscopy to inspect the angle.<br /></br>• Core takeaway: Anytime you see progressive peripheral vision loss + optic cupping, the next step is to measure IOP.</p></br><p>Documentation points if real patient: Document visual field deficit, cup-to-disc ratio, IOP reading, BP (risk factor), and plan for ophthalmology referral and pressure-lowering therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Open-angle glaucoma = progressive death of retinal ganglion cells from chronically elevated IOP due to impaired aqueous humor outflow through the trabecular meshwork/Schlemm canal.<br /></br>• Risk factors: Age &gt;60, African ancestry, family history, hypertension, diabetes, corticosteroid use.<br /></br>• Optic nerve cupping reflects loss of neural tissue; visual loss starts peripherally.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• First-line therapy after diagnosis: topical prostaglandin analogues (latanoprost, bimatoprost).<br /></br>• Add beta-blocker drops (timolol), alpha-2 agonist (brimonidine), carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (dorzolamide) as needed.<br /></br>• Laser trabeculoplasty or trabeculectomy for refractory cases.<br /></br>• Additional tests once pressure is documented high: visual field perimetry, optical coherence tomography (OCT) of optic nerve.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Tips<br /></br>• “CUPped coffee needs PRESSure” → Optic CUP + loss of peripheral vision → check PRESSure with tonometry.<br /></br>• Open-angle = Open “door” (trabecular meshwork) but exit still clogged → slow, painless.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Carotid duplex ultrasonography – Ordered for transient monocular blindness (amaurosis fugax) or central retinal artery occlusion suspicion; optic cupping is not a feature.<br /></br>B. Fluorescein angiography – Evaluates retinal vasculature (e.g., diabetic or hypertensive retinopathy, macular degeneration). Not needed for glaucoma diagnosis.<br /></br>C. Gonioscopy – Visualizes anterior chamber angle to distinguish open vs closed angle, but only after elevated IOP is confirmed; it is not the initial diagnostic step.<br /></br>D. MRI of the brain and neck – Used when bitemporal hemianopsia suggests optic chiasm compression (pituitary adenoma) or when optic neuritis, stroke, or tumor is suspected; again, optic cupping + peripheral loss points to glaucoma, not mass lesion.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Progressive peripheral vision loss with optic disc cupping virtually equals chronic open-angle glaucoma. The first diagnostic confirmation is measurement of intra-ocular pressure with tonometry.</p>
159. A 20-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-day history of a rapidly progressive rash over his extremities and trunk. Initially, he noticed nontender, flat, reddish brown lesions, but now the lesions have formed into blisters and he has burning pain. He has a 7-year history of epilepsy; 10 days ago, he was switched from phenobarbital to carbamazepine therapy after having breakthrough seizures. He has no other history of serious illness and no known allergies. On arrival, he is in distress. His temperature is 37.4°C (99.3°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 110/74 mm Hg. Examination shows confluent erythema over the face, trunk, and extremities. A photograph of the skin lesions is shown. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. In addition to discontinuing carbamazepine, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Antistaphylococcal antibiotic therapy</br>b. Antiviral therapy</br>c. Corticosteroid therapy</br>d. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy</br>e. Intensive support and wound management in a burn unit</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E.  Intensive support and wound management in a burn unit  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – “Rapidly progressive rash … nontender, flat, reddish-brown lesions → blisters with burning pain” signals evolution from macules to bullae/epidermal detachment.<br /></br>  – Onset 7–10 days after starting a high-risk anticonvulsant (carbamazepine).<br /></br>  – Confluent erythema over face, trunk, extremities (photo shows violaceous macules with blistering).<br /></br>  – Low-grade fever, tachycardia, but no purulence or focal infection.  </p></br><p>These classic temporal and morphologic findings point to Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) / toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) spectrum triggered by carbamazepine.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. New medication within ≤3 weeks?<br /></br>2. Skin pain &gt; itch, dusky macules, mucous membrane involvement?<br /></br>3. Rapidly coalescing bullae/epidermal sloughing?<br /></br>“Yes” to all → think SJS/TEN. Next step: stop culprit drug + treat like a thermal burn.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>SJS (&lt;10 % BSA) and TEN (&gt;30 % BSA) require the same initial management:<br /></br>• Immediate withdrawal of offending agent.<br /></br>• Aggressive supportive care identical to major burn management because the chief threat is massive fluid loss, infection risk, and multiorgan failure.<br /></br>Evidence shows mortality drops in specialized burn/ICU units where meticulous fluid resuscitation, electrolyte monitoring, temperature regulation, nutritional support, pain control, and sterile handling are available.  </p></br><p>Take-home: For SJS/TEN, “Supportive care in burn unit” is first-line after drug cessation.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): total BSA detached, mucosal sites involved, drugs started in last 8 weeks, fluid balance, ophthalmology consult.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high yield)<br /></br>• Type IV hypersensitivity → CD8⁺ cytotoxic T-cell–mediated apoptosis of keratinocytes expressing drug-derived antigens.<br /></br>• Results in full-thickness epidermal necrosis, subepidermal bullae, Nikolsky sign positive.<br /></br>• Mortality correlates with SCORTEN score (age, HR, BUN, bicarbonate, etc.).</p></br><p>High-risk drugs to memorize (mnemonic “SATAN”):<br /></br>S – Sulfonamides<br /></br>A – Allopurinol<br /></br>T – Tetracyclines (and TMP-SMX)<br /></br>A – Anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, lamotrigine, phenytoin, phenobarbital)<br /></br>N – NSAIDs (oxicams)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. Burn-unit supportive care (fluids, electrolytes, wound dressings, nutritional support, infection surveillance).<br /></br>2. Ophthalmology evaluation (ocular sequelae common).<br /></br>3. Consider IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) in extensive TEN; data for systemic steroids or cyclosporine remain conflicting—NOT first-step on exams.<br /></br>4. Lab work: CBC, CMP, coagulation, cultures if febrile; but do not delay transfer.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Steven’s car burns the skin”:<br /></br>Steven Johnson started a new CARbamazepine → his skin looks like BURNs → manage in a burn unit.</p></br><p>Visual hook: Picture SJS/TEN patients wrapped like burn victims in white sheets instead of clothing—reinforces burn-unit care.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Antistaphylococcal antibiotic therapy<br /></br>   • Bullous impetigo/SSSS from Staph aureus present with superficial epidermal splitting in infants/young children and is usually widespread but not linked to new drugs; also pustules, no target lesions. Culture/PCR + treat with nafcillin/oxacillin, not indicated here.  </p></br><p>b. Antiviral therapy<br /></br>   • HSV can trigger erythema multiforme (target lesions, limited mucosal involvement, &lt;1 cm) but not widespread necrolysis. Acyclovir would not halt TEN progression.  </p></br><p>c. Corticosteroid therapy<br /></br>   • Historically used but now controversial; retrospective data suggest increased infection and mortality. USMLE first step after drug cessation is supportive burn care, not steroids.  </p></br><p>d. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy<br /></br>   • Utilized for gas gangrene, carbon monoxide poisoning, decompression sickness—not SJS/TEN.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core takeaway: SJS/TEN is a dermatologic emergency precipitated by high-risk drugs; after stopping the agent, immediate transfer for burn-unit–level supportive care is lifesaving.</p>
160. An 82-year-old woman with hypertension comes to the physician because of a 9-month history of moderate pain in her hips and knees. She has an active lifestyle and lives independently with her husband. Her medications are hydrochlorothiazide, metoprolol, and a calcium supplement. Her blood pressure is 128/84 mm Hg. Examination shows mild limitation on external rotation of both hips. On standing, the patient has pain in the right knee that she rates as an 8 on a 10-point scale. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL</br>Hematocrit 39 percent</br>Leukocyte count 7800/mm3</br>Serum</br>Na+ 140 mEq/L</br>K+ 3.9 mEq/L</br>Cl– 110 mEq/L</br>HCO3– 26 mEq/L</br>Urea nitrogen 12 mg/dL</br>Creatinine 1 mg/dL</br>The patient asks if she can take an over-the-counter nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) for her arthritis. If treatment with an NSAID is begun, this patient is at greatest risk for which of the following adverse effects?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Altered mental status</br>b. Impaired renal function</br>c. Renal papillary necrosis</br>d. Thrombocytopenia</br>e. There is no increased risk for adverse effects</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Impaired renal function  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 82-year-old (advanced age = ↓renal reserve).<br /></br>   • Chronic hypertension, on hydrochlorothiazide (diuretic → relative volume depletion).<br /></br>   • Wants to begin an NSAID.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>How clues narrow the differential<br /></br>   • Elderly + diuretic = kidney depends on renal prostaglandin–mediated afferent arteriolar vasodilation to maintain GFR.<br /></br>   • NSAIDs block cyclo-oxygenase ⇒ ↓prostaglandins ⇒ constricted afferent arteriole ⇒ abrupt fall in renal blood flow &amp; GFR.<br /></br>   • The most immediate, common toxicity in this setting is an acute rise in creatinine (prerenal/ischemic acute kidney injury).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • Baseline creatinine normal (1 mg/dL) → any rise after NSAID would be attributable to the drug.<br /></br>   • No history of chronic high-dose analgesic use or hemoglobinopathy → papillary necrosis less likely.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: For any question that pairs “elderly + diuretic/ACEi/ARB” with “start NSAID,” think “prostanglandin blockade → compromised renal perfusion → AKI.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Identify risk factors for NSAID nephrotoxicity<br /></br>   • Age &gt;65 yr<br /></br>   • Volume depletion/diuretic use<br /></br>   • Chronic HTN, CKD, ACEi/ARB  </p></br><p>Step 2: Match most common, clinically significant toxicity in that risk group<br /></br>   • Acute, reversible decline in GFR (“impaired renal function”).  </p></br><p>Take-home message: NSAID-induced afferent arteriolar vasoconstriction is the leading cause of drug-related prerenal AKI in the elderly. Document baseline creatinine and counsel patient about monitoring renal function within 1–2 weeks of starting therapy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Prostaglandins (mainly PGE₂, PGI₂) dilate the afferent arteriole → preserve RBF &amp; GFR, especially when renal perfusion is marginal.<br /></br>• NSAIDs inhibit COX-1/COX-2 → ↓prostaglandins → unopposed vasoconstriction of afferent arteriole → ↓RBF → ↓GFR → azotemia.<br /></br>• Typically presents 3–7 days after initiation; creatinine rises &gt;30%. Reversible with drug discontinuation.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Monitoring  </p></br><ol></br><li>Avoid or use lowest effective dose; consider topical NSAID or acetaminophen first.  </li></br><li>Baseline and follow-up serum creatinine &amp; BUN within 1–2 weeks.  </li></br><li>Maintain hydration; avoid concurrent ACEi/ARB if possible (“triple whammy”: ACEi/ARB + diuretic + NSAID).  </li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“PGE DILates the Afferent; NSAIDs take the ‘PG’ away, so the ‘Afferent’ gets A-squeezed and GFR drops.”  </p></br><p>Think “NSAID = No-Spare-Arteriole In Dialysis” (risk of dialysis-requiring AKI).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Altered mental status – Not a common direct effect of NSAIDs; more typical of opioids, anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, or severe uremia secondary to renal failure (a downstream effect, not primary toxicity).  </p></br><p>c. Renal papillary necrosis – Can occur with very high-dose, chronic NSAID or combined analgesic abuse, sickle cell disease, diabetes; much less common and not the greatest <em>immediate</em> risk compared with AKI.  </p></br><p>d. Thrombocytopenia – NSAIDs impair platelet aggregation qualitatively (reversible COX-1 inhibition with most agents, irreversible with aspirin) but do not lower platelet count. Quantitative thrombocytopenia is rare and idiosyncratic.  </p></br><p>e. No increased risk – Incorrect; elderly patients on diuretics clearly have heightened susceptibility to NSAID-induced renal impairment.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In an elderly, diuretic-treated hypertensive patient, the most likely and clinically significant adverse effect of starting an NSAID is an acute, reversible decline in renal function due to prostaglandin-dependent afferent arteriolar vasoconstriction.</p>
161. A 62-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 35-minute history of severe chest pain. He has had mild intermittent chest pain with exertion during the past 3 weeks. The pain has typically lasted a few minutes and resolved either with rest or spontaneously. He has not had nausea or dyspnea. He has hypertension treated with hydrochlorothiazide and enalapril and type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with insulin glargine. His temperature is 37.1°C (98.9°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 140/86 mm Hg. Physical findings are otherwise normal. An ECG and chest x-ray show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Outpatient exercise stress test</br>b. CT scan of the chest</br>c. Proton pump inhibitor therapy</br>d. Ibuprofen therapy</br>e. Admission to the hospital for observation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>e. Admission to the hospital for observation</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Red flags: 62-year-old man (coronary disease risk age), long-standing HTN and diabetes (major CAD risk factors), new-onset “severe chest pain” lasting 35 min.<br /></br>• Change in pattern: He previously had exertional chest discomfort that remitted with rest (stable angina). Now the pain is (1) more intense, (2) at rest, (3) &gt;20 min. That is classic for unstable angina (UA) or a non–ST-elevation MI (NSTEMI).<br /></br>• Normal ECG &amp; CXR do NOT exclude ACS—up to 30–50 % of UA/NSTEMI patients have an initial nondiagnostic ECG. Initial troponin can also be negative within the first 6 h.<br /></br>Key test-taking tip: Any intermediate- or high-risk patient with possible ACS requires hospital-based evaluation (serial ECGs, cardiac enzymes, telemetry) before lower-risk outpatient strategies can be considered.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process</br>1. Chest pain in an older diabetic male is ACS until proven otherwise.<br /></br>2. Categorize pain type: rest pain &gt;20 min → unstable angina/NSTEMI risk.<br /></br>3. Initial ED work-up (ECG, CXR, troponin) is done, but diagnostic sensitivity is limited early after symptom onset.<br /></br>4. Next action: place patient on continuous cardiac monitoring, obtain serial cardiac biomarkers and ECGs, and begin anti-ischemic/antithrombotic therapy per ACS protocol. This requires admission (telemetry unit or ICU).</p></br><p>Take-home message: Normal initial studies never “rule out” ACS in a high-risk presentation; observation with serial testing is mandatory.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): “Chest pain concerning for UA/NSTEMI. Plan: admit to telemetry, serial troponins q3-6 h ×3, repeat ECGs, start aspirin, heparin, β-blocker, statin, nitrates as needed.”</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>Unstable angina = acute plaque rupture with subtotal coronary occlusion → ischemia but no myocyte necrosis (troponins normal/negative). NSTEMI = same process but with necrosis → positive troponins. Both lie on the acute coronary syndrome (ACS) spectrum and carry high early morbidity/mortality.</p></br><p>Risk factors in vignette: age, HTN, diabetes. Diabetes often blunts classic ischemic symptoms; hence atypical presentations—another reason to keep a low threshold for admission.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Overview</br>In the ED/telemetry unit:</br>• Serial troponin I/T and CK-MB at 0, 3-6, and 6-12 h.<br /></br>• Repeat ECGs looking for dynamic ST-T changes.<br /></br>• Initiate guideline-directed therapy:<br /></br>  – Antiplatelet: chewable aspirin ± P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel).<br /></br>  – Anticoagulation: unfractionated heparin or LMWH.<br /></br>  – Anti-ischemic: β-blocker (e.g., metoprolol), nitrates, high-intensity statin.<br /></br>• Early cardiology consult; determine need for urgent coronary angiography/revascularization.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“NEW” chest pain lasting &gt;20 miN, occurs at rESt, or Worse than usual → N.E.W. = admit for ACS work-up.<br /></br>First ECG &amp; troponin can “LIE” (Look Innocent Early), so watch them over time.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Outpatient exercise stress test<br /></br>   • Contraindicated while active chest pain is ongoing or within 48 h of suspected ACS; risk of provoking infarction.</p></br><p>b. CT scan of the chest<br /></br>   • Consider for aortic dissection or pulmonary embolism when clinical picture fits (tearing back pain, unequal pulses, pleuritic dyspnea). Vignette lacks those features; ACS is far more likely.</p></br><p>c. Proton pump inhibitor therapy<br /></br>   • GERD can mimic chest pain, but always exclude life-threatening ACS first, especially in high-risk demographics.</p></br><p>d. Ibuprofen therapy<br /></br>   • Musculoskeletal or pericarditic pain may improve with NSAIDs, but again, never treat presumptively before ruling out ACS in this patient profile.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Core Takeaway</br>In an older patient with multiple CAD risk factors and new prolonged rest chest pain, NORMAL initial ECG/chest X-ray never rules out ACS. Admit for observation with serial ECGs and cardiac enzymes; this is the safest, guideline-consistent first step.</p>
162. A 38-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of increasing pressure in his chest and shortness of breath with exertion. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He has smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 20 years and consumes four mixed alcoholic drinks weekly. His pulse is 65/min, and blood pressure is 155/90 mm Hg. Examination shows a brisk carotid upstroke. On cardiac examination, there is a grade 4/6 systolic murmur heard best along the left sternal border at the fourth intercostal space. The murmur increases on Valsalva maneuver and with standing. An ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Digoxin</br>b. Furosemide</br>c. Lisinopril</br>d. Metoprolol</br>e. Nitroglycerin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Metoprolol  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Location &amp; quality of murmur<br /></br>   • Harsh crescendo–decrescendo systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border (4th ICS) → classic for hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM)/LV outflow-tract (LVOT) obstruction rather than aortic stenosis (which is loudest at the right 2nd ICS and radiates to the carotids).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Dynamic maneuvers<br /></br>   • Murmur intensity ↑ with Valsalva and standing (both ↓ preload).<br /></br>   • Only two common systolic murmurs behave this way: HOCM and mitral valve prolapse. Mitral prolapse usually has a mid-systolic click, not described here.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Supporting data<br /></br>   • “Brisk carotid upstroke” (normal or hyperdynamic pulse) rather than the delayed upstroke of aortic stenosis.<br /></br>   • ECG: left-ventricular hypertrophy—expected in HOCM.<br /></br>   • Age (38 y) fits genetic HOCM; aortic stenosis at this age would be unusual unless bicuspid.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach for future questions<br /></br>• When a systolic murmur changes with preload/afterload maneuvers, quickly place it into the dynamic-murmur table (HOCM &amp; MVP louder with ↓ preload; all others softer).<br /></br>• Always ask: “What therapy reduces LVOT gradient by slowing the heart and increasing ventricular filling?” → β-blocker, verapamil, or disopyramide.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why Metoprolol?)  </p></br><p>HOCM pathophysiology: asymmetric septal hypertrophy + systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve → dynamic LVOT obstruction. The obstruction worsens when the LV cavity is smaller (low preload, low afterload, or increased contractility).  </p></br><p>Goals of therapy:<br /></br>1. Slow HR → prolong diastole → ↑ preload/ventricular filling.<br /></br>2. Reduce contractility to decrease the Venturi effect that pulls the mitral valve anteriorly.  </p></br><p>Metoprolol (β1-selective blocker) accomplishes both goals and is first-line. It has mortality benefit (↓ sudden cardiac death) and symptom relief.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls for a real encounter<br /></br>• Counsel about activity restrictions (no high-intensity sports)<br /></br>• Screen first-degree relatives<br /></br>• Plan for echocardiography and consider ICD if high-risk features present  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-yield)  </p></br><p>• Inherited autosomal dominant mutations of sarcomeric proteins (β-myosin heavy chain, myosin-binding protein C).<br /></br>• Outflow obstruction is dynamic, not fixed. Anything that 1) ↓ preload (Valsalva, standing, dehydration) or 2) ↑ contractility (exercise, digoxin) makes the murmur—and symptoms—worse.<br /></br>• Presents with exertional dyspnea, chest pain, presyncope/syncope, or sudden death in athletes.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Algorithm  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>First-line pharmacotherapy<br /></br>   • β-blocker (eg, metoprolol)<br /></br>   • If β-blocker contraindicated → nondihydropyridine CCB (verapamil)<br /></br>   • Add disopyramide if symptoms persist  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Avoid drugs that ↓ preload/afterload or ↑ contractility: nitrates, ACE inhibitors, diuretics, digoxin, dopamine/dobutamine.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Invasive options (if refractory):<br /></br>   • Septal myectomy or alcohol septal ablation<br /></br>   • ICD for secondary prevention or high-risk primary prevention  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“HOCM gets LOUD when the LV cavity is L-OW.”<br /></br>Low preload/afterload → Loud murmur.<br /></br>Treat with “MVD”:<br /></br>M = Metoprolol (slow HR)<br /></br>V = Verapamil (if β-blocker not tolerated)<br /></br>D = Disopyramide (add-on if needed)  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Digoxin<br /></br>• Positive inotrope increases contractility → smaller LV cavity → worsens LVOT obstruction; contraindicated in HOCM unless concomitant atrial fibrillation with refractory rate control needs (rare).  </p></br><p>B. Furosemide<br /></br>• Loop diuretic markedly lowers preload → exacerbates obstruction and symptoms; reserved only for frank volume overload (pulmonary edema) and used cautiously.  </p></br><p>C. Lisinopril<br /></br>• ACE inhibitor decreases afterload → allows the LV to empty more completely, reducing cavity size and worsening obstruction. Not first-line; generally avoided unless hypertension truly requires multiple agents.  </p></br><p>E. Nitroglycerin<br /></br>• Venodilator reduces preload and afterload → same deleterious effect as above. NTG often provokes syncope in HOCM patients—should be avoided.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy presents with a crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur that INCREASES with maneuvers that decrease preload (Valsalva, standing). The first-line therapy is a β-blocker such as metoprolol, which slows the heart and blunts contractility, thereby reducing the dynamic LVOT gradient and preventing sudden cardiac death.</p>
163. A 67-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. Three days ago, he went to the emergency department because of a prolonged nosebleed that was not related to any injuries; after undergoing nasal packing for 4 hours, he was discharged and advised to schedule a follow-up examination with his primary care physician. During the past 3 years, he has had two similar nosebleeds, one of which required cauterization. He has had two episodes of upper gastrointestinal bleeding during the past 10 years. He has psoriasis and osteoarthritis. Current medications include acetaminophen and topical betamethasone cream. He avoids use of aspirin and aspirin-containing medications. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 45 years. He drinks one alcoholic beverage daily. He does not use illicit drugs. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 130/76 mm Hg. Jugular venous pressure is 7 cm H2O. A photograph of the lips and tongue is shown. The lungs are clear to auscultation. There are no murmurs or gallops. The abdomen is soft and nontender with no hepatosplenomegaly. There is no peripheral edema. Laboratory studies from 3 days ago show:</br>Hematocrit         34 percent</br>Platelet count     285,000/mm3</br>Prothrombin time   11.6 sec (INR =1.05)</br>Serum creatinine   1.1 mg/dL</br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Cirrhosis with portal hypertension</br>b. Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia</br>c. Mallory-Weiss syndrome</br>d. Peutz-Jeghers syndrome</br>e. von Willebrand disease</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Recurrent spontaneous bleeding<br /></br>   • ≥3 lifetime epistaxes (two prior, one current)<br /></br>   • Two documented upper-GI bleeds</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Normal coagulation studies<br /></br>   • Platelets, PT/INR all normal → argues against a systemic coagulopathy (eg, vWD).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Characteristic mucocutaneous lesions (photo)<br /></br>   • Numerous small, red, blanching dilated vessels on lips and tongue = telangiectasias.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>No stigmata of portal HTN or cirrhosis (normal abdominal exam, no splenomegaly, ascites, palmar erythema).  </p></br></li></br><li>Age and smoking history are distractors and do not fit any other option as well as HHT.</li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>When a question shows 1) mucocutaneous vascular spots + 2) lifelong, unexplained epistaxis/GI bleeding with normal coagulation tests, jump to the “bleeding telangiectasia” rule-out list—HHT is virtually pathognomonic.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why choice B is correct)  </p></br><p>Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT) is an autosomal-dominant vascular disorder caused by mutations in TGF-β signaling genes (eg, ENG, ACVRL1).<br /></br>• Pathology: fragile telangiectasias and larger arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) in skin, mucosa, GI tract, lung, liver, and brain.<br /></br>• Clinical triad:<br /></br>  1. Recurrent epistaxis (most common initial sign)<br /></br>  2. Mucocutaneous telangiectasias (lips, oral cavity, fingers, nose)<br /></br>  3. Visceral AVMs → iron-deficiency anemia, GI bleeding, high-output heart failure, paradoxical emboli/brain abscesses.<br /></br>• Coagulation studies are typically normal because bleeding is mechanical (vessel wall) rather than coagulopathic.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Normal platelets/PT + visible telangiectasias + recurrent mucosal bleeding = HHT.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls for real life: note frequency/severity of bleeding episodes, iron status, presence of AVM-related complications, need for ENT/GI referral, genetic counseling.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Gene mutations (ENG, ACVRL1, SMAD4) disrupt endothelial TGF-β signaling → defective elastic lamina → thin-walled vessels susceptible to rupture.<br /></br>• AVMs form direct artery-to-vein shunts lacking capillaries → high-flow lesions bleed easily.<br /></br>• Autosomal-dominant with variable penetrance; family history often (+) but can be subtle.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management  </p></br><ol></br><li>Labs: CBC (look for iron deficiency), routine coagulation tests (usually normal).  </li></br><li>Imaging:<br /></br>   • Contrast CT/MRI chest/brain or echocardiographic bubble study to screen for pulmonary/ cerebral AVMs.  </li></br><li>Treatment algorithm<br /></br>   • Epistaxis: humidification, topical hemostatic agents, laser cauterization; severe cases → nasal septal dermoplasty or embolization.<br /></br>   • GI bleeding/iron-deficiency anemia: endoscopic therapy (argon plasma coagulation), iron supplementation, transfusion as needed.<br /></br>   • AVMs: embolization or surgical resection (pulmonary), radiosurgery (cerebral).<br /></br>   • Anti-angiogenic therapy (bevacizumab) for refractory bleeding.<br /></br>   • Genetic counseling for patient and family.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“HHT” mnemonic:<br /></br>H – Hereditary (AD)<br /></br>H – Hemorrhages (epistaxis, GI)<br /></br>T – Telangiectasias (lips, tongue, skin)  </p></br><p>“Osler Wields Red Nose”: Osler-Weber-Rendu causes red (telangiectatic) nose bleeds.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>a. Cirrhosis with portal hypertension<br /></br>   • Would expect stigmata: jaundice, spider angiomas on chest, palmar erythema, ascites, splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia and/or elevated INR. None are present; telangiectasias in HHT are mucosal, not “spiders” on the trunk.  </p></br><p>c. Mallory-Weiss syndrome<br /></br>   • Acute longitudinal mucosal tear at GE junction from forceful vomiting/alcohol binge → single episode of hematemesis, not chronic epistaxis or oral telangiectasias.  </p></br><p>d. Peutz-Jeghers syndrome<br /></br>   • Hamartomatous polyps + dark blue-brown mucocutaneous macules (melanin deposition), not blanching red telangiectasias. Bleeding is usually from polyps causing intussusception/anemia, not recurrent nosebleeds.  </p></br><p>e. von Willebrand disease<br /></br>   • Produces mucocutaneous bleeding, but labs typically show prolonged bleeding time ± elevated aPTT; telangiectasias are absent. Bleeding would begin in childhood; picture lesions inconsistent.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway  </p></br><p>Recurrent mucosal bleeding with visible red-blanching telangiectasias and normal coagulation studies is classic for hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia.</p>
164. A 34-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after he was involved in a motor vehicle collision in which he was the restrained driver. He drove off the road and hit a tree. The air bags did not deploy. On arrival, he appears to be in distress. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Temperature is 35.0°C (95.0°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 80/40 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98 percent. Examination shows a 2-cm laceration over the right aspect of the forehead and ecchymoses over the face, torso, and all extremities. There is jugular venous distention. Heart sounds are distant. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Arterial blood gas analysis  </br>b. Complete blood count  </br>c. CT scan of the chest  </br>d. CT scan of the head  </br>e. ECG  </br>f. Echocardiography  </br>g. X-ray of the chest</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Echocardiography (bedside FAST/echo)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis – Key Clues<br /></br>• Mechanism: High-speed MVC with torso impact → risk of blunt cardiac injury or great-vessel injury.<br /></br>• Vital signs: Profound hypotension (80/40 mm Hg) with only mild tachycardia and normal O₂ sat → obstructive or cardiogenic shock more likely than hypoxemic/hemorrhagic shock alone.<br /></br>• Physical exam triad (“Beck triad”):<br /></br>  – Jugular venous distention<br /></br>  – Muffled (distant) heart sounds<br /></br>  – Hypotension<br /></br>  These three together are classic for acute cardiac tamponade.<br /></br>• No breath-sound abnormalities, tracheal deviation, or hypoxia → tension pneumothorax less likely.<br /></br>• Cognitive road-map: In any trauma patient with shock + JVD, think “obstructive shock: tamponade vs. tension pneumo.” Breath sounds and oxygenation separate the two.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1 – Generate differential for obstructive shock in trauma.<br /></br>Step 2 – Apply physical findings (Beck triad) → tamponade to top.<br /></br>Step 3 – Choose the fastest, bedside test that both confirms diagnosis and can guide immediate therapy: Focused transthoracic echo (FAST pericardial window) shows pericardial fluid and diastolic RV collapse.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In an unstable trauma patient with suspected tamponade, bedside echocardiography is the diagnostic test of choice; it is quick, non-invasive, and available in the resuscitation bay.</p></br><p>Documentation note: “Bedside FAST demonstrates large pericardial effusion with right-ventricular diastolic collapse consistent with cardiac tamponade. Plan: emergent pericardiocentesis / ED thoracotomy.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Blunt chest trauma → tear of coronary or great-vessel branches → rapid accumulation of blood in pericardial sac.<br /></br>• Pericardium is non-compliant → small volume rise (100–200 mL) acutely impairs diastolic filling.<br /></br>• ↓Venous return → ↓stroke volume → hypotension.<br /></br>• Raised intrapericardial pressure backs up into SVC → JVD.<br /></br>• Fluid muffles transmission of heart sounds.<br /></br>• Other classic findings (may or may not be present): pulsus paradoxus, electrical alternans on ECG.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot<br /></br>1. Primary survey, oxygen, IV access.<br /></br>2. FAST echo → if tamponade confirmed AND patient hemodynamically unstable → immediate pericardiocentesis or resuscitative thoracotomy.<br /></br>3. Definitive surgical repair of cardiac injury.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“Beck triad = Beck ‘JVD’”:<br /></br>• J − JVD<br /></br>• V − Very low BP<br /></br>• D − Distant heart sounds<br /></br>“FAST answers FAST”: FAST exam answers tamponade quickly in the trauma bay.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Arterial blood gas – May show metabolic acidosis from shock but does not confirm tamponade.<br /></br>b. Complete blood count – Can detect anemia but not cause of obstructive shock.<br /></br>c. CT chest – Good detail but too slow and unsafe for an unstable patient; patient may arrest in scanner.<br /></br>d. CT head – Evaluates intracranial injury, unrelated to obstructive shock signs.<br /></br>e. ECG – Could show electrical alternans or low-voltage QRS, but sensitivity is low and it cannot visualize fluid; still need echo before invasive intervention.<br /></br>g. Chest X-ray – Acute tamponade often shows a normal cardiac silhouette (takes time to enlarge) and CXR does not visualize pericardial fluid well; cannot reliably rule in/out tamponade.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: ordering CT or CXR in an unstable patient instead of immediate bedside echo delays life-saving pericardiocentesis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Blunt-trauma patient with hypotension, JVD, and muffled heart sounds → think cardiac tamponade. The fastest, most definitive confirmation is bedside echocardiography (FAST), which directly visualizes pericardial fluid and guides emergent intervention.</p>
165. A 3-year-old girl is brought to the office by her mother because of a 1-month history of moderate leg pain when she runs more than 20 yards. The pain does not occur at rest or when she walks. She was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and spontaneous vaginal delivery. She has no history of serious illness and receives no medications. Immunizations are up-to-date. She is at the 40th percentile for height and 50th percentile for weight. Her pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 130/85 mm Hg in both upper extremities. No murmurs are heard on cardiac examination. An epigastric bruit is heard. Pulses are 3+ in the upper extremities and 2+ in the lower extremities. Muscle strength is 5/5 in all extremities. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Coarctation of the aorta</br>b. Congenital heart disease</br>c. Hypoplasia of the iliac arteries</br>d. Liddle syndrome</br>e. Mesenteric artery stenosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Coarctation of the aorta  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key findings<br /></br>  – Exercise-induced leg pain (“claudication”) → suggests low perfusion to lower extremities during increased demand.<br /></br>  – Blood pressure 130/85 mm Hg in a 3-year-old = stage 2 pediatric hypertension.<br /></br>  – Bounding (3+) pulses in the arms but diminished (2+) pulses in the legs → pulse amplitude gradient.<br /></br>  – Epigastric bruit → collateral flow through enlarged intercostal &amp; internal mammary arteries.<br /></br>  – No cardiac murmur (up to 30% of coarctations, especially discrete, have none).  </p></br><p>• Pertinent negatives<br /></br>  – Normal cardiac auscultation otherwise; no systemic illness; normal growth → rules out congestive or systemic disease.<br /></br>  – Leg strength intact, so pain is vascular rather than neurologic or muscular.  </p></br><p>Cognitive roadmap for similar questions<br /></br>1. Pediatric hypertension? → Always think “secondary cause.”<br /></br>2. Compare upper vs lower extremity BP/pulse.<br /></br>3. If gradient + claudication ± bruit → coarctation until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Coarctation is a discrete narrowing of the proximal descending aorta (just distal to the origin of the left subclavian).<br /></br>• Proximal to the lesion: ↑ systolic pressure → arm hypertension, bounding radial pulses.<br /></br>• Distal to the lesion: ↓ systolic pressure → weak femoral/dorsalis pedis pulses, exercise leg pain.<br /></br>• Collateral vessels develop (intercostal, internal thoracic) → continuous epigastric or infraclavicular bruits; eventually rib notching on CXR.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Pulse/BP differential between upper and lower extremities plus pediatric HTN = coarctation.  </p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real patient<br /></br>– Four-extremity BPs with &gt;20 mm Hg arm-leg gradient<br /></br>– Pulse contour (brachial-femoral delay)<br /></br>– Presence of bruits<br /></br>– Plan for echo +/- CT/MR angiography and cardiothoracic referral  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Etiology: congenital defect, often discrete “juxtaductal” shelf. Associated with Turner syndrome, bicuspid aortic valve, and circle-of-Willis aneurysms.<br /></br>Hemodynamics: Obstruction → LV pressure overload → concentric hypertrophy; chronic proximal HTN with distal hypoperfusion.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management<br /></br>Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Echocardiography (first-line) shows Doppler gradient across narrowing.<br /></br>2. CT or MR angiography delineates anatomy before repair.<br /></br>3. CXR: figure-3 sign, inferior rib notching (older children).  </p></br><p>Management<br /></br>• Definitive: Surgical resection with end-to-end anastomosis OR balloon angioplasty with stent (especially older children).<br /></br>• Medical: β-blocker or nitroprusside infusion for severe HTN before surgery; prostaglandin E1 in neonates with duct-dependent obstruction.<br /></br>• Long-term: Monitor for persistent HTN, re-coarctation, or aneurysm formation; screen for intracranial aneurysms.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“3 C’s of Coarctation”<br /></br>1. Child with Claudication<br /></br>2. Collateral bruits &amp; rib notching<br /></br>3. (blood) Cuff difference between arms &amp; legs  </p></br><p>Visual mnemonic: Picture an aorta with a “bungee cord” constricting just after the left subclavian—blood backs up (bulging arms) and trickles down (skinny legs).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>b. Congenital heart disease (nonspecific). Most lesions produce murmurs and do NOT generate an arm-leg BP gradient with epigastric bruits unless specifically a coarctation, which is already listed.  </p></br><p>c. Hypoplasia of the iliac arteries would reduce leg pulses but would not cause systemic upper-body HTN or an epigastric bruit because pressure proximal to the iliacs remains normal.  </p></br><p>d. Liddle syndrome (gain-of-function ENaC) causes early familial hypertension with low renin/aldosterone but presents with equal pulses and no claudication; it is a renal salt-retention disorder, not vascular obstruction.  </p></br><p>e. Mesenteric artery stenosis produces post-prandial abdominal pain (“intestinal angina”) and weight loss in adults, with no extremity pulse gradient or childhood HTN.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Take-Home Message<br /></br>In a hypertensive child, always measure all four extremity BPs. Arm hypertension + diminished leg pulses and exercise leg pain = coarctation of the aorta until proven otherwise.</p>
166. A previously healthy 4-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 3-day history of fever, cough, and runny nose. He has not had wheezing, vomiting, or diarrhea. He is at the 75th percentile for height and 10th percentile for weight. His temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F). The skin is warm and pink. Capillary refill time is 2 seconds. Examination shows clear rhinorrhea. Breath sounds are normal. Cardiac examination is shown. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Congestive heart failure</br>b. Idiopathic pulmonary hypertension</br>c. Pericarditis</br>d. Upper respiratory tract infection</br>e. Ventricular septal defect</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>d. Upper respiratory tract infection (viral URI)</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key positives<br /></br>   • Age: 4-year-old (high incidence of self-limited viral URIs)<br /></br>   • Duration: 3 days (acute, typical for viral colds)<br /></br>   • Symptoms: fever, cough, clear rhinorrhea, runny nose<br /></br>   • Exam: afebrile/low-grade temp 37.5 °C, warm pink skin, CRT 2 s, clear lungs, normal heart sounds on audio clip (no murmur, rub, gallop)  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Key negatives<br /></br>   • No wheezing (argues against asthma/bronchiolitis)<br /></br>   • No vomiting/diarrhea (less likely systemic infection)<br /></br>   • No tachypnea, retractions, hypoxia (pneumonia less likely)<br /></br>   • No hepatomegaly, S3, gallop, or peripheral edema (rules out CHF)<br /></br>   • Cardiac audio normal (rules out pericarditis rub, VSD murmur, loud P2 of pulmonary HTN)  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern: Short viral prodrome + entirely benign cardiorespiratory exam = simple viral URI, the most common outpatient pediatric diagnosis.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On test day, always match duration + constellation of “cold” symptoms to benign viral etiology unless a red-flag (respiratory distress, focal lung findings, murmur, toxic appearance) forces you away.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><p>Step 1 – Localize the problem: Symptoms are confined to the upper airway (nasal congestion, mild cough).<br /></br>Step 2 – Severity check: Vitals nearly normal, excellent perfusion, well-appearing child.<br /></br>Step 3 – Cardiac exam: Normal S1/S2, no extra sounds; thus heart disease choices drop.<br /></br>Step 4 – Occam’s razor: The single diagnosis that fits all data with no contradictions is an uncomplicated viral URI.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: A well-appearing child with short-lived coryza, cough, and normal cardiopulmonary findings almost always has a viral upper respiratory tract infection; extensive work-up or pharmacotherapy is unnecessary.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Alert, nontoxic, normal perfusion, lungs clear, no cardiac murmurs; assessment – viral URI; plan – symptomatic care, return precautions.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Usually rhinovirus, coronavirus, adenovirus, RSV, etc.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Viral replication in nasopharyngeal epithelium → cytokine-mediated congestion and cough.<br /></br>• Course: Peaks by day 3–5, resolves within 7–10 days.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>• No labs or imaging indicated in an otherwise healthy child with classic URI presentation.<br /></br>• Supportive care: hydration, nasal saline, antipyretics (acetaminophen/ibuprofen).<br /></br>• Avoid unnecessary antibiotics (no evidence of bacterial infection).</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“MILD”:<br /></br>M – Mild fever<br /></br>I – Interval &lt;1 week<br /></br>L – Lungs clear<br /></br>D – Discharge (clear rhinorrhea)  </p></br><p>If a pediatric cold is MILD, think viral URI.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Congestive heart failure<br /></br>   • Would show tachycardia, gallop (S3), hepatomegaly, crackles, edema, poor perfusion, and often feeding intolerance/weight gain issues. None are present.</p></br><p>b. Idiopathic pulmonary hypertension<br /></br>   • Presents with exertional dyspnea, fatigue, syncope, loud, single S2 or systolic ejection murmur, RV heave—not a benign cardiac exam.</p></br><p>c. Pericarditis<br /></br>   • Sharp chest pain relieved by leaning forward, pericardial friction rub, possible low-grade fever. The vignette lacks chest pain and rub; auscultation provided was normal.</p></br><p>e. Ventricular septal defect<br /></br>   • Classic harsh holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border ± thrill; large VSD may cause failure to thrive and CHF signs. Audio clip was normal, and child is well-perfused.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: A 4-year-old with 3 days of coryza, cough, and a completely normal cardio-pulmonary exam has a simple viral upper respiratory tract infection—no further work-up required.</p>
167. A 23-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-year history of an intermittent cough productive of copious amounts of yellow-green sputum. She has been taking oral antibiotics; an inhaled, short-acting β-adrenergic agonist; and an inhaled, long-acting corticosteroid for the past 12 months. The episodes of cough and sputum production recur every 4 to 6 weeks and last 3 to 5 days. She had severe pneumonia at the age of 13 years that required hospitalization and placement of a chest tube. She has no other history of serious illness. She has never smoked cigarettes. She is not in acute distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no clubbing. Occasional end-expiratory wheezes are heard posteriorly; there are no crackles or rhonchi. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. A chest x-ray shows bronchial thickening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Adult-onset asthma</br>b. Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis</br>c. Bronchiectasis</br>d. Kartagener syndrome</br>e. Löffler syndrome</br>f. Sarcoidosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>C. Bronchiectasis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis: How we know it is bronchiectasis<br /></br>1. Chronic, recurrent, productive cough<br /></br>   • “Intermittent cough productive of copious yellow-green sputum” that flares every 4–6 weeks → classic for bronchiectasis.<br /></br>2. History of a major pulmonary insult<br /></br>   • Severe necrotizing pneumonia at age 13 requiring a chest tube → common trigger that permanently damages bronchial walls.<br /></br>3. Imaging clue<br /></br>   • Chest-x-ray: “bronchial thickening” (so-called tram-track lines) → suggests airway dilation/remodeling.<br /></br>4. Physical exam<br /></br>   • Possible wheezes but no clubbing (clubbing is common but not mandatory).<br /></br>5. Negative clues<br /></br>   • No asthma since childhood, no allergic symptoms, no sinusitis, no systemic features (fever, hilar adenopathy, eosinophilia) that might point elsewhere.</p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>• Step 1 – Decide if cough is productive (purulent) → think infections or structural lung disease.<br /></br>• Step 2 – Look for cycle “infection → antibiotics → temporary improvement → recurrence” → think bronchiectasis or chronic bronchitis.<br /></br>• Step 3 – Young patient + past severe infection + radiologic bronchial thickening = bronchiectasis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why C is right)<br /></br>Bronchiectasis = irreversible dilation and destruction of bronchial walls → impaired mucociliary clearance → pooling of secretions → chronic infection/inflammation → symptomatic flares.<br /></br>Key takeaway: Any patient with repeated bouts of large-volume purulent sputum and a history of lung injury (infection, obstruction, immune defect, cystic fibrosis, etc.) should trigger the bronchiectasis algorithm.</p></br><p>Documentation note (real life): Must record frequency/severity of flares, volume/character of sputum, prior imaging, possible etiologies (post-infectious, CF, immunodeficiency), and plan for high-resolution CT to confirm.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiologies: post-infectious (pneumonia, TB), CF, primary ciliary dyskinesia, airway obstruction, immune deficiencies.<br /></br>• Mechanism: chronic neutrophil-driven inflammation → elastase, proteases destroy cartilage → bronchial dilation, loss of tapering (“tram tracks,” “signet-ring sign” on CT).<br /></br>• Clinical triad: chronic daily cough, mucopurulent sputum, recurrent infections ± hemoptysis, crackles/wheezes, possible digital clubbing.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls<br /></br>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Gold standard = high-resolution CT (HRCT): dilated airways &gt; adjacent vessel, lack of tapering, cystic changes.<br /></br>• Work-up for cause: sputum cultures, sweat chloride/CFTR testing, immunoglobulin levels, ciliary studies as indicated.</p></br><p>Treatment algorithm:<br /></br>1. Airway clearance: chest physiotherapy, oscillatory vests, postural drainage.<br /></br>2. Bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) if coexistent obstruction.<br /></br>3. Treat acute flares: culture-directed oral or IV antibiotics (e.g., ciprofloxacin for Pseudomonas coverage).<br /></br>4. Chronic macrolide therapy (azithromycin) for anti-inflammatory &amp; anti-pseudomonal effect in frequent exacerbators.<br /></br>5. Vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal) and smoking avoidance.<br /></br>6. Surgical resection or lobectomy rarely for localized, refractory disease.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aid<br /></br>“Bronch-Ecto-SIS = Bronchi ‘Excrete Thick, Offensive Sputum In Surges.’”<br /></br>Think “tram-track” rails on x-ray → bronchi turned into parallel pipes.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Adult-onset asthma<br /></br>• Asthma flares cause non-purulent cough, wheeze, dyspnea; sputum usually scant and mucoid, not copious yellow-green.<br /></br>• Airflow obstruction episodic, reversible; no structural bronchial thickening.  </p></br><p>B. Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)<br /></br>• Occurs in asthmatics/CF; presents with fleeting infiltrates, central bronchiectasis, severe asthma, eosinophilia, ↑IgE, skin-test positivity to Aspergillus.<br /></br>• None of these features described; patient’s disease predates any asthma history.  </p></br><p>D. Kartagener syndrome (Primary ciliary dyskinesia with situs inversus)<br /></br>• Would see chronic sinusitis, otitis, infertility, dextrocardia/situs inversus on imaging. None are mentioned.  </p></br><p>E. Löffler syndrome<br /></br>• Transient migratory pulmonary infiltrates plus marked eosinophilia due to parasitic larval migration (Ascaris, Strongyloides). Presents with non-productive cough and wheeze, resolves spontaneously. Not chronic purulent sputum.  </p></br><p>F. Sarcoidosis<br /></br>• Non-caseating granulomas causing dry cough, dyspnea, erythema nodosum; x-ray shows bilateral hilar adenopathy, not bronchial thickening with purulent sputum.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Point<br /></br>Recurrent episodes of large-volume purulent sputum + prior severe lung infection + “tram-track” bronchial thickening = bronchiectasis until proven otherwise.</p>
168. A 68-year-old man comes to the office because of a 1-year history of a dry cough and progressive dyspnea on exertion. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Medications are metformin, atorvastatin, losartan, hydrochlorothiazide, and daily aspirin. The patient smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 50 years but quit 6 months ago. He says that since that time, his symptoms have been rapidly worsening. He does not drink alcoholic beverages. He is a retired power plant worker for a local electric company. He is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 87 kg (193 lb); BMI is 30 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 147/80 mm Hg. Auscultation of the lungs discloses fine, dry inspiratory crackles at the bases and no friction rub. Cardiac examination discloses an increased S2 with a right ventricular lift. CT scan of the chest shows 1- to 2-cm bilateral subpleural densities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Asbestosis</br>b. Berylliosis</br>c. Emphysema</br>d. Sarcoidosis</br>e. Tuberculosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Asbestosis  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Chronic, progressive exertional dyspnea + dry cough → think interstitial lung disease (ILD) rather than obstructive disease or infection.  </li></br><li>Fine, dry “Velcro” inspiratory crackles at lung bases → classic auscultatory finding of pulmonary fibrosis/ILD.  </li></br><li>Loud P2 + right-ventricular (RV) lift → pulmonary hypertension secondary to chronic lung disease.  </li></br><li>Chest CT: “1- to 2-cm bilateral subpleural densities” → pleural plaques/fibrotic subpleural nodules typical of asbestos exposure.  </li></br><li>Occupational clue: retired power-plant worker. Power plants (especially older facilities with turbines, boilers, pipe insulation, and fire-proofing) frequently used asbestos.  </li></br><li>Age 68 + 50-pack-year smoking history → latency period fits (asbestosis usually manifests ≥20–30 yrs after exposure; smoking synergistically increases risk of asbestos-related lung disease and malignancy).</li></br></ol></br><p>High-yield pattern: Older man with long-term industrial/insulation exposure + basal crackles + subpleural plaques = asbestosis.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for similar questions:<br /></br>Step 1 – Decide if the lung process is obstructive vs restrictive vs infectious vs granulomatous.<br /></br>Step 2 – Scan for occupational/environmental buzzwords.<br /></br>Step 3 – Match imaging pattern (pleural plaques vs hilar nodes vs upper-lobe cavitary changes) to exposures.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Asbestosis is a pneumoconiosis caused by inhalation of asbestos fibers → macrophage ingestion → release of cytokines and reactive oxygen species → diffuse interstitial fibrosis, predominantly in the lower lobes and subpleural regions. Fibrosis leads to reduced lung compliance, hypoxemia, and secondary pulmonary hypertension (loud P2, RV lift). CT typically shows:<br /></br>• Basilar reticulation and honeycombing<br /></br>• Pleural plaques (pathognomonic; calcified or non-calcified)<br /></br>• “Shaggy heart” or subpleural curvilinear lines  </p></br><p>Documentation in a real patient: history of occupational exposure (type, duration), respiratory symptoms, exam findings, PFTs (restrictive pattern), imaging results, counseling on surveillance for lung cancer/mesothelioma, and management plan (influenza/pneumococcal vaccines, O2, consider pulmonary rehab, screen for pulmonary HTN).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Subpleural plaques + power-plant/shipyard/construction exposure = asbestosis (lower-lobe fibrosis).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (Concise)  </p></br><p>• Fibers lodge in distal airways → alveolar macrophages cannot digest → chronic inflammation → fibrosis.<br /></br>• Lower lobes most affected because asbestos fibers settle there (heavier).<br /></br>• Major complications: bronchogenic carcinoma (risk ↑ dramatically with smoking), malignant mesothelioma, pleural effusions, and pulmonary hypertension → cor pulmonale.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Pearls  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• PFTs: restrictive (↓FVC, ↓TLC) with normal or ↑FEV1/FVC. ↓DLCO common.<br /></br>• Imaging: CT &gt; CXR for early disease; look for pleural plaques.<br /></br>• If needed, lung biopsy: ferruginous bodies (iron-coated asbestos fibers on Prussian blue stain).  </p></br><p>Management<br /></br>• Remove exposure, smoking cessation, vaccinations.<br /></br>• Pulmonary rehab, supplemental O2 for hypoxemia.<br /></br>• Manage pulmonary HTN/cor pulmonale as indicated.<br /></br>• Surveillance for lung cancer (low-dose CT in heavy smokers) and mesothelioma.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“ABC for Asbestos”:<br /></br>A – Asbestos bodies (ferruginous), Airplane/shipyard/boiler plant worker<br /></br>B – Basilar fibrosis, pleural (Beneath lung surface) plaques<br /></br>C – Cancer (Bronchogenic <Carcinoma> &gt; Mesothelioma)  </p></br><p>Or visualize a power-plant boiler wrapped in white insulation (asbestos) forming hard plaques on the outside—just like subpleural plaques on CT.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Berylliosis<br /></br>• Seen in aerospace, nuclear, electronics (fluorescent light bulbs).<br /></br>• Non-caseating granulomas (mimics sarcoid) with primarily upper-lobe nodular interstitial pattern; pleural plaques uncommon. Requires corticosteroids for granulomatous inflammation. Vignette lacks exposure clue and shows pleural plaques.  </p></br><p>c. Emphysema<br /></br>• Obstructive disease: prolonged expiratory phase, decreased breath sounds, hyperresonant chest, flattened diaphragms on CT (centrilobular destruction), NOT basilar crackles. Initial lung exam and CT findings inconsistent.  </p></br><p>d. Sarcoidosis<br /></br>• Usually younger (&lt;50), African American, bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, uveitis. CT shows perilymphatic nodules, not pleural plaques.  </p></br><p>e. Tuberculosis<br /></br>• Constitutional symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss), upper-lobe cavitary lesions or miliary pattern on imaging. Crackles may be present but plaques are not.  </p></br><p>Key discriminator: Only asbestosis combines a long latent occupational exposure, basal “Velcro” crackles, restrictive physiology, and subpleural pleural plaques on imaging.</p>
169. A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 2-day history of mild abdominal discomfort, fever, and diarrhea. He has had three episodes of similar symptoms during the past 2 years. During two episodes, metronidazole therapy provided mild relief. He has no history of serious illness and currently takes no medications. There is no family history of serious illness. The patient has been sexually active with three male partners during the past 3 years; he uses condoms inconsistently. None of his partners has HIV infection. Results of the patient's HIV antibody test were negative 7 months ago. He works as a waiter and often eats leftover restaurant food. His temperature is 38°C (100.4°F). His blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg when supine and 110/70 mm Hg when standing. Examination shows dry mucous membranes. The abdomen is mildly distended; on palpation, there is diffuse tenderness and boggy fullness of the right lower quadrant. Bowel sounds are increased. Rectal examination shows multiple perianal fissures. Laboratory studies show a hematocrit of 40 percent, a leukocyte count of 17,000/mm³, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 49 mm/h. Results of liver function tests are within the reference ranges. Test of the stool is negative for ova and parasites and positive for leukocytes; culture grows no bacteria. An abdominal x-ray shows an enlarged ileocecal mass and a narrow cecal lumen. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Colonoscopy</br>b. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy</br>c. Metronidazole therapy</br>d. MRI of the abdomen</br>e. Ultrasonography of the abdomen</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Colonoscopy  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis (How the test-writer tips you off)<br /></br>1. Epidemiology &amp; time course<br /></br>   • 28-year-old man with recurrent (3 episodes/2 yr) abdominal pain, low-grade fever, and diarrhea → think chronic inflammatory bowel process rather than an acute infection.<br /></br>2. Key positives<br /></br>   • Right-lower-quadrant “boggy fullness” + x-ray showing “enlarged ileocecal mass and a narrow cecal lumen” → classic for disease centered at the terminal ileum/cecum (Crohn disease).<br /></br>   • Perianal fissures → Crohn &gt; ulcerative colitis (transmural inflammation causes fistulas, fissures, skin tags).<br /></br>   • Stool leukocytes present, but culture and O&amp;P negative → inflammatory, non-infectious diarrhea.<br /></br>   • Elevated WBC (17 K) &amp; ESR (49 mm/h) → systemic inflammation.<br /></br>3. Key negatives<br /></br>   • Normal LFTs (rules out amebic liver abscess for which metronidazole would help).<br /></br>   • No travel; metronidazole only “mild relief” → makes giardiasis/amebiasis unlikely.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>“Recurrent abdominal pain + RLQ mass + perianal disease + negative stool cultures” → Suspect Crohn ➜ next step = establish diagnosis endoscopically with colonoscopy &amp; ileoscopy + biopsies.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Before treating, you must confirm inflammatory bowel disease (IBD).<br /></br>• Colonoscopy allows:<br /></br>  – Direct visualization of cobblestoning, skip lesions, aphthous ulcers.<br /></br>  – Intubation of the terminal ileum (site of suspected mass).<br /></br>  – Biopsy for histology (transmural lymphoid aggregates, non-caseating granulomas).<br /></br>• Imaging (CT/MRI) is useful after diagnosis for fistulas/abscesses, but not first.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Suspected IBD requires colonoscopy with biopsy as the diagnostic gold standard.  </p></br><p>Documentation in real life: “Plan: Colonoscopy with ileal intubation and multiple biopsies to confirm suspected Crohn disease given RLQ mass, perianal fissures, recurrent inflammatory diarrhea.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>Crohn disease<br /></br>• Transmural granulomatous inflammation anywhere mouth→anus; terminal ileum most common.<br /></br>• Leads to strictures (“string sign”/narrow cecal lumen), fistulas, perianal disease, malabsorption (B12, bile salts).<br /></br>• Etiology: immune dysregulation (Th1-mediated), genetic (NOD2), environmental (smoking worsens).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Algorithm<br /></br>1. Diagnostic work-up<br /></br>   • Colonoscopy with ileoscopy + biopsy = definitive.<br /></br>   • If severe flare or obstruction suspected → CT/MRI enterography next.<br /></br>2.
