Treatment (once confirmed)<br /></br>   • Mild–mod ileocolonic: budesonide PO.<br /></br>   • Moderate: systemic corticosteroids then steroid-sparing (azathioprine, 6-MP, methotrexate).<br /></br>   • Refractory/fistulizing: biologics (infliximab, adalimumab).<br /></br>   • Perianal disease: add antibiotics (cipro/metronidazole).<br /></br>   • Smoking cessation, vaccines, colon CA surveillance 8 yr after dx.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memory Tips<br /></br>“Crohn skips to the CROnies at the end (terminal ileum) and CRacks the anus (perianal fissures).”<br /></br>“Before you treat, you must SEE it: Colonoscopy to SEE the bowel.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>B. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy – Visualizes esophagus → duodenum only; misses ileum/colon, so not diagnostic here.<br /></br>C. Metronidazole therapy – Empiric therapy without a diagnosis. Prior courses gave only “mild relief” and no infectious evidence. Treating before confirming IBD risks masking symptoms &amp; delaying care.<br /></br>D. MRI of the abdomen – Useful for penetrating disease or abscess mapping after diagnosis. First step is endoscopic confirmation.<br /></br>E. Ultrasonography of the abdomen – Limited for bowel wall detail; may find appendicitis or abscess but not diagnostic for Crohn.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Jumping to empiric antibiotics or advanced imaging before securing the tissue diagnosis in chronic inflammatory diarrhea. Always think “scope first.”</p>
170. A 77-year-old man comes to the physician because of a painless mass in front of his right earlobe that he first noticed 3 years ago; it has increased in size during this time. He has not had difficulty chewing or swallowing or changes in his hearing. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. His medications are atenolol, lisinopril, and glipizide. He does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 62/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg. Examination shows a 2-cm, firm, irregular, nontender mass at the angle of the right aspect of the mandible. Cranial nerves are intact. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Serum viral titer</br>b. Sialography</br>c. CT scan of the head and neck</br>d. Cephalexin therapy</br>e. Fine-needle aspiration biopsy of the mass</br>f. Surgical excision of the mass</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Fine-needle aspiration biopsy of the mass  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that narrow the differential to a salivary-gland neoplasm:<br /></br>• Location – “in front of his right earlobe … at the angle of the mandible” = parotid region.<br /></br>• Quality – firm, irregular, nontender, slowly enlarging over 3 years → neoplasm (benign &gt; malignant).<br /></br>• Size – 2 cm.<br /></br>• Absence of red flags – no facial nerve palsy, no cervical nodes, no fever/pain → unlikely infection or aggressive cancer.  </p></br><p>High-yield pattern: Painless, slow-growing preauricular mass = pleomorphic adenoma until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>Approach to salivary-gland mass on exams:<br /></br>1. Localize (parotid vs submandibular vs minor).<br /></br>2. Assess for malignant signs (pain, rapid growth, facial nerve dysfunction, nodes).<br /></br>3. Obtain tissue diagnosis with image-guided fine-needle aspiration (FNA).<br /></br>4. Plan definitive therapy (usually superficial parotidectomy for benign, total parotidectomy ± neck dissection for malignant).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why FNA is the next step:<br /></br>• Salivary-gland tumors have a broad histologic spectrum; management hinges on pathology.<br /></br>• FNA is minimally invasive, highly sensitive/specific, and carries &lt;1 % risk to the facial nerve.<br /></br>• Results guide the surgeon (benign vs malignant, high- vs low-grade) and help counsel the patient before any definitive procedure.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Any persistent salivary-gland mass without signs of infection should undergo tissue diagnosis with fine-needle aspiration before definitive treatment.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): size, consistency, mobility, facial nerve exam, intra-oral exam, plan for image-guided FNA and pathology follow-up.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Pleomorphic adenoma<br /></br>• Most common salivary-gland tumor (parotid &gt; others).<br /></br>• Mixed epithelial &amp; myoepithelial elements in chondromyxoid stroma.<br /></br>• Slow, painless growth; can undergo malignant transformation if long-standing.  </p></br><p>Mucoepidermoid carcinoma<br /></br>• Most common malignant parotid tumor; may present similarly but grows faster, may cause pain.  </p></br><p>Warthin tumor<br /></br>• Cystic, often in older men, strong smoking association; benign.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics Summary  </p></br><ol></br><li>Office evaluation → image-guided FNA.  </li></br><li>If benign: elective superficial parotidectomy with facial-nerve preservation; avoids recurrence/malignant change.  </li></br><li>If malignant: staging imaging (CT/MRI), total parotidectomy ± neck dissection, adjuvant RT.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Parotid P-T” mnemonic for workflow:<br /></br>P – Painless Pre-auricular mass<br /></br>T – Tap it first (FNA) before Touching with surgery  </p></br><p>Another: “FNA before F-acial nerve danger.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Serum viral titer – EBV titers used for nasopharyngeal carcinoma/lymphoma, not for isolated parotid mass.  </p></br><p>B. Sialography – Obsolete for tumor work-up; used historically for ductal stones/strictures, not solid masses.  </p></br><p>C. CT scan of head &amp; neck – Provides anatomic detail but cannot replace cytology; imaging is adjunct after FNA or if deep-lobe/extensive.  </p></br><p>D. Cephalexin therapy – No signs of bacterial sialadenitis (would expect tender, erythematous gland, possibly purulent saliva, fever).  </p></br><p>F. Surgical excision now – Definitive treatment indeed, but standard of care is to obtain tissue diagnosis first to plan extent of surgery and counseling; jumping straight to surgery risks unnecessary morbidity or inadequate resection.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A painless, slow-growing parotid mass in an elderly patient mandates a minimally invasive tissue diagnosis with fine-needle aspiration; this step guides all subsequent management.</p>
171. A 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 5-day history of headache, severe, diffuse abdominal pain, and nausea and vomiting. The pain is exacerbated by eating and relieved by vomiting. The vomitus initially consisted of a large amount of semisolid material mixed with a clear green liquid but now consists of clear yellow liquid only. She has not had any other symptoms. She has Crohn disease treated with prednisone; she was instructed to taper her dosage 2 weeks ago. Additional medications include mesalamine and azathioprine. Her last menstrual period was 7 weeks ago. She is sexually active with one male partner, and they use condoms inconsistently. She appears ill and is in moderate distress. Her temperature is 38.4°C (101.2°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 90/50 mm Hg. Examination shows dry oral mucosa and pale conjunctivae. The abdomen is distended, diffusely tender, and tympanic. Bowel sounds are decreased. Pelvic examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit 31 percent</br>Leukocyte count 15,000/mm3</br>Serum</br>    Amylase 300 U/L</br>    Lipase 9 U/L (N=14–280)</br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: G</br></br>a. Acute pancreatitis</br>b. Gastric outlet obstruction</br>c. Gastroparesis</br>d. Migraine</br>e. Pelvic inflammatory disease</br>f. Pregnancy</br>g. Small-bowel obstruction</br>h. Viral gastroenteritis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>g. Small-bowel obstruction (SBO)</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues</br>  – Known Crohn disease → high-yield risk factor for fibrotic intestinal strictures and SBO.<br /></br>  – Pain “exacerbated by eating and relieved by vomiting” = classic obstructive pattern; food bolus ↑ intraluminal pressure, vomiting decompresses.<br /></br>  – Emesis progression: food → green (bilious) → clear/yellow (intestinal fluid) = contents coming from past the pylorus, localizing the obstruction to the small bowel, not the stomach.<br /></br>  – Distended, tympanitic abdomen + ↓ bowel sounds = mechanical obstruction.<br /></br>  – Dehydration, hypotension, tachycardia, leukocytosis = systemic response to obstruction/third-spacing.<br /></br>  – Slightly ↑ amylase with normal/low lipase is common in SBO (salivary and intestinal isoenzymes are absorbed into the blood when the gut is obstructed).</p></br><p>Approach / cognitive framework</br>1. Recognize obstructive pain pattern (post-prandial worsening, relief with emesis).<br /></br>2. Look for exam signs of obstruction (distension, tinkling/absent sounds).<br /></br>3. Use vomitus character to localize: non-bilious = gastric outlet; bilious = obstruction distal to ampulla of Vater.<br /></br>4. Overlay risk factors (postsurgical adhesions, hernia, Crohn stricture, tumors).<br /></br>5. Check labs: lipase to rule out pancreatitis; WBC for complications.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise link to choice G:</br>1. Risk factor: Crohn → fibrotic stricture in terminal ileum/jejunum.<br /></br>2. Mechanical obstruction triad: crampy pain, vomiting, distension.<br /></br>3. Bilious emesis rules out gastric outlet obstruction.<br /></br>4. Lipase not elevated → excludes pancreatitis.<br /></br>5. No pelvic findings → excludes PID; pregnancy test pending but pregnancy does not explain distension/tympany.<br /></br>Take-home: In any Crohn patient with progressive vomiting + distension, think SBO from a stricture.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)</br>• HPI detailing timing of pain vs meals, emesis character/volume.<br /></br>• Last bowel movement/flatus (obstipation?).<br /></br>• Hydration status, vitals indicating sepsis/shock.<br /></br>• Abdominal exam: distension, bowel sounds.<br /></br>• Assessment: “High suspicion for mechanical SBO, likely Crohn stricture; plan for NG tube, IVF, CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast, surgical consult.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>Crohn disease → transmural inflammation → cyclic ulceration &amp; healing → fibrotic concentric strictures → luminal narrowing. Proximal dilatation causes bowel wall edema, venous congestion, third-spacing, bacterial translocation → fever, leukocytosis, hemoconcentration.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Management (high-yield)</br>1. Upright abdominal X-ray or CT scan with oral/IV contrast → multiple air-fluid levels, “string sign” at stricture.<br /></br>2. Initial therapy:<br /></br>   • Aggressive IV crystalloids, electrolyte repletion.<br /></br>   • NG tube for proximal decompression.<br /></br>   • Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics if signs of ischemia/perforation.<br /></br>3. Definitive: Endoscopic balloon dilation or surgical resection/strictureplasty if complete, complicated, or refractory obstruction.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“SBO = 4 V’s”<br /></br>• Vomiting (bilious)<br /></br>• Visceral pain (colicky)<br /></br>• Voluminous distension<br /></br>• Vital sign changes (hypovolemia)</p></br><p>Green = small bowel scene → seeing green vomit means obstruction is “Seen Beyond” the stomach.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Acute pancreatitis – Requires lipase ≥3× ULN; patient’s lipase is low. Pain classically epigastric radiating to back, not relieved by vomiting; abdominal exam shows tenderness, not marked distension.<br /></br>b. Gastric outlet obstruction – Produces NON-bilious, food-laden emesis, succussion splash, early satiety; distension is mainly epigastric.<br /></br>c. Gastroparesis – Usually diabetic, chronic, painless bloating; no systemic inflammatory response, no marked leukocytosis or severe distension.<br /></br>d. Migraine – Abdominal migraine causes pain without distension and normal abdominal exam; headaches alone do not explain systemic findings.<br /></br>e. Pelvic inflammatory disease – Lower abdominal/pelvic pain, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent discharge, not bilious vomiting or tympanic abdomen.<br /></br>f. Pregnancy – Could explain nausea but not high-grade fever, leukocytosis, distension, or bilious emesis; pelvic exam normal.<br /></br>h. Viral gastroenteritis – Vomiting &amp; diarrhea, hyperactive bowel sounds, no severe distension, usually afebrile or low-grade temp, resolves within 48–72 h.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway</br>In a Crohn patient, bilious vomiting + distended, tympanitic abdomen = think fibrotic small-bowel stricture causing mechanical obstruction.</p>
172. A 42-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-day history of fever and cough productive of thick yellow sputum. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. His temperature is 38.9°C (102°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 130/70 mm Hg. On pulmonary examination, crackles are heard at the base of the left lung. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin                     14 g/dL</br>Mean corpuscular volume        90 μm3</br>Leukocyte count                12,000/mm3</br>Platelet count                 350,000/mm3</br>Prothrombin time               12 sec (INR=1)</br>Red cell distribution width    13 percent (N=13 percent–15 percent)</br>Serum</br>  Bilirubin, total             2.1 mg/dL</br>  Indirect                     1.7 mg/dL</br>  AST                          30 U/L</br>  ALT                          30 U/L</br>Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's laboratory findings?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Congenital reduction of glucuronyltransferase</br>b. Disseminated intravascular coagulation</br>c. Hepatic vein thrombosis</br>d. Intravascular hemolysis secondary to pneumonia</br>e. Obstruction of the bile duct</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Congenital reduction of glucuronyl-transferase (Gilbert syndrome)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Pattern of hyperbilirubinemia<br /></br>   • Total bilirubin = 2.1 mg/dL (mild)<br /></br>   • Indirect (unconjugated) fraction = 1.7 mg/dL → predominantly indirect.<br /></br>   • Normal AST, ALT, PT/INR, platelet count, &amp; MCV.  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Triggering stressor<br /></br>   • Acute febrile illness (community-acquired pneumonia) is a classic precipitant for Gilbert syndrome flares.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>No evidence of hemolysis or liver dysfunction<br /></br>   • Hemoglobin 14 g/dL, normal RDW, no elevated AST/ALT, LDH, or schistocytes reported.<br /></br>   • Normal coagulation studies and platelets exclude synthetic liver failure or DIC.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>High-yield cue: “Mild isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with normal CBC, smear, and liver enzymes in the setting of stress” → think Gilbert.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When bilirubin is &lt;3 mg/dL, indirect &gt; direct, and all other labs are normal, first rule in Gilbert before hunting for exotic causes.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Stepwise)<br /></br>1. Identify bilirubin type → indirect predominance.<br /></br>2. Look for hemolysis labs → none (normal Hb, RDW, smear not abnormal).<br /></br>3. Check liver function (AST/ALT) &amp; synthetic capacity (PT) → normal.<br /></br>4. Consider benign conjugation defect → Gilbert syndrome (↓ UDP-glucuronyl-transferase activity).<br /></br>5. Precipitating factors (infection, fasting, stress) present → supports diagnosis.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Gilbert syndrome = intermittent mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia precipitated by stressors; no hemolysis or hepatocellular injury.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>• “Mild isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia likely due to Gilbert syndrome exacerbated by current infection. No evidence of hemolysis or hepatic dysfunction.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Autosomal recessive ↓ activity (≈30%) of hepatic UDP-glucuronyl-transferase → impaired conjugation of bilirubin.<br /></br>• Baseline bilirubin often normal to slightly high; rises (usually &lt;4 mg/dL) with fasting, exercise, illness, or stress.<br /></br>• Completely benign; no treatment required.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>• None needed for Gilbert; reassure patient.<br /></br>• Address precipitating illness (here, treat community-acquired pneumonia with appropriate antibiotics, e.g., azithromycin or doxycycline if outpatient, ceftriaxone + azithromycin if inpatient).<br /></br>• No further hepatic work-up if typical presentation/labs; consider genetic confirmation only if atypical.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Gil gets Jaundiced When STRESSED”<br /></br>Gilbert<br /></br>Jaundice<br /></br>When = times of<br /></br>Stress (Sepsis, Starvation, Sports)  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture “Gil” sitting under a yellow stress-ball he squeezes whenever he gets sick.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Disseminated intravascular coagulation<br /></br>• Would show thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT/PTT, ↑ D-dimer, schistocytes, possibly bleeding—none present.</p></br><p>C. Hepatic vein thrombosis (Budd–Chiari)<br /></br>• Causes hepatomegaly, RUQ pain, ascites, markedly ↑ AST/ALT, and usually conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.</p></br><p>D. Intravascular hemolysis secondary to pneumonia<br /></br>• Would lower Hb, ↑ RDW, ↑ reticulocytes, ↑ LDH, ↓ haptoglobin, possibly schistocytes and hemoglobinuria. Patient’s Hb, RDW, PT are normal; bilirubin elevation is modest.</p></br><p>E. Obstruction of the bile duct<br /></br>• Produces conjugated (direct) hyperbilirubinemia, cholestatic pattern (↑ ALP, GGT), dark urine, pale stools—labs normal except for indirect bilirubin.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Isolated mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia + normal liver function + stressor = Gilbert syndrome due to congenital reduction of UDP-glucuronyl-transferase activity.</p>
173. A 65-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 6-month history of moderate upper abdominal pain that radiates to the back and foul-smelling diarrhea. She says the pain is often worse after eating. She also has had occasional nausea and vomiting during this time. She has hypertension and impaired glucose tolerance. Her only medication is lisinopril. She does not smoke cigarettes. She drinks four glasses of wine each evening. Her pulse is 68/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood pressure is 142/87 mm Hg. Abdominal and rectal examinations show no abnormalities. Results of a complete blood count, measurement of serum electrolyte concentrations, measurement of amylase and lipase activities, and liver function tests are within the reference ranges. MR cholangiopancreatography shows calcifications and pancreatic duct obstruction. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Celiac plexus block</br>b. Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy</br>c. Omeprazole therapy</br>d. Pancreatic enzyme supplementation</br>e. Pancreatic resection</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Pancreatic enzyme supplementation  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 6-month history of epigastric pain radiating to the back, worse after meals → classic for chronic pancreatitis (CP).<br /></br>  – Foul-smelling diarrhea → steatorrhea from fat malabsorption.<br /></br>  – Impaired glucose tolerance → progressive loss of islet-cell function (“pancreatic diabetes”).<br /></br>  – MR cholangiopancreatography: pancreatic calcifications &amp; ductal obstruction → radiographic hallmark of CP.<br /></br>  – Long-standing daily alcohol use but normal amylase/lipase (often normal in CP).  </p></br><p>• Pertinent negatives<br /></br>  – Normal LFTs, no gallstones → points away from biliary disease.<br /></br>  – No weight loss, no palpable mass → less suggestive of pancreatic cancer requiring resection.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Recognize the classic pain + steatorrhea + calcifications triad → chronic pancreatitis.<br /></br>2. Ask: “What is first-line therapy once diagnosis is made?”<br /></br>3. Framework:<br /></br>   a. Conservative → alcohol abstinence, small low-fat meals, enzyme supplementation, analgesia.<br /></br>   b. Endoscopic / surgical → for stone/duct obstruction refractory to medical therapy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Goal = treat malabsorption + reduce post-prandial pain. Giving exogenous pancreatic enzymes (lipase, amylase, protease) does both:<br /></br>• Improves steatorrhea and fat-soluble vitamin deficiency.<br /></br>• Decreases CCK release → lowers pancreatic stimulation → less pain after meals.  </p></br><p>Thus enzyme replacement is the most appropriate next step. Document that patient was educated on alcohol cessation, given pancreatic enzyme prescription (e.g., pancrelipase 25,000–40,000 USP lipase units/meal), and instructed on dosing with every meal/snack.  </p></br><p>Take-home: In symptomatic chronic pancreatitis with steatorrhea, start oral pancreatic enzyme replacement before proceeding to invasive interventions.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Alcohol → repeated inflammation → fibrosis, calcification, ductal strictures → exocrine insufficiency (↓lipase first) → steatorrhea, malnutrition; later endocrine loss → diabetes. Pain originates from increased ductal pressures and neural inflammation; enzymes blunt CCK-mediated secretion, lowering intraductal pressure.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. Conservative (first-line)<br /></br>   • Alcohol/smoking cessation<br /></br>   • Pancreatic enzymes ± proton-pump inhibitor (to prevent acid-mediated enzyme degradation)<br /></br>   • Fat-soluble vitamin &amp; B12 supplementation<br /></br>   • Low-fat diet, small frequent meals<br /></br>   • Analgesics (NSAIDs → weak opioids if needed)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Endoscopic/surgical (if debilitating pain or complications despite medical therapy)<br /></br>   • ESWL or ERCP w/ stenting for obstructing stones/strictures<br /></br>   • Celiac plexus block (pain control, usually late/rescue)<br /></br>   • Partial pancreatectomy / drainage procedures (Puestow)  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“3 C’s of Chronic Pancreatitis” → Calcifications, Celiac-CKK (pain after meals), and Creon (brand name for pancreatic enzymes) is the Cure first-line.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Celiac plexus block<br /></br>   • Used for refractory severe pain (often malignancy). Does not correct steatorrhea and not first-line in CP.  </p></br><p>b. Extracorporeal shock-wave lithotripsy<br /></br>   • Considered when a single large obstructing ductal stone persists despite enzymes and endoscopic therapy. Too invasive as an initial step.  </p></br><p>c. Omeprazole therapy<br /></br>   • A PPI alone will not fix enzyme deficiency. PPIs are added to enzyme therapy (to protect exogenous enzymes) but are not adequate monotherapy for malabsorption.  </p></br><p>e. Pancreatic resection<br /></br>   • Reserved for localized disease or suspicion of cancer, or refractory pain after all other measures. High morbidity—not indicated as first management.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In chronic pancreatitis presenting with steatorrhea and post-prandial pain, start oral pancreatic enzyme supplementation; it treats malabsorption and decreases pancreatic stimulation, improving pain.</p>
174. A 68-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-day history of blood and clots in his urine. He has not had pain or other difficulties with urination. He thinks he may have lost several pounds during the past 3 months, but he has not weighed himself recently. He has hypertension and chronic bronchitis, exacerbations of which occur once or twice yearly and require antibiotic therapy; his most recent antibiotic course was completed 1 month ago. Routine medications are amlodipine and hydrochlorothiazide. The patient smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 40 years but quit 15 years ago. He is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 66 kg (145 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 100/min, and blood pressure is 142/88 mm Hg. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. CT scan of the abdomen with contrast shows a 2.5-cm enhancing cystic mass lesion with internal necrosis in the mid pole of the left kidney. Chest x-ray shows no abnormalities.</br>Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Biopsy of the kidney mass</br>b. Initiation of sunitinib therapy</br>c. Left nephrectomy</br>d. Repeat CT scan of the abdomen in 3 months</br>e. Urine cytologic study</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Left nephrectomy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Painless gross hematuria in a 68-year-old → think renal malignancy or urothelial cancer.<br /></br>• CT abdomen with IV contrast: “2.5-cm enhancing cystic mass with internal necrosis” in the renal parenchyma.<br /></br>  – Enhancement after contrast = solid tumor, not a simple cyst.<br /></br>  – Internal necrosis is classic for clear-cell renal cell carcinoma (RCC).<br /></br>• No metastatic disease on chest x-ray.<br /></br>Key pattern: Small (&lt;7 cm), localized, contrast-enhancing renal mass → surgical excision is curative.  </p></br><p>Approach framework for painless hematuria:<br /></br>1. Localize bleeding (imaging).<br /></br>2. If imaging shows an enhancing renal mass, assume RCC.<br /></br>3. Localized (&lt;7 cm, no metastases) → nephron-sparing (partial) or radical nephrectomy depending on tumor location. No biopsy first.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-by-step:<br /></br>1. Contrast enhancement rules out simple cyst (Bosniak I/II).<br /></br>2. Size 2.5 cm = Stage T1a (≤4 cm, confined to kidney).<br /></br>3. For T1 RCC, standard management is surgical resection: partial nephrectomy if technically feasible, otherwise radical nephrectomy. NBME answer choice lumps both under “nephrectomy.”<br /></br>Core takeaway: “Enhancing renal mass = excision.”  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real life): tumor size, location (upper/mid/lower pole), evidence of metastasis (negative), surgical candidacy, informed consent for nephron-sparing surgery.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• RCC arises from proximal tubular epithelial cells; most common histology = clear cell (lipid-rich → “clear”).<br /></br>• Risk factors: smoking, HTN, obesity, chronic dialysis/cysts, von Hippel-Lindau.<br /></br>• Hematogenous spread → lung &amp; bone; but localized disease is often curable with surgery.<br /></br>• Biopsy avoided: risk of bleeding/tumor seeding and high false-negative rate (tumor heterogeneity).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Algorithm (localized RCC)<br /></br>1. ≤7 cm, confined to kidney<br /></br>   – Partial nephrectomy (nephron-sparing) preferred when possible.<br /></br>   – Radical nephrectomy if central/hilar or technically difficult.<br /></br>2. &gt;7 cm or locally advanced → radical nephrectomy ± adrenalectomy.<br /></br>3. Metastatic or unresectable → targeted therapy (TKIs: sunitinib, pazopanib; immune checkpoint inhibitors).<br /></br>4. Ablative options (RFA/cryotherapy) only for poor surgical candidates with very small tumors.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “Enhancing = Excise.”<br /></br>• Bosniak classification:<br /></br>  I–II (simple/benign) → Ignore, observe.<br /></br>  III–IV (enhancing/complex) → Remove.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Biopsy of the kidney mass<br /></br>   – Not recommended for clearly enhancing renal masses; does not change management and risks tumor seeding.  </p></br><p>b. Initiation of sunitinib therapy<br /></br>   – TKIs are for metastatic or unresectable RCC. This patient has localized disease and is surgical candidate.  </p></br><p>d. Repeat CT scan of the abdomen in 3 months<br /></br>   – Appropriate only for indeterminate, NON-enhancing cystic lesions &lt;1 cm. Delay here risks progression of a malignancy that is already diagnosable and curable by surgery.  </p></br><p>e. Urine cytologic study<br /></br>   – Used to detect urothelial (transitional-cell) carcinoma of bladder/ureter when imaging is negative. Here, imaging has already identified a renal mass, making cytology unnecessary.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Take-Home Message<br /></br>A contrast-enhancing renal mass, regardless of size, is presumed RCC until proven otherwise and requires surgical excision (partial or radical nephrectomy). Do NOT biopsy or delay treatment with additional tests.</p>
175. A 23-year-old primigravid woman at 40 weeks’ gestation is admitted to the hospital in labor. The cervix is completely dilated, and the vertex is at –1 station in an occipitoanterior position. One hour later, there is no change in station. Intravenous oxytocin therapy is started. Four hours later, despite good contractions and good pushing, the station remains unchanged. Fetal heart tones remain normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Continuing pushing for 1 hour  </br>b. Intravenous morphine therapy to allow the patient to rest  </br>c. Vacuum extraction  </br>d. Forceps delivery  </br>e. Cesarean delivery</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Cesarean delivery  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Patient is nulliparous (primigravida) at term (40 wk).<br /></br>  – Cervix fully dilated (so in SECOND stage of labor).<br /></br>  – Initial station −1, occipito-anterior (favorable position).<br /></br>  – After 1 h, no descent ⇒ oxytocin started.<br /></br>  – After an additional 4 h with “good contractions and good pushing,” station still −1.<br /></br>  – Fetal tracing reassuring.  </p></br><p>• Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>  + Adequate uterine activity (after oxytocin).<br /></br>  + Adequate maternal expulsive effort.<br /></br>  – No descent for ≥4 h.<br /></br>  – Vertex still above the ischial spines (not engaged).  </p></br><p>Pattern: Arrest of descent (second-stage arrest) despite adequate labor → think cephalopelvic disproportion (CPD) → operative vaginal delivery impossible because head not engaged.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Identify labor stage.<br /></br>2. Ask: “Adequate power (contractions/pushing)?” If yes.<br /></br>3. Ask: “Passenger &amp; pelvis proportionate?” If no descent with adequate power → CPD likely.<br /></br>4. Ask: “Can I use vacuum/forceps?” Only if head is engaged (station ≥ +2) and cervix fully dilated. If not, proceed to cesarean.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Second-stage arrest in a nulliparous woman is diagnosed when:<br /></br>  – No descent after ≥3 h of pushing with anesthesia (≥2 h without) AND adequate contractions (≥200 Montevideo units).<br /></br>  – This patient has 5 h total → meets criteria.  </p></br><p>• Because the vertex is still at −1 (not engaged), operative vaginal delivery is contraindicated. Continuing to push only prolongs maternal fatigue without likelihood of success.  </p></br><p>• Therefore the safest and definitive management is cesarean delivery.</p></br><p>Take-home concept: In second-stage arrest with adequate power but lack of progress and an unengaged head, assume CPD → go to C-section.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real life): length of second stage, adequacy of contractions (MVUs), maternal effort, fetal status, pelvic assessment, and informed consent for cesarean.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Cephalopelvic disproportion = mismatch between fetal head size/position and maternal pelvic dimensions. With adequate uterine force the head should descend; failure suggests mechanical obstruction. Risk factors: android/platypelloid pelvis, macrosomia, malposition, contracted pelvis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics / Management Algorithm<br /></br>1. Assess contractions → augment with oxytocin if inadequate.<br /></br>2. Reassess after ≥2–3 h of adequate labor.<br /></br>3. If descent achieved → continue. If no descent:<br /></br>   a. If head engaged (+ station) → consider operative vaginal delivery (vacuum/forceps) if criteria met (full dilation, ruptured membranes, known position, no CPD).<br /></br>   b. If head not engaged (– station) or criteria unmet → Cesarean delivery.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Power, Passenger, Pelvis” triad for labor dystocia.<br /></br>CPD mnemonic: Can’t Pass Down → need C-section.<br /></br>Vacuum/forceps need the 5 P’s: Position known, Pelvis adequate, full dilatation Passage complete, Pushing mom, Positive (+) station.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Continue pushing for 1 h – Already exceeded recommended duration (≥3 h with epidural); further pushing unlikely to help and increases maternal/fetal risks.  </p></br><p>B. IV morphine to rest – Sedation does not correct mechanical obstruction and may depress neonate.  </p></br><p>C. Vacuum extraction – Requires ENGAGED head (at least +2). At −1 station the cup cannot create safe traction; high risk of scalp injury, hemorrhage, failure.  </p></br><p>D. Forceps delivery – Same prerequisites as vacuum (engaged head and low station). At −1 station forceps application is dangerous and contraindicated.  </p></br><p>E. Cesarean delivery – Only option that addresses probable CPD when operative vaginal delivery is not feasible. (Correct)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway: Arrest of descent with an unengaged head despite adequate contractions = presume cephalopelvic disproportion → proceed to cesarean delivery.</p>
176. A 37-year-old woman, gravida 5, para 4, at 35 weeks' gestation comes to the physician for a routine prenatal visit. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. All of her previous pregnancies ended in cesarean deliveries at term. Physical examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 35-week gestation. Pelvic ultrasonography at 21 weeks' gestation showed an anterior placenta previa. This patient is at greatest risk for which of the following pregnancy complications?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Amniotic fluid embolism</br>b. Placenta accreta</br>c. Pulmonary embolism</br>d. Vasa previa</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Placenta accreta (placenta accreta spectrum: accreta &gt; increta &gt; percreta)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • 37-year-old (advanced maternal age).<br /></br>   • G5P4 with FOUR prior cesarean deliveries → multiple uterine incisions.<br /></br>   • Current pregnancy complicated by placenta previa diagnosed at 21 weeks.  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • No mention of trauma, hypertension, rupture of membranes, or acute cardiopulmonary distress (argues against abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, PE).<br /></br>   • Normal fetal growth; no bleeding yet, but the type and location of the placenta are the central issues.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>When you see “placenta previa + prior C-sections,” immediately think “placenta accreta spectrum” because prior uterine scars allow the placenta to invade abnormally where decidua basalis is absent.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify major risk factors → placenta previa + previous uterine surgery + AMA.<br /></br>2. Recall the complication most tightly linked to the coexistence of these risk factors → abnormally adherent placenta (placenta accreta spectrum).<br /></br>3. Choose “placenta accreta” from the list.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Placenta previa overlying a uterine scar predisposes to abnormal placental attachment and life-threatening postpartum hemorrhage.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls for a real patient:<br /></br>• Clearly note “placenta previa over prior uterine scar—high risk for PAS (placenta accreta spectrum).”<br /></br>• Plan elective C-section with placenta-directed surgical team and blood products ready at ~34–36 weeks.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Normal implantation: trophoblast attaches to decidua basalis.<br /></br>Accreta spectrum: Loss of decidua (often over a scar) → chorionic villi attach directly to myometrium (accreta), invade it (increta), or traverse it (percreta).<br /></br>Risk factors<br /></br>• Placenta previa (placenta grows over lower uterine segment where decidua is thin)<br /></br>• Prior C-section or myomectomy<br /></br>• ≥ 3 pregnancies (grand multiparity)<br /></br>• Advanced maternal age  </p></br><p>Clinical manifestations<br /></br>• Typically discovered on antenatal ultrasound with placental lacunae, thin myometrium, turbulent Doppler flow.<br /></br>• Catastrophic postpartum hemorrhage when placenta fails to detach; may require hysterectomy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management Algorithm (high-yield)<br /></br>1. Antenatal suspicion → refer to tertiary center.<br /></br>2. Plan scheduled C-section with possible cesarean hysterectomy at 34–36 weeks after corticosteroid course.<br /></br>3. Prepare massive transfusion protocol.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Previa over a previa scar = AccredIA.”<br /></br>ACCretA sticks like Crazy Glue (A-C-C).<br /></br>Risk triad for accreta: PrevIA + ScAr (c-section scar) + Age (AMA).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Amniotic fluid embolism<br /></br>• Typically occurs during rapid labor, delivery, or immediately postpartum with sudden hypoxia, DIC, and cardiovascular collapse.<br /></br>• Not strongly linked to placenta previa or prior C-sections.</p></br><p>C. Pulmonary embolism<br /></br>• Leading cause of maternal mortality postpartum but risk factors are venous stasis, thrombophilia, obesity, not placenta previa.  </p></br><p>D. Vasa previa<br /></br>• Fetal vessels traverse membranes over cervical os; associated with velamentous cord insertion, multiple gestations, IVF, NOT primarily with prior cesareans.<br /></br>• While placenta previa can coexist, the combination of previa plus uterine scars far more strongly predicts accreta than vasa previa.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Placenta previa implanted over multiple prior cesarean scars in an older multiparous woman almost screams “placenta accreta spectrum”—memorize this association for every NBME/Step question.</p>
177. A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-week history of vaginal odor and a gray vaginal discharge. Over-the-counter clotrimazole therapy has not resolved her symptoms. Seven days ago, she completed a 14-day course of antibiotic therapy for an ear infection; she does not recall the name of the medication. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals. She is sexually active with one male partner; they use condoms consistently. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Pelvic examination shows an erythematous vagina and thin, gray vaginal discharge. The uterus is nontender and normal in size and shape. There is no adnexal tenderness. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Wet mount preparation of the discharge</br>b. Culture of the discharge</br>c. Culture of the urine</br>d. Metronidazole therapy</br>e. Terconazole therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: A. Wet mount preparation of the discharge  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Red-flag clues: “fishy” vaginal odor, thin gray discharge, recent antibiotic course, no response to intravaginal clotrimazole (antifungal).<br /></br>• Pattern: classic for bacterial vaginosis (BV), not vulvovaginal candidiasis (would be thick, white, clumpy), and not Trichomonas (frothy yellow-green ± strawberry cervix).<br /></br>• Key negatives: consistent condom use (↓ risk of STIs), no pelvic or adnexal tenderness (rules out PID).<br /></br>• Metacognitive approach: When you see malodorous thin gray discharge, think BV → confirm with office microscopy (Amsel criteria).</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Suspect BV based on history/PE.<br /></br>Step 2: Apply Amsel diagnostic criteria (need ≥3 of 4).<br /></br> 1. Homogeneous thin gray-white discharge<br /></br> 2. Vaginal pH &gt;4.5<br /></br> 3. Positive “whiff” (amine) test with 10% KOH<br /></br> 4. ≥20% clue cells on saline wet mount<br /></br>Step 3: The only bedside test that directly demonstrates clue cells is a saline wet mount; therefore, that is the immediate next step.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Symptomatic vaginitis requires directed office testing before choosing therapy to (1) confirm diagnosis, (2) avoid unnecessary antibiotics, and (3) distinguish from other causes.</p></br><p>Documentation reminder: note discharge appearance, pH, whiff result, and microscopy findings in the chart.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• BV = polymicrobial overgrowth (Gardnerella vaginalis + anaerobes) after loss of protective Lactobacillus.<br /></br>• RFs: recent antibiotics, douching, inconsistent condom use, new/multiple partners.<br /></br>• Amine production by anaerobes → ↑ vaginal pH and fishy odor.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>After confirming BV:<br /></br>• 1st-line: Metronidazole 500 mg PO bid × 7 days OR Metronidazole 0.75% gel intravaginal qHS × 5 days<br /></br>• Alternatives: Clindamycin 2% cream intravaginal × 7 days<br /></br>No partner treatment needed (not an STI).</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“BV’s 4-point CLUE”:<br /></br>C – Clue cells<br /></br>L – Loss of Lactobacilli → pH &gt;4.5<br /></br>U – Unpleasant (fishy) odor with whiff test<br /></br>E – Even, thin grEy discharge  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>B. Culture of the discharge – BV is a clinical/microscopic diagnosis; cultures are nonspecific and not recommended.<br /></br>C. Culture of the urine – Would evaluate UTI, unrelated to vaginal odor/discharge.<br /></br>D. Metronidazole therapy – Appropriate treatment once BV is confirmed, but guidelines (and test writers) expect an office diagnostic step first.<br /></br>E. Terconazole therapy – An imidazole antifungal for candidiasis, which presents with thick curd-like discharge and vulvar pruritus—features not seen here.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In a symptomatic woman with suspected BV, perform a saline wet mount to look for clue cells (part of Amsel criteria) before initiating metronidazole.</p>
178. A previously healthy 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 17-kg (37-lb) weight gain over the past year. During this period, menses have occurred at irregular intervals with light flow. She bruises easily. Her blood pressure is 152/96 mm Hg while sitting. Examination shows dark facial hair on the chin and lateral cheeks and a fat pad at the dorsal base of the neck. Abdominal examination shows violaceous striae and truncal obesity. Her extremities are thin. There are a few old bruises on the forearms and 1+ pitting edema at both ankles. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Measurement of serum ACTH concentration</br>b. Measurement of serum corticotropin-releasing hormone concentration</br>c. Measurement of 8:00 AM serum cortisol concentration</br>d. 24-Hour urinary collection for measurement of free cortisol</br>e. CT scan of the adrenal glands</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. 24-hour urinary collection for measurement of free cortisol  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Key Clues<br /></br>• Rapid 17-kg weight gain, truncal/centripetal obesity → classic for cortisol excess.<br /></br>• Hypertension, violaceous abdominal striae, easy bruising, thin extremities, “buffalo hump,” hirsutism, menstrual irregularity → textbook Cushing syndrome findings.<br /></br>• No exogenous glucocorticoid exposure is mentioned → evaluate for endogenous hypercortisolism.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Recognize the stigmata of Cushing syndrome.<br /></br>2. Remember: ALWAYS confirm cortisol excess biochemically before localizing the source.<br /></br>3. Three equivalent first-line screening tests:<br /></br>   • 24-h urinary free cortisol (UFC) ×2<br /></br>   • Late-night salivary cortisol ×2<br /></br>   • Overnight 1-mg low-dose dexamethasone suppression test<br /></br>4. If any screen is positive, THEN measure plasma ACTH to classify ACTH-dependent vs ACTH-independent disease.<br /></br>5. Imaging (pituitary MRI or adrenal CT) is the final step after biochemical confirmation &amp; ACTH interpretation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning for the Correct Choice<br /></br>• 24-h UFC directly measures the fraction of cortisol not bound to CBG (corticosteroid-binding globulin) and reflects total cortisol production over an entire circadian cycle, eliminating diurnal variation pitfalls.<br /></br>• It has high sensitivity and specificity for detecting sustained cortisol hypersecretion and is one of the three guideline-approved screening tests recommended by the Endocrine Society.<br /></br>• Thus, in a patient with classic Cushing phenotype, a 24-h UFC is an appropriate, guideline-concordant first step.  </p></br><p>Take-home: “Biochemical confirmation FIRST; 24-hour urinary free cortisol is a gold-standard screen.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Brief Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Chronic cortisol excess → protein catabolism (thin extremities, muscle wasting, bruising), lipolysis with redistribution (truncal obesity, dorsocervical fat pad, moon facies), mineralocorticoid receptor activation (hypertension, edema), androgen excess (hirsutism, menstrual changes), impaired collagen synthesis (purple striae).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Incorrect<br /></br>a. Serum ACTH concentration<br /></br>   • Appropriate only AFTER hypercortisolism is confirmed to determine ACTH-dependent vs ACTH-independent disease. Jumping to ACTH misses the crucial confirmation step and risks false positives/negatives.  </p></br><p>b. Serum corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) concentration<br /></br>   • CRH levels are not used in the standard diagnostic algorithm; CRH stimulation testing (not basal level) is occasionally used later to differentiate pituitary from ectopic ACTH sources. Not a screening test.  </p></br><p>c. Single 8 AM serum cortisol<br /></br>   • Cortisol has a diurnal rhythm; a single morning level can be normal even in Cushing syndrome. Poor sensitivity; not recommended as a stand-alone screen.  </p></br><p>e. CT scan of the adrenal glands<br /></br>   • Imaging is performed only after biochemical proof of hypercortisolism and determination of ACTH status; otherwise, incidentalomas lead to diagnostic confusion and unnecessary procedures.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid<br /></br>“UFC RAVENS” for Cushing Screen Options (need TWO abnormal):<br /></br>U – Urinary Free Cortisol (24 h)<br /></br>F – Fasting late-night Salivary cortisol<br /></br>C – Cortisol suppression by low-dose Dexamethasone (1 mg)  </p></br><p>If any “RAVENS” test abnormal ➜ ACTH level next.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Documentation Pearl<br /></br>• Assessment: “Suspected endogenous Cushing syndrome based on rapid weight gain, centripetal obesity, violaceous striae, hypertension, hirsutism.”<br /></br>• Plan: “Order 24-hour urinary free cortisol × 2 to confirm hypercortisolism; will proceed to plasma ACTH measurement if elevated.”  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Always establish biochemical evidence of cortisol excess (24-h UFC, late-night salivary cortisol, or low-dose dexamethasone test) before chasing the source.</p>
179. A 62-year-old man with alcoholism has blurred vision 3 weeks after treatment of an intra-abdominal abscess secondary to diverticulitis with perforation. He underwent diverting colostomy, colon resection, and abscess drainage. His postoperative course has been complicated by three episodes of sepsis successfully treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics. He is tolerating a regular diet. His temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 85/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Funduscopic examination shows patchy white infiltrates bilaterally and no papilledema. The remainder of the neurologic examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s visual problems?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Alcohol withdrawal</br>b. Brain abscess</br>c. Candidal ophthalmitis</br>d. Vitamin B1 (thiamine) deficiency</br>e. Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: c. Candidal ophthalmitis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Timeline &amp; Setting<br /></br>   • 3 weeks after major abdominal surgery with multiple episodes of postoperative sepsis → high risk for prolonged candidemia (IV lines, broad-spectrum antibiotics, bowel perforation).<br /></br>2. Key ocular finding<br /></br>   • “Patchy white infiltrates bilaterally” on dilated fundus exam. In a septic patient this is classic for Candida chorioretinitis/endophthalmitis (“cotton-ball” or “fluffy white” lesions extending into the vitreous).<br /></br>3. Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No papilledema → argues against intracranial mass/abscess causing increased ICP.<br /></br>   • Normal overall neurologic exam → makes nutritional optic neuropathies less likely this acutely.<br /></br>4. Risk factors in the stem<br /></br>   • Broad-spectrum antibiotics (suppresses bacterial flora, favors yeast overgrowth).<br /></br>   • Indwelling catheters, TPN, intra-abdominal infection → all common sources of candidemia.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>“Post-op sepsis + new eye symptoms = think about hematogenous seeding of the retina.”<br /></br>Look for: immunosuppression, IV lines, antibiotics, abdominal source → Candida; viral (CMV) in AIDS; bacterial emboli in endocarditis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why choice C is right)<br /></br>Step 1: Identify systemic candidemia risk (broad antibiotics, lines, GI perforation).<br /></br>Step 2: Connect candidemia to classic fundus appearance (bilateral white chorioretinal/vitreous lesions).<br /></br>Step 3: Exclude intracranial or nutritional etiologies because there is isolated visual complaint with a characteristic retinal lesion, not global neurologic signs.<br /></br>Take-home: In any hospitalized patient with candidemia, a screening dilated eye exam is recommended to look for Candida endophthalmitis.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>• Visual acuity, ocular pain, photophobia.<br /></br>• Description, number, and location of retinal lesions.<br /></br>• Blood culture results, current/previous antifungals.<br /></br>• Plan for systemic (IV echinocandin or amphotericin B) ± intravitreal therapy and ophthalmology consult.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Candida species enter bloodstream → seed highly vascular choroid → invade retina → “cotton-ball” aggregates protruding into vitreous (“fungus balls”).<br /></br>• Bilaterality common because spread is hematogenous. Without treatment can progress to pan-ophthalmitis and permanent vision loss.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>First-line therapy (non-neutropenic):<br /></br>• IV echinocandin (e.g., micafungin) OR amphotericin B ± flucytosine for ≥6 weeks AND<br /></br>• Intravitreal antifungal injection if vitritis present; vitrectomy for severe cases.<br /></br>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Dilated fundoscopy is diagnostic; ocular ultrasound if view is obscured.<br /></br>• Always obtain repeat blood cultures to ensure clearance of candidemia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonics / Memory Aid<br /></br>“WHITE CANDles in the VITreous”: WHITE retinal patches = CANDidal endophthalmitis seeding the VITreous in patients with IV lines or Intra-abdominal infections.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Alcohol withdrawal<br /></br>   • Occurs 6–72 h after last drink, presents with tremor, agitation, seizures, hallucinosis—not isolated blurred vision weeks later. No characteristic retinal lesions.  </p></br><p>b. Brain abscess<br /></br>   • Would cause headache, fever, focal neurologic deficits, possibly papilledema or visual field cuts from mass effect—not discrete retinal infiltrates.  </p></br><p>d. Vitamin B1 (thiamine) deficiency<br /></br>   • Wernicke encephalopathy triad: ophthalmoplegia/nystagmus, ataxia, confusion. Retinal infiltrates are NOT a feature. Fundus often normal or may show optic disc edema but not patchy white lesions.  </p></br><p>e. Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency<br /></br>   • Causes subacute combined degeneration → paresthesias, ataxia, anemia; optic neuropathy is rare and presents with central scotoma, not chorioretinal infiltrates. Develops over months-years, not weeks.  </p></br><p>Common trap: seeing “alcoholism” and reflexively choosing a vitamin deficiency. Always match the ocular finding (white infiltrates) with the systemic context (candidemia).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core takeaway<br /></br>Post-operative or ICU patient + candidemia risk factors + bilateral fluffy white retinal/vitreous lesions = Candida chorioretinal/endophthalmitis → start systemic and intravitreal antifungals promptly.</p>
180. A study is conducted to assess the prevalence of cataracts in all active United States Navy personnel compared with all active Army personnel. The medical records of all active Navy and Army personnel are reviewed for the diagnosis of cataracts. It is most appropriate to calculate which of the following to describe the data obtained in this study?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Prevalence ratio</br>b. Prevalence ratio ± standard deviation</br>c. Prevalence ratio ± standard error</br>d. Prevalence ratio ± 90 percent confidence interval (CI)</br>e. Prevalence ratio ± 99 percent CI</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Prevalence ratio  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases that drive the decision:<br /></br>• “All active United States Navy personnel” and “all active Army personnel” – this is a true census, not a sample.<br /></br>• “Medical records … are reviewed for the diagnosis of cataracts” – single point in time → cross-sectional → prevalence, not incidence.<br /></br>• Goal: “assess the prevalence of cataracts … compared with …” – we need a measure that directly compares the two prevalences.  </p></br><p>Approach framework for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Determine whether the study is incidence (new cases) or prevalence (existing cases).<br /></br>2. Decide if the investigators drew a sample (⇒ must estimate population parameters → need standard error/CI) or reviewed the whole population (⇒ descriptive only).<br /></br>3. Pick the simplest statistic that answers the research question without unnecessary inferential add-ons.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Statistical Reasoning)  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Calculate cataract prevalence separately in Navy and Army = (# with cataracts)/(total in branch).<br /></br>2. Compare those two proportions with a prevalence ratio (PR) = prevalence in Navy ÷ prevalence in Army (or vice-versa).<br /></br>3. Because the study encompasses the entire population of interest, the prevalence ratio itself completely describes the comparison; there is no sampling variability to estimate.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway:<br /></br>When you have a census of the target population, you report a point estimate only. Confidence intervals (CI), standard deviation (SD), and standard error (SE) quantify uncertainty due to sampling and are unnecessary if no sampling occurred.  </p></br><p>What would be documented in the chart/article:<br /></br>“Among all 335,000 active Navy personnel, cataract prevalence was 0.6%. Among all 472,000 active Army personnel, prevalence was 0.4%. Prevalence ratio = 1.5.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise, because pathophys isn’t being tested)  </p></br><p>Cataract = lens opacification → painless progressive vision loss. Risk factors include age, diabetes, corticosteroids, UV light, radiation, smoking, and trauma. The study simply quantifies its occurrence in two military populations.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments / Diagnostics Review  </p></br><p>Not applicable to the statistical concept being tested.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Census? Then no ± nonsense.”<br /></br>– If you study the whole population (census), just report the statistic.<br /></br>– If you study a sample, you must attach an SE or CI.  </p></br><p>Think “PR = Point estimate when Population is Reviewed.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Prevalence ratio ± standard deviation<br /></br>   • SD describes variability of individual measurements (prevalence is a proportion, not a continuous variable) and refers to within-sample variation, irrelevant here.  </p></br><p>c. Prevalence ratio ± standard error<br /></br>   • SE reflects sampling error. With a complete population review, there is no sampling error to estimate.  </p></br><p>d. Prevalence ratio ± 90% CI<br /></br>e. Prevalence ratio ± 99% CI<br /></br>   • Confidence intervals quantify uncertainty around an estimate derived from a sample; they are unnecessary and, strictly speaking, meaningless when the parameter for the entire population is known.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: automatically attaching CIs/SEs to every reported statistic without asking whether the data came from a sample. Always check if the investigators examined a subset or the full population.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Because the investigators reviewed every active-duty Navy and Army member (a census) at one point in time, the appropriate descriptive statistic is simply the prevalence ratio—no measure of variability is required.</p>
181. A study is conducted to compare the prevalence of dental caries in a community that drinks fluoridated water (Community A) with that in a community that drinks nonfluoridated water (Community B). Two thousand children ranging in age from 4 to 12 years are observed for 1 year. Subjects in the two community study groups are matched by age, gender, socioeconomic status, and dental insurance coverage. The results show:</br>                     Community A   Community B   Total</br>Number of Children with Caries    200            500           700</br>Number of Children without Caries 800            500          1300</br>Total                             1000           1000         2000</br>Which of the following is the most accurate interpretation of these results?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. The results are invalid because of a randomization error</br>b. The results are invalid because of selection bias</br>c. The risk for development of dental caries in Community B is 1.6 times greater than that in Community A</br>d. The risk for development of dental caries in Community B is four times greater than that in Community A</br>e. The risk for development of dental caries was reduced by 40 percent in Community A compared to Community B</br>f. The risk for development of dental caries was reduced by 60 percent in Community A compared to Community B</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>f. The risk for development of dental caries was reduced by 60 percent in Community A compared to Community B  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key numbers:<br /></br>  – Community A: 200/1000 children developed caries (risk = 0.20)<br /></br>  – Community B: 500/1000 children developed caries (risk = 0.50)<br /></br>• Design clues: prospective, 1-year follow-up, cohorts were matched on major confounders (age, gender, SES, insurance).<br /></br>• Testing concept: how to calculate relative risk (RR) and relative risk reduction (RRR).</p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Calculate the incidence (risk) in each exposure group.<br /></br>2. Compute the RR = riskexposed / riskunexposed.<br /></br>3. Derive RRR = 1 – RR (or, if phrased the other way, RRreduction = 1 – RR).<br /></br>4. Translate the number into the wording of the answer choice.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Risk in Community A (fluoridated water) = 200 ÷ 1000 = 0.20.<br /></br>2. Risk in Community B (non-fluoridated)      = 500 ÷ 1000 = 0.50.<br /></br>3. Relative risk (RR) of caries in A vs B      = 0.20 ÷ 0.50 = 0.40.<br /></br>4. Relative risk reduction (RRR) = 1 – 0.40 = 0.60, i.e., a 60 % reduction.<br /></br>The wording of choice f matches this exactly.</p></br><p>Take-away message: Fluoridated water decreases caries risk by 60 % compared with non-fluoridated water (RR = 0.4).</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In real practice you would record the incidence of caries, exposure status (fluoridation), matching variables, and calculated RR with CI.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)<br /></br>Fluoride incorporates into enamel as fluorapatite, increasing acid resistance and inhibiting cariogenic bacterial metabolism—hence lower new-caries incidence where fluoride exposure is higher.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>Not applicable beyond noting that community water fluoridation is a proven preventive public-health intervention; no lab tests required.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“RRR = 1 – RR” — think “1 minus the Fraction.”<br /></br>Visual: Imagine a pie where Community B eats half (0.5) and Community A eats only a fifth (0.2); A “leaves” 60 % of the pie untouched → 60 % reduction.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Randomization error: The study is observational with matched cohorts, not randomized. Lack of randomization does not automatically invalidate results.<br /></br>b. Selection bias: Matching on major variables mitigates (doesn’t eliminate) selection bias; nothing in the vignette indicates flawed selection severe enough to invalidate results.<br /></br>c. “Risk … 1.6 times greater”: RR of B vs A = 0.50/0.20 = 2.5, not 1.6.<br /></br>d. “Risk … four times greater”: Overstates the effect; correct multiplier is 2.5.<br /></br>e. “Risk … reduced by 40 percent”: Confuses RR (0.4) with RRR (0.6). A 40 % relative risk means a 60 % relative risk reduction.  </p></br><p>Common trap: Mixing up relative risk (0.4) with risk reduction (1 – 0.4 = 0.6).</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Calculate RR first (0.4), then subtract from 1 to get the relative reduction (0.6 = 60 %). Fluoridated water cuts caries incidence by about two-thirds compared with non-fluoridated water.</p>
182. A case-control study is conducted to determine if the relationship between estrogen replacement therapy (ERT) and the risk for breast cancer is affected by estrogen formulation. Cases consist of 705 women with a history of breast cancer; controls consist of 692 randomly selected, age-matched women. All participants range in age from 55 to 74 years. Researchers record the following ERT data from participants and stratify the data accordingly: cumulative dosage, type of formulation, and duration of treatment. Researchers also record patient height and weight, family history of breast cancer, and reproductive history. Results show that the type of estrogen formulation does not affect the risk for breast cancer. Which of the following raises the most concern regarding the validity of this conclusion?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Inadequate number of controls</br>b. Inclusion of premenopausal women</br>c. Lack of matching on cumulative dosage</br>d. Patient recall bias</br>e. Pharmacologic differences among estrogen products</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Patient recall bias  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Study design: retrospective case-control. Exposure (ERT formulation, dose, duration) is ascertained after subjects have already developed or not developed breast cancer.<br /></br>• Ages 55-74 y → post-menopausal; avoids confounding from endogenous cycling estrogens.<br /></br>• Key outcome: “type of estrogen formulation does not affect risk.”<br /></br>• How was exposure measured? Almost certainly by patient interview or self-report because it involves historical prescription data across many years in two large community samples. This immediately raises concern for differential misclassification—cases (women with breast cancer) are generally far more motivated to recall, research, or even over-report prior hormone exposures than controls. That classic differential error is recall bias.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Identify study type.<br /></br>2. Ask: is the exposure measured prospectively or retrospectively? If retrospective, suspect information (recall) bias.<br /></br>3. Ask: are there obvious unmatched confounders? Here age was matched; dose &amp; duration were stratified.<br /></br>4. Ask: will any bias be differential between cases and controls? If yes, big threat to validity.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Core concept: In case-control studies, the major threat is information bias—especially recall bias—because exposure is collected after outcome status is known.<br /></br>• If cases remember (or even exaggerate) the exact formulation they used while controls forget, exposure misclassification differs by outcome group. This can either inflate or attenuate associations and could easily lead to the erroneous conclusion that formulation has no effect when in fact it does.<br /></br>• In a real patient-care scenario, documentation would include how medication history was verified (eg, pharmacy records) to mitigate this bias.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)<br /></br>• Exogenous estrogen can promote breast tissue proliferation. Formulation differences (conjugated equine estrogen vs 17-β-estradiol vs transdermal, etc.) may theoretically alter risk due to varying hepatic first-pass stimulation of estrogen-responsive genes. Hence accurate exposure classification is critical.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>(Not directly tested; key is research-methods.) The “treatment” here is ERT; gold-standard exposure ascertainment would be pharmacy records or electronic prescribing data, not patient memory.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>RECALL bias = Retrospective Exposure Ascertained after Case outcome Leading to Lopsided memory.<br /></br>“Case thinks harder, Control can’t recall.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Inadequate number of controls<br /></br>• 705 cases vs 692 controls is nearly a 1:1 ratio—ample for power; numbers themselves don’t bias validity if selection is proper.  </p></br><p>B. Inclusion of premenopausal women<br /></br>• Ages 55-74 are overwhelmingly postmenopausal; this was explicitly controlled, so not a threat.  </p></br><p>C. Lack of matching on cumulative dosage<br /></br>• Investigators “record the following ERT data … cumulative dosage.” They can stratify or adjust for dose in analysis. Lack of matching is acceptable if adjusted statistically; recall bias is still larger threat.  </p></br><p>E. Pharmacologic differences among estrogen products<br /></br>• Those differences are precisely what the study seeks to evaluate. As long as formulation is correctly classified, pharmacologic diversity doesn’t threaten internal validity; it’s a real-world feature, not a bias.  </p></br><p>Take-home Message<br /></br>In retrospective case-control studies, differential recall of past exposures by cases versus controls (recall bias) is the single most common and serious threat to internal validity. Always ask: “Who is reporting the exposure, and when?”</p>
183. Studies of a new intravenous fluid that acts as a plasma expander have shown positive results in laboratory animals. An emergency department (ED) physician believes that the new fluid could save human lives and wants to research its effects on critical trauma patients who require urgent care in the ED. Because the most critical patients are often unable to provide informed consent, which of the following best describes how the ED physician should proceed with his research?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Acquire a court injunction to proceed with research</br>b. Apply to the institutional review board for approval</br>c. Continue without informed consent</br>d. Obtain informed consent from a critical patient’s family member</br>e. Use the new fluid only in patients who can provide informed consent</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Apply to the institutional review board (IRB) for approval  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “new intravenous fluid,” “research,” “critical trauma patients,” “unable to provide informed consent.”<br /></br>• The project is clearly experimental (a product not yet approved for routine clinical use) and involves vulnerable patients (critically ill, decision-ally incapacitated).<br /></br>• Any intervention performed primarily to generate generalizable knowledge = human subjects research → triggers federal regulations (45 CFR 46, “Common Rule”) and FDA rules (21 CFR 50/56).<br /></br>• Investigators must obtain prospective IRB review to assure patient safety, scientific merit, and appropriate consent/waiver procedures.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: The moment you see “new drug/device/biologic” + “humans” + “can’t consent,” think “IRB first.” Only an IRB can grant permission to waive or modify informed-consent requirements.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify that this is research, not standard emergency therapy.<br /></br>2. Ask: “Who has the authority to permit a consent waiver?” → Only an IRB, never the investigator alone.<br /></br>3. IRB will decide whether the study meets criteria for “exception from informed consent for emergency research” (EICER):<br /></br>   • Life-threatening condition,<br /></br>   • Existing therapies unproven or unsatisfactory,<br /></br>   • Informed consent not feasible,<br /></br>   • Potential direct benefit to subjects,<br /></br>   • Community consultation &amp; public disclosure plans in place, etc.<br /></br>4. Therefore the physician’s next step is to submit a protocol to the IRB.</p></br><p>Take-home message: No matter how promising an intervention seems, investigators must obtain IRB approval before enrolling incapacitated patients in research; the IRB is the gatekeeper for any consent waiver.  </p></br><p>Documentation if real case: Full research protocol, risk/benefit analysis, data-safety–monitoring plan, consent-waiver justification, community-consultation plan.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield tie-in)<br /></br>• Plasma expanders (e.g., albumin, starches, dextrans) aim to increase intravascular oncotic pressure, improve preload, and avoid crystalloid overload in hemorrhagic shock.<br /></br>• Risks include anaphylaxis, coagulopathy with some colloids, kidney injury. Rigorous evaluation is required before routine use.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>Not directly tested here, but remember the standard resuscitation algorithm for trauma (ABC, balanced blood component therapy, permissive hypotension in some cases). Any new fluid must be studied under controlled conditions after IRB approval.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“R-U-S-H to the IRB”:<br /></br>R = Research idea<br /></br>U = Unproven intervention<br /></br>S = Subjects can’t consent<br /></br>H = Head to the IRB</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Court injunction: Courts do not oversee routine research approval; they intervene mainly in legal disputes. Adds unnecessary legal burden and is not the standard pathway.<br /></br>C. Continue without informed consent: Violates federal regulations; only an IRB can grant an emergency consent waiver. Investigator-initiated waiver is unethical and illegal.<br /></br>D. Obtain consent from family member: Surrogate consent is desirable, but the question states that patients are “often unable” and family may not be immediately available. You still need IRB approval first; surrogate consent alone doesn’t satisfy regulatory requirements for emergency research.<br /></br>E. Use only in patients who can consent: Would systematically exclude the sickest patients, creating selection bias and defeating the purpose of studying life-saving potential in critical trauma. Still requires IRB oversight even if all subjects can consent.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>For any clinical research—especially in vulnerable, incapacitated patients—the first and non-negotiable step is IRB review and approval.</p>
184. A 25-year-old woman comes to the physician for an initial prenatal examination at 12 weeks’ gestation. She has schizoaffective disorder and had been taking oral haloperidol sporadically until 2 months ago. She says that she was impregnated by evil and that she needs to get rid of the “Devil’s baby.” She states that she hears “angelic voices telling me to get an abortion.” She says that she likes children and has two of her own but “obviously cannot have this one because it is evil!” She demands an abortion. Physical examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 12-week gestation. On mental status examination, she is agitated. In addition to admitting the patient to the hospital, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Defer the decision about abortion and observe the patient</br>b. Defer the decision about abortion and begin valproic acid</br>c. Defer the decision about abortion and resume haloperidol</br>d. Arrange for an abortion and begin valproic acid</br>e. Arrange for an abortion and resume haloperidol</br>f. Arrange for an abortion and resume haloperidol after the abortion</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Defer the decision about abortion and resume haloperidol</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases that raise concern for loss of decision-making capacity<br /></br>   • “Impregnated by evil,” “Devil’s baby,” “angelic voices telling me to get an abortion” → fixed delusions + command auditory hallucinations.<br /></br>   • Agitated on exam → active psychosis.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pregnancy facts<br /></br>   • 12 weeks gestation (1st trimester).<br /></br>   • Off antipsychotic for 2 months (“sporadically” prior) → relapse of schizoaffective disorder.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Safety issues<br /></br>   • Command hallucinations to harm fetus → danger to fetus &amp;
