possibly to herself.<br /></br>   • Needs inpatient care (already decided in stem).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Pearls / Metacognition<br /></br>• First ask: “Does the patient have capacity?” If not, stabilize the acute psychiatric illness first.<br /></br>• For a pregnant psychotic patient: choose an antipsychotic that is safest in pregnancy (high-potency typicals such as haloperidol).<br /></br>• Never perform an irreversible procedure (abortion) on a patient who currently lacks capacity.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)</p></br><p>Step 1 – Determine capacity: Acute psychosis = lacks capacity.</p></br><p>Step 2 – Ensure safety: Hospitalize (already in stem) and start medication.</p></br><p>Step 3 – Drug choice:<br /></br>   • Typical antipsychotics (haloperidol) have the longest safety record in pregnancy; risk of extrapyramidal symptoms is far outweighed by benefit.<br /></br>   • Valproic acid is highly teratogenic (neural-tube, cardiac, craniofacial defects) and contraindicated in 1st trimester.</p></br><p>Step 4 – Re-address elective, irreversible decisions (abortion) only after psychosis resolves and capacity is restored.</p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>In pregnancy, treat acute psychosis with the safest effective antipsychotic (haloperidol) and delay elective irreversible procedures until capacity is regained.</p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real chart: capacity assessment result, rationale for involuntary admission, medication risks/benefits discussion with surrogate (if needed), plan to reassess decision about pregnancy termination once stabilized.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Schizoaffective disorder = baseline psychosis + mood episodes. Non-adherence commonly precipitates relapse.<br /></br>• Pregnancy itself does not protect against relapse; hormonal changes and medication cessation often worsen disease.<br /></br>• Untreated psychosis carries maternal risks (self-harm, poor prenatal care) and fetal risks (prematurity, growth restriction), so treatment is essential.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Considerations  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>First-line antipsychotics in pregnancy<br /></br>   • High-potency typicals (haloperidol)<br /></br>   • Some SGAs (e.g., olanzapine, risperidone) are acceptable but less long-term data.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Drugs to avoid<br /></br>   • Valproic acid (neural tube defects)<br /></br>   • Carbamazepine (NTD, craniofacial)<br /></br>   • Lithium (Ebstein anomaly, use only if necessary).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Monitoring<br /></br>   • Baseline CBC, CMP.<br /></br>   • Fetal US at 18–20 weeks for anatomic survey.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“HALO protects mom &amp; baby”<br /></br>H – Haloperidol<br /></br>A – Acute<br /></br>L – Labor (pregnancy) safe<br /></br>O – On hold elective procedures until psychosis gone</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Defer decision &amp; observe only – Fails to treat acute psychosis; patient/fetus remain at risk.  </p></br><p>B. Defer decision &amp; begin valproic acid – Treats mood, not psychosis; teratogenic in 1st trimester.  </p></br><p>D. Arrange abortion &amp; begin valproic acid – Two major errors: no capacity for consent + teratogen.  </p></br><p>E. Arrange abortion &amp; resume haloperidol – Abortion still performed without competent consent.  </p></br><p>F. Arrange abortion &amp; resume haloperidol after abortion – Same capacity issue; delays needed treatment.  </p></br><p>Key difference: All “arrange abortion” options ignore lack of capacity. The valproic acid options ignore teratogenicity. “Observe only” ignores need for treatment of active psychosis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Active psychosis = no decision-making capacity. Hospitalize, restart a pregnancy-safe antipsychotic (haloperidol), and postpone any elective irreversible procedure until capacity returns.</p>
185. A 47-year-old woman with a 1-month history of rheumatoid arthritis comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. She has had moderate fatigue and morning joint stiffness. Current medications include ibuprofen and prednisone. There is swelling, tenderness, and decreased range of motion of the wrist and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Test of the stool for occult blood is negative. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit 31 percent</br>Mean corpuscular volume 85 µm3</br>Serum</br>Iron 55 µg/dL</br>Total iron-binding capacity 200 µg/dL (N=250-400)</br>X-rays of the hands show bony erosions of the proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: I</br></br>a. Analgesic therapy</br>b. Blood transfusion</br>c. Discontinuation of drug therapy</br>d. Erythropoietin therapy</br>e. Exchange transfusion</br>f. Folic acid supplementation</br>g. Hydroxyurea therapy</br>h. Iron supplementation</br>i. Methotrexate therapy</br>j. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin) supplementation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>i. Methotrexate therapy (initiation of a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug, DMARD)</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 47-year-old woman with newly diagnosed rheumatoid arthritis (RA) (morning stiffness, symmetric PIP/wrist involvement, erosions on x-ray).<br /></br>  – Active inflammation despite NSAID + low-dose prednisone.<br /></br>  – Normocytic anemia (Hct 31%, MCV 85).<br /></br>  – Iron studies: low serum iron, low TIBC → typical of anemia of chronic disease (ACD), not iron deficiency (which would have ↑TIBC).<br /></br>  – Negative stool guiac (rules out GI blood loss).</p></br><p>• Pertinent positives: active joint disease + bony erosions → needs aggressive therapy.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no evidence of occult GI bleed; MCV not low → favors ACD over iron deficiency.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Identify the primary problem to address (ongoing RA activity with joint damage).<br /></br>2. Recognize the anemia pattern (ACD) as a manifestation of uncontrolled inflammation rather than a separate hematologic deficiency.<br /></br>3. Recall treatment algorithm: NSAID/steroids for symptom control, DMARD (eg, methotrexate) as next step to prevent further joint destruction.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• RA management goals: relieve symptoms + halt structural damage early.<br /></br>• DMARDs are started as soon as persistent RA is confirmed; methotrexate is first-line because of proven efficacy, weekly dosing, and survival benefit.<br /></br>• Controlling inflammation (DMARD) also treats ACD by reducing IL-6–mediated hepcidin up-regulation that traps iron in macrophages.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In RA, persistent synovitis or radiographic erosions mandate initiation of a DMARD—most commonly methotrexate—regardless of mild normocytic anemia.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient): disease activity score, functional status, joint count, plan to start methotrexate, baseline labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs), folic acid co-prescription, contraception counseling.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Rheumatoid arthritis: autoimmune synovitis → pannus formation → cartilage/bone erosion.<br /></br>ACD: chronic inflammation → ↑IL-6 → ↑hepcidin → ↓iron release/TIBC, normocytic (or microcytic) anemia.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Initiate methotrexate 7.5–25 mg weekly + daily folic acid 1 mg to limit stomatitis/hepatic toxicity.<br /></br>2. Baseline and periodic CBC &amp; LFTs.<br /></br>3. Continue NSAID/low-dose prednisone as bridge therapy until DMARD takes effect (6–8 weeks).<br /></br>4. Escalate or add biologic (e.g., TNF-α inhibitor) if inadequate response.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“METhotrexate METhods to stop Erosions”: If you see RA + Erosions → Jump to MeThotrexate.<br /></br>ACD pattern: “Low-Low-Normal/High” (Low iron, Low TIBC, Normal/High ferritin).</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Analgesic therapy – NSAIDs relieve pain but do not halt progression or correct ACD.<br /></br>b. Blood transfusion – reserved for symptomatic anemia (Hgb &lt;7–8 g/dL) or acute loss; does not address cause.<br /></br>c. Discontinuation of drug therapy – would worsen RA.<br /></br>d. Erythropoietin therapy – ACD responds better to treating inflammation; expensive and unnecessary here.<br /></br>e. Exchange transfusion – used for severe crises (e.g., sickle cell) not RA.<br /></br>f. Folic acid supplementation – useful only if methotrexate already started or folate deficiency (would show macrocytosis).<br /></br>g. Hydroxyurea therapy – cytoreductive for myeloproliferative disorders, not RA.<br /></br>h. Iron supplementation – TIBC low, ferritin likely high; iron would be sequestered and ineffective.<br /></br>j. Vitamin B12 supplementation – MCV normal; no neuro signs, no macrocytosis.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Early methotrexate is the standard next step for active, erosive rheumatoid arthritis; it also indirectly improves the associated anemia of chronic disease.</p>
186. A 27-year-old woman who received the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis 8 months ago comes to the physician because of severe fatigue during the past 2 months. She currently experiences double vision and vertigo once or twice weekly, but her most problematic symptom is persistent fatigue. She says that there has been no change in her sleep pattern. She eats five servings of vegetables daily and drinks one glass of wine in the evening. She exercises three times weekly; her regimen consists of 30 minutes of running outdoors. She notes that it has been a particularly hot summer and says that the heat bothers her more than it used to. She has no other history of serious illness. Current medications include interferon beta and a multivitamin. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation to decrease this patient's fatigue?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Avoid outdoor activity on hot days</br>b. Discontinue alcohol consumption</br>c. Increase the frequency of exercise</br>d. Switch to a high-protein/low-carbohydrate diet</br>e. Increase the dosage of interferon beta</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Avoid outdoor activity on hot days  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “multiple sclerosis,” “severe fatigue,” “particularly hot summer,” “heat bothers her more than it used to,” stable sleep/exercise/alcohol, normal exam.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: History of MS; reports heat sensitivity; no other medical contributors (sleep apnea, depression, anemia, infection) suggested.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: Normal neurologic exam (no relapse); no medication side-effects (e.g., anticholinergics, sedatives); no lifestyle change except ambient temperature.<br /></br>Pattern recognition: MS + fatigue that is disproportionately worse with heat → Uhthoff phenomenon (transient conduction block in demyelinated axons precipitated by ↑ body temperature).  </p></br><p>Cognitive strategy for future questions:<br /></br>1. Always screen for secondary causes of MS fatigue (meds, depression, sleep problems) → none here.<br /></br>2. Look for environmental precipitants; “heat intolerance” is classic.<br /></br>3. If Uhthoff suspected, first-line = cooling/heat-avoidance (air-conditioning, cooling vest, swim instead of run). Disease-modifying therapy adjustment is not the answer to symptomatic heat-induced fatigue.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Fatigue is common in MS; can be primary, secondary, medication related, or Uhthoff.<br /></br>2. History reveals clear temporal association with hot weather, not with exertion itself, medications, or alcohol.<br /></br>3. Physical exam is stable → not a relapse, so steroids or DMT escalation unnecessary.<br /></br>4. Therefore, symptomatic management aimed at minimizing heat exposure is most appropriate.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In MS, heat (fever, hot baths, summer weather) can transiently worsen conduction in demyelinated fibers, producing fatigue or neurologic blurring; cooling strategies are first-line.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real clinic): Document fatigue severity, triggers (heat), absence of relapse signs, patient education on cooling, plan for occupational/physical therapy if needed.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• MS causes patchy CNS demyelination → slows saltatory conduction.<br /></br>• Temperature rise (~0.5–1°C) further impairs conduction → transient symptoms (Uhthoff).<br /></br>• Cooling lowers membrane resistance, improving conduction velocity.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Review<br /></br>Symptomatic fatigue management in MS:<br /></br>1. NON-PHARM: Cooling (avoid heat, cooling vests, A/C, morning/evening exercise, swimming).<br /></br>2. PHARM if persistent: Amantadine, modafinil, methylphenidate; treat comorbid depression or sleep disorders.<br /></br>Diagnostic tests unnecessary when classic history and no new deficits.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“MS gets in a hot mess” → Heat → Messed-up conduction → fatigue/blurred vision.<br /></br>Think “Uh-HOT-off” (Uhthoff) = HOT triggers OFF conduction.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>B. Discontinue alcohol consumption<br /></br>• One glass of wine nightly rarely causes debilitating fatigue; no heat relationship.  </p></br><p>C. Increase the frequency of exercise<br /></br>• Exercise is good but outdoor running in the heat would worsen Uhthoff fatigue; benefit only if done in cool environment—first teach heat avoidance.  </p></br><p>D. Switch to a high-protein/low-carbohydrate diet<br /></br>• No evidence diet modification alleviates MS-related fatigue; not linked to heat sensitivity.  </p></br><p>E. Increase the dosage of interferon beta<br /></br>• Interferon beta is disease-modifying, not for symptomatic fatigue; higher dose does not treat heat-induced fatigue and may increase flu-like side effects (worsening fatigue!).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In MS, new or worsening fatigue precipitated by heat is classic Uhthoff phenomenon. The simplest and most effective first step is to minimize heat exposure—avoid outdoor activity on hot days and employ cooling strategies.</p>
187. A 22-month-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 2-week history of difficulty sleeping. His parents note that each night he awakens screaming 1 to 2 hours after falling asleep and cannot be consoled. During these episodes, he sits or stands in the crib with his eyes open but does not focus his eyes on them. After 10 minutes, the episode resolves, and he falls asleep. He has asthma treated with nebulized albuterol. His maternal cousin has benign rolandic epilepsy of childhood. The patient is above the 95th percentile for head circumference. Examination shows a prominent forehead. The anterior fontanelle cannot be palpated. EEG shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Reassurance</br>b. Add acetazolamide to the medication regimen</br>c. Add carbamazepine to the medication regimen</br>d. Add propranolol to the medication regimen</br>e. Discontinue albuterol therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Reassurance  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Key phrases that point to the diagnosis<br /></br>   • “Awakens screaming 1–2 h after falling asleep” – first third of the night = deep N3 (slow-wave) sleep.<br /></br>   • “Cannot be consoled … eyes open but does not focus … no recall afterward” – classic description of NON-REM parasomnia (sleep terror).<br /></br>   • Duration 5–15 min, goes back to sleep spontaneously.  </li></br><li>Pertinent negatives that rule out other entities<br /></br>   • No tonic-clonic movements, no loss of bladder/bowel ⇒ makes epileptic seizures unlikely.<br /></br>   • EEG normal.<br /></br>   • No morning headache, vomiting, papilledema ⇒ argues against ↑ICP.<br /></br>   • Albuterol is being used only PRN for asthma—β-agonists may cause jitteriness but do not produce stereotyped, amnestic episodes confined to stage N3 sleep.  </li></br><li>Red‐herring information and how to handle it<br /></br>   • Macrocephaly + prominent forehead – often benign/familial in toddlers; anterior fontanelle closed (normal by 18–24 mo). While it grabs attention, there is no clinical or imaging evidence of hydrocephalus ⇒ don’t chase it.<br /></br>   • “Maternal cousin has benign rolandic epilepsy” – family history is noise; focus on the child’s presentation + normal EEG.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: For any pediatric night-time event first decide REM vs NON-REM vs seizure. Timing (first third), autonomic arousal, inconsolability, no memory = sleep terror → reassurance.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Identify episode timing (1–2 h after sleep onset).<br /></br>2. Determine state (non-REM N3).<br /></br>3. Look for autonomic signs + absent responsiveness.<br /></br>4. Check for post-ictal phenomena or morning recall (none).<br /></br>5. Confirm normal neuro exam/EEG ⇒ parasomnia, not epilepsy.<br /></br>6. Standard management: reassurance, sleep hygiene; optional “scheduled awakenings” if frequent.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Night terrors are benign, self-limited NON-REM parasomnias in toddlers – treat with reassurance unless safety risk.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): Character and timing of episodes, absence of daytime seizure activity, counseling provided, plan for routine follow-up.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Night terrors<br /></br>• Occur during slow-wave sleep (stage N3) in first third of night.<br /></br>• Immature CNS arousal pathways → partial arousal with autonomic discharge.<br /></br>• Children are amnestic because cortical awakening does not occur.<br /></br>• Usually resolve by age 6–7 y as sleep architecture matures.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: sleep deprivation, fever, stress.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>First-line:<br /></br>• Parental reassurance and education.<br /></br>• Optimize sleep hygiene (regular bedtime, avoid caffeine).<br /></br>• If recurrent/severe → scheduled awakenings 15 min before typical event time.  </p></br><p>Pharmacologic therapy (benzodiazepines) is rare and reserved for extreme, injurious cases. No labs or imaging are required when the history is classic and neuro exam is normal.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“3-S NIGHT TERROR” mnemonic<br /></br>S – Stage 3 (N3)<br /></br>S – Screaming/Sympathetic surge<br /></br>S – Self-limited (minutes)<br /></br>Nighttime – first third of night<br /></br>T – Toddler<br /></br>E – Eyes open but empty stare<br /></br>R – Recall absent in morning<br /></br>R – Reassure parents  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Add acetazolamide – Used for idiopathic intracranial hypertension; patient lacks symptoms/signs of ↑ICP (no papilledema, vomiting, downward gaze palsy). Macrocephaly alone is not an indication.  </p></br><p>C. Add carbamazepine – Appropriate for focal seizures (e.g., benign rolandic epilepsy). Night terrors have normal EEG, no motor focality, occur exclusively in N3 sleep, and resolve spontaneously.  </p></br><p>D. Add propranolol – Utilized for essential tremor, infantile hemangioma, thyroid storm, migraine prophylaxis; no role in parasomnias.  </p></br><p>E. Discontinue albuterol – β-agonists may cause tremor or insomnia, but they do not create stereotyped autonomic episodes with amnesia 1–2 h after sleep onset. Keeping asthma controlled outweighs theoretical sleep disturbance.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>A toddler who screams and is inconsolable 1–2 hours after falling asleep with no recall the next day is experiencing sleep (night) terrors. Provide reassurance and sleep-hygiene advice—no medications or further work-up needed.</p>
188. A 47-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of a lesion on his forehead. The affected area is not painful or itchy. Three years ago, he underwent a renal transplant for chronic renal failure secondary to IgA nephropathy. Examination of the left forehead shows a 2 x 0.5-cm, hyperkeratotic, erythematous, crusted plaque with areas of ulceration. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Actinic keratosis</br>b. Contact dermatitis</br>c. Lichen planus</br>d. Molluscum contagiosum</br>e. Squamous cell carcinoma</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC)</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 47-year-old man with a single, slowly enlarging lesion for 6 months<br /></br>• Hyperkeratotic, erythematous, crusted plaque with focal ulceration<br /></br>• Painless and non-pruritic<br /></br>• History of solid-organ (renal) transplant → chronic immunosuppression  </p></br><p>How these clues narrow the diagnosis<br /></br>1. Morphology: Hyperkeratosis + crust + ulceration on sun-exposed skin = classic description of cutaneous SCC.<br /></br>2. Time course: Gradual growth over months (malignant, not inflammatory).<br /></br>3. Risk factors: UV exposure, fair skin, AND strongest acquired risk = chronic immunosuppression in solid-organ transplant recipients (65- to 200-fold ↑ incidence of SCC vs general population).<br /></br>4. Absence of pruritus or vesicles argues against inflammatory dermatoses (contact dermatitis, lichen planus).<br /></br>5. Size (2 cm) and ulceration exceed typical actinic keratosis (&lt;1 cm, no deep ulceration).</p></br><p>Test-taking framework<br /></br>• Step 1: Ask, “Is this malignant, infectious, or inflammatory?”<br /></br>• Step 2: Malignancy? → look for chronicity, enlargement, ulceration, risk factors (sun, immunosuppression).<br /></br>• Result: SCC pattern recognized.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why SCC is correct<br /></br>• SCC = malignant proliferation of keratinocytes in epidermis → hyperkeratotic or ulcerated nodules/plaques.<br /></br>• Transplant recipients receive calcineurin inhibitors ± azathioprine → impaired immune surveillance → aggressive SCC.<br /></br>• Documentation: size, borders, depth/ulceration, regional nodes. Punch or excisional biopsy next.</p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>“Solid-organ transplant + scaly/ulcerated plaque on sun-exposed site = think SCC first.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Etiology<br /></br>• Cumulative UV-B–induced DNA damage (pyrimidine dimers) + impaired p53 repair.<br /></br>• Additional carcinogens: chronic wounds, HPV, arsenic.<br /></br>• Immunosuppression removes T-cell tumor surveillance.</p></br><p>Clinical features<br /></br>• Firm, indurated, scaling or verrucous papule, plaque, or nodule.<br /></br>• May ulcerate or bleed (“non-healing ulcer”).<br /></br>• Common on head/neck, dorsal hands, lower lip (actinic cheilitis).</p></br><p>Prognosis<br /></br>• Metastatic potential 3-5% overall, higher in immunosuppressed or lesions on ear/lip.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Full-thickness (punch or excisional) biopsy to confirm.<br /></br>2. Staging with imaging for high-risk lesions (depth &gt;4 mm, perineural invasion).</p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>First-line:<br /></br>• Surgical excision with 4-6 mm margins or Mohs micrographic surgery (best for face).<br /></br>Adjunct/alternatives (small, in-situ):<br /></br>• Curettage &amp; electrodesiccation, cryotherapy, topical 5-fluorouracil or imiquimod, photodynamic therapy.<br /></br>Prevention:<br /></br>• Broad-spectrum sunscreen, sun-protective clothing, regular dermatology follow-up in transplant recipients.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“S-TAR” for SCC risks:<br /></br>S – Sun (UV light)<br /></br>T – Transplant immunosuppression<br /></br>A – Arsenic/Actinic keratosis antecedent<br /></br>R – Radiotherapy &amp; chronic scars  </p></br><p>Visual mnemonic: Picture a “crusty red lighthouse (plaque) on the forehead beach” with a transplant kidney symbol shining an “S” beam.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Actinic keratosis<br /></br>• Pre-malignant, rough “sandpaper” papules &lt;1 cm, no deep ulceration. Would describe multiple lesions in sun-exposed areas. Size (2 cm) &amp; ulceration favor SCC.</p></br><p>b. Contact dermatitis<br /></br>• Intensely pruritic, erythematous papules/vesicles with weeping; linked to allergen exposure; resolves with avoidance—none of these present.</p></br><p>c. Lichen planus<br /></br>• Pruritic, purple, polygonal, planar papules on wrists, ankles, oral mucosa (Wickham striae). Lesion here is erythematous, crusted, non-pruritic.</p></br><p>d. Molluscum contagiosum<br /></br>• Umbilicated, pearly papules due to poxvirus, common in children or HIV; not hyperkeratotic or ulcerated plaques.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom line: In a chronically immunosuppressed transplant patient, a non-healing, scaly or ulcerated plaque on sun-exposed skin should be presumed to be squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise.</p>
189. A 62-year-old man comes for evaluation prior to undergoing total knee replacement. He has rheumatoid arthritis treated with prednisone. He has no known allergies. Examination shows pain with knee motion. Which of the following is the most appropriate management regarding prophylactic antibiotic therapy?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Parenteral cefazolin</br>b. Parenteral clindamycin</br>c. Parenteral penicillin and gentamicin</br>d. Parenteral vancomycin</br>e. No prophylactic antibiotics are indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. Parenteral cefazolin  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases &amp; clues<br /></br>• “Total knee replacement” → clean‐orthopedic procedure that involves prosthetic material; highest risk = skin flora (Staphylococcus aureus &amp; S. epidermidis).<br /></br>• “Rheumatoid arthritis treated with prednisone” → immunosuppressed; reinforces need for prophylaxis but does not change the drug of choice.<br /></br>• “No known allergies” → can safely receive a ß-lactam.  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: elective joint arthroplasty, no ß-lactam allergy.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no MRSA colonization, no history of anaphylaxis to penicillin/cephalosporin.  </p></br><p>Framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Identify surgical wound class (clean vs contaminated).<br /></br>2. Ask: Is prosthetic material implanted? (Yes → must cover skin flora.)<br /></br>3. Determine allergies or MRSA colonization.<br /></br>4. Choose agent:<br /></br>   • First-line = cefazolin (or cefuroxime) within 60 min pre-incision.<br /></br>   • Vancomycin or clindamycin only if MRSA colonized or severe ß-lactam allergy.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why cefazolin?<br /></br>• Excellent coverage of methicillin-susceptible S. aureus &amp; coagulase-negative staphylococci.<br /></br>• Long half-life, good bone penetration, inexpensive, narrow spectrum (limits resistance).<br /></br>• National Surgical Infection Prevention Project &amp; AAOS guidelines: give 2 g IV (3 g if ≥120 kg) within 60 min before incision; repeat dose if surgery &gt;4 h or blood loss &gt;1500 mL.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In clean orthopedic implant surgery, IV cefazolin is standard prophylaxis unless the patient is colonized with MRSA or has a severe ß-lactam allergy.  </p></br><p>Documentation note: “2 g IV cefazolin given 30 min pre-incision for surgical prophylaxis; no ß-lactam allergy.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Prosthetic joints can seed hematogenously; initial biofilm often originates peri-operatively from skin flora.<br /></br>• Biofilm-forming staphylococci adhere to metal/polyethylene surfaces; once established, eradication is difficult.<br /></br>• Preventive antibiotic achieves high tissue levels at time of skin incision to “coat” the wound before bacterial inoculation.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Pre-op labs/imaging: none required solely for antibiotic selection. Nasal MRSA screening may guide vancomycin choice in some institutions.<br /></br>Antibiotic algorithm for orthopedic prosthesis:<br /></br>1. Give cefazolin 1–2 g IV ≤60 min pre-incision.<br /></br>2. Redose every 4 h intra-op or with significant blood loss.<br /></br>3. Post-op doses generally not needed beyond 24 h.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Ortho C-Z” mnemonic:<br /></br>C = Clean implant surgery<br /></br>Z = “Zolin” (cefaZolin) first choice unless:<br /></br>  V = Vancomycin for MRSA+/severe allergy<br /></br>  C = Clindamycin if ß-lactam anaphylaxis  </p></br><p>Visual trick: Picture a shiny new knee (prosthesis) wearing a “C-shield” (cefazolin) to block staph sticking to it.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Parenteral clindamycin<br /></br>• Used only when there is a severe immediate hypersensitivity to ß-lactams. This patient has none.  </p></br><p>C. Parenteral penicillin and gentamicin<br /></br>• Targets gram-positive and gram-negative organisms in contaminated abdominal/urogenital surgery; unnecessarily broad &amp; nephrotoxic; not guideline-supported for clean orthopedic cases.  </p></br><p>D. Parenteral vancomycin<br /></br>• Reserved for documented MRSA colonization/infection or ß-lactam anaphylaxis. Overuse promotes resistance and nephrotoxicity.  </p></br><p>E. No prophylactic antibiotics<br /></br>• Prosthetic joint implantation is high-risk for deep infection; omission of prophylaxis increases periprosthetic infection rates 2- to 5-fold.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core concept: Clean orthopedic implant surgeries require pre-incision IV cefazolin in patients without ß-lactam allergy to prevent staphylococcal prosthetic joint infection.</p>
190. A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a lump in her left thigh since she fell on the ice while playing hockey 4 months ago. At that time, she noticed bruising on her left thigh that has resolved, but the lump has increased in size. She has not had fever, night sweats, or weight loss. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Examination shows a 5 × 6-cm, painless, firm, soft tissue mass on the anterolateral aspect of the left thigh. The overlying skin is intact, and there is no discoloration. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy. Distal pulses are normal, and sensation is intact. Range of motion of the hips and knees is full, and muscle strength is normal. X-rays of the left femur and knee show no abnormalities. An MRI of the left femur shows a deep soft tissue mass with inhomogeneous signal on T1- and T2-weighted images. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. CT scan of the thigh</br>b. Core-needle biopsy</br>c. Ultrasonography</br>d. Wide resection of the mass</br>e. Observation and reevaluation in 1 month</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Core-needle biopsy  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues:<br /></br>  – 42-year-old (adult; sarcomas most common in 40–60 yr).<br /></br>  – Mass is 5 × 6 cm (≥5 cm = “large” red flag).<br /></br>  – Deep to fascia (anterolateral thigh musculature) and painless.<br /></br>  – Growing over 4 months despite resolution of bruise (growth is malignant-sign).<br /></br>  – MRI already obtained shows inhomogeneous T1/T2 signal → necrosis/hemorrhage typical of sarcoma.<br /></br>  – No systemic symptoms but that does not exclude malignancy.  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: Any soft-tissue mass that is (1) &gt;5 cm, (2) deep, or (3) enlarging should be assumed malignant until proven otherwise → imaging (MRI) followed by tissue diagnosis.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Use the “5-5-Deep” rule—mass &gt;5 cm, present &gt;5 weeks, and/or deep to fascia → MRI → biopsy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach to a suspicious soft-tissue mass:<br /></br>1. Detailed history &amp; physical.<br /></br>2. Cross-sectional imaging (MRI is modality of choice for extremities).<br /></br>3. Tissue diagnosis before definitive therapy.<br /></br>Core-needle biopsy provides:<br /></br>• Adequate tissue for histology, cytogenetics, immunostaining.<br /></br>• Minimal morbidity.<br /></br>• Can be placed along the eventual resection path to avoid tumor seeding of new tissue planes.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Never proceed directly to wide excision of a suspected sarcoma without first confirming the diagnosis and obtaining grade/ subtype information that will dictate surgical margins ± neoadjuvant therapy.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): size, depth, neurovascular status, MRI findings, informed consent for biopsy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Soft-tissue sarcomas arise from mesenchymal cells (muscle, fat, fibrous, vascular). Risk factors: prior radiation, genetic syndromes (Li-Fraumeni, NF1), chronic lymphedema, but most are sporadic. They present as painless enlarging masses; pain usually indicates neurovascular involvement or rapidly growing tumor. On MRI they show heterogeneity due to hemorrhage, necrosis, cystic change.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Algorithm<br /></br>After core-needle biopsy confirms sarcoma:<br /></br>• Staging: CT chest ± abdomen/pelvis (hematogenous spread, lungs first).<br /></br>• Multidisciplinary discussion (surgical oncology, ortho onc, radiation oncology).<br /></br>• Neoadjuvant RT or chemo for large/high-grade lesions, then limb-sparing wide excision with clear margins.<br /></br>• Adjuvant therapy as indicated by grade, size, margins.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “CORE first, CUT later” → Core biopsy precedes wide resection.<br /></br>• 5-5-Deep rule for sarcoma suspicion.<br /></br>• MRI = “Map it”; Biopsy = “Name it”; Surgery = “Nuke it.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>A. CT scan of the thigh: Provides additional anatomic detail but still lacks tissue diagnosis; MRI already gives superior soft-tissue contrast. CT is used for staging (chest) after diagnosis, not for primary tissue confirmation.  </p></br><p>C. Ultrasonography: Good for cystic vs solid and guiding aspiration, but poor at delineating extent within muscle compartments; not adequate for pre-operative mapping of a sarcoma.  </p></br><p>D. Wide resection of the mass (excisional biopsy): Contraindicated before diagnosis. Risk of inadequate margins and tumor seeding; may necessitate more extensive re-resection or amputation.  </p></br><p>E. Observation and reevaluation in 1 month: Delays diagnosis of a potentially aggressive malignancy and violates the “5-5-Deep” rule.  </p></br><p>───────────────────</p>
191. A 14-year-old boy who has sickle cell trait is brought to the emergency department because of a 1-day history of severe pain in his left thigh and knee. He has been unable to bear weight on his left leg since the pain began. He has not had swelling, locking, or "catching" of his knee. Six days ago, he was evaluated in the emergency department after he twisted the knee during a basketball game. An x-ray of the knee taken at that time is shown. He was instructed to take a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug and apply ice packs to the knee. He also was given crutches and told to bear weight as tolerated. His symptoms improved during the next 3 days, and he discontinued use of the crutches and medication at that time. On arrival, he is walking with the assistance of crutches. He is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 95 kg (210 lb); BMI is 32 kg/m2. His temperature is 37 °C (98.6 °F), and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Examination of the left lower extremity shows joint line tenderness of the knee; there is no effusion. Range of motion of the left hip is limited by pain. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. His leukocyte count is 8000/mm3, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate is 10 mm/h. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. X-ray of the lumbar spine</br>b. X-ray of the left hip</br>c. Whole-body bone scan</br>d. MRI of the left knee</br>e. Aspiration of the left knee</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. X-ray of the left hip (anteroposterior &amp; frog-leg lateral views)</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Framework<br /></br>1. Age &amp; body habitus: 14-year-old, BMI 32 kg/m² → classic demographic for slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE).<br /></br>2. Pain location: “severe pain in his left thigh and knee” yet knee exam is essentially benign (no effusion, only mild joint-line tenderness). Knee pain out of proportion to knee findings = referred pain from the hip.<br /></br>3. Hip findings: limited range of motion due to pain → hip pathology.<br /></br>4. Weight-bearing difficulty &amp; sudden worsening → concern for an unstable SCFE (epiphysis acutely slipped further).<br /></br>5. Normal inflammatory markers (WBC 8 000/ESR 10) → makes septic arthritis/osteomyelitis unlikely.<br /></br>Key pattern: Obese adolescent + knee or thigh pain + limited hip ROM = obtain hip imaging to look for SCFE.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Whenever knee pain in an adolescent does not match the knee exam, immediately “scan up the limb” and think hip → order hip radiographs first.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning &amp; Core Takeaway<br /></br>SCFE is a Salter-Harris type I fracture through the proximal femoral physis causing posterior-inferior displacement of the femoral head. Early recognition is essential; delay risks avascular necrosis, chondrolysis, and early osteoarthritis. Diagnostic test of choice: AP and frog-leg lateral radiographs of the pelvis/hip.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls if this were a real encounter:<br /></br>• Onset, severity, inability to bear weight<br /></br>• Complete neurovascular exam of limb<br /></br>• Hip ROM findings (limited internal rotation)<br /></br>• Plan: urgent pelvic/hip X-ray; non-weight bearing until orthopedic evaluation</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield Points)<br /></br>• Risk factors: obesity, rapid growth spurt, endocrine disease (hypothyroidism, GH deficiency).<br /></br>• Mechanism: physis weakens during growth; shear stress causes epiphysis to slip posteriorly.<br /></br>• Presentation: dull hip, groin, thigh, or referred knee pain; limp; decreased internal rotation, abduction, flexion.<br /></br>• Complications: avascular necrosis of femoral head, chondrolysis.<br /></br>• Treatment: prompt surgical screw fixation across physis; non-weight bearing pre-op.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. X-ray of the lumbar spine<br /></br>   – Lumbosacral radiculopathy would present with neurologic deficits, back pain, positive straight-leg raise, not isolated hip ROM limitation.<br /></br>C. Whole-body bone scan<br /></br>   – Too broad, expensive, and low specificity; not first-line when a single anatomic site (hip) is suspected.<br /></br>D. MRI of the left knee<br /></br>   – Would assess meniscus/ligament injury, but knee exam lacks swelling/instability; hip signs point away from knee pathology.<br /></br>E. Aspiration of the left knee<br /></br>   – Used for suspected septic arthritis (fever, elevated WBC/ESR, effusion); none are present.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonic &amp; Memory Aid<br /></br>“SKIFFY kids are HIP-heavy”:<br /></br>S – Slipped<br /></br>K – k(c)apital<br /></br>F – femoral epiphysis<br /></br>F – fat (obese) teen<br /></br>Y – Youth presents with Knee pain from the HIP.</p></br><p>Visualize an ice-cream cone: the scoop (femoral head) sliding off the cone (neck) → reminds you to order the “picture” (X-ray) before it drops further.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In an overweight adolescent with knee pain that does not fit knee findings, think SCFE. The first and most appropriate step is an X-ray of the hip; prompt diagnosis allows urgent surgical pinning and prevents permanent joint damage.</p>
192. An 87-year-old woman who is a resident of a skilled nursing care facility is brought to the physician by staff because of fever for 1 day. A urinary catheter was placed 2 weeks ago because of urinary incontinence. She has dementia, Alzheimer type, and is unable to communicate verbally. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 86/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 120/74 mm Hg. Mucous membranes are moist and pink. Urinalysis shows:</br>Color cloudy brown</br>pH 8.8</br>Blood 2+</br>Glucose negative</br>Protein 2+</br>RBC too numerous to count</br>WBC 20–25/hpf</br>Nitrites 3+</br>Leukocyte esterase 3+</br>Bacteria many</br>A Gram stain of urine shows gram-negative bacilli. Which of the following measures is most likely to have prevented this patient’s current condition?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Use of incontinence briefs instead of the catheter</br>b. Changing the catheter daily</br>c. Cleaning the urethral orifice with povidone-iodine daily</br>d. Flushing the catheter with an antibiotic solution daily</br>e. Oral antibiotic prophylaxis</br>f. Oral oxybutynin therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Use of incontinence briefs instead of the catheter  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Elderly nursing-home resident with an indwelling Foley catheter for 2 weeks → classic set-up for catheter-associated urinary-tract infection (CAUTI).<br /></br>• Fever is the only new symptom (common in demented, non-verbal patients).<br /></br>• UA: cloudy, alkaline (pH 8.8), many WBCs, positive nitrite/leukocyte esterase, gram-negative rods → Escherichia coli or Proteus most likely.<br /></br>Key testable clue: “Catheter was placed 2 weeks ago.” Exams often test that the single most effective prevention strategy for CAUTI is to avoid or promptly remove unnecessary indwelling catheters.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>1. Whenever you see a Foley in the stem, ask “Was it really necessary?”<br /></br>2. On test day, remember the hierarchy of infection control:<br /></br>   • Eliminate the device &gt; Aseptic insertion &gt; Closed drainage &gt; Maintenance care.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Stepwise logic to the correct answer:<br /></br>1. Identify diagnosis – CAUTI.<br /></br>2. Recall prevention guidelines (CDC/HICPAC): “Do not use an indwelling catheter for routine incontinence; instead employ external devices or absorbent products.”<br /></br>3. Choose the option that eliminates the Foley altogether → incontinence briefs.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: The FIRST and MOST effective preventive measure for catheter-related UTI is to not insert (or to promptly remove) the catheter when it isn’t absolutely indicated.  </p></br><p>What would be documented if this were a real patient?<br /></br>• Reason for catheter insertion, reassessment of need, and plan for removal/alternate incontinence management.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Indwelling catheter = foreign body → bacteria ascend via biofilm along catheter surface.<br /></br>• Risk rises 3–7% per catheter-day; nearly universal bacteriuria by 30 days.<br /></br>• Alkaline urine (pH &gt;8) suggests urease-producing Proteus or Klebsiella, which can form struvite stones.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Notes<br /></br>Acute management (not asked): remove/replace catheter, obtain urine culture, start empiric antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone) tailored to sensitivities.  </p></br><p>Prevention hierarchy:<br /></br>1. Avoid catheters unless clear indications (urinary retention, accurate I’s &amp; O’s in critically ill, peri-op for select surgery, stage III/IV sacral ulcer with urine contamination).<br /></br>2. If needed, use aseptic insertion, closed drainage, unobstructed flow, daily review of necessity.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“No cath, no CAUTI.”<br /></br>Think “BE FAST” for catheter indications—<br /></br>B: Bladder outlet obstruction/retention<br /></br>E: Exact urine measurement in ICU<br /></br>F: Facilitate surgery/post-op healing<br /></br>A: Assist healing of open sacral wounds<br /></br>S: Severe disability needing prolonged immobilization<br /></br>T: Terminal/end-of-life comfort  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Changing the catheter daily – Biofilm forms within hours; daily replacement does NOT prevent infection and increases trauma/entry of new organisms.<br /></br>c. Cleaning the urethral orifice with povidone-iodine – Routine meatal cleansing beyond standard hygiene provides no added benefit.<br /></br>d. Flushing with antibiotic solution – Not recommended; fosters resistance and can irritate urothelium.<br /></br>e. Oral antibiotic prophylaxis – Leads to multidrug resistance and C. difficile; guidelines advise against routine prophylaxis.<br /></br>f. Oral oxybutynin – Treats urge incontinence but would not eliminate need for Foley in this cognitively impaired patient; still inferior to simply using briefs.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: All other options attempt to mitigate risk while leaving the root cause (the indwelling catheter) in place; only option A removes the primary risk factor.</p>
193. A 27-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 2, at 8 weeks' gestation comes to the physician for her first prenatal visit. She had an unknown sexually transmitted disease 1 year ago that was treated with penicillin. Her sexual partner was recently treated for nongonococcal urethritis. The uterus is consistent in size with an 8-week gestation. Speculum examination shows a heavy mucopurulent discharge and a friable cervix. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Acyclovir</br>b. Azithromycin</br>c. Cefazolin</br>d. Gentamicin</br>e. Metronidazole</br>f. Penicillin</br>g. Spectinomycin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Azithromycin  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>1. “Heavy mucopurulent discharge” + “friable cervix” → classic buzzwords for acute cervicitis.<br /></br>2. Partner “recently treated for nongonococcal urethritis” → most common cause is Chlamydia trachomatis.<br /></br>3. First-trimester pregnancy (8 weeks) → the chosen drug must be safe in pregnancy; doxycycline and fluoroquinolones are contraindicated.<br /></br>4. Prior STI “treated with penicillin” tells you she does not have a penicillin allergy and that the old infection was likely syphilis, but today’s findings match chlamydia, not syphilis.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive framework:<br /></br>• Ask: “What infection produces a mucopurulent cervicitis, is common in sexually active women, and is tied to ‘nongonococcal urethritis’ in men?” → Chlamydia.<br /></br>• Next: “What is the CDC/ACOG first-line drug for chlamydia in pregnancy?” → Azithromycin 1-g PO once (or amoxicillin 500 mg TID × 7 d). Pick the one listed.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Identify syndrome (cervicitis).<br /></br>2. Determine most likely pathogen (Chlamydia trachomatis).<br /></br>3. Cross-check gestational age &amp; drug safety profile.<br /></br>4. Choose recommended regimen (azithromycin single-dose).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway:<br /></br>Azithromycin is the first-line, single-dose therapy for chlamydial cervicitis during pregnancy.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real encounter):<br /></br>• Pelvic exam findings, gestational age, patient-reported exposure.<br /></br>• Tests ordered: NAAT for C. trachomatis &amp; N. gonorrhoeae, syphilis, HIV, hepatitis B/C.<br /></br>• Counseling: abstain 7 days after treatment, test-of-cure in 3–4 weeks, treat partner.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Chlamydia trachomatis serovars D–K infect columnar epithelium of endocervix → inflammatory exudate → friability &amp; mucopurulent discharge. Untreated infection in pregnancy can cause preterm labor, PPROM, postpartum endometritis, and neonatal conjunctivitis/pneumonia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pregnant patient with suspected chlamydia<br /></br>   • First-line: Azithromycin 1 g PO ×1.<br /></br>   • Alternative: Amoxicillin 500 mg PO TID ×7 d.<br /></br>   • Doxycycline is teratogenic (tooth discoloration, bone growth inhibition).  </li></br><li>Empiric dual therapy (ceftriaxone) is commonly given if risk of gonorrhea is high, but the NBME stem asks for “most appropriate pharmacotherapy,” i.e., the single agent specifically targeting chlamydia.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Follow-up: Re-test (NAAT) 3–4 weeks after completion because pregnancy increases risk of persistent infection.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Azithro for utero.”<br /></br>Visualize a pregnant uterus wearing an “A-Z” T-shirt handing out single pills—reminds you azithromycin single dose is preferred in pregnancy for chlamydia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Acyclovir – Treats HSV; cervicitis from HSV shows painful ulcers, not mucopurulent discharge with friability.  </p></br><p>C. Cefazolin – A first-generation cephalosporin used for peri-operative prophylaxis or MSSA infections; not effective against chlamydia.  </p></br><p>D. Gentamicin – Aminoglycoside for severe gram-negative infections or pyelonephritis in pregnancy; nephro/ototoxic and does not cover chlamydia.  </p></br><p>E. Metronidazole – Treats bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis (which would show thin gray discharge or frothy green discharge with motile trichomonads). Does not eradicate chlamydia.  </p></br><p>F. Penicillin – First-line for syphilis (Treponema pallidum). Syphilitic infection gives painless chancre or systemic findings, not mucopurulent cervicitis.  </p></br><p>G. Spectinomycin – Second-line drug for gonorrhea if ceftriaxone unavailable; no activity against chlamydia and not first choice in pregnancy.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Confusing treatment for gonorrhea (ceftriaxone) with chlamydia. Remember:<br /></br>• Gonorrhea → ceftriaxone IM.<br /></br>• Chlamydia → azithro (pregnancy) or doxy (non-pregnant).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Pearl  </p></br><p>In pregnancy, treat chlamydia with azithromycin 1-g PO once and always arrange test-of-cure due to higher risk of persistence and pregnancy complications.</p>
194. A 27-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. She feels well, has no history of serious illness, and takes no medications. Eight months ago, she delivered a healthy newborn at term following spontaneous vaginal delivery. She stopped breast-feeding 2 months ago. She is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 66 kg (145 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m2. Her blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Examination shows no axillary lymphadenopathy. The breasts are symmetric, and there are no skin changes. Examination of the right breast shows a 2-cm, well-defined, mobile, nontender mass in the upper inner quadrant; there are no masses in the left breast. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Follow-up examination in 6 months</br>b. Mammography of the right breast</br>c. Ultrasonography of the right breast</br>d. Core-needle biopsy of the mass</br>e. Excisional biopsy of the mass</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>C. Ultrasonography of the right breast  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Age &amp; reproductive status: 27-year-old, recently pregnant/lactating → breast tissue is dense.<br /></br>• Presenting finding: solitary 2-cm, well-circumscribed, mobile, nontender mass (classically benign‐appearing fibroadenoma, lactational adenoma, or galactocele).<br /></br>• No “red-flag” signs: no skin dimpling, nipple retraction, axillary nodes, systemic symptoms.<br /></br>• Key test-taking clue: Any NEW palpable mass must be imaged; algorithm depends primarily on age (&lt;30 vs ≥30).</p></br><p>Metacognitive framework for any breast mass:</br>1. Clinical exam.<br /></br>2. Age-appropriate imaging (U/S if &lt;30; mammogram ± U/S if ≥30).<br /></br>3. Image-guided tissue sampling if imaging is suspicious/indeterminate.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step 1  Is the mass real? → Yes (palpable on exam).<br /></br>Step 2  Choose initial imaging:<br /></br>      • &lt;30 y/o → breasts are radiographically dense → ultrasound is superior for distinguishing cystic vs solid lesions and guiding aspiration/biopsy.<br /></br>      • ≥30 y/o → start with diagnostic mammography ± targeted U/S.<br /></br>Step 3  Act on U/S findings:<br /></br>      • Simple cyst → aspirate/observe.<br /></br>      • Solid circumscribed (likely fibroadenoma) → core biopsy or short-interval follow-up.<br /></br>      • Suspicious/complex → image-guided core-needle biopsy.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: For women under 30 with a new palpable breast mass, targeted breast ultrasonography is the mandatory first diagnostic step.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Palpable 2-cm mobile, circumscribed mass R breast UIQ → order diagnostic breast U/S to characterize lesion.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (High Yield)</br>Common benign masses in young/post-partum women:<br /></br>• Fibroadenoma – hormonally sensitive, well-circumscribed, “rubbery”; often decreases after pregnancy/lactation.<br /></br>• Galactocele – milk retention cyst; occurs after cessation of breast-feeding.<br /></br>• Lactational adenoma – lobular hyperplasia during pregnancy/lactation.</p></br><p>All can mimic each other clinically; hence imaging is essential.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic Tests &amp; Management</br>Ultrasound findings guide next steps:<br /></br>• Anechoic, thin-walled → simple cyst → aspirate or 6-mo U/S.<br /></br>• Homogeneous solid, oval, parallel to skin → probable fibroadenoma → core biopsy vs short-interval follow-up.<br /></br>• Irregular borders, posterior shadowing, microcalcifications → BI-RADS 4/5 → core-needle biopsy.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memory Tips</br>“U-30 needs U/S first” (U for Under &amp; Ultrasound).<br /></br>Think of dense “white-out” mammogram snow in young breasts → U/S sees through the snow.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Follow-up examination in 6 months<br /></br>   • Delay violates the triple-assessment rule; any new palpable mass warrants immediate imaging.</p></br><p>B. Mammography of the right breast<br /></br>   • Mammography is less sensitive in dense breast tissue typical of women &lt;30. Used first line only at ≥30 or if U/S is indeterminate.</p></br><p>D. Core-needle biopsy of the mass<br /></br>   • Tissue sampling is not the FIRST step. Perform after imaging if the lesion is suspicious or indeterminate.</p></br><p>E. Excisional biopsy of the mass<br /></br>   • Most invasive; reserved when core biopsy is nondiagnostic or imaging shows discordant findings. Not initial management.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway</br>For women younger than 30 years with a new palpable breast mass, ALWAYS begin with targeted breast ultrasonography to characterize the lesion before considering biopsy or mammography.</p>
195. A previously healthy 18-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain for 24 hours. He has had a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight loss over the past month despite no change in appetite. He has not had fever, chills, or diarrhea and has not traveled recently. He is lethargic but arousable and oriented. His pulse is 118/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 98/58 mm Hg. Examination shows dry mucous membranes. Abdominal examination shows right upper quadrant tenderness with no guarding. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 16.3 g/dL</br>Leukocyte count 12,000/mm3</br>Serum</br>Na+ 135 mEq/L</br>Cl- 101 mEq/L</br>K+ 4.4 mEq/L</br>HCO3- 14 mEq/L</br>Urea nitrogen 48 mg/dL</br>Glucose 250 mg/dL</br>Creatinine 2.3 mg/dL</br></br>Arterial blood gas analysis on room air:</br>pH 7.32</br>Pco2 30 mm Hg</br>Po2 105 mm Hg</br></br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Ethylene glycol ingestion  </br>b. Diabetes insipidus  </br>c. Diabetic ketoacidosis  </br>d. Hyperosmolar coma  </br>e. Hypoaldosteronism  </br>f. Inappropriate secretion of ADH (vasopressin)  </br>g. Methanol ingestion</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>c. Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis</br>Key clues that should immediately raise the DKA flag:</br>• Young patient (18 yr) with recent 4-week unintentional weight loss → classic new-onset type 1 DM age group.<br /></br>• Gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diffuse/“RUQ” abdominal pain) → common in ketoacidosis and often mislabeled as an “acute abdomen.”<br /></br>• Dehydration signs: dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, mild hypotension, hemoconcentrated Hgb and BUN/Cr.<br /></br>• Labs:<br /></br>  – Moderate hyperglycemia (250 mg/dL).<br /></br>  – High-anion-gap metabolic acidosis: AG = 135 – 101 – 14 = 20 mEq/L (&gt;12).<br /></br>  – Low HCO₃⁻ (14 mEq/L).<br /></br>  – Respiratory compensation: PaCO₂ 30 mm Hg (Winters formula: expected PaCO₂ ≈ 1.5[HCO₃⁻] + 8 ±2 = 29 ±2 → appropriate).<br /></br>  – Potassium appears “normal” (4.4 mEq/L) but total-body K⁺ is depleted—classic in DKA.<br /></br>These three findings—hyperglycemia, AG metabolic acidosis, and (implied) ketosis—form the diagnostic triad of DKA.</p></br><p>Cognitive framework to approach similar questions<br /></br>1. Age + rapid weight loss + polyuria/polydipsia symptoms → think new-onset type 1 DM.<br /></br>2. Any combination of abdominal pain, vomiting, or “altered” vitals → check glucose and acid–base status.<br /></br>3. In AG metabolic acidosis, run the MUDPILES mnemonic; when glucose is high, DKA usually wins unless toxin history is strong.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-by-step chain:<br /></br>1. Stress (infection, missed insulin, idiopathic) → absolute insulin deficiency.<br /></br>2. ↑ Counter-regulatory hormones (glucagon, catecholamines) → lipolysis → hepatic free fatty acid oxidation → β-hydroxybutyrate &amp; acetoacetate (ketones).<br /></br>3. Ketone accumulation + lactic acid → AG metabolic acidosis.<br /></br>4. Hyperglycemia → osmotic diuresis → dehydration, pre-renal azotemia (↑BUN/Cr), electrolyte loss.<br /></br>5. Acidosis/volume depletion → abdominal pain, Kussmaul respirations, altered sensorium.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): mental status, vitals, finger-stick glucose, serum/urine ketones, BMP, ABG, anion gap, calculated serum osmolality, initial fluid bolus, insulin and potassium replacement plan.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)</br>Etiology: absolute insulin deficiency (type 1 DM &gt; type 2 under stress).<br /></br>Mechanism: unchecked gluconeogenesis &amp; lipolysis → hyperglycemia + ketosis.<br /></br>Clinical triad: hyperglycemia (&gt;250), AG acidosis (HCO₃⁻ &lt;18), ketonemia/ketonuria.<br /></br>Typical triggers: infection, missed insulin, MI, trauma.</p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests</br>1. Immediate: large-volume isotonic saline (NS) to restore perfusion.<br /></br>2. Regular IV insulin infusion after K⁺ ≥3.3 mEq/L.<br /></br>3. Potassium repletion (even when serum K⁺ normal).<br /></br>4. Consider bicarbonate only if pH &lt;6.9.<br /></br>5. Search for precipitating cause (e.g., CBC, CXR, UA for infection).</p></br><p>Memorization Tips</br>“DKA is the 3 K’s”<br /></br>• Ketones<br /></br>• Kussmaul breathing<br /></br>• K+ looks high but is actually low  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Ethylene glycol ingestion – Also causes AG metabolic acidosis but usually presents with flank pain, hematuria, calcium oxalate crystals, and no hyperglycemia. Osmolar gap high, not given here.<br /></br>b. Diabetes insipidus – Produces hypernatremia and dilute polyuria without acidosis or hyperglycemia.<br /></br>d. Hyperosmolar coma (HHS) – Severe hyperglycemia (&gt;600–1000 mg/dL), minimal/no ketosis, bicarbonate usually &gt;18, occurs in older type 2 patients.<br /></br>e. Hypoaldosteronism – Causes non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis with hyperkalemia, not AG acidosis and not hyperglycemia.<br /></br>f. SIADH – Hyponatremia with euvolemia; acid–base status typically normal.<br /></br>g. Methanol ingestion – AG metabolic acidosis + visual disturbances (“snow-storm” vision), usually no hyperglycemia.</p></br><p>Core Takeaway</br>Any young patient with weight loss plus moderate hyperglycemia and high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis = DKA until proven otherwise; start fluids, insulin, and K⁺ promptly.</p>
196. A 42 -year-old woman comes to the physician because of a nontender lump on the right side of her neck that she discovered 1 month ago. She has no history of serious illness and does not smoke. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 122/80 mm Hg. Examination shows a 2-cm mass in the right pretracheal area; the mass moves when the patient swallows. There is no lymphadenopathy. Which of the following studies is most likely to establish a diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Thyroid function tests</br>b. CT scan of the neck</br>c. 131I scintigraphy</br>d. Direct laryngoscopy</br>e. Fine-needle aspiration biopsy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Fine-needle aspiration biopsy (FNA)</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 42-year-old woman (thyroid nodules are common in women 30-60 yr)<br /></br>  – “Nontender lump … discovered 1 mo ago” → incidental solitary thyroid nodule<br /></br>  – 2-cm, pre-tracheal, moves with swallowing → classic physical description of a thyroid nodule<br /></br>  – Afebrile, normal vitals, no cervical adenopathy → no acute infection, no obvious spread  </p></br><p>• Pertinent positives: solitary, ≥1 cm, euthyroid (no symptoms, normal vitals).<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no pain, no systemic illness, no LAD → lowers suspicion for inflammatory or infectious process.  </p></br><p>Cognitive framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Recognize “thyroid nodule” (midline, moves with swallowing).<br /></br>2. Recall standard algorithm:<br /></br>   a. TSH ± ultrasound for risk stratification.<br /></br>   b. Diagnostic test that provides cytology = FNA.<br /></br>3. Question asks: “Which study is most likely to establish a diagnosis?” → pick the test that yields tissue.  </p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Why FNA?<br /></br>• Only direct method that provides cytologic/histologic material to distinguish benign colloid nodule vs malignancy (papillary, follicular, medullary, anaplastic).<br /></br>• Sensitivity &amp; specificity &gt;90 % for papillary &amp; medullary cancer.<br /></br>• Recommended for:<br /></br>  – Any solid thyroid nodule ≥1 cm with concerning US features OR<br /></br>  – Any nodule ≥2 cm even if low-risk.<br /></br>This patient’s 2-cm solitary nodule meets criteria.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Tissue is the issue—FNA is the definitive first-line diagnostic study for most palpable thyroid nodules.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real encounter)<br /></br>• Size, location, mobility, cervical LAD, compressive symptoms, risk factors (radiation, family hx).<br /></br>• Plan: thyroid US + US-guided FNA, discuss possible results and follow-up.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Thyroid nodules<br /></br>• 4-7 % of U.S. adults palpably, up to 50 % on US.<br /></br>• Etiologies: colloid nodules, cysts, adenoma, thyroiditis, carcinoma.<br /></br>• Malignancy risk factors: childhood head/neck radiation, rapid growth, hoarseness, firm/fixed, LAD, family hx MEN2 or thyroid cancer.  </p></br><p>Fine-needle aspiration<br /></br>• Obtains cells without full excision; minimal risk, office-based.<br /></br>• Bethesda system classifies cytology → guides surgery vs surveillance.  </p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Snapshot<br /></br>1. Initial labs: TSH ± free T4 (determine “hot” vs “cold”).<br /></br>2. Neck US for size, echogenicity, calcifications, margins.<br /></br>3. FNA for nodules meeting size/US criteria.<br /></br>4. If malignant or Bethesda III–VI → thyroidectomy or lobectomy depending on type/size.<br /></br>5. “Hot” (functioning) nodules are rarely malignant; may treat with radioiodine or surgery if symptomatic.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “Size decides the needle”: ≥1 cm + suspicious OR ≥2 cm → FNA.<br /></br>• “When it moves when you swallow, think thyroid; when you want to know what it is, stick a needle.”<br /></br>• Algorithm mnemonic “TUF”: TSH/Ultrasound first, then FNA for answers.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Thyroid function tests<br /></br>   – Identify hyper- or hypothyroidism but cannot differentiate benign vs malignant pathology; most malignant nodules are euthyroid.<br /></br>B. CT scan of the neck<br /></br>   – Useful for large goiters with retrosternal extension or pre-op planning but lacks cytologic information.<br /></br>C. 131I scintigraphy<br /></br>   – Distinguishes “hot” from “cold” nodules. Cold nodules are more often malignant, but the scan alone is not diagnostic; still need FNA. Current guidelines reserve scans for suppressed TSH levels.<br /></br>D. Direct laryngoscopy<br /></br>   – Evaluates vocal cords if hoarseness or prior neck surgery; does not diagnose the nodule’s nature.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>High-Yield Take-Home Message<br /></br>In an otherwise healthy patient with a palpable thyroid nodule, fine-needle aspiration is the single best test to establish a definitive diagnosis because it provides cytologic evidence of malignancy or benignity.</p>
197. An asymptomatic 57-year-old woman comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 40 years. She drinks wine occasionally at social events. She takes vitamin D daily. Examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit                             42 percent</br>Serum</br>  Ca2+                                11.5 mg/dL</br>  Urea nitrogen                       15 mg/dL</br>  Creatinine                          1 mg/dL</br>  Phosphorus                          2.8 mg/dL</br>  Parathyroid hormone, intact        12 pg/mL (N=10–65)</br>  Parathyroid hormone-related protein 12 pmol/L (N<1.3)</br>An x-ray of the chest shows a 2-cm nodule in the right upper lobe of the lung. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Metastatic carcinoma of the colon</br>b. Primary hyperparathyroidism</br>c. Sarcoidosis</br>d. Small cell carcinoma of the lung</br>e. Squamous cell carcinoma of the lung</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Squamous cell carcinoma of the lung  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• 57-year-old long-time smoker (40 pack-years) → strongly points toward smoking-related lung cancer.<br /></br>• Asymptomatic, found incidentally on chest x-ray → solitary 2-cm right-upper-lobe nodule.<br /></br>• Hypercalcemia (Ca2+ 11.5 mg/dL) with:<br /></br>  – Suppressed/low-normal intact PTH (12 pg/mL) → rules out primary hyperparathyroidism.<br /></br>  – Markedly elevated PTH-related protein (PTHrP 12 pmol/L; normal &lt;1.3) → classic paraneoplastic mediator.<br /></br>• Phosphorus low-normal (PTHrP acts like PTH on kidney → phosphaturia).  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: “Smoker + lung mass + high PTHrP + hypercalcemia” = Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy most commonly produced by squamous cell carcinoma of the lung.  </p></br><p>How to approach similar questions:<br /></br>1. Determine whether hypercalcemia is PTH-mediated (↑PTH) or non-PTH-mediated (↓PTH).<br /></br>2. If non-PTH-mediated, check PTHrP, vitamin D metabolites, malignancy/cancer history, granulomatous disease clues, medications.<br /></br>3. Correlate with risk factors (e.g., smoking) and imaging findings.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Hypercalcemia present.<br /></br>2. PTH suppressed → primary hyperparathyroidism unlikely.<br /></br>3. PTHrP massively elevated → humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy.<br /></br>4. CT/x-ray shows lung nodule in chronic smoker → lung cancer is source.<br /></br>5. Among lung cancers, squamous cell carcinoma is the classic producer of PTHrP (mnemonic: “S’s—Squamous = Stones (hypercalcemia)”).  </p></br><p>Take-home concept: Squamous cell carcinoma of the lung is the prototypical cause of PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Note degree of hypercalcemia, associated lab abnormalities, and plan for cancer work-up plus acute hypercalcemia management (IV saline, bisphosphonate).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Squamous cell carcinoma arises in central bronchi, strongly linked to smoking.<br /></br>• Tumor ectopically secretes PTHrP → binds PTH receptors →<br /></br>  – ↑Bone resorption (osteoclast activation).<br /></br>  – ↑Renal Ca2+ reabsorption, ↓Phosphate reabsorption.<br /></br>• Result: Hypercalcemia, low phosphorus, suppressed native PTH.<br /></br>• PTHrP does NOT stimulate renal 1-α-hydroxylase, so 1,25-OH vitamin D stays normal/low.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Notes  </p></br><p>Work-up:<br /></br>• Chest CT to further characterize nodule and stage disease.<br /></br>• Consider PET scan, bronchoscopy/biopsy.  </p></br><p>Acute hypercalcemia management:<br /></br>1. Vigorous IV isotonic saline.<br /></br>2. IV bisphosphonate (e.g., zoledronic acid) to inhibit osteoclasts.<br /></br>3. Calcitonin for rapid temporary lowering if Ca &gt;14 mg/dL or symptomatic.<br /></br>4. Definitive therapy → treat underlying tumor (resection, chemo-radiation depending on stage).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“4 S’s of squamous”:<br /></br>• Smoking<br /></br>• Sentral (central)<br /></br>• cavitating Syndrome (Pancoast, etc.)<br /></br>• Stones (PTHrP → hypercalcemia)  </p></br><p>Picture a stony (hypercalcemic) “S-quamous” dragon breathing smoke in the center of a lung.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Metastatic carcinoma of the colon<br /></br>   – Colon primaries rarely secrete PTHrP; would expect GI bleeding, iron-deficiency anemia, liver lesions rather than solitary lung nodule.  </p></br><p>b. Primary hyperparathyroidism<br /></br>   – Would show elevated PTH, often mildly ↑PTHrP, but here PTH is suppressed.  </p></br><p>c. Sarcoidosis<br /></br>   – Granulomas ↑1-α-hydroxylase → ↑1,25-OH-vitamin D, hypercalcemia with low-normal PTHrP. Patient lacks constitutional symptoms, erythema nodosum, bilateral hilar LAD.  </p></br><p>d. Small cell carcinoma of the lung<br /></br>   – Paraneoplastic syndromes: SIADH (↑ADH), ACTH/Cushing, Lambert-Eaton. PTHrP secretion is uncommon.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway:<br /></br>When you see a smoker with a lung mass and high PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia, think squamous cell carcinoma of the lung.</p>
198. A hospitalized 22-year-old woman with acute myelogenous leukemia undergoes evaluation prior to discharge. She has received induction chemotherapy and transfusions via a subcutaneous central venous catheter for the past 4 weeks. Two days ago, results of a complete blood count and bone marrow biopsy showed no abnormalities. Examination shows well-healed skin over the catheter. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. The physician reviews the patient's discharge plan with her and her parents. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation to avoid complications related to the catheter?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Avoiding bathing in a shower or bathtub</br>b. Hand washing with an antibacterial soap prior to contact with the catheter</br>c. Placement of a new sterile dressing at the site of access</br>d. Topical application of mupirocin at the site of access</br>e. Oral penicillin therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Hand washing with an antibacterial soap prior to contact with the catheter  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Foreign body: tunneled/implanted central venous catheter (CVC) still in-situ for future chemotherapy.<br /></br>• Well-healed skin; no current infection. Goal = prevent a new catheter-related bloodstream infection (CLABSI) in the outpatient setting.<br /></br>• Key clue: physician is giving discharge instructions (“prior to contact with the catheter”) → focuses on day-to-day handling rather than insertion technique or active infection treatment.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar questions<br /></br>1. Ask: “What is the most common and most easily modifiable source of infection?”<br /></br>2. Recall the central‐line prevention bundle: hand hygiene → maximal barrier at insertion → chlorhexidine skin prep → optimal site → daily line necessity review.<br /></br>3. When the line is already healed and will be manipulated at home, hand hygiene is the #1 intervention.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Catheter hubs are seeded primarily by organisms carried on the hands of patients, family, or health-care workers (Staphylococcus aureus, coagulase-negative staph, enteric gram-negatives).<br /></br>• Rigorous hand washing immediately before flushing or accessing the line removes transient flora and is proven to lower CLABSI rates.<br /></br>• Other measures (antibiotic prophylaxis, routine topical antibiotics, keeping the area dry) either lack efficacy or promote resistance.<br /></br>Take-home message: “Clean hands are the single most effective, evidence-based way to prevent central-line infections.”</p></br><p>Documentation (real-world)<br /></br>– “Patient and caregivers instructed in meticulous hand washing with antibacterial soap prior to any catheter handling. Teach-back confirmed.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• The catheter’s external hub/port can become colonized → intraluminal biofilm → bacteremia/fungemia.<br /></br>• Hand carriage of skin flora is the dominant route. Eliminating organisms at that interface prevents initial colonization.<br /></br>• Routine systemic or topical antibiotics foster resistant organisms and do not target biofilm once formed.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Considerations<br /></br>• No prophylactic antibiotics are recommended unless patient is profoundly neutropenic and febrile.<br /></br>• Dressing changes are only needed when the port is accessed or if soiled.<br /></br>• Patients may shower 24–48 h after insertion once exit site is sealed.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Five fingers, five steps” for CLABSI prevention:<br /></br>1. Fingers clean (hand hygiene) – MOST important.<br /></br>2. Full barrier at insertion.<br /></br>3. Foam (chlorhexidine).<br /></br>4. Farthest site feasible (subclavian &gt; jugular &gt; femoral).<br /></br>5. Frequent need review (remove line ASAP).</p></br><p>Mnemonic: “Clean Hands Keep Catheters Clear.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Avoiding bathing in a shower or bathtub<br /></br>• Unnecessary once the incision is healed; routine hygiene actually lowers skin colonization.</p></br><p>C. Placement of a new sterile dressing at the site of access<br /></br>• Implanted port has no external exit site that requires continuous dressing; dressings are changed only when accessed.</p></br><p>D. Topical application of mupirocin at the site of access<br /></br>• Mupirocin is used intranasally to decolonize MRSA carriers pre-op, not for routine catheter prophylaxis; promotes resistance.</p></br><p>E.
