Oral penicillin therapy<br /></br>• No evidence for continual systemic antibiotic prophylaxis; penicillin lacks activity against common catheter pathogens (e.g., MRSA, gram-negatives) and would breed resistance/adverse effects.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>The simplest, cheapest, and most evidence-based way to prevent catheter-related infection is meticulous hand washing with antibacterial soap before any catheter manipulation.</p>
199. A 57-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife because of a 3-hour history of progressive lethargy. He has a 5-year history of myasthenia gravis; thymectomy did not improve his symptoms. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 30 years and drinks one glass of wine daily. Current medications include prednisone and azathioprine. On arrival, he is lethargic and difficult to arouse. Examination shows marked bilateral ptosis. Breath sounds are slightly decreased with no wheezes or crackles. Arterial blood gas analysis on room air shows:</br>pH      7.19</br>Pco2    70 mm Hg</br>Po2     70 mm Hg</br>HCO3-   25 mEq/L</br>Which of the following is the most likely acid-base disorder?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Acute respiratory failure only</br>b. Chronic respiratory failure only</br>c. Chronic respiratory failure with an acute exacerbation</br>d. Metabolic acidosis</br>e. Metabolic alkalosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Acute respiratory failure only  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues in the stem<br /></br>• “3-hour history of progressive lethargy” – very acute onset.<br /></br>• Known myasthenia gravis on prednisone/azathioprine – prone to myasthenic crisis → diaphragm &amp; intercostal weakness → alveolar hypoventilation.<br /></br>• Marked bilateral ptosis – supports neuromuscular exacerbation.<br /></br>• ABG: pH 7.19 (marked acidemia) + PCO₂ 70 mm Hg (severe hypercapnia) + HCO₃⁻ 25 mEq/L (essentially normal).<br /></br>• PO₂ 70 mm Hg – mild hypoxemia, typical of hypoventilation (no intrinsic lung dz on exam).</p></br><p>Pertinent positives: acute neuromuscular trigger, severe hypercapnia, very low pH.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: No chronic lung findings, no elevated bicarbonate that would suggest renal compensation over days.</p></br><p>Framework for similar questions<br /></br>1. Decide primary disturbance (look at pH &amp; CO₂).<br /></br>2. Apply compensation formulas.<br /></br>   • Acute respiratory acidosis: HCO₃⁻ ↑ 1 mEq/L for every 10 mm Hg ↑ in PCO₂ above 40.<br /></br>   • Chronic respiratory acidosis (&gt;3–5 days): HCO₃⁻ ↑ 3–4 mEq/L per 10 mm Hg.<br /></br>3. Compare expected vs measured HCO₃⁻ to classify as acute vs chronic.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-by-step thought process<br /></br>1. Primary disorder = respiratory acidosis (low pH, high CO₂).<br /></br>2. ΔPCO₂ = 70 – 40 = 30 mm Hg.<br /></br>3. Expected HCO₃⁻ rise<br /></br>   • Acute: 1 mEq/L × 3 = +3 → 24 + 3 ≈ 27 mEq/L.<br /></br>   • Chronic: 3–4 mEq/L × 3 = +9–12 → 33–36 mEq/L.<br /></br>4. Actual HCO₃⁻ = 25 mEq/L (≈ baseline).<br /></br>5. Much closer to the acute prediction and far below the chronic prediction → acute respiratory failure without chronic compensation.</p></br><p>Take-home message: A normal/slightly elevated HCO₃⁻ with a markedly elevated CO₂ and low pH indicates an acute, not chronic, ventilatory problem.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient): mental status, neuromuscular strength, serial vital capacity, ABG results, plan for intubation &amp; IVIG/plasmapheresis for myasthenic crisis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Myasthenic crisis → antibody-mediated blockade of nicotinic ACh receptors → fatigable skeletal and respiratory muscle weakness → hypoventilation → CO₂ retention → rapid fall in pH (respiratory acidosis).<br /></br>Renal compensation (↑ HCO₃⁻ reabsorption) takes 48–72 h; thus in a 3-hour event the kidneys have not had time to correct.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Immediate: protect airway, consider intubation/mechanical ventilation.<br /></br>Treat crisis: IVIG or plasmapheresis + high-dose corticosteroids (already on prednisone, may need escalation). Search for precipitants (infection, drugs).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“1 &amp; 3 rule” for respiratory acidosis:<br /></br>• Acute → HCO₃⁻ rises 1 per 10 CO₂.<br /></br>• Chronic → HCO₃⁻ rises 3 per 10 CO₂.<br /></br>(For alkalosis think “2 &amp; 4”.)</p></br><p>Visual: picture a speedometer—short trip (acute) moves the bicarbonate needle only a little; long road trip (chronic) lets the needle climb much higher.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Chronic respiratory failure only<br /></br>• Would show HCO₃⁻ ≈ 33–36 mEq/L and pH closer to normal (7.32–7.36). Not seen here.</p></br><p>c. Chronic respiratory failure with an acute exacerbation<br /></br>• Mixed picture: very high HCO₃⁻ from chronic + further drop in pH from acute CO₂ spike. Again, HCO₃⁻ here is near normal.</p></br><p>d. Metabolic acidosis<br /></br>• Primary metabolic acidosis gives ↓ pH, ↓ HCO₃⁻, and respiratory compensation (↓ CO₂). Our CO₂ is markedly increased, not decreased.</p></br><p>e. Metabolic alkalosis<br /></br>• Would have elevated pH and elevated HCO₃⁻, opposite of the vignette findings.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core concept: In pure acute respiratory acidosis the kidneys have not yet raised bicarbonate—look for a severely low pH, very high CO₂, and near-normal HCO₃⁻.</p>
200. A previously healthy 24-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of severe abdominal pain for 4 hours. The pain began after she ate lunch following an aerobics class. She is not sexually active. Her temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), and pulse is 120/min. Abdominal examination shows left lower quadrant tenderness and rebound to deep palpation. Pelvic examination shows an exquisitely tender left ovary; the introitus is normal. There is no discharge in the vaginal vault and no cervical motion tenderness. Her leukocyte count is 15,300/mm^3 (80 percent segmented neutrophils, 2 percent eosinophils, 15 percent lymphocytes, and 3 percent monocytes). Urinalysis is within normal limits. In addition to measurement of serum β-hCG concentration, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. X-ray of the abdomen</br>b. Hysterosalpingography</br>c. Transvaginal ultrasonography</br>d. CT scan of the abdomen</br>e. Culdocentesis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Transvaginal ultrasonography  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Cognitive Framework<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • Sudden, severe unilateral (left-sided) lower-quadrant pain that began “after she ate lunch following an aerobics class” → classic trigger for ovarian torsion (sudden change in body position).<br /></br>   • Exquisitely tender left ovary on bimanual exam; no vaginal discharge, no cervical motion tenderness → argues against PID.<br /></br>   • Afebrile-to-low-grade fever (38.2 °C) and leukocytosis → inflammatory response common with torsion or ovarian necrosis; not specific for GI causes.<br /></br>   • Not sexually active → ectopic pregnancy or PID less likely, yet β-hCG is still checked in every reproductive-age patient with acute pelvic pain.<br /></br>   • Normal urinalysis → less likely UTI or nephrolithiasis.  </p></br><ol></br><li>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   “Acute female pelvic pain” differential = 5 big buckets:<br /></br>   A. Gynecologic (torsion, ruptured cyst, ectopic, PID, endometriosis)<br /></br>   B. GI (appendicitis, diverticulitis)<br /></br>   C. Urinary (stones, infection)<br /></br>   D. Vascular (AAA, ischemia)<br /></br>   E. Pregnancy-related (always r/o with β-hCG)  </li></br></ol></br><p>First branch point after pregnancy test → imaging. For adnexal pathology, high-resolution transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) with Doppler flow is the test of choice.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic to reach answer C:<br /></br>1. Rule out pregnancy → obtain serum β-hCG (already planned in stem).<br /></br>2. Next, evaluate adnexa directly because pelvic exam localizes pain to the ovary.<br /></br>3. Most sensitive, readily available modality for ovarian/tubal pathology = TVUS.<br /></br>   • Can identify an enlarged, edematous ovary ± absent or decreased Doppler flow consistent with torsion.<br /></br>   • Also detects hemorrhagic or ruptured cyst, tubo-ovarian abscess, or ectopic pregnancy if present.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In any reproductive-aged woman with acute unilateral adnexal pain, transvaginal ultrasound (with color Doppler) is the first-line diagnostic test after pregnancy has been excluded.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): onset/character of pain, pregnancy test result, pelvic exam findings, hemodynamic stability, decision for emergent pelvic ultrasound.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review – Ovarian Torsion<br /></br>• Etiology: twisting of infundibulopelvic ligament → compromise of venous/lymphatic outflow, then arterial inflow → ischemia.<br /></br>• Risk factors: ovarian cysts or masses &gt;5 cm (esp. teratoma), pregnancy, ovulation induction, prior torsion.<br /></br>• Clinical triad: acute unilateral pelvic pain, adnexal tenderness, nausea/vomiting ± low-grade fever/leukocytosis.<br /></br>• Complication: ovarian necrosis → infertility; requires prompt laparoscopy for detorsion ± oophoropexy.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Algorithm<br /></br>1. Pregnancy test → negative.<br /></br>2. TVUS with Doppler:<br /></br>   • Enlarged ovary (&gt;4 cm), peripheral follicles, decreased or absent blood flow → torsion likely.<br /></br>3. Surgical management: emergent diagnostic laparoscopy with detorsion.<br /></br>   • If ovary nonviable → oophorectomy.<br /></br>   • Fix contralateral ovary if recurrent risk.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid<br /></br>“Twist &amp; T(V)US”<br /></br>Twist (torsion) of the ovary → Think TransVaginal UltraSound first.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Plain abdominal X-ray<br /></br>   • Good for obstruction, perforation (free air), or radio-opaque stones, but poor soft-tissue resolution for adnexal structures.  </p></br><p>B. Hysterosalpingography<br /></br>   • Contrast study of uterine cavity/tubes used in infertility evaluation; not for acute abdomen.  </p></br><p>D. CT scan of abdomen<br /></br>   • CT pelvis can show torsion, but greater radiation, slower to obtain, and lower sensitivity than Doppler TVUS for adnexal blood flow. Reserve if US nondiagnostic or GI process suspected.  </p></br><p>E. Culdocentesis<br /></br>   • Historical test to detect hemoperitoneum from ruptured ectopic pregnancy; largely obsolete since ultrasound became widely available.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Jumping to CT because of leukocytosis and fever. Always match imaging modality to the organ system that exam findings pinpoint.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>After ruling out pregnancy, transvaginal ultrasound with Doppler flow is the immediate, most appropriate diagnostic step for a woman with acute unilateral adnexal pain suspicious for ovarian torsion.</p>
